topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Communication Engineering** is the branch of electrical and electronics engineering that deals with the **transmission, reception, and processing of information** between a sender (source) and a receiver (destination) over a physical medium, using electrical, electromagnetic, or optical signals.

A **Communication System** is the ensemble of devices, circuits, and the propagation medium that together enable the faithful transfer of information from one point in space and time to another. The fundamental aim of any communication system is to exchange information with **maximum fidelity, minimum delay, and optimal utilization of resources (bandwidth and power)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The introductory module establishes the foundation by covering the generic block diagram of a communication system, classification of communication systems, and the absolute necessity of **modulation** in modern radio communication. Mastery of this topic is a pre-requisite for AM, FM, and Pulse Modulation (Modules 2 & 3).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two-Person Conversation Analogy**
>
> Imagine Person A (the **Information Source**) wanting to tell a story to Person B (the **Destination**), but they are separated by a noisy construction site (the **Channel / Medium**).
>
> 1. Person A's thoughts exist only in their brain → they cannot physically cross the noise.
> 2. A **Microphone (Input Transducer)** converts the spoken voice into an electrical signal.
> 3. A **Transmitter** then imprints this electrical information onto a high-frequency carrier wave (analogous to putting a letter into an envelope), making it robust against the noise and capable of long-distance travel.
> 4. The signal travels through air/wire (**Channel**) — getting corrupted slightly by noise.
> 5. A **Receiver** filters the weak, noisy signal, extracts the information, and amplifies it.
> 6. A **Speaker (Output Transducer)** converts the electrical signal back into sound waves that Person B can hear.
>
> The core insight: **Communication Engineering is essentially the science of fighting noise and distance while preserving the original message.**

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Foundation for the entire course)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | $BW$ | Range of frequencies occupied by the signal/channel | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| **Signal Power** | $P_s$ | Power carried by the message signal | Watts (W) / dBm |
| **Noise Power** | $P_n$ | Unwanted random electrical energy | Watts (W) / dBm |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $SNR$ | Ratio of signal power to noise power | Dimensionless (dB) |
| **Channel Capacity** | $C$ | Maximum rate of reliable information transfer | **bits per second (bps)** |
| **Bit Duration** | $T_b$ | Time taken to transmit one bit | Seconds (s) |
| **Bit Rate** | $R_b$ | Number of bits transmitted per second | **bits per second (bps)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Elements of a Generic Communication System

A communication system is built from seven canonical blocks. Each block has a strictly defined role:

1. **Information Source** — Originates the message. It may be analog (voice, video, temperature sensor) or digital (computer data, telemetry). The output is referred to as the **message signal** $m(t)$.
2. **Input Transducer** — Converts the physical form of the source (sound, light, pressure) into an equivalent **electrical signal**. Example: Microphone, photodiode, thermocouple.
3. **Transmitter** — Performs signal conditioning (amplification, filtering) and **modulation** to shift the message into a frequency band suitable for the channel. It also boosts power for transmission.
4. **Channel** — The physical medium connecting transmitter and receiver. It introduces **attenuation, distortion, interference, and noise**.
5. **Noise Source** — Injected throughout the channel. Examples: Thermal noise, shot noise, atmospheric noise, industrial interference.
6. **Receiver** — Extracts the original message from the modulated, noisy carrier. It performs **demodulation, amplification, and filtering**.
7. **Output Transducer** — Converts the recovered electrical signal back into its original physical form (speaker, display, motor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Engineering Battles:** Every communication system is designed to win three simultaneous battles:
> 1. **Attenuation** — Signal weakening over distance → solved by *amplification*.
> 2. **Noise** — Random corruption → solved by *modulation + filtering + coding*.
> 3. **Bandwidth Limitation** — Finite spectral room → solved by *multiplexing and efficient modulation schemes*.

### 2.2 Types of Communication Systems

| Classification | Sub-Types | Key Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **By Signal Nature** | Analog Communication | Message varies continuously; infinite amplitude levels |
| | Digital Communication | Message is discrete (binary); superior noise immunity |
| **By Transmission Mode** | Simplex | One-way (e.g., radio broadcast) |
| | Half-Duplex | Two-way, but not simultaneous (e.g., walkie-talkie) |
| | Full-Duplex | Simultaneous two-way (e.g., telephone) |
| **By Medium** | Wired (Line) | Twisted pair, coaxial, optical fiber |
| | Wireless (Radio) | Free-space propagation; requires antennas |
| **By Modulation** | Continuous Wave (CW) | AM, FM, PM |
| | Pulse Modulation | PAM, PWM, PPM, PCM |

### 2.3 The Critical Need for Modulation

Modulation is **not optional** in radio communication. It is a fundamental requirement for the following reasons:

- **Antenna Size Reduction** — For efficient radiation, an antenna must be at least $\lambda/4$ long, where $\lambda = c/f$. A 3 kHz voice signal would require an antenna of **25 km** — practically impossible. Modulation shifts the frequency to a higher carrier $f_c$, making the antenna physically small.
- **Multiplexing** — Multiple low-frequency signals (e.g., many radio stations) can be shifted to non-overlapping frequency bands and transmitted simultaneously over a single channel (FDM).
- **Improved Noise Immunity** — AC-coupled amplifiers reject low-frequency drift and 1/f noise. Shifting the message to a higher band avoids these impairments.
- **Power Efficiency** — Transmission at higher frequencies can be focused into narrow beams, increasing effective radiated power and reducing wastage.
- **Assignment of Radio Spectrum** — Regulatory bodies (e.g., FCC, ITU) allocate specific frequency bands. Modulation places signals into these assigned bands.

### 2.4 The Electromagnetic Spectrum (Allocated Radio Bands)

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VLF** (Very Low Frequency) | 3 – 30 kHz | 100 – 10 km | Submarine communication, navigation |
| **LF** (Low Frequency) | 30 – 300 kHz | 10 – 1 km | AM broadcast (long wave) |
| **MF** (Medium Frequency) | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | 1 km – 100 m | AM radio broadcast (540–1640 kHz) |
| **HF** (High Frequency) | 3 – 30 MHz | 100 – 10 m | Shortwave radio, amateur radio |
| **VHF** (Very High Frequency) | 30 – 300 MHz | 10 – 1 m | FM radio, TV broadcast |
| **UHF** (Ultra High Frequency) | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 m – 10 cm | TV, mobile, GPS, Wi-Fi |
| **SHF** (Super High Frequency) | 3 – 30 GHz | 10 – 1 cm | Radar, satellite, 5G mmWave |
| **EHF** (Extremely High Frequency) | 30 – 300 GHz | 1 cm – 1 mm | mmWave, advanced radar |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **analytical backbone** of the entire Communication Engineering course. Memorize these for Module 1 and beyond.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ | Standard sinusoidal message signal |
| 2 | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | High-frequency carrier signal |
| 3 | $s(t) = [A_c + m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Generic AM (DSB-LC) modulated wave |
| 4 | $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Modulation index (depth) for AM |
| 5 | $BW_{AM} = 2 f_m$ | Bandwidth of conventional AM (DSB-LC) |
| 6 | $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | Total transmitted power in AM |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$ | Transmission efficiency of AM |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength–frequency relation ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) |
| 9 | $L_{ant} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4} = \dfrac{c}{4f}$ | Quarter-wave antenna length |
| 10 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | SNR in decibels |
| 11 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon–Hartley channel capacity (bps) |
| 12 | $N_0 = kT$ | Thermal noise PSD (W/Hz), $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |

**Real-World Utility:** The Shannon-Hartley theorem (Formula 11) is the **fundamental limit** of all modern digital systems — from 4G LTE to undersea optical cables. It mathematically proves that you cannot transmit data faster than the channel capacity $C$ without errors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Mathematical Origin of Modulation — Why Antennas Demand High Frequencies

**Problem Statement:** A human voice has a maximum frequency of $f_m = 3\,\text{kHz}$. Compute the minimum antenna length required to radiate this signal efficiently. Then show how modulation solves the problem.

**Step 1: Define the radiated wavelength.**
For a signal of frequency $f$, the wavelength in free space is given by the universal wave equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$ is the **speed of light in vacuum**.

**Step 2: State the antenna efficiency condition.**
For an efficient quarter-wave monopole antenna, the physical length must be at least:

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{4}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the voice frequency directly.**

$$L = \frac{c}{4 f_m} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 3 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the result.**

$$L = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.2 \times 10^4} = 25{,}000\,\text{m} = 25\,\text{km}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**
A 25 km antenna is **physically impossible** for any portable or even fixed device. This proves that audio signals **cannot be radiated directly** as electromagnetic waves.

**Step 6: Apply modulation to solve the problem.**
Let the message $m(t)$ modulate a carrier at $f_c = 1\,\text{MHz}$ (typical AM broadcast band). The new wavelength becomes:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{c}{f_c} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1 \times 10^6} = 300\,\text{m}$$

The new required antenna length is:

$$L = \frac{\lambda_c}{4} = \frac{300}{4} = 75\,\text{m}$$

This is still long, but the same voice signal radiated at **100 MHz (FM band)** would require:

$$L = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 100 \times 10^6} = 0.75\,\text{m}$$

This is the size of a typical **car radio antenna** — practically realizable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the Derivation:** Modulation shifts the message from a low frequency $f_m$ to a high carrier frequency $f_c$, thereby reducing the required antenna length by a factor of $f_c / f_m$. For voice: this ratio is $\sim 333\times$ at 1 MHz, and $\sim 33{,}333\times$ at 100 MHz.

### 3.2 Derivation: Transmission Efficiency of Conventional AM

**Step 1: Write the AM signal.**

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2: Expand using the trigonometric identity** $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_c \mu}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \tfrac{A_c \mu}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the three spectral components.**

| Component | Frequency | Amplitude | Power (across $R$ ohms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier | $f_c$ | $A_c$ | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ |
| Lower Sideband (LSB) | $f_c - f_m$ | $\tfrac{A_c \mu}{2}$ | $P_{LSB} = \dfrac{A_c^2 \mu^2}{8R}$ |
| Upper Sideband (USB) | $f_c + f_m$ | $\tfrac{A_c \mu}{2}$ | $P_{USB} = \dfrac{A_c^2 \mu^2}{8R}$ |

**Step 4: Compute the total transmitted power.**

$$P_t = P_c + P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + 2 \cdot \frac{A_c^2 \mu^2}{8R} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

Since $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$, this can be compactly written as:

$$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Compute the useful sideband power.**

$$P_{SB} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2}$$

**Step 6: Define and compute transmission efficiency $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful power (sidebands)}}{\text{Total transmitted power}} = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_c \cdot \mu^2 / 2}{P_c(1 + \mu^2 / 2)} = \frac{\mu^2 / 2}{1 + \mu^2 / 2} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 7: Evaluate boundary cases.**

- For $\mu = 0$ (no modulation): $\eta = 0\%$ — All power is in the useless carrier.
- For $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation): $\eta = 1/3 \approx 33.33\%$ — Maximum practical efficiency.
- For $\mu > 1$ (over-modulation): Illegal; causes severe distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This result proves a fundamental limitation of conventional AM — at best, **two-thirds of the transmitted power is wasted** in the carrier. This is precisely why **DSB-SC, SSB, and VSB** modulation schemes were developed (covered later in Module 2).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Communication Modes

| Parameter | Analog | Digital |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Nature** | Continuous amplitude & time | Discrete levels (bits) |
| **Noise Immunity** | Poor (noise is additive, irreversible) | Excellent (regenerative repeaters) |
| **Bandwidth** | Low (e.g., voice: 4 kHz) | Higher (e.g., PCM voice: 64 kbps) |
| **Hardware** | Simple, cheap | Complex (ADC, DSP, sync needed) |
| **Security** | Difficult to encrypt | Easy encryption (AES, RSA) |
| **Error Performance** | SNR-based | BER-based ($P_e$) |
| **Storage** | Magnetic tape (degrades) | Digital memory (lossless copy) |
| **Example** | AM/FM Radio | GSM, 4G/5G, Wi-Fi |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Information Source] --> nodeT1[Input Transducer]
    nodeT1 --> nodeTX[Transmitter]
    nodeTX --> nodeCH[Channel]
    nodeNS[Noise Source] -.-> nodeCH
    nodeCH --> nodeRX[Receiver]
    nodeRX --> nodeT2[Output Transducer]
    nodeT2 --> nodeD[Destination]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** depict the forward flow of the message signal from source to destination.
- The **dashed arrow** from the Noise Source indicates parasitic injection of unwanted signals into the channel — this is the universal enemy of all communication systems.

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIn[Message Signal m of t] --> nodeAmp[Preamplifier]
    nodeAmp --> nodeFilt[Bandpass Filter]
    nodeFilt --> nodeMod[Modulator]
    nodeOSC[Carrier Oscillator] --> nodeMod
    nodeMod --> nodePA[Power Amplifier]
    nodePA --> nodeAntA[Transmit Antenna]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The message is first amplified and filtered, then mixed with a stable carrier inside the modulator. The modulated wave is boosted by a power amplifier before being radiated by the antenna.

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a Receiver (Superheterodyne)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAntB[Receive Antenna] --> nodeLNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    nodeLNA --> nodeRF1[RF Tuned Filter]
    nodeRF1 --> nodeMix[Frequency Mixer]
    nodeLO[Local Oscillator] --> nodeMix
    nodeMix --> nodeIF[IF Amplifier at fixed f_IF]
    nodeIF --> nodeDem[Demodulator]
    nodeDem --> nodeAF[Audio Amplifier]
    nodeAF --> nodeOut[Speaker Output]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical **superheterodyne receiver** used in nearly every AM/FM radio and television. The mixing with a local oscillator down-converts any incoming RF signal to a fixed intermediate frequency (IF), simplifying subsequent amplification and demodulation.

### 4.4 Modulation Concept — Frequency Domain Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLow[Baseband Message\nf near 0 to f_m] -->|Modulation Process| nodeHigh[Modulated Wave\ncentered at f_c with BW 2 f_m]
    nodeLow2[Baseband Voice\n0 to 4 kHz] -->|AM Modulation| nodeHigh2[AM Band Signal\n540 to 1640 kHz]
    nodeLow3[Baseband Video\n0 to 4.5 MHz] -->|FM Modulation| nodeHigh3[FM TV Signal\n88 to 108 MHz TV band]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Modulation performs a **frequency translation**, moving the low-frequency information spectrum up to a high-frequency band centered on $f_c$. The bandwidth is preserved (for AM) or expanded (for FM).

### 4.5 Electromagnetic Spectrum Overview (Communication Bands)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeELF[ELF and SLF\n3 to 300 Hz] --> nodeVF[VF and VLF\n300 Hz to 30 kHz]
    nodeVF --> nodeLF[LF and MF\n30 kHz to 3 MHz\nAM Radio]
    nodeLF --> nodeHF[HF\n3 to 30 MHz\nShortwave]
    nodeHF --> nodeVHF[VHF\n30 to 300 MHz\nFM Radio and TV]
    nodeVHF --> nodeUHF[UHF\n300 MHz to 3 GHz\nTV Mobile WiFi]
    nodeUHF --> nodeSHF[SHF\n3 to 30 GHz\nRadar Satellite]
    nodeSHF --> nodeEHF[EHF\n30 to 300 GHz\n5G mmWave]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The spectrum is a **finite, non-renewable resource** shared by all global communication. Regulatory bodies divide it into bands to prevent mutual interference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define a communication system. List its basic elements with a neat block diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A communication system is an arrangement of devices and the propagation medium that enables the transfer of information from a source to a destination with acceptable fidelity.

The **basic elements** of a communication system are:

1. **Information Source** – produces the message (voice, data, video).
2. **Input Transducer** – converts physical message into electrical form.
3. **Transmitter** – conditions and modulates the signal for transmission.
4. **Channel** – the physical medium (air, cable, fiber).
5. **Noise Source** – corrupts the signal during transmission.
6. **Receiver** – extracts and reconstructs the original message.
7. **Output Transducer** – converts electrical signal back to physical form.
8. **Destination** – the intended recipient of the information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark, List of elements = 1 mark, Block diagram = 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*State and briefly explain any **three** advantages of modulation in communication systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Reduction of antenna size** — By translating the message to a high-frequency carrier, the required $\lambda/4$ antenna length becomes physically realizable. Example: A 3 kHz audio signal needs a 25 km antenna; modulating onto 1 MHz reduces it to 75 m.
2. **Multiplexing capability** — Multiple messages can be shifted to non-overlapping frequency bands and transmitted over a single channel using Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM).
3. **Improved noise immunity** — Modulation shifts the signal away from low-frequency noise sources (e.g., 1/f noise, hum, dc drift) and permits the use of bandpass filtering to reject out-of-band interference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative acceptable answers:** Assignment of frequency spectrum by regulatory bodies, reduction of propagation losses, and improved power efficiency via narrow-beam directional transmission.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question in Module 1 carries an **internal choice**. Two fully worked-out alternative questions are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the elements of a generalized communication system. Explain the functions of the transmitter, channel, and receiver in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An audio signal of bandwidth 4 kHz modulates a 1 MHz carrier using conventional AM. Calculate:
- (i) The minimum antenna length required for efficient radiation.
- (ii) The transmission efficiency if modulation index $\mu = 0.8$.
- (iii) The total radiated power if $P_c = 1\,\text{kW}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The block diagram of a generalized communication system is given in Section 4.1 of these notes (drawn and described). Key functional descriptions:

- **Transmitter:** Performs three core tasks — (i) amplification of the weak baseband signal, (ii) modulation with a high-frequency carrier for channel compatibility, and (iii) power amplification for radiation. It may also include a bandpass filter to suppress out-of-emission harmonics.
- **Channel:** The physical medium (free space, coaxial cable, optical fiber, waveguide). It introduces (i) **attenuation** (loss over distance), (ii) **distortion** (due to non-flat frequency response), and (iii) **additive noise** (thermal, atmospheric).
- **Receiver:** The reverse of the transmitter. It performs (i) selective tuning and RF amplification, (ii) demodulation to retrieve the baseband message, (iii) further amplification to drive the output transducer, and (iv) noise filtering.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $f_m = 4\,\text{kHz}$, $f_c = 1\,\text{MHz} = 10^6\,\text{Hz}$, $\mu = 0.8$, $P_c = 1\,\text{kW} = 1000\,\text{W}$.

**(i) Minimum antenna length:**

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{c}{4 f_c} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 1 \times 10^6} = \frac{300}{4} = 75\,\text{m}$$

**(ii) Transmission efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\% = \frac{0.64}{2 + 0.64} \times 100\% = \frac{0.64}{2.64} \times 100\% \approx 24.24\%$$

**(iii) Total radiated power:**

$$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 1000\left(1 + \frac{0.64}{2}\right) = 1000 \times 1.32 = 1320\,\text{W} = 1.32\,\text{kW}$$

Useful sideband power: $P_{SB} = P_t \cdot \eta = 1320 \times 0.2424 = 320\,\text{W}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **electromagnetic spectrum** allocated for various communication services. Mention any **five** frequency bands with their typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **modulation index** for AM. Derive the expression for the **transmission efficiency** of a conventional AM wave. Hence calculate the efficiency when $\mu = 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The electromagnetic spectrum is the continuum of all possible electromagnetic wave frequencies from DC to cosmic rays ($\sim 10^{22}$ Hz). For communication purposes, the spectrum is divided into bands as tabulated in Section 2.4 of these notes.

**Five Key Frequency Bands:**

1. **MF (Medium Frequency, 300 kHz – 3 MHz):** AM radio broadcast (540–1640 kHz). Ground-wave propagation, follows Earth's curvature.
2. **HF (High Frequency, 3 – 30 MHz):** Shortwave radio. Sky-wave propagation via ionospheric reflection — used for long-distance international broadcast.
3. **VHF (Very High Frequency, 30 – 300 MHz):** FM radio (88–108 MHz), VHF TV channels (1–12), aircraft navigation (VOR, ILS).
4. **UHF (Ultra High Frequency, 300 MHz – 3 GHz):** Mobile cellular (GSM 900/1800 MHz), GPS (1.575 GHz), Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz), DTH TV.
5. **SHF (Super High Frequency, 3 – 30 GHz):** Satellite communication (C-band 4/6 GHz, Ku-band 12/14 GHz), radar, 5G mid-band.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition:** The **modulation index** $\mu$ of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

For distortionless demodulation, $\mu \leq 1$.

**Derivation of efficiency** is given in full in Section 3.2 of these notes. The final result is:

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$$

**Calculation for $\mu = 1$:**

$$\eta = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{3} \times 100\% \approx 33.33\%$$

**Interpretation:** At maximum legal modulation ($\mu = 1$), the **carrier still consumes 66.67%** of the total transmitted power while carrying **no information**. This is the main drawback of conventional AM.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 1 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing the noise block** in the block diagram — Examiners *expect* the noise source to be explicitly shown. Drawing only 6 blocks (omitting noise) is treated as incomplete. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — Antenna length in km vs. m, power in W vs. dBm. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark per quantity.**
> 3. **Confusing bandwidth of AM** ($2 f_m$, where $f_m$ is the message frequency) with $f_m$ itself. If the question says "audio signal of bandwidth 4 kHz", students often wrongly use $f_m = 2\,\text{kHz}$ instead of $f_m = 4\,\text{kHz}$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Not stating the constraint** $\mu \leq 1$ when defining modulation index — Examiners deduct for failing to mention distortionless condition.
> 5. **Efficiency formula** — Writing the formula correctly but computing the sideband power and total power separately shows deeper understanding. Examiners reward this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Introduction)**
>
> ✅ A communication system has **7 (+1 noise) blocks**: Source, Input Transducer, Transmitter, Channel, **Noise**, Receiver, Output Transducer, Destination.
>
> ✅ Communication modes: **Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex**.
>
> ✅ Signals can be **Analog (continuous)** or **Digital (discrete bits)**.
>
> ✅ **Modulation is mandatory** for the following reasons: small antenna size, multiplexing, noise immunity, spectrum allocation, and power efficiency.
>
> ✅ **Antenna length formula:** $L = \lambda/4 = c / (4 f)$. For audio at 3 kHz, $L \approx 25\,\text{km}$ — practically impossible without modulation.
>
> ✅ **AM modulation index:** $\mu = A_m / A_c$, with $\mu \leq 1$ to avoid distortion.
>
> ✅ **AM bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m$ (twice the highest message frequency).
>
> ✅ **AM total power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$.
>
> ✅ **AM efficiency:** $\eta = \mu^2 / (2 + \mu^2) \leq 33.33\%$ — the carrier is the "wasteful overhead" of conventional AM.
>
> ✅ **Standard carrier frequencies:** AM radio (540–1640 kHz), FM radio (88–108 MHz), Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz), 5G (sub-6 GHz and mmWave at 24–52 GHz).
>
> ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, Boltzmann's $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
>
> ✅ **Shannon's capacity limit:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the absolute ceiling on information rate for any channel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Communication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of Communication Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Elements of Communication Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Communication System** is a collection of interconnected devices, circuits, and components engineered to facilitate the transfer of information (voice, data, video, or any intelligence-bearing signal) from a **source (origin)** to a **destination (sink)** over a physical or wireless **transmission medium**, while compensating for the corrupting effects of **noise** and **channel impairments**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"An electrical communication system is a system that conveys information from one point to another via an electrical signal propagating through a transmission medium, employing a chain of functional blocks — Information Source → Input Transducer → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver → Output Transducer → Destination."*

The fundamental objective of any communication system is to **reproduce the input message at the output with an acceptable level of fidelity**, measured using parameters such as **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, **Bit Error Rate (BER)**, **Channel Capacity**, and **Distortion**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Telephone Game" Model

Imagine two friends, **Aakash** (in Delhi) and **Bhavana** (in Kerala), trying to share a story over a noisy college fest:

| Step | Real-World Action | Communication System Equivalent |
|------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | Aakash **thinks** of the story | **Information Source** (message signal) |
| 2 | His **voice box** converts thought to sound | **Input Transducer** (e.g., microphone) |
| 3 | He **shouts** into a megaphone | **Transmitter** (amplifier + modulator) |
| 4 | Sound travels through **air** | **Channel** (transmission medium) |
| 5 | Loud **music** interferes with his voice | **Noise** |
| 6 | Bhavana's friend **writes down** what she hears | **Receiver** (amplifier + demodulator) |
| 7 | She **reads** it aloud to Bhavana | **Output Transducer** (e.g., speaker) |
| 8 | Bhavana **understands** the story | **Destination** (sink) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Insight:** The transmitter *modifies* the original message (e.g., shifts it to a higher frequency band) so that it can propagate efficiently over the channel. The receiver *undoes* this transformation to recover the message. This entire orchestration is what we call a **Communication System**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Cardinal Goals of Communication (Shannon's Perspective)

Claude Shannon (1948) established that a communication system must simultaneously address three engineering trade-offs:

1. **Efficiency of Transmission** — How fast can information be sent? (Bits per second)
2. **Reliability of Reception** — How error-free is the recovered message? (BER)
3. **Power Economy** — How much transmitter power is needed? (Watts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Trade-off Equation (Shannon-Hartley Theorem):**
> $$\boxed{C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \ \text{bits/second}}$$
> where $C$ = Channel Capacity, $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio.

---

## 1.4 Block Diagram of a Generic Communication System

A standard communication system comprises **seven fundamental blocks** (often grouped into three subsystems: **Transmitter**, **Channel**, and **Receiver**):

```
┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
│INFORMATION│ → │  INPUT   │ → │ TRANSMITTER │ → │ CHANNEL  │ → │  RECEIVER   │ → │  OUTPUT   │ → │ DESTINATION │
│  SOURCE   │    │TRANSDUCER│    │ (Modulator) │    │(+ NOISE) │    │(Demodulator)│    │TRANSDUCER │    │   (SINK)    │
└──────────┘    └──────────┘    └─────────────┘    └──────────┘    └─────────────┘    └──────────┘    └─────────────┘
   m(t)            m̂(t)              s(t)           r(t)=s(t)+n(t)    m̂'(t)            y(t)              User
```

The **message signal $m(t)$** (baseband, low-frequency) is transformed by the transmitter into a **modulated signal $s(t)$** (bandpass, high-frequency), which after channel corruption is **recovered** by the receiver.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis of the Seven Building Blocks

## 2.1 Detailed Functional Breakdown

### 🔹 Block 1: Information Source

The **information source** generates the original intelligence to be transmitted. It can be:

- **Analog Source** — continuous amplitude & continuous time (e.g., human voice, music waveform, temperature sensor output)
- **Discrete Source** — finite alphabet (e.g., typed characters from a keyboard)
- **Digital Source** — bit streams (e.g., output of a computer's USB port)

The output of the source is a **message signal $m(t)$** characterized by:
- **Bandwidth $W$** — the range of frequencies it occupies (Hz)
- **Power $P_m$** — average signal power (Watts)
- **Dynamic Range** — ratio of largest to smallest signal amplitude (dB)

> [!NOTE]
> **Textbook Insight:** The human voice occupies a bandwidth of approximately **300 Hz to 3.4 kHz** (i.e., $W_{\text{voice}} \approx 3\text{ kHz}$), which is why telephone systems were historically designed with a 4 kHz channel spacing.

---

### 🔹 Block 2: Input Transducer

A **transducer** converts energy from one form to another. The *input transducer* converts the source's non-electrical form (sound, light, pressure) into an **electrical signal $m_{\text{elec}}(t)$**.

| Source Type | Input Transducer | Electrical Output |
|-------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Voice / Music | Microphone (dynamic / condenser) | Audio voltage (mV) |
| Image / Video | CCD / CMOS sensor | Analog video signal |
| Temperature | Thermistor / RTD | Resistance change → Voltage |
| Pressure | Strain gauge | Wheatstone bridge output |

**Key Performance Metrics of a Transducer:**
- **Sensitivity** — output per unit input (V/Pa for microphones)
- **Frequency Response** — flat response across the signal bandwidth
- **Linearity** — output ∝ input (no harmonic distortion)

---

### 🔹 Block 3: Transmitter

The **transmitter** performs **signal conditioning** to make the message suitable for the channel. Its sub-functions are:

1. **Amplification** — boosts weak transducer output to usable levels
2. **Modulation** — superimposes $m(t)$ onto a high-frequency **carrier** $c(t)$
3. **Coding / Encryption** — adds redundancy for error protection
4. **Filtering** — limits bandwidth to allocated spectrum
5. **Power Amplification** — final stage to drive the antenna

**General Transmitted Signal Form:**
$$s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

This is a generic form of **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**, where:
- $A_c$ = carrier amplitude
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency
- $m(t)$ = modulating (message) signal

---

### 🔹 Block 4: Channel (Transmission Medium)

The **channel** is the physical path between transmitter and receiver. It is classified as:

| Channel Type | Examples | Bandwidth | Attenuation |
|--------------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Wired (Guided)** | Twisted pair, Coaxial cable, Optical fiber | 1 MHz – THz | Low to moderate |
| **Wireless (Unguided)** | Free space, underwater acoustic, ionospheric sky-wave | kHz – GHz | High, path-loss dependent |

**The received signal** (before receiver processing) is:
$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t) + i(t)$$

where $n(t)$ is **noise** and $i(t)$ is **interference** from other users.

---

### 🔹 Block 5: Noise

**Noise** is any unwanted disturbance that obscures or interferes with the desired signal. It is the **fundamental physical limitation** on communication performance.

**Five Canonical Noise Types:**

| Noise Type | Origin | PSD Behavior |
|------------|--------|--------------|
| **Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist)** | Random electron motion in resistors | White — flat up to $\sim 10^{12}$ Hz |
| **Shot** | Discrete charge carrier flow in BJTs / diodes | White |
| **Flicker ($1/f$)** | Imperfections in semiconductor devices | Increases as $f \rightarrow 0$ |
| **Burst (Popcorn)** | Imperfections in ICs | Impulsive |
| **Cosmic / Atmospheric** | Solar radiation, lightning | High-frequency dominant |

**Thermal Noise Power (Johnson-Nyquist Formula):**
$$\boxed{P_n = k T B}$$
where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$ (Boltzmann's constant), $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz).

> At **room temperature** ($T_0 = 290 \ \text{K}$), $kT_0 = 4 \times 10^{-21} \ \text{W/Hz}$, which yields the famous **−174 dBm/Hz** noise floor.

---

### 🔹 Block 6: Receiver

The **receiver** performs the inverse of the transmitter's functions:
1. **Antenna + RF Amplifier** — captures weak signal, low-noise amplification (LNA)
2. **Mixer + Local Oscillator** — frequency down-conversion to IF
3. **IF Amplifier + Filter** — selectivity (bandpass filtering)
4. **Demodulator** — extracts $m(t)$ from modulated carrier
5. **Decoder / De-interleaver** — error correction
6. **Audio / Baseband Amplifier** — drives output transducer

**Receiver Output Signal:**
$$m_{\text{out}}(t) \approx m(t) + n_{\text{eff}}(t)$$
where $n_{\text{eff}}(t)$ is the noise *added* by the receiver's own front-end (dominated by LNA).

---

### 🔹 Block 7: Output Transducer

Converts the recovered electrical signal back to its original form:
- **Speaker** → sound waves
- **Display (LCD/LED)** → light
- **Actuator** → mechanical motion

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity | bits/s |
| 2 | $P_n = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | Watts |
| 3 | $N_0 = kT$ | Noise spectral density | W/Hz |
| 4 | $N_{\text{dBm}} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$ | Noise in dBm at 290 K | dBm |
| 5 | $s(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | AM signal | Volts |
| 6 | $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{sideband}}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | Modulation efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{\text{RMS}}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt$ | Signal power (1Ω load) | W |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB | dB |
| 9 | $G_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$ | Power gain in dB | dB |
| 10 | $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"SENDER — Source, Encoder, Noise, Demod, Error-correction, Receiver"* — walk through this in reverse at the receiver side.

---

## 2.3 Why Each Block is Indispensable — Engineering Justification

- **Why Modulate?** Three reasons: (i) **Antenna size** — efficient radiation requires antenna $\geq \lambda/10$, (ii) **Multiplexing** — many low-frequency signals can share one channel via frequency division, (iii) **Reduced noise/interference** — bandpass signals reject baseband noise.
- **Why Coding?** Adds **controlled redundancy** so errors can be detected/corrected at the receiver.
- **Why Match Impedances?** Maximum power transfer theorem requires source impedance = load impedance ($Z_s = Z_L^*$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity

We derive the maximum achievable data rate over a band-limited noisy channel.

### Step 1 — Define the Channel Model

Consider an **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel with:
- Bandlimited to $B$ Hz (i.e., $0 \leq f \leq B$)
- Received signal: $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$
- Signal power $\leq S$, Noise power $= N = N_0 B$ where $N_0$ is the two-sided PSD.

### Step 2 — Sampling Theorem Application

By the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**, a signal bandlimited to $B$ Hz is uniquely represented by samples spaced at intervals:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{2B}$$

The number of independent signal samples transmitted per second is therefore:

$$n_{\text{samples}} = 2B$$

### Step 3 — Quantization Levels Achievable

Each sample has a maximum amplitude of $\sqrt{S}$ and a noise jitter of $\sqrt{N}$. The number of distinguishable amplitude levels is:

$$M = \sqrt{\frac{S + N}{N}} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}$$

### Step 4 — Information Per Sample

Using the **logarithmic information measure** (Hartley, 1928), the number of bits per sample is:

$$H = \log_2(M) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \ \text{bits/sample}$$

### Step 5 — Total Channel Capacity

Multiplying bits per sample by samples per second:

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= n_{\text{samples}} \times H \\
  &= 2B \times \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
  &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \ \text{bits/second}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Physical Interpretation

- **Doubles $B$** → Capacity increases **linearly**.
- **Doubles $S$** → Capacity increases **logarithmically** (diminishing returns).
- **Infinite $S$** → $C \rightarrow \infty$ only in theory; in practice, $S$ is power-limited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noiseless Channel Limit ($N \rightarrow 0$):** $C \rightarrow \infty$, meaning infinite bandwidth is needed to send finite info noiselessly. This proves the *necessity* of noise-aware design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Noise Power Across a Resistor

We derive the Johnson-Nyquist noise formula using classical thermodynamics (equipartition theorem).

### Step 1 — Energy Equipartition

In thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, the average energy per degree of freedom of a noise source is:

$$\langle E \rangle = \frac{1}{2} k T$$

### Step 2 — RLC Resonator as a Bandpass Filter

A parallel **RLC circuit** has a natural resonance at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ and a quality factor $Q$. The mean-squared noise voltage across the capacitor is:

$$\langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{kT}{C}$$

### Step 3 — PSD of Thermal Noise

A first-order analysis gives the **two-sided Power Spectral Density**:

$$S_n(f) = \frac{kT}{2} \quad \text{(W/Hz, two-sided)}$$

This is **white** (frequency-independent) up to frequencies where quantum effects become dominant (~$10^{12}$ Hz at room temperature).

### Step 4 — Noise Power in a Bandwidth $B$

Integrating the PSD over the band of interest:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_n &= \int_{-B/2}^{B/2} S_n(f)\, df \\
    &= \int_{-B/2}^{B/2} \frac{kT}{2}\, df \\
    &= \frac{kT}{2} \cdot B \\
    &= kTB
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_n = kTB}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check

At $T = 290$ K and $B = 1$ Hz:

$$P_n = (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(290)(1) = 4 \times 10^{-21} \ \text{W}$$

In dBm:

$$P_n\,(\text{dBm}) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \times 10^{-21}}{10^{-3}}\right) = -174 \ \text{dBm/Hz}$$

This is the well-known **−174 dBm/Hz noise floor** used in every link-budget calculation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal Power of a Modulated Wave

For an AM signal $s(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, with $|k_a m(t)| \leq 1$ and $m(t)$ zero-mean:

### Step 1 — Mean-Squared Value

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle s^2(t) \rangle &= \langle A_c^2 [1 + k_a m(t)]^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \rangle \\
  &= \frac{A_c^2}{2} \langle [1 + 2k_a m(t) + k_a^2 m^2(t)] \rangle \quad \left(\text{since } \langle \cos^2 \rangle = 1/2\right) \\
  &= \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left[1 + 0 + k_a^2 \langle m^2(t) \rangle\right] \quad (\langle m \rangle = 0)\\
  &= \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left[1 + k_a^2 P_m\right]
\end{aligned}$$

where $P_m = \langle m^2(t) \rangle$ is the message signal power.

### Step 2 — Carrier Power vs. Sideband Power

$$\boxed{P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}, \quad P_{\text{SB}} = \frac{A_c^2 k_a^2 P_m}{2}, \quad P_{\text{total}} = P_c + P_{\text{SB}}}$$

### Step 3 — Modulation Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{SB}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{k_a^2 P_m}{1 + k_a^2 P_m}$$

For **100% modulation** ($k_a^2 P_m = 1$):

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Worthy Fact:** Conventional AM is at most **33.3% efficient** for a *single-tone* sine message (since for $m(t) = \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $P_m = 1/2$ so $k_a^2 P_m = 1/2$, giving $\eta = 1/3$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Receiver Figures of Merit

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | KTU-Relevant Equation |
|-----------|--------|------------|------------------------|
| **Noise Figure** | $F$ | Degradation of SNR by receiver | $F = (S/N)_{\text{in}} / (S/N)_{\text{out}}$ |
| **Noise Temperature** | $T_e$ | Equivalent input noise temp | $T_e = T_0(F - 1)$ |
| **Friis' Formula** | $F_{\text{tot}}$ | Cascaded noise figure | $F_{\text{tot}} = F_1 + (F_2 - 1)/G_1 + (F_3 - 1)/(G_1 G_2) + \cdots$ |
| **Receiver Sensitivity** | $P_{\min}$ | Minimum detectable signal | $P_{\min} = kT_0 B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{\min}$ |
| **Dynamic Range** | DR | Max / Min input power | $\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = \frac{1}{3}(IIP_3 - MDS)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generalized Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Information Source] -->|m(t)| xdcr1[Input Transducer]
    xdcr1 -->|m_hat(t)| enc[Encoder and Compressor]
    enc -->|m_enc(t)| mod[Modulator with Carrier]
    mod -->|s(t)| pa[Power Amplifier]
    pa -->|s_tx(t) radiating| chan[Channel with Noise Injection]
    chan -->|r(t) = s(t) plus n(t)| lna[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lna -->|r_amp(t)| mix[Mixer and Local Oscillator]
    mix -->|IF signal| ifamp[IF Amplifier and BPF]
    ifamp -->|filtered IF| demod[Demodulator]
    demod -->|m_hat_prime(t)| dec[Decoder and Decompressor]
    dec -->|y(t)| xdcr2[Output Transducer]
    xdcr2 -->|Final output| sink[Destination Sink]
    noise[(Noise Source n(t))] -.->|additive| chan
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Analog vs. Digital System Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANALOG["ANALOG COMMUNICATION SYSTEM"]
        a1[Voice Source] --> a2[Mic Transducer]
        a2 --> a3[Analog Modulator AM or FM]
        a3 --> a4[Channel plus Noise]
        a4 --> a5[Analog Demodulator]
        a5 --> a6[Speaker]
        a6 --> a7[Listener]
    end
    subgraph DIGITAL["DIGITAL COMMUNICATION SYSTEM"]
        d1[Data Source] --> d2[A to D Converter]
        d2 --> d3[Source Encoder]
        d3 --> d4[Channel Encoder FEC]
        d4 --> d5[Digital Modulator BPSK QPSK]
        d5 --> d6[Channel plus Noise]
        d6 --> d7[Digital Demodulator]
        d7 --> d8[Channel Decoder Viterbi]
        d8 --> d9[Source Decoder]
        d9 --> d10[D to A Converter]
        d10 --> d11[Speaker or Display]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Communication System Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Communication Systems]
    root --> analog[Analog Systems]
    root --> digital[Digital Systems]
    analog --> am[Amplitude Modulation DSB SC DSB FC SSB VSB]
    analog --> fm[Frequency Modulation Narrowband FM Wideband FM]
    analog --> pm[Phase Modulation]
    digital --> ask[Amplitude Shift Keying]
    digital --> fsk[Frequency Shift Keying]
    digital --> psk[Phase Shift Keying BPSK QPSK]
    digital --> qam[Quadrature Amplitude Modulation]
    digital --> ofdm[Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing]
    digital --> spread[Spectrum Spread DS SS FH SS]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transmitter Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Baseband Signal m of t] --> preemp[Pre Emphasis Filter]
    preemp --> audproc[Audio Processing and Limiter]
    audproc --> vco[Voltage Controlled Oscillator]
    vco -->|generates f_c| mult[Multiplier Modulator]
    mult --> bpf1[Bandpass Filter]
    bpf1 --> mixup[Upconversion Mixer]
    mixup -->|f_c + f_IF| pamp[RF Power Amplifier]
    pamp --> ant[Antenna]
    lo[Frequency Synthesizer LO] --> mixup
    lo --> vco
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Superheterodyne Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ant[Antenna] --> rfamp[RF Amplifier with AGC]
    rfamp --> rfbf[RF Bandpass Filter Preselector]
    rfbf --> rfmix[RF Mixer]
    rfmix --> ifbf[IF Bandpass Filter]
    ifbf --> ifamp[IF Amplifier chain]
    ifamp --> det[Detector Demodulator]
    det --> afamp[Audio Amplifier]
    afamp --> spk[Speaker Output]
    lo2[Local Oscillator tunable] --> rfmix
    ifamp --> agc[AGC feedback] -.-> rfamp
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:** When drawing these in the exam, always show the **direction of signal flow** with clear arrows and label each signal mathematically (e.g., $m(t)$, $s(t)$, $r(t)$). Examiners allocate 2-3 marks purely for a clean, well-labeled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a communication system. List any four essential elements of a communication system with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A communication system is an arrangement of devices and circuits designed to transfer information (voice, data, or video) from a source to a destination over a transmission medium, while overcoming the effects of noise and distortion.

**Four Essential Elements:**
1. **Information Source** — origin of the message signal $m(t)$
2. **Transmitter** — performs modulation, encoding, and power amplification
3. **Channel** — physical medium (wired or wireless) that carries the signal, subject to noise
4. **Receiver** — performs demodulation, decoding, and amplification of the weak received signal

*Auxiliary elements:* Input transducer, output transducer, destination.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Source → Transducer → Transmitter → Channel(+Noise) → Receiver → Transducer → Destination
```

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is noise? Explain thermal noise and write the expression for thermal noise power.**

**Model Answer:**

**Noise** is any unwanted electrical disturbance that interferes with the desired signal, degrading the SNR at the receiver.

**Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):** It arises from the random thermal agitation of electrons in a conductor or resistor at temperature $T > 0$ K. It has a uniform (white) power spectral density up to very high frequencies.

**Expression for Thermal Noise Power:**

$$P_n = kTB \ \text{(Watts)}$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$, $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz).

At $T_0 = 290$ K, this gives the standard **noise floor of −174 dBm/Hz**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a generalized communication system and explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a superheterodyne receiver. State its advantages over a tuned-radio-frequency (TRF) receiver. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The generalized communication system consists of **seven blocks**:

| Block | Function | Key Points |
|-------|----------|------------|
| 1. Information Source | Generates the message $m(t)$ | May be analog or digital |
| 2. Input Transducer | Converts non-electrical energy → electrical signal | e.g., microphone, camera |
| 3. Transmitter | Modulates $m(t)$ onto carrier $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Performs modulation, encoding, amplification |
| 4. Channel | Conveys the signal to receiver | Suffers attenuation, noise, distortion |
| 5. Receiver | Demodulates and recovers $m(t)$ | Provides amplification and filtering |
| 6. Output Transducer | Converts electrical signal back to original form | e.g., loudspeaker, display |
| 7. Destination | Final user of the information | e.g., human ear/eye, machine input |

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{(Transmitter output)}$$

$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t) \quad \text{(Channel output, including noise)}$$

The signal is processed by the receiver to extract $\hat{m}(t) \approx m(t)$, which is then transduced to its original form for the destination.

> **Marking Note:** Full 7 marks require: (i) Correct block diagram with arrows, (ii) Brief but specific function of each block, (iii) Mathematical signal labels.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Superheterodyne Receiver — Working Principle:**

The term *superheterodyne* means *above the heterodyne* (mixing two frequencies to produce a third, the Intermediate Frequency).

**Block Diagram:**

```
Antenna → RF Amp → RF BPF → Mixer ← Local Oscillator
                                          ↓
Speaker ← Audio Amp ← Detector ← IF Amp ← IF BPF
```

**Working Steps:**

1. **RF Stage:** The antenna picks up the weak RF signal $r(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)$. The RF amplifier boosts it, and the preselector BPF rejects image frequencies.

2. **Mixer + LO:** The mixer multiplies the incoming signal with the local oscillator $f_{\text{LO}}$. Using the trigonometric identity:

$$r(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{\text{LO}} t) = \frac{A}{2}\big[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_{\text{LO}})t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_{\text{LO}})t)\big]$$

The lower frequency $f_{\text{IF}} = f_{\text{LO}} - f_c$ (or $f_c - f_{\text{LO}}$) is the **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**. For AM broadcast, $f_{\text{IF}} = 455$ kHz; for FM, $f_{\text{IF}} = 10.7$ MHz.

3. **IF Stage:** The IF BPF selects the IF band, and the IF amplifier provides most of the receiver's gain (typically 60-80 dB) and selectivity.

4. **Detector:** The detector (envelope detector for AM, discriminator for FM) demodulates the IF signal to recover the baseband message $m(t)$.

5. **Audio Stage:** The audio amplifier drives the loudspeaker.

**Advantages over TRF Receiver:**

| Aspect | TRF Receiver | Superheterodyne |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Selectivity | Poor — many tuned RF stages needed | Excellent — IF filter is fixed and sharp |
| Sensitivity | Low (limited gain at RF) | High — most gain at fixed IF |
| Stability | Poor (tuning affects gain) | Excellent (IF amplifier is fixed-frequency) |
| Image Rejection | Not applicable | Achievable via RF preselector |

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of communication channels. Compare wired and wireless channels with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Shannon-Hartley theorem for channel capacity. State the significance of the theorem. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

A **communication channel** is the physical medium used to convey the signal from transmitter to receiver.

**Types of Communication Channels:**

**(i) Guided (Wired) Channels:**

1. **Twisted-Pair Cable** — Two insulated copper wires twisted to reduce EMI. Used in landline telephony and Ethernet. Bandwidth: a few kHz to hundreds of MHz.
2. **Coaxial Cable** — Central conductor with a concentric shield. Used in cable TV and broadband. Bandwidth: up to ~1 GHz.
3. **Optical Fiber** — Glass/plastic fiber guiding light via total internal reflection. Extremely low loss and enormous bandwidth (THz).

**(ii) Unguided (Wireless) Channels:**

1. **Free-Space / Terrestrial** — VHF/UHF for FM radio, TV, cellular
2. **Sky-Wave (Ionospheric)** — HF radio reflected from ionosphere for long-distance communication
3. **Satellite** — Geostationary / LEO satellites (e.g., GPS, Starlink)
4. **Underwater Acoustic** — Used for submarine communication

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Wired Channel | Wireless Channel |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| Medium | Physical conductor (Cu, fiber) | Electromagnetic waves in free space |
| Bandwidth | High (fiber: THz) | Limited by spectrum allocation |
| Attenuation | Low, predictable | High, environment-dependent |
| Security | High (physically contained) | Low (eavesdropping easy) |
| Mobility | Restricted to cable path | High (mobile users) |
| Installation cost | High (trenching) | Low (just an antenna) |
| Examples | Telephone line, LAN, fiber backhaul | Cellular, Wi-Fi, satellite TV |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Outline):**

**Assumptions:**
- Channel is **bandlimited** to $B$ Hz
- Channel is **AWGN** (Additive White Gaussian Noise)
- Signal power is limited to $S$
- Noise power is $N = N_0 B$

**Step 1 — Sampling:**

A signal bandlimited to $B$ Hz can be represented by $2B$ independent samples per second (Nyquist).

**Step 2 — Distinguishable Levels:**

The number of amplitude levels distinguishable per sample (in the presence of RMS noise $\sqrt{N}$):

$$M = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Bits per Sample:**

$$H = \log_2(M) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \ \text{bits/sample}$$

**Step 4 — Channel Capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= (\text{samples per second}) \times (\text{bits per sample}) \\
  &= 2B \times \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \ \text{bits/second}}$$

**Significance of the Shannon-Hartley Theorem:**

1. **Theoretical Upper Bound:** It gives the absolute maximum error-free data rate over a noisy channel. No real system can exceed this rate.
2. **Trade-off Visualization:** Engineers can see how $B$ and $S/N$ trade against each other.
3. **Bandwidth vs. Power:** Doubling $B$ doubles $C$, but doubling $S$ only marginally increases $C$ — hence bandwidth-efficient schemes (like QAM, OFDM) are preferred over power-hungry ones in spectrum-limited systems.
4. **Noiseless Channel:** If $N \to 0$, $C \to \infty$ — infinite bandwidth is needed to send finite information noiselessly.
5. **Limit Imposed by Nature:** The theorem establishes that noise (not technology) is the fundamental bottleneck, motivating the development of error-correction coding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ but **omit the units** (bits/second). Always write units explicitly.
> - Common mistake: Confusing **Channel Capacity** ($C$, the maximum rate) with **Data Rate** ($R$, the actual rate). $R \leq C$ always.
> - Another common error: Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_2$ — for digital systems, base-2 is mandatory.
> - In the SNR, use **power ratio** (dimensionless), not dB, in the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ A **communication system** has 7 blocks: Source → Input Transducer → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver → Output Transducer → Destination.
- ⭐ The **transmitter** modulates, encodes, and amplifies; the **receiver** does the inverse operations.
- ⭐ **Modulation** is essential for efficient radiation, multiplexing, and noise reduction.
- ⭐ **Channel** may be guided (twisted pair, coax, fiber) or unguided (free-space, satellite, ionospheric).
- ⭐ **Noise** sets the fundamental performance limit; thermal noise $P_n = kTB$.
- ⭐ At $T_0 = 290$ K, the noise floor is **−174 dBm/Hz**.
- ⭐ **Shannon-Hartley:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ is the absolute upper limit on error-free data rate.
- ⭐ **SNR in dB:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$.
- ⭐ **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.
- ⭐ Conventional AM has maximum **theoretical efficiency of 33.3%** (single-tone) or **50%** (full modulation).
- ⭐ **Superheterodyne receiver** uses a fixed IF (455 kHz for AM, 10.7 MHz for FM) for superior selectivity and sensitivity.
- ⭐ For KTU block-diagram questions, **always label signals** ($m(t)$, $s(t)$, $r(t)$) and use **clear directional arrows**.
- ⭐ Remember the **Friis formula** for cascaded noise figure: $F_{\text{tot}} = F_1 + (F_2 - 1)/G_1 + \cdots$
- ⭐ Human voice bandwidth ≈ **3 kHz**; high-fidelity audio ≈ **20 kHz**; video ≈ **5 MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain the elements of a communication system," start with a labeled block diagram (3 marks), then briefly state the function of each block (2-3 marks), and end with the mathematical signal notation $m(t) \rightarrow s(t) \rightarrow r(t) \rightarrow \hat{m}(t)$ (1-2 marks). This structure consistently fetches full marks in the 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Modulation, Amplitude Modulation: Modulation Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Need for Modulation, Amplitude Modulation: Modulation Index ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Need for Modulation — Formal Definition In communication engineering, the process of **modulation** is defined as the systematic variation of one or more parameters (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency **carrier signal** in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of a low-frequency **message signal** (also called the modulating or baseband signal). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Modulation is the process of superimposing a low-frequency information signal onto a high-frequency carrier wave to enable efficient transmission over a communication channel.* ### 1.2 Why Do We Need Modulation? (Key Reasons) The need for modulation arises from the fundamental limitations of transmitting raw low-frequency signals directly. The five primary reasons are: 1. **Reduction of Antenna Height** — For efficient radiation, the antenna size must be comparable to $\lambda/4$ of the transmitted wavelength. Audio signals (20 Hz – 20 kHz) would require antennas of impractical length. 2. **Multiplexing** — Modulation allows simultaneous transmission of multiple signals over a single channel using **FDM**, **TDM**, or **CDM**. 3. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — Translation to higher frequencies reduces the impact of $1/f$ (low-frequency) noise. 4. **Narrowbanding** — Modulation shifts signals to allocated frequency bands (FCC/ITU regulated spectrum). 5. **Long-Distance Transmission** — Low-frequency signals suffer severe attenuation; modulated signals travel further. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Postal Parcel" 🧠 > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you want to send a small handwritten letter (your **message signal**) from Kerala to London via a fast courier. The letter alone is too light and fragile to travel on a cargo ship alone. So you place it inside a **container** (the **carrier signal**) which is sturdy, fast, and follows a known route. The container carries the letter efficiently. Similarly, the carrier wave "carries" the information signal across long distances with minimal loss. The receiver "unpacks" the container to retrieve the letter — this unpacking process is called **demodulation**. ### 1.4 Amplitude Modulation (AM) — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a modulation technique in which the **amplitude of the high-frequency carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant. The general AM signal is mathematically represented as: $$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a \, m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ where: - $A_c$ = amplitude of the carrier - $f_c$ = frequency of the carrier (in Hz) - $m(t)$ = message signal - $k_a$ = amplitude sensitivity of the modulator (constant) ### 1.5 Modulation Index — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modulation Index (m)** is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the **extent or depth** to which the carrier amplitude is varied by the modulating signal. $$m = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$ where $A_m$ is the peak amplitude of the message signal and $A_c$ is the peak amplitude of the carrier. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Reasons for Modulation (Logical Breakdown) #### A. Practical Antenna Size Considerations - An efficient radiating antenna must have a length $L = \lambda/4$. - For an audio signal at $f_m = 1 \text{ kHz}$: - Wavelength $\lambda = c / f = (3 \times 10^8) / 1000 = 300{,}000 \text{ meters}$ - Required antenna length: $300{,}000 / 4 = 75{,}000 \text{ meters} = 75 \text{ km}$ — **physically impossible!** - If the same signal is modulated to $f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$: - $\lambda = 300 \text{ m}$, requiring only a **75 m antenna** — practical. #### B. Multiplexing - Multiple baseband signals can be shifted to different carrier frequencies and combined. - This is the basis of **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** used in radio and TV broadcasting. #### C. Noise Reduction - Industrial and atmospheric noise have a power spectral density proportional to $1/f^2$ (low-frequency dominant). - By translating signals to higher frequencies via modulation, we **avoid the noisy low-frequency spectrum**, improving SNR. #### D. Spectrum Allocation - Regulatory bodies (ITU, FCC) allocate specific frequency bands for different services (AM radio: 540–1600 kHz, FM: 88–108 MHz). - Modulation places the signal in its assigned band. #### E. Reduced Propagation Losses - Higher-frequency signals can be **directional** (using dish antennas) and suffer less ground-wave attenuation. ### 2.2 Types of Amplitude Modulation | Type | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | Double Sideband Full Carrier (DSB-FC) | AM | Standard AM with carrier; modulation index $0 \le m \le 1$ | | Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC) | AM-SC | Carrier suppressed; only sidebands transmitted | | Single Sideband (SSB) | SSB | Only one sideband transmitted | | Vestigial Sideband (VSB) | VSB | One full sideband + trace of other (used in TV) | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU FORMULAS — Memorize These for Board Exam** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Modulation Index (sinusoidal) | $m = A_m / A_c$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le m \le 1$ for standard AM | | 2 | Modulation Index (non-sinusoidal) | $m = \dfrac{A_m^{\max} - A_m^{\min}}{A_m^{\max} + A_m^{\min}}$ | Based on envelope extremes | | 3 | Modulation Index (%) | $m \% = m \times 100$ | Percentage form | | 4 | AM Signal (general) | $s(t) = A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $k_a m(t) = m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ | | 5 | AM Signal (single tone) | $s(t) = A_c \left[1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Single-tone modulation | | 6 | Expanded AM | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{m A_c}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c+f_m)t) + \dfrac{m A_c}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c-f_m)t)$ | Three spectral components | | 7 | Carrier Power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2 R}$ | W (across load $R$) | | 8 | Total Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = \dfrac{m^2 A_c^2}{4R}$ | W (both sidebands combined) | | 9 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \dfrac{m^2}{2}\right)$ | W | | 10 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \dfrac{m^2}{2 + m^2}$ | Maximum at $m=1$ → 33.33% | | 11 | Upper Sideband (USB) Frequency | $f_{USB} = f_c + f_m$ | Hz | | 12 | Lower Sideband (LSB) Frequency | $f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m$ | Hz | | 13 | Bandwidth of AM | $BW = 2 f_m^{\max}$ | Hz | | 14 | Envelope Maximum | $A_{max} = A_c(1 + m)$ | Peak positive envelope | | 15 | Envelope Minimum | $A_{min} = A_c(1 - m)$ | Peak negative envelope | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AM Radio Broadcasting (540–1600 kHz)** — long-range, simple receivers. - **Aircraft Communication (118–137 MHz)** — uses AM due to robustness. - **Citizens Band (CB) Radio (27 MHz)** — uses AM and SSB. - **Cost-effective telemetry** in industrial and scientific instrumentation. - **AM remains the foundation** of all subsequent modulation schemes (DSB-SC, SSB, VSB, QAM). ### 2.5 Overmodulation, Undermodulation & Critical Modulation > [!WARNING] > **Overmodulation ($m > 1$):** Causes envelope distortion → "phase reversal" at the receiver → **distortion of recovered message**. This is **forbidden** in standard AM. > **Undermodulation ($m < 1$):** Safe but inefficient (low sideband power). > **Critical Modulation ($m = 1$):** Boundary case, $A_{min} = 0$, maximum efficiency. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard AM Waveform Equation **Given:** - Message signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ - Carrier signal: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ - Assumption: $f_c \gg f_m$ (so envelope can be tracked) **Step 1:** Write the general form of the AM signal. The amplitude of the carrier is varied proportionally to the message signal. Thus, the instantaneous amplitude becomes: $$A(t) = A_c + k_a \, m(t)$$ where $k_a$ is the modulator's amplitude sensitivity constant. **Step 2:** Substitute $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ into the amplitude expression. $$A(t) = A_c + k_a A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$ **Step 3:** Define modulation index $m = k_a A_m / A_c$ (or equivalently, $k_a A_m = m A_c$). $$A(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$$ **Step 4:** The complete AM signal is the amplitude-modulated carrier. $$s(t) = A(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ $$\boxed{s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)}$$ **Step 5:** Use the trigonometric product-to-sum identity: $$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$$ Applying this with $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $B = 2\pi f_m t$: $$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{m A_c}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \frac{m A_c}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)$$ **Step 6:** Identify the three frequency components. - **Carrier Component:** $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — at frequency $f_c$ - **Lower Sideband (LSB):** $\frac{m A_c}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t)$ — at frequency $f_c - f_m$ - **Upper Sideband (USB):** $\frac{m A_c}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)$ — at frequency $f_c + f_m$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** An AM wave contains the carrier and **two sidebands** symmetrically placed around $f_c$. The total bandwidth is $BW = 2 f_m$ (for single-tone modulation) or $BW = 2 f_{m,\max}$ (for general modulation). ### 3.2 Derivation of Modulation Index in Terms of Envelope Extremes **Given:** Envelope maximum $A_{max}$ and minimum $A_{min}$ of the AM waveform. **Step 1:** From the AM equation, the envelope is given by: $$A(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$$ **Step 2:** The envelope is maximum when $\cos(2\pi f_m t) = +1$. $$A_{max} = A_c (1 + m)$$ **Step 3:** The envelope is minimum when $\cos(2\pi f_m t) = -1$. $$A_{min} = A_c (1 - m)$$ **Step 4:** Add $A_{max}$ and $A_{min}$ to solve for $A_c$. $$A_{max} + A_{min} = A_c(1 + m) + A_c(1 - m) = 2 A_c$$ $$A_c = \frac{A_{max} + A_{min}}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Subtract to solve for $m$. $$A_{max} - A_{min} = A_c(1 + m) - A_c(1 - m) = 2 m A_c$$ $$m = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$$ This is the **standard oscilloscope measurement formula** for modulation index in KTU labs. ### 3.3 Derivation of Power Relations **Given:** AM signal $s(t)$ delivered to a load resistance $R$. **Step 1:** Carrier Power. The carrier is $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. Its average power across $R$: $$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$$ **Step 2:** Power in each sideband. Each sideband has amplitude $\frac{m A_c}{2}$, so its average power is: $$P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{(m A_c / 2)^2}{2R} = \frac{m^2 A_c^2}{8R}$$ **Step 3:** Total sideband power. $$P_{SB} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{m^2 A_c^2}{4R}$$ **Step 4:** Total transmitted power. $$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + \frac{m^2 A_c^2}{4R} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left(1 + \frac{m^2}{2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{m^2}{2}\right)}$$ **Step 5:** Transmission efficiency. $$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m^2 A_c^2 / 4R}{A_c^2/2R \cdot (1 + m^2/2)} = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2}$$ **Step 6:** Maximum efficiency (at $m = 1$). $$\eta_{max} = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** Even at 100% modulation, only **one-third of the total transmitted power** is in the information-carrying sidebands. The remaining **two-thirds is wasted in the carrier** — this is why DSB-SC and SSB are used in power-critical applications. ### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Typical Style) **Problem:** A 600 kHz carrier is amplitude-modulated by a 2 kHz audio signal. The peak carrier amplitude is 20 V, and the peak message amplitude is 8 V. **Solution:** **Given:** - $f_c = 600 \text{ kHz}$, $f_m = 2 \text{ kHz}$, $A_c = 20 \text{ V}$, $A_m = 8 \text{ V}$ **(a) Modulation Index:** $$m = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{8}{20} = 0.4 \quad \text{(or 40%)}$$ **(b) Sideband Frequencies:** $$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 602 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 598 \text{ kHz}$$ **(c) Bandwidth:** $$BW = 2 f_m = 4 \text{ kHz}$$ **(d) AM Signal Equation:** $$s(t) = 20 \left[1 + 0.4 \cos(2\pi \cdot 2000 \cdot t)\right] \cos(2\pi \cdot 600{,}000 \cdot t)$$ **(e) Transmission Efficiency (across $R = 50 \, \Omega$):** $$\eta = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2} = \frac{0.16}{2.16} = 0.0741 = 7.41\%$$ This shows that undermodulation drastically reduces the efficiency — a common KTU exam trap. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Flow — Why Modulation is Essential ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Baseband Message Signal m of t
Low Frequency 20 Hz to 20 kHz] --> B{Direct Transmission?} B -- Yes --> C[Antenna size required: 75 km
Severe Attenuation
Low SNR due to 1 by f noise] B -- No --> D[Modulator Block] D --> E[High Frequency Carrier c of t
f sub c in MHz range] E --> F[Modulated Signal s of t] F --> G[Channel: Free Space, Cable, Fiber] G --> H[Demodulator / Receiver] H --> I[Recovered Message m of t hat] style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333 style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333 style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style I fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram of an AM Modulator (Product Modulator) ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Message Signal m of t] --> S[Summing Node] DC[DC Bias A sub c] --> S S --> P[Product Modulator / Multiplier] C[Carrier cos of 2 pi f sub c t] --> P P --> OUT[AM Output s of t
= A sub c times 1 plus m of t times cos 2 pi f sub c t] style M fill:#FFE4B5 style DC fill:#DDA0DD style C fill:#87CEEB style P fill:#90EE90 style OUT fill:#FFA07A ``` ### 4.3 Frequency Spectrum of an AM Signal (Single-Tone Modulation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SPECTRUM[Frequency Domain Representation of AM Signal] direction LR F1[f sub c minus f sub m
LSB
Amp: m A sub c by 2] --- F2[f sub c
CARRIER
Amp: A sub c] --- F3[f sub c plus f sub m
USB
Amp: m A sub c by 2] end style F1 fill:#FFB6C1 style F2 fill:#FFD700 style F3 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` ### 4.4 AM Waveform in Time Domain — Envelope Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph T1[Time Domain AM Waveform] direction TB Q1[Modulation Index m less than 1
Undermodulation
Envelope does not cross zero] --> Q2[Modulation Index m equals 1
Critical Modulation
Envelope touches zero at minima] Q2 --> Q3[Modulation Index m greater than 1
Overmodulation
Envelope crosses zero and phase reversal occurs] end style Q1 fill:#90EE90 style Q2 fill:#FFD700 style Q3 fill:#FF6347 ``` ### 4.5 Power Distribution Pie-Chart Representation (At m = 1) ```mermaid pie title Power Distribution in AM Signal at m=1 "Carrier Power (66.67%)" : 66.67 "Upper Sideband Power (16.67%)" : 16.67 "Lower Sideband Power (16.67%)" : 16.67 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Interpretation:** The carrier consumes **two-thirds** of the total power despite carrying **no information**. This is the key motivation for suppressed-carrier systems. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define modulation index. What is its significance in amplitude modulation?* **Model Answer:** The modulation index $m$ is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal: $$m = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity. Its significance includes: 1. It indicates the **depth of modulation** — how strongly the message signal modulates the carrier. 2. For standard AM, $m \le 1$ to avoid envelope distortion. 3. It directly determines the **sideband power**, **transmission efficiency**, and **bandwidth utilization**. 4. For $m = 0$, there is no modulation (only carrier present); for $m = 1$, it is critical modulation with maximum efficiency of 33.33%. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any four reasons for the necessity of modulation in communication systems.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Reduction of antenna size:** Low-frequency signals require impractically large antennas; modulation shifts signals to higher frequencies. 2. **Multiplexing:** Allows simultaneous transmission of multiple signals (FDM, TDM) over a single channel. 3. **Improved SNR:** Avoids the low-frequency noise spectrum (atmospheric/industrial $1/f$ noise). 4. **Spectrum allocation:** Enables signals to occupy regulated frequency bands (e.g., AM broadcast band 540–1600 kHz). 5. **Long-distance communication:** Higher-frequency signals travel further with less attenuation. --- ### **Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)** --- #### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) Derive the mathematical expression for a single-tone amplitude-modulated wave. Sketch its frequency spectrum and write the time-domain equation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the message and carrier signals.** $$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$ $$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Step 2 — Express the modulated amplitude.** The instantaneous amplitude of the AM signal varies as: $$A(t) = A_c + k_a m(t) = A_c + k_a A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$ Defining modulation index $m = \frac{k_a A_m}{A_c}$: $$A(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$$ **Step 3 — Write the complete AM waveform.** $$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Step 4 — Expand using the trigonometric identity** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$: $$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{m A_c}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \frac{m A_c}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)$$ **Step 5 — Identify the spectrum components:** - Carrier: $A_c$ at $f_c$ - LSB: $\frac{mA_c}{2}$ at $f_c - f_m$ - USB: $\frac{mA_c}{2}$ at $f_c + f_m$ **Step 6 — Frequency Spectrum Sketch:** ``` Amplitude ↑ │ USB Carrier LSB │ │ │ │ │ mAc/2│ Ac mAc/2 │ ┌───┐ │ ┌───┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ └───┴───┴────────────┴──────────────┴───┴───→ Frequency (fc-fm) fc (fc+fm) BW = 2fm ``` **(b) For an AM broadcast transmitter with a carrier frequency of 1 MHz and modulating signal of 5 kHz, calculate: (i) modulation index if $A_m = 15$ V and $A_c = 30$ V, (ii) USB and LSB frequencies, (iii) bandwidth, (iv) transmission efficiency. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$, $f_m = 5 \text{ kHz}$, $A_m = 15 \text{ V}$, $A_c = 30 \text{ V}$ **(i) Modulation Index:** $$m = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.5 \quad \text{or} \quad 50\%$$ **(ii) Sideband Frequencies:** $$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 1{,}000 + 5 = 1005 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 1{,}000 - 5 = 995 \text{ kHz}$$ **(iii) Bandwidth:** $$BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$ **(iv) Transmission Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2} = \frac{0.25}{2.25} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%$$ This means only 11.11% of the total transmitted power is in the useful sidebands. --- #### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]** **(a) Explain the five major reasons justifying the need for modulation. With suitable examples, show how modulation reduces antenna size. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **The five reasons for modulation are:** **1. Antenna Size Reduction:** For efficient radiation, antenna length $L = \lambda/4$. For a 1 kHz audio signal: $$L = \frac{c}{4 f_m} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 1000} = 75{,}000 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ km}$$ This is impractical. If modulated to $f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$: $$L = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^6} = 75 \text{ m} \quad \text{(practical)}$$ **2. Multiplexing:** Different baseband signals can be modulated onto different carrier frequencies and combined (FDM), allowing simultaneous transmission. Example: Multiple radio stations broadcasting over a single wideband channel. **3. Noise Reduction:** Industrial and atmospheric noise has PSD $\propto 1/f^2$. Modulation shifts signals to higher frequencies, avoiding this noise-rich region, thereby improving SNR. **4. Spectrum Allocation:** Regulatory bodies (ITU, FCC) allocate specific frequency bands.
