Many students write $P_t / P_c = I_t / I_c$, which is **wrong** and leads to a completely incorrect answer. Always square the current ratio when computing power ratios.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** A 1000 kHz carrier is amplitude modulated by a 500 Hz sinusoidal signal to a depth of 60%. If the carrier amplitude is 20 V, determine: (i) the modulation index, (ii) the amplitudes of the USB and LSB components, and (iii) the bandwidth of the AM signal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with waveforms the concept of modulation index for a single-tone AM signal. Show mathematically that distortion occurs when $m > 1$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given data: $f_c = 1000$ kHz, $f_m = 500$ Hz, $m = 0.6$, $A_c = 20$ V.

**Step 2:** The modulation index is given directly: $m = 0.6$.

**Step 3:** The amplitude of each sideband is $A_{SB} = A_c m / 2$.

$$A_{SB} = \frac{20 \times 0.6}{2} = 6 \text{ V}$$

So, both the Upper Sideband (USB) and the Lower Sideband (LSB) have amplitudes of **6 V**.

**Step 4:** The frequency of the USB is:

$$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 1000000 + 500 = 1000500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5:** The frequency of the LSB is:

$$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 1000000 - 500 = 999500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6:** The bandwidth of an AM signal is the difference between the USB and LSB frequencies.

$$BW = f_{USB} - f_{LSB} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For a single-tone AM signal, the modulating signal is sinusoidal.

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2:** The carrier is also sinusoidal.

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3:** The AM signal is generated by multiplying the carrier by $[1 + k_a m(t)]$ where $k_a$ is the amplitude sensitivity of the modulator.

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $m = k_a A_m$ is the modulation index.

**Step 4:** The envelope of $s(t)$ is $A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]$.

**Step 5:** For distortion-free demodulation (envelope detection), the envelope must remain **non-negative** at all times.

$$1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } t$$

**Step 6:** The minimum value of the cosine term is $-1$. Therefore, the condition becomes:

$$1 - m \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m \leq 1$$

**Step 7:** When $m > 1$, the envelope becomes negative near the cosine minima, causing the **envelope detector** to fail (it cannot track negative values). This results in a **phase reversal of $180°$** in the carrier at the envelope zero-crossings, producing severe harmonic distortion and out-of-band spectral splatter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In part (b), students often forget to explain *why* the envelope detector fails when $m > 1$. Simply stating "distortion occurs" without mathematical justification (Steps 5–7 above) will result in a deduction of **at least 2–3 marks**. Always tie the physical mechanism (envelope detector's inability to track negative amplitudes) to the algebraic condition $m \leq 1$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $m = A_m / A_c$ is the ratio of the modulating signal amplitude to the carrier amplitude; it is **dimensionless**.
- **Range:** $0 \leq m \leq 1$ for distortion-free AM; $m = 1$ is the **ideal limit** (100% modulation).
- **Over-modulation:** $m > 1$ causes **envelope distortion**, **phase reversal**, and **out-of-band splatter**.
- **Envelope Formula:** $m = (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) / (E_{\max} + E_{\min})$ — used for oscilloscope-based measurements.
- **Power Formulas:**
  - $P_c = A_c^2 / 2R$
  - $P_{SB} = (m^2 / 2) P_c$
  - $P_t = P_c(1 + m^2/2)$
- **Maximum Efficiency:** At $m = 1$, $\eta_{\max} = 1/3 = 33.33\%$. The carrier wastes 66.67% of the total power.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m$ (twice the highest modulating frequency).
- **Spectrum:** Contains the carrier at $f_c$ and two sidebands at $f_c \pm f_m$.
- **Multi-tone Modulation:** Effective $m = \sqrt{m_1^2 + m_2^2 + \dots + m_n^2}$ (powers add, not amplitudes).
- **KTU Lab Tip:** In the AM modulation/demodulation experiment, always use a **trapezoidal pattern** on the CRO to measure $m$ directly from the slope of the sides.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing **antenna current ratio** with **power ratio**. Remember $P \propto I^2$, so $(I_t/I_c)^2 = 1 + m^2/2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation and Spectrum of FM Signal, Narrow and Wide Band FM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equation and Spectrum of FM Signal, Narrow and Wide Band FM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Frequency Modulation (FM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is a continuous-wave angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the sinusoidal carrier wave is varied linearly in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the **amplitude of the carrier remains constant**.

The standard FM signal is mathematically represented as:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

where every term carries a precise physical meaning in the KTU 2024 Scheme communication engineering framework.

### 1.2 Term-by-Term Physical Interpretation

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A_c$ | Constant carrier amplitude | Volts (V) |
| $f_c$ | Unmodulated carrier frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $k_f$ | Frequency sensitivity of the FM modulator | Hz per Volt (Hz/V) |
| $m(t)$ | Modulating message/baseband signal | Volts (V) |
| $\theta_i(t)$ | Instantaneous phase of the FM wave | Radians (rad) |
| $\omega_i(t)$ | Instantaneous angular frequency | Radians/second (rad/s) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Siren and the Spinning Wheel"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners):**
> Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a circular track, and your friend on the ground is blowing a police siren at you.
> - When your friend **shouts loudly** (high message amplitude) into the siren, the **pitch (frequency)** of the sound you hear rises rapidly.
> - When your friend **whispers** (low message amplitude), the pitch falls.
> - The **loudness (amplitude) of the siren itself does not change** — only its pitch (frequency) does.
> This is exactly how Frequency Modulation works: the **amplitude of the carrier is fixed at $A_c$**, but its **instantaneous frequency deviates** in direct proportion to the message signal $m(t)$.
> A **loud message** $\Rightarrow$ **bigger frequency deviation** $\Rightarrow$ **wider spectrum** $\Rightarrow$ **better noise immunity** (this is the famous advantage of FM over AM).

### 1.4 Why FM Matters in Real Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FM is the backbone of **VHF radio broadcasting (88–108 MHz)**, **two-way police/ambulance communication**, **analog television audio**, and the **first-generation cellular telephony (1G/AMPS)**. Its **superior noise rejection** (a direct consequence of trading bandwidth for Signal-to-Noise Ratio, or **SNR**, by Carson's rule) is the primary reason it replaced AM in high-fidelity applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Frequency Modulation — Step by Step

The derivation of the FM equation proceeds through four rigorous conceptual steps, each of which is a frequent target in KTU university examinations.

- **Step 1 — Define the General Angle-Modulated Wave.**
  Any angle-modulated signal can be written as $s(t) = A_c \cos(\theta_i(t))$. The defining decision in FM is how $\theta_i(t)$ is constructed from the message.

- **Step 2 — Express Instantaneous Frequency.**
  By definition, the instantaneous frequency $f_i(t)$ is the time-derivative of phase divided by $2\pi$:
  $$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$
  For FM, we impose that $f_i(t)$ is **linearly proportional** to $m(t)$:
  $$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\, m(t)$$

- **Step 3 — Integrate to Recover the Phase.**
  Integrating the above gives:
  $$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau$$

- **Step 4 — Substitute Back Into the Carrier.**
  This produces the canonical FM waveform introduced earlier in SECTION 1.

### 2.2 Frequency Deviation and Modulation Index

Two metrics quantify "how strong" the modulation is, and both are **board-favorite 3-mark and 14-mark topics**.

- **Frequency Deviation $\Delta f$:** the maximum instantaneous departure of the carrier from $f_c$, given by
  $$\Delta f = k_f \,\vert m(t)\vert_{\max}$$

- **Modulation Index $\beta$ (also denoted $m_f$):** the ratio of frequency deviation to the highest message frequency $W$ (or $f_m$ for a pure tone):
  $$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{W} = \frac{k_f \,\vert m(t)\vert_{\max}}{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind $\beta$:** $\beta$ is a dimensionless number that **decides whether the FM is Narrowband or Wideband**. A $\beta$ close to $0$ means the frequency wobbles only slightly; a large $\beta$ means the frequency swings widely. The **classification threshold is universally $\beta = 1$** — though some texts use $\beta \ll 1$ (NBFM) versus $\beta \gg 1$ (WBFM).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table before every KTU exam for this module. Every formula here has appeared in a KTU university paper at least once.**

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Conditions / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | FM Signal (General) | $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t}\! m(\tau)d\tau\right)$ | Continuous-time, $m(t)$ real |
| 2 | Instantaneous Frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ | Linear in $m(t)$ |
| 3 | Instantaneous Phase | $\phi_i(t) = 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t}\! m(\tau)d\tau$ | Excess phase (rad) |
| 4 | Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f = k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$ | Hz, always $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | Modulation Index (General) | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Modulation Index (Single-tone) | $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ | $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ |
| 7 | Single-tone FM Signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$ | Phase modulation index = $\beta$ |
| 8 | Bessel Expansion | $s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big)$ | $J_n(\beta)$ = Bessel of 1st kind, order $n$ |
| 9 | Carrier Amplitude | $A_c J_0(\beta)$ | $J_0$ is the 0th-order Bessel |
| 10 | nth Sideband Amplitude | $A_c J_n(\beta)$ | $\vert n \vert \geq 1$ |
| 11 | Carson's Rule Bandwidth | $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(\beta + 1)W$ | Universal approximation |
| 12 | NBFM Bandwidth | $B_{NBFM} \approx 2W$ | Valid when $\beta \ll 1$ |
| 13 | WBFM Bandwidth | $B_{WBFM} \approx 2\Delta f$ | Valid when $\beta \gg 1$ |
| 14 | Average FM Power | $P_{FM} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Independent of $\beta$ |
| 15 | Bessel Identity | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$ | Power-conservation identity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equation and spectrum of FM underpin the design of every **superheterodyne FM receiver** in existence. The intermediate frequency (IF) section typically operates at **10.7 MHz** — chosen because Carson's rule for an FM broadcast signal with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $W = 15$ kHz gives $B = 2(75 + 15) = 180$ kHz, which fits neatly into a 200 kHz channel allocation. Engineers must also size the **IF bandwidth-limiting filter** using the exact Bessel-weighted spectrum, otherwise adjacent-channel interference destroys SNR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Single-Tone FM Spectrum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single-tone derivation is the most repeated 14-mark problem in KTU Communication Engineering papers from 2019 onwards. Master every line below.**

We begin with a pure-tone modulating signal:

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Line 1 — Substitute $m(t)$ into the FM signal equation:**

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

**Line 2 — Evaluate the inner integral explicitly:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\,d\tau = A_m \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi f_m t)}{2\pi f_m} = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Line 3 — Recognize the modulation index. By definition:**

$$\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

Multiplying the integral result by $2\pi k_f$ gives exactly $\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$2\pi k_f \cdot \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t) = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t) = \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Line 4 — Compact single-tone FM equation:**

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)$$

**Line 5 — Apply the Bessel function identity (Jacobi–Anger expansion):**

The Jacobi–Anger identity states that for any real $\beta$:

$$\cos\big(\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big) = J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n}(\beta) \cos(2\pi (2n) f_m t)$$

$$\sin\big(\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big) = 2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{2n+1}(\beta) \sin(2\pi (2n+1) f_m t)$$

**Line 6 — Expand the FM signal as a trigonometric product. Using $\cos(A+B)=\cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= A_c\Big[\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\big(\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big) \\
&\qquad - \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\big(\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)\Big]
\end{aligned}$$

**Line 7 — Substitute the Jacobi–Anger expansions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\left[J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n}(\beta)\cos(4\pi n f_m t)\right] \\
&\quad - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\left[2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{2n+1}(\beta)\sin(2\pi(2n+1)f_m t)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Line 8 — Use the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B)+\cos(A+B)]$ and $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B)-\cos(A+B)]$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= A_c J_0(\beta)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \\
&\quad + A_c \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n}(\beta)\Big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - 2nf_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + 2nf_m)t\Big] \\
&\quad + A_c \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{2n+1}(\beta)\Big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - (2n+1)f_m)t - \cos 2\pi(f_c + (2n+1)f_m)t\Big]
\end{aligned}$$

**Line 9 — Combine the two summations into a single infinite series indexed by integer $n \in \mathbb{Z}$:**

> [!NOTE]
> The minus sign on the odd sidebands is absorbed by allowing $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$, which is the parity property of Bessel functions. After this algebraic step we obtain the **canonical FM spectrum**:

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\,\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big)\,}$$

This single equation is worth **3 marks on its own** in any KTU 14-mark derivation question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Carson's Rule

**Step 1 — Quantify the spectrum width.** From the spectrum derived above, sidebands exist at $f_c \pm n f_m$ for every integer $n$ with non-negligible $J_n(\beta)$. Empirically, the Bessel function $J_n(\beta)$ becomes smaller than **0.04** (i.e., less than 1% of the carrier in amplitude) when $\vert n \vert > \beta + 1$.

**Step 2 — Define the "significant" bandwidth.** The highest significant sideband order is therefore $n_{\max} = \beta + 1$. The total spread between $f_c - n_{\max} f_m$ and $f_c + n_{\max} f_m$ is:

$$B = 2 n_{\max} f_m = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$:**

$$B = 2\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to a message of bandwidth $W$** (not a pure tone):

$$\boxed{\,B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(\beta + 1) W\,}$$

This is **Carson's rule**, and it carries a guaranteed 2–3 marks in KTU papers whenever a numerical bandwidth problem is posed.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the type of numerical KTU frequently asks for full 14 marks. Solve it in your answer booklet exactly in the format shown below.**

**Given:** $f_c = 100$ MHz, $A_c = 10$ V, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $A_m = 2$ V, $k_f = 15$ kHz/V.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta f$:**

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 15{,}000 \times 2 = 30{,}000~\text{Hz} = 30~\text{kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{30{,}000}{5{,}000} = 6$$

**Step 3 — Classify the FM type.** Since $\beta = 6 \gg 1$, this is a **Wideband FM** (WBFM) signal.

**Step 4 — Apply Carson's rule for bandwidth:**

$$B_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2 \times 7 \times 5{,}000 = 70~\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Read Bessel coefficients from a standard table (these are examinable):**

| $n$ | $J_n(6)$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $+0.1506$ |
| 1 | $-0.2767$ |
| 2 | $+0.2429$ |
| 3 | $-0.1149$ |
| 4 | $+0.0346$ |
| 5 | $-0.0070$ |
| 6 | $+0.0004$ |
| 7 | $\approx 0$ |

**Step 6 — Verify power conservation:**

$$\frac{A_c^2}{2}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(6) = \frac{10^2}{2}(1) = 50~\text{W}$$

This confirms the **constant-power** property of FM: increasing $\beta$ redistributes power among more sidebands, but **total power is always $A_c^2/2$**.

**Step 7 — Count the significant sidebands:** $\beta + 1 = 7$, so sidebands from $n = -7$ to $n = +7$ are significant. This yields **15 spectral components** in the WBFM spectrum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FM Modulator–Demodulator Chain

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram traces the entire signal path from message to reconstructed message, highlighting the role of the FM equation and Bessel spectrum at each stage. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Message Signal m of t] --> A1[Differentiation / Pre-emphasis Filter]
    A1 --> A2[FM Modulator Block]
    A2 --> A3[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO]
    A3 --> A4[FM Signal s of t with Frequency Deviation]
    A4 --> A5[Channel with Additive White Gaussian Noise AWGN]
    A5 --> A6[Receiving Bandpass Filter centered at fc]
    A6 --> A7[Limiter to Remove AM Interference]
    A7 --> A8[Frequency Discriminator or Phase-Locked Loop PLL]
    A8 --> A9[De-emphasis Filter]
    A9 --> A10[Recovered Message Signal m hat of t]
    A4 -. Side-Information .-> B1[Bessel Function Spectrum Analyzer]
    B1 --> B2[Compute J sub n of beta for all n]
    B2 --> B3[Plot Spectral Lines at fc plus n times fm]
    B3 --> B4[Apply Carson Rule 2 of beta plus 1 times fm]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the FM Spectrum — Single Tone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Carrier Component at fc, Amplitude Ac J0 of beta] --> S1[Upper Sidebands at fc + n fm, Amplitude Ac J sub n of beta]
    S0 --> S2[Lower Sidebands at fc - n fm, Amplitude Ac J sub n of beta]
    S1 --> S3[Apply Carson Rule: n max approx beta + 1]
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Total Bandwidth equals 2 of beta + 1 times fm]
    S4 --> S5[Classify: NBFM if beta less than 1, WBFM if beta greater than 1]
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: NBFM vs WBFM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Start: Compute beta equals kf times Am divided by fm] --> D2{Is beta much less than 1?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[NBFM Narrowband FM]
    D3 --> D4[Bandwidth approx 2 fm]
    D3 --> D5[Spectrum resembles AM]
    D3 --> D6[Power mostly in carrier]
    D2 -- No --> D7{Was beta much greater than 1?}
    D7 -- Yes --> D8[WBFM Wideband FM]
    D8 --> D9[Bandwidth approx 2 of beta + 1 times fm]
    D8 --> D10[Many significant sidebands]
    D8 --> D11[High SNR trade-off for bandwidth]
    D7 -- No --> D12[General FM: use exact Bessel expansion]
```

### 4.4 Physical Interpretation of the Bessel-Function Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[FM Equation: s of t equals Ac cos 2 pi fc t plus beta sin 2 pi fm t] --> P2[Apply Jacobi-Anger Expansion]
    P2 --> P3[Carrier at fc with coefficient J0 of beta]
    P2 --> P4[Infinite Sidebands at fc plus or minus n fm]
    P4 --> P5[Bessel Function Properties]
    P5 --> P6[J sub minus n of beta equals minus 1 to n times J sub n of beta]
    P5 --> P7[J sub n of beta decreases as n grows beyond beta plus 1]
    P5 --> P8[Sum of J sub n squared of beta equals 1, total power conserved]
    P3 --> P9[Final Spectrum: discrete lines spaced fm apart]
    P4 --> P9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**
Define Frequency Modulation. Derive the expression for the instantaneous frequency of an FM signal.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Frequency Modulation is the process in which the instantaneous frequency of the carrier is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, while the carrier amplitude remains constant.
>
> **Derivation (2 Marks):** For an FM signal $s(t) = A_c \cos(\theta_i(t))$, the instantaneous frequency is
> $$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$
> The phase of the FM wave is
> $$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau$$
> Differentiating and dividing by $2\pi$:
> $$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\, m(t)$$

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**
What is Carson's rule? Write its expression for the bandwidth of an FM signal.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Carson's rule is an empirical formula that estimates the transmission bandwidth of an FM signal as twice the sum of the frequency deviation and the highest modulating frequency.
>
> **Expression (1 Mark):**
> $$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(\beta + 1)W$$
> where $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation and $W$ is the message bandwidth.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### 5.2.1 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A (a)** Derive the equation of a single-tone FM signal and obtain its spectrum using Bessel functions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We start with a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Substitute $m(t)$ into the FM signal formula:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Evaluate the integral:

$$2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\,d\tau = \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

where $\beta = k_f A_m / f_m$ is the modulation index.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The compact single-tone equation:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply the Jacobi–Anger identity and the product-to-sum identity (full expansion shown in SECTION 3.1, lines 5–8). After combining even and odd $n$ terms using the Bessel parity property $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$:

$$s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big)$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Spectral interpretation:
- Carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c J_0(\beta)$.
- Infinite sidebands at $f_c \pm n f_m$ with amplitude $A_c J_n(\beta)$.
- The amplitudes follow Bessel function values from standard tables.
- Total power is constant at $A_c^2/2$ (Parseval's theorem on Bessel series).

**A (b)** For an FM broadcast system, the message bandwidth is $15$ kHz and the peak frequency deviation is $75$ kHz. Calculate the modulation index, classify the FM, and determine the bandwidth using Carson's rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute modulation index:
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{W} = \frac{75}{15} = 5$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Classification: $\beta = 5 \gg 1$, so this is **Wideband FM (WBFM)**.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply Carson's rule:
$$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(75 + 15) = 180~\text{kHz}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Significant sidebands: $n_{\max} = \beta + 1 = 6$. So 13 spectral lines (carrier + 6 upper + 6 lower) are significant.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Conclusion: This matches the standard FM broadcast allocation of 200 kHz per channel, validating Carson's rule as a design-grade estimator.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**B (a)** With neat mathematical steps, derive the expression for the frequency deviation and modulation index of an FM signal for a general (non-tonal) message $m(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** The instantaneous frequency for FM is:
$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** The maximum deviation from $f_c$ defines the peak frequency deviation:
$$\Delta f = \max\big\vert f_i(t) - f_c \big\vert = k_f \cdot \big\vert m(t) \big\vert_{\max}$$

This is the **frequency deviation** in Hz.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** For a message of bandwidth $W$ Hz, the modulation index is:
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{W}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $W = f_m$ and $\vert m(t) \vert_{\max} = A_m$, so:
$$\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

This dimensionless number governs whether the FM is narrowband ($\beta \ll 1$) or wideband ($\beta \gg 1$).

---

**B (b)** A $25$ MHz carrier is frequency-modulated by a $400$ Hz tone, producing a peak deviation of $10$ kHz. **(i)** Find the modulation index. **(ii)** Determine the approximate bandwidth using Carson's rule. **(iii)** How many significant sidebands are generated? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) (2 Marks):** Modulation index:
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{10{,}000}{400} = 25$$

**(ii) (2 Marks):** Carson's rule bandwidth:
$$B_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2 \times 26 \times 400 = 20{,}800~\text{Hz} \approx 20.8~\text{kHz}$$

**(iii) (3 Marks):** Significant sidebands count:
$$n_{\max} = \beta + 1 = 26$$
Hence there are $2 \times 26 = 52$ significant sideband components (26 upper and 26 lower), plus 1 carrier line, totaling **53 spectral lines**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Bessel parity property $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$** when writing the spectrum loses 2 marks. Always show the **single-sided** form $J_n(\beta)$ with $n$ positive if asked for amplitude, but **double-sided** if asked for the full spectrum.
> 2. **Mixing up NBFM and AM bandwidths** is a classic blunder. NBFM bandwidth is $2W$ only **when $\beta \ll 1$**; otherwise, always apply Carson's rule.
> 3. **Units in numerical problems** — Examiners deduct half a mark if $\Delta f$ is written in kHz while $W$ is in Hz, or if MHz and kHz are mixed without conversion. Always normalize to Hz.
> 4. **Power calculation** — Students often forget that $\sum J_n^2(\beta) = 1$ and write $P = A_c^2 \sum J_n^2(\beta)$ instead of $P = A_c^2/2$. The factor of $1/2$ comes from $\langle \cos^2 \rangle = 1/2$.
> 5. **Misclassifying FM** — Saying "FM is narrowband when $\beta = 1$" is wrong. The strict condition is $\beta \ll 1$ (some texts use $\beta < 0.5$ or $\beta < 0.25$). At $\beta = 1$, the FM is "moderate" and Carson's rule must be used verbatim.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Revise This 10 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- **FM equation (General):** $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$
- **FM equation (Single-tone):** $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)$
- **FM Spectrum (Bessel expansion):** $s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big)$
- **Instantaneous frequency:** $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ — *this is the defining equation of FM.*
- **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f \vert m(t) \vert_{\max}$
- **Modulation index (General):** $\beta = \Delta f / W$
- **Modulation index (Single-tone):** $\beta = k_f A_m / f_m$
- **Carson's rule (Universal bandwidth):** $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(\beta + 1) W$
- **NBFM:** $\beta \ll 1 \Rightarrow B \approx 2W$, spectrum looks like AM.
- **WBFM:** $\beta \gg 1 \Rightarrow B \approx 2\Delta f$, many sidebands.
- **Number of significant sidebands:** $2(\beta + 1) + 1$ (including the carrier).
- **Average FM power:** $P = A_c^2 / 2$ — **independent of $\beta$**, a unique FM property.
- **Bessel function identities to memorize:**
  - $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$
  - $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$
  - $J_0(0) = 1$, $J_n(0) = 0$ for $n \neq 0$
  - $J_n(\beta) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ for any fixed $\beta$
- **Real-world benchmarks (commonly asked as numerical problems):**
  - FM broadcast: $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $W = 15$ kHz, $\beta = 5$, $B = 180$ kHz.
  - TV audio (NTSC): $\Delta f = 25$ kHz, $W = 15$ kHz, $\beta = 5/3$, $B = 80$ kHz.
  - Two-way radio (narrowband): $\Delta f = 5$ kHz, $W = 3$ kHz, $\beta = 5/3$, $B = 16$ kHz.
- **Examiner's mantra:** "Show every step, draw the spectrum, cite Carson's rule, and remember the Bessel identity."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of AM and FM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of AM and FM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Amplitude Modulation (AM)?

**Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a continuous-wave modulation technique in which the **amplitude** of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency message (modulating) signal, while the **frequency and phase** of the carrier are kept constant.

Mathematically, an AM signal is expressed as:
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + k_a\,m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_c$ is the carrier frequency, $m(t)$ is the message signal, and $k_a$ is the amplitude sensitivity of the modulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (EST/CST – Communication Engineering):** AM is the **simplest form of analog modulation** and forms the foundation for understanding all other modulation schemes. The carrier *envelope* carries the information.

## 1.2 What is Frequency Modulation (FM)?

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is a continuous-wave modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, while the **amplitude of the carrier** is held constant.

The FM signal is expressed as:
$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f\!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

where $k_f$ (Hz/volt) is the frequency sensitivity of the modulator.

> [!NOTE]
> In FM, the *frequency deviation* from the carrier frequency encodes the message, not the amplitude. This gives FM its well-known **noise immunity** and **fidelity advantages**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Two Musicians, One Song"

Imagine two musicians playing the **same melody** for a listener (the receiver):

- **AM Musician:** He sings at a **fixed pitch** (constant frequency), but his **loudness (amplitude) rises and falls** with the melody's intensity. The melody's rhythm is conveyed only through volume changes. If the room gets noisy (additive noise hits the amplitude directly), his melody becomes hard to "hear."

- **FM Musician:** He sings at a **fixed loudness (constant amplitude)**, but he **shifts his pitch up and down** with the melody. As long as the listener can detect *changes in pitch* (frequency), the message comes through clearly — even if the room is noisy, because noise primarily disturbs amplitude, not pitch.

> [!TIP]
> This analogy is what the KTU examiner expects when asking *"Why is FM more noise-immune than AM?"* The answer is rooted in the fact that additive Gaussian noise predominantly corrupts amplitude, not frequency.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlights)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Carrier Frequency** | $f_c$ | High-frequency sinusoid that transports the message |
| **Modulation Index (AM)** | $\mu$ | Ratio $\mu = A_m / A_c$ |
| **Modulation Index (FM)** | $\beta$ | Ratio $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ |
| **Maximum Frequency Deviation** | $\Delta f$ | Peak deviation of instantaneous frequency from $f_c$ |
| **Bandwidth** | $B$ | Spectral width of the modulated signal |
| **Channel SNR** | $(S/N)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio at the receiver input |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" Behind AM

The message signal $m(t)$ is a **low-frequency** signal (typically audio, 20 Hz – 20 kHz). Transmitting it directly would require **impractically large antennas** (since antenna length $\approx \lambda/4$, and $\lambda$ is huge at low frequencies). By multiplying it with a high-frequency carrier $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, we **translate** the message spectrum to a band centered around $f_c$, where reasonable antennas work.

**Step-by-step logical flow in AM:**

1. Start with the carrier: $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$
2. Vary its **amplitude** linearly with $m(t)$: amplitude becomes $A_c + k_a\,m(t)$
3. Multiply the modified amplitude with the carrier sinusoid: $s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + k_a\,m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$
4. Expand to observe the spectrum: $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + k_a\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$
5. The first term is the **carrier**; the second term is the **message shifted to $\pm f_c$** → this creates the **Upper Sideband (USB)** and **Lower Sideband (LSB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> AM is a **linear** modulation scheme because the modulated signal is a **linear function** of the message signal. This is a key KTU board point.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind FM

In FM, the message signal $m(t)$ controls the **instantaneous frequency** $f_i(t)$ of the carrier:
$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\,m(t)$$

The phase is the integral of frequency, so the total phase angle is:
$$\phi(t) = 2\pi\!\int_{-\infty}^{t} f_i(\tau)\,d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f\!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Substituting this phase into the carrier:
$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f\!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

**Step-by-step logical flow in FM:**

1. The carrier has **constant amplitude** $A_c$ (power is constant — a key advantage).
2. The message $m(t)$ **modulates the rate of change of phase** (i.e., the frequency).
3. The **deviation** $\Delta f = k_f \cdot \max\!\vert m(t)\vert$ tells us how far the instantaneous frequency swings.
4. The **modulation index** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ determines the spectral richness of FM.
5. Unlike AM, FM is a **non-linear** modulation scheme (the modulated signal is a non-linear function of $m(t)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The instantaneous amplitude of an FM signal is **always constant** at $A_c$, regardless of the message. Therefore, **all transmitted power is useful carrier power** (no sideband power in the AM sense). Noise that perturbs amplitude is clipped off by a **limiter** in the FM receiver.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | AM Formula | FM Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulated Signal | $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $s(t) = A_c\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$ |
| 2 | Modulation Index | $\mu = A_m / A_c$ (also called $\,m_a$) | $\beta = \Delta f \,/\, f_m = k_f A_m \,/\, f_m$ |
| 3 | Range for No Distortion | $0 \le \mu \le 1$ | $\beta > 0$ (no theoretical upper limit) |
| 4 | Carrier Power | $P_c = A_c^2 / 2$ | $P_c = A_c^2 / 2$ |
| 5 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | $P_t = A_c^2 / 2$ (constant) |
| 6 | Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \mu^2 / 2$ | $0$ (no discrete sidebands for infinite spectrum) |
| 7 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ | $100\%$ (in theory) |
| 8 | Bandwidth (exact, Bessel) | $B_{AM} = 2f_m$ | $B_{FM} = 2(\beta + n)f_m$, where $n$ = number of significant sidebands |
| 9 | Bandwidth (Carson’s Rule) | Not applicable | $B_{FM} \approx 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1)f_m$ |
| 10 | Noise Performance (post-demod) | SNR scales linearly with input SNR | SNR improves as $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ for large $\beta$ (Capture Effect) |
| 11 | Bandwidth Efficiency | **High** (narrow BW) | **Low** (wide BW) |
| 12 | Power Efficiency | **Low** (carrier wastes power) | **High** (constant envelope, all power is useful) |
| 13 | Complexity of Modulator | Simple (multiplier + adder) | Complex (VCO, PLL, reactance modulator) |
| 14 | Complexity of Demodulator | Simple envelope detector | Complex (PLL, FM discriminator, slope detector) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Exams:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert x \vert` (used for absolute value) is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table layout. **The above table is valid for use in KTU answer sheets.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AM Applications:** AM broadcast radio (MW, 540 kHz – 1640 kHz), aviation VHF communication (118–137 MHz), Citizens Band (CB) radio.
- **FM Applications:** FM broadcast radio (88–108 MHz), two-way radio (police, taxi), TV sound carrier, wireless microphones, analog cellular (1G/2G) and many satellite links.
- **Why FM dominates high-fidelity audio:** Wide bandwidth + noise immunity → superior Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) at the audio output.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the AM Signal Expression and Total Power

**Given:**
- Carrier: $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$
- Message: $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$
- Modulation index: $\mu = k_a A_m$ (with $k_a$ being modulator sensitivity)

**Step 1:** Define the AM signal by varying the carrier's amplitude:
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Expand the product to isolate carrier and sidebands:
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \mu A_c\cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos A\cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big)$$

**Step 4:** Identify the three spectral components:
- Carrier term: $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ → at $f_c$, amplitude $A_c$
- LSB: $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big)$ → at $(f_c - f_m)$
- USB: $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big)$ → at $(f_c + f_m)$

**Step 5:** Compute total power over a 1 Ω load. Since the three components are orthogonal (different frequencies), powers add:
$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} + \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:
$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Hence:
$$\boxed{P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 8:** Transmission efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

For maximum legal modulation $\mu = 1$, $\eta_{max} = 1/3 = 33.33\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of FM Bandwidth Using Carson's Rule

**Given:** Message $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ → maximum frequency deviation $\Delta f = k_f A_m$, modulation index $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$.

**Step 1:** The FM signal has a Bessel-function spectrum. The number of significant sideband pairs is roughly $n = \beta + 1$.

**Step 2:** Each significant sideband is spaced $f_m$ away from the next. So the total occupied bandwidth is:
$$B = 2n f_m = 2(\beta + 1)f_m$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$:
$$B = 2\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right)\!f_m = 2\Delta f + 2f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

$$\boxed{B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) \quad \text{(Carson's Rule)}}$$

**Step 4:** Comparison with AM: $B_{AM} = 2f_m$. FM uses more bandwidth by the factor:
$$\frac{B_{FM}}{B_{AM}} = \beta + 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** If $\beta \ll 1$ (narrowband FM), then $B_{FM} \approx 2f_m$, identical to AM. This is **NBFM**, which is why it is sometimes called *"AM with phase modulation."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Engineering Trade-off)

| Engineering Trade-off | Winner | Quantitative Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth efficiency | **AM** | $B_{AM} = 2f_m$ vs. $B_{FM} = 2(\beta+1)f_m$ |
| Power efficiency | **FM** | $\eta_{AM,max} = 33\%$, $\eta_{FM} = 100\%$ |
| Noise immunity | **FM** | FM SNR gain $\approx 3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ for large $\beta$ |
| Circuit simplicity (modulator + demodulator) | **AM** | Envelope detector vs. PLL/FM discriminator |
| Audio fidelity (high SNR) | **FM** | Commercial FM uses $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $\beta = 5$ |
| Co-channel interference rejection | **FM** | Capture effect (typically 1 dB → AM) |
| Suitability for low-power handheld | **AM** | Simple receiver, lower battery drain |
| Susceptibility to amplitude noise | **AM** (very high) | **FM** (immune via limiter) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** AM and FM are not "better" or "worse" universally — they are **complementary trade-offs**. AM is bandwidth-efficient but power-inefficient; FM is power-efficient but bandwidth-expensive. This trade-off is the heart of every KTU comparison question.

---

## 3.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio Comparison (Derivation Skeleton)

For an **input** channel SNR $(S/N)_i$:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o,\,AM} = \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_i$$

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o,\,FM} = 3\beta^2(\beta + 1)\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_i \quad \text{(for } \beta \gg 1\text{)}$$

Hence, the **FM SNR gain over AM** is:
$$G_{FM/AM} = 3\beta^2(\beta + 1)$$

For $\beta = 5$ (commercial FM broadcast): $G \approx 3(25)(6) = 450 \approx 26.5$ dB — a huge improvement.

> [!TIP]
> This is the **mathematical proof** of why FM is used for high-fidelity music broadcast: ~26 dB better audio SNR than AM for the same transmitter power and input noise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Comparison: AM vs. FM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX_A[AM TRANSMITTER]
        M1[Message Signal m of t] --> SUMMER1[Summing Node]
        OSC1[DC Bias A_c] --> SUMMER1
        SUMMER1 --> MULT1[Multiplier]
        CAR1[Carrier A_c cos 2 pi fc t] --> MULT1
        MULT1 --> AMP1[Linear Power Amplifier]
        AMP1 --> ANT1[Antenna]
    end

    subgraph RX_A[AM RECEIVER]
        ANT1b[Antenna] --> TUNE1[RF Tuned Amplifier]
        TUNE1 --> MIX1[Mixer]
        LO1[Local Oscillator] --> MIX1
        MIX1 --> IF1[IF Amplifier at 455 kHz]
        IF1 --> DET1[Envelope Detector]
        DET1 --> AF1[Audio Amplifier]
        AF1 --> SPK1[Speaker Output m hat of t]
    end

    ANT1 -. wireless channel .-> ANT1b

    subgraph TX_F[FM TRANSMITTER]
        M2[Message Signal m of t] --> VCO[Voltage Controlled Oscillator]
        CRY[Crystal Reference fc] --> VCO
        VCO --> PA2[Class C Power Amplifier]
        PA2 --> LIM_PRE[Pre Emphasis Network]
        LIM_PRE --> ANT2[Antenna]
    end

    subgraph RX_F[FM RECEIVER]
        ANT2b[Antenna] --> TUNE2[RF Amplifier with Limiter]
        TUNE2 --> MIX2[Mixer]
        LO2[Local Oscillator] --> MIX2
        MIX2 --> IF2[IF Amplifier at 10.7 MHz]
        IF2 --> LIM2[Hard Limiter]
        LIM2 --> DISC[FM Discriminator PLL]
        DISC --> DE_EMP[De Emphasis Network]
        DE_EMP --> AF2[Audio Amplifier]
        AF2 --> SPK2[Speaker Output m hat of t]
    end

    ANT2 -. wireless channel .-> ANT2b
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram (for KTU viva):**
> - The **AM transmitter** uses a *summing node + multiplier* — linear modulation.
> - The **FM transmitter** uses a **VCO** (Voltage Controlled Oscillator) — frequency is directly modulated; a *class-C amplifier* is allowed because amplitude carries no information.
> - The **FM receiver** contains a **Hard Limiter** before the discriminator — this is the element that *removes amplitude noise* (the secret of FM's noise immunity).
> - The **AM receiver** has no limiter — any amplitude noise passes through to the detector.

## 4.2 Spectral Comparison Diagram (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AM_SPEC[AM SPECTRUM FOR SINGLE TONE MODULATION]
        direction LR
        LSB_A["LSB  at fc minus fm  Amplitude mu Ac over 2"] --- CAR_A["CARRIER  at fc  Amplitude Ac"] --- USB_A["USB  at fc plus fm  Amplitude mu Ac over 2"]
    end

    subgraph FM_SPEC[FM SPECTRUM FOR SINGLE TONE MODULATION]
        direction LR
        SB1["f1"] --- SB2["f2"] --- SB3["f3"] --- CAR_F["f_c  Amplitude Ac x J0 beta"] --- SB4["f4"] --- SB5["f5"] --- SB6["f6"]
    end

    AM_SPEC ~~~ FM_SPEC
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Point:** AM has only **two sidebands** (one pair) per modulating tone. FM has **infinitely many** sidebands (Bessel function amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$), with theoretically infinite bandwidth — practically truncated by Carson's rule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define modulation index for AM. Why must it be ≤ 1?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **modulation index for AM** is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal $A_m$ to the peak amplitude of the carrier $A_c$:
$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = k_a \cdot A_m$$

It must satisfy $\mu \le 1$ to avoid **envelope distortion** (over-modulation). If $\mu > 1$, the message amplitude exceeds the carrier amplitude, causing the modulated envelope to cross zero and invert — the envelope detector then fails to recover the original message faithfully. This is a critical KTU point.

### Question 2: What is frequency deviation in FM? On what factor does it depend?
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**Frequency deviation** is the instantaneous difference between the frequency of the FM signal and the carrier frequency. The **maximum frequency deviation** $\Delta f$ is the peak deviation, defined as:
$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m$$

It depends on (i) the **amplitude of the message signal** $A_m$ and (ii) the **frequency sensitivity** $k_f$ of the modulator (Hz/volt). It does **not** depend on the modulating frequency $f_m$ — that affects only the **modulation index** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for an AM signal when the modulating signal is a single-tone cosine wave. Sketch its frequency spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare AM and FM systems on the basis of (i) bandwidth, (ii) power efficiency, (iii) noise immunity, and (iv) complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

Let the message be $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and the carrier be $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

**Step 1:** Apply the AM definition (amplitude of carrier varied linearly with message):
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Multiply out:
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \mu A_c\cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3:** Use $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:
$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big)$$

**Step 4:** Sketch the spectrum with three impulses at $f_c - f_m$, $f_c$, and $f_c + f_m$ with heights $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$, $A_c$, and $\frac{\mu A_c}{2}$ respectively.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

| Parameter | AM | FM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Bandwidth | $B_{AM} = 2f_m$ (narrow) | $B_{FM} = 2(\beta+1)f_m$ (wide) |
| (ii) Power Efficiency | $\eta = \mu^2/(2+\mu^2)$, max 33.3% | $100\%$ (constant envelope) |
| (iii) Noise Immunity | Poor — noise on amplitude passes through | Excellent — limiter removes amplitude noise; SNR gain $= 3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ |
| (iv) Complexity | Simple modulator (multiplier) + simple demodulator (envelope detector) | Complex modulator (VCO) + complex demodulator (PLL/discriminator) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked *"Compare AM and FM,"* students often give a *one-sided answer* (e.g., "FM is better"). Marks are deducted for not balancing the comparison. Always state **both** advantages and disadvantages for **each** system, then conclude with the application-driven choice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the instantaneous frequency and the FM signal for a single-tone modulating signal. Define modulation index for FM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial FM broadcast system uses a maximum frequency deviation of 75 kHz and a maximum modulating frequency of 15 kHz. Calculate (i) the modulation index, (ii) the bandwidth using Carson's rule, and (iii) compare it with the AM bandwidth for the same message. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Step 1:** For a message $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the instantaneous frequency is:
$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t) = f_c + k_f A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2:** Maximum deviation:
$$\Delta f = k_f A_m$$

**Step 3:** Total phase angle (integral of $2\pi f_i(\tau)$):
$$\phi(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into $s(t) = A_c\cos(\phi(t))$:
$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)\right) = A_c\cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)$$

where $\beta = k_f A_m / f_m = \Delta f / f_m$ is the **modulation index for FM**.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:** $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz.

**(i) Modulation index:**
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75}{15} = 5$$

**(ii) Bandwidth by Carson's rule:**
$$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(75 + 15) = 2 \times 90 = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) AM bandwidth for the same message:**
$$B_{AM} = 2f_m = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ kHz}$$

**Comparison:**
$$\frac{B_{FM}}{B_{AM}} = \frac{180}{30} = 6$$

So **FM uses 6 times more bandwidth** than AM for the same audio message — a clear bandwidth vs. fidelity trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Comparison of AM and FM" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the formula for total power in AM** — $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ is a **must-derive** result in KTU paper III.
> 2. **Confusing bandwidth formulas** — AM has $2f_m$ exactly; FM uses Carson's rule $2(\Delta f + f_m)$.
> 3. **Stating "FM is noise-free"** — incorrect. FM is *more noise-immune*, not noise-free. The SNR gain is $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$.
> 4. **Missing the "modulation index" definitions** — write $\mu = A_m/A_c$ for AM and $\beta = \Delta f/f_m$ for FM explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting units** — bandwidth in Hz, deviation in Hz, modulation index dimensionless.
> 6. **Mixing up the role of carrier amplitude** — In AM, $A_c$ is fixed; *sidebands* carry information. In FM, $A_c$ is fixed; *frequency deviation* carries information.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AM (Amplitude Modulation):** Carrier **amplitude** is varied; carrier frequency/phase held constant; **linear** modulation; $B = 2f_m$.
- **FM (Frequency Modulation):** Carrier **frequency** is varied; carrier amplitude held constant; **non-linear** modulation; $B = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$.
- **AM Modulation Index:** $\mu = A_m/A_c$ (must be $\le 1$ to avoid envelope distortion).
- **FM Modulation Index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ (no upper limit; larger $\beta$ → better noise immunity but wider BW).
- **AM Total Power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ → carrier wastes power → low efficiency.
- **FM Total Power:** $P_t = A_c^2/2$ → constant → 100% efficiency (theoretical).
- **AM Bandwidth:** $B_{AM} = 2f_m$ (fixed by message, independent of modulation depth).
- **FM Bandwidth (Carson):** $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1)f_m$ (grows with $\beta$).
- **Noise Performance:** FM is superior due to the **hard limiter** in the receiver that strips amplitude noise; the SNR gain over AM is $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$.
- **Applications:** AM → broadcast radio, aviation, CB. FM → broadcast radio (88–108 MHz), TV sound, two-way mobile, satellite links.
- **Trade-off Principle:** AM is *bandwidth-efficient* but *power-inefficient*; FM is *power-efficient* but *bandwidth-expensive*. The choice is application-driven.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize (KTU Board):** $\mu$, $\beta$, $P_t$, $\eta$, $B_{AM}$, $B_{FM}$ (Carson), SNR gain.
- **NBFM Special Case:** When $\beta \ll 1$, $B_{FM} \approx 2f_m$ and FM behaves like AM with phase modulation — useful conceptual link.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Digital Communication - Sampling Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 2 — Principles of Digital Communication: The Sampling Theorem

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem):**
> A **band-limited analog signal** $x(t)$ whose **highest frequency component is $f_m$ Hz** can be **completely reconstructed** from its discrete samples, provided that the **sampling frequency $f_s$** is at least **twice the maximum frequency** of the signal.
>
> $$\boxed{\,f_s \;\geq\; 2 f_m\,}$$
>
> The minimum sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ is the **Nyquist Interval**.

In the 2024 KTU Scheme, this theorem is the gateway concept of Module 2 — it bridges the continuous-time analog world and the discrete-time digital domain that the rest of the module (PCM, DPCM, DM, ADM) is built upon.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Movie Reel" Analogy 🎬

Imagine you are watching a **spinning ceiling fan**. The fan blades rotate so fast that your eyes see a continuous blur. Now imagine a **slow-motion camera** that captures the fan at 30 frames per second. If the blades complete **more than 15 full revolutions per second**, the captured frames will show the blades in **completely wrong positions** — what we call **aliasing** (the wagon-wheel effect in movies).

If instead the camera captures the fan at, say, **60 frames per second (twice the rotation speed)**, the playback is smooth and faithful. The Sampling Theorem is precisely this rule applied to **electrical signals**:

| Real-World Element | Signal-Processing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Spinning fan | Continuous analog signal $x(t)$ |
| Blade speed (rev/sec) | Signal frequency $f_m$ |
| Camera frame rate | Sampling frequency $f_s$ |
| Wagon-wheel illusion | **Aliasing** (irrecoverable distortion) |
| Smooth playback | **Perfect reconstruction** at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Sampling is a *lossless compression in time* — we are throwing away 99 % of the signal's time-axis values, yet keeping **all the information**, *provided* the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlight Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Sampling Rates used in Practice:**
> - **Speech signal (telephony, 4 kHz bandwidth):** $f_s = \mathbf{8 \; kHz}$ (Nyquist rate)
> - **CD-quality audio (20 kHz bandwidth):** $f_s = \mathbf{44.1 \; kHz}$
> - **Studio audio:** $f_s = \mathbf{48 \; kHz}$
> - **Nyquist frequency** (the folding limit): $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$

# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step by Step

The sampling theorem is not just one equation. The KTU examiner expects you to understand the **full pipeline** of how an analog signal becomes discrete and back. Let us break it down into a structured, six-stage logic flow:

### Step 1 — Identify the Band-Limited Signal
- The signal $x(t)$ must be **strictly band-limited** to $f_m$ Hz, i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$.
- A practical signal is first passed through an **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_m$ — to enforce this.

### Step 2 — Choose the Sampling Frequency
- Pick $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. Common engineering practice: $f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $4 f_m$ to leave a **guard band**.

### Step 3 — Generate the Sampling Pulse Train
- The **sampling function** is a periodic train of narrow pulses:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

- Here $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the **sampling period** and $\delta(\cdot)$ is the **Dirac delta**.

### Step 4 — Multiply the Signal with the Pulse Train
- The **sampled signal** is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

- The sample values $x(nT_s)$ are called the **discrete-time representation** of $x(t)$.

### Step 5 — Observe the Frequency-Domain Effect
- Multiplication in time becomes **convolution in frequency**:

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

- The original spectrum $X(f)$ is now **replicated** at every integer multiple of $f_s$. As long as the replicas do not overlap (i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_m$), each copy remains intact.

### Step 6 — Reconstruct the Original Signal
- Pass $x_s(t)$ through an **Ideal Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** of bandwidth $f_m$ Hz.
- The filter isolates the central replica $X(f)$ and outputs the original $x(t)$ perfectly.

---

## 2.2 Why Does It Work? — The "Why & How" Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Reason:** Because $x(t)$ is *band-limited*, its spectrum contains no content above $f_m$. As long as the shifted copies of $X(f)$ — located at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ — **do not overlap**, we can carve out a single copy with a low-pass filter. The replicas act as **guard rails** keeping the central information intact.

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Why multiply by a pulse train? | To "freeze" the signal value at regular time instants. |
| Why does the spectrum replicate? | Convolution of $X(f)$ with an impulse train in frequency. |
| Why $f_s \geq 2 f_m$? | To prevent adjacent spectral replicas from **overlapping** (aliasing). |
| Why an ideal LPF for reconstruction? | It passes only the baseband replica and discards higher-order images. |
| Why an anti-aliasing filter before sampling? | To band-limit the real-world (non-ideal) signal so the theorem's premise holds. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Condition | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist Rate** | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Hz | Minimum legal sampling rate |
| 2 | **Nyquist Interval** | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ | seconds | Maximum legal sampling period |
| 3 | **General Sampling Condition** | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz | No-aliasing rule |
| 4 | **Aliasing Frequency** (when $f_s < 2 f_m$) | $f_a = \vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f/f_s) \vert$ | Hz | Computes the spurious frequency |
| 5 | **Sampled Signal (Impulse)** | $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$ | V or A | Math model of ideal sampling |
| 6 | **Spectrum of Sampled Signal** | $X_s(f) = f_s \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ | V/Hz | Shows the replicated baseband |
| 7 | **Natural Sampling Output** | $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$ | V | Pulse amplitude tracks input |
| 8 | **Flat-top (Sample-and-Hold) Output** | $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\, \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$ | V | Holds value for duration $\tau$ |
| 9 | **Aperture Effect Roll-off** | $\dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau} = \text{sinc}(f \tau)$ | dimensionless | High-frequency loss in flat-top sampling |
| 10 | **Reconstruction Filter Bandwidth** | $B = f_m$ | Hz | Cut-off of ideal LPF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** *"Twice the top — that is the stop."* Sample at *twice* the maximum frequency, or the spectrum *stops* working.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Signal | $f_m$ | $f_s$ (Nyquist) | Deployed $f_s$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Telephone (PSTN)** | Voice | 3.4 kHz | 6.8 kHz | **8 kHz** |
| **CD Audio** | Music | 20 kHz | 40 kHz | **44.1 kHz** |
| **Digital TV (SD)** | Video | 6 MHz | 12 MHz | **13.5 MHz** (ITU-R 601) |
| **EEG (medical)** | Brain waves | 100 Hz | 200 Hz | **256–512 Hz** |
| **Vibration analysis** | Machine vibration | 1 kHz | 2 kHz | **5–10 kHz** |

The sampling theorem is what enables **every digital system on Earth** — from the smartphone in your pocket to MRI scanners in hospitals.

# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spectrum of an Impulse-Sampled Signal

We will derive the most important equation in the topic: $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k} X(f - k f_s)$.

**Given:**
- Input signal $x(t)$ is band-limited to $f_m$ Hz.
- Sampling pulse train:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 1 — Write the sampled signal in the time domain.**

The sampled signal is obtained by multiplying $x(t)$ with $p(t)$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for $p(t)$.**

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the multiplication (sifting property of the delta).**

By the sifting property, $x(t)\,\delta(t - nT_s) = x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$:

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

This is the **discrete-time representation** — a weighted impulse train whose weights are the sample values.

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform of both sides.**

We use linearity and the shift property: $\mathcal{F}\{\delta(t - nT_s)\} = e^{-j2\pi f n T_s}$:

$$X_s(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, e^{-j2\pi f n T_s}$$

**Step 5 — Express this as a convolution in the frequency domain.**

The Fourier Transform of the product $x(t)\,p(t)$ equals $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\, X(f) * P(f)$:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\, X(f) * P(f)$$

**Step 6 — Find the Fourier Transform of the impulse train $p(t)$.**

The Fourier series of a periodic impulse train with period $T_s$ is itself an impulse train in the frequency domain with period $f_s = 1/T_s$ and weights $1/T_s$:

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 7 — Convolve $X(f)$ with $P(f)$.**

Convolution with a frequency-domain impulse train **replicates** $X(f)$ at every $k f_s$:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) * \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 8 — Apply the sifting property of convolution.**

$X(f) * \delta(f - k f_s) = X(f - k f_s)$:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{2\pi T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 9 — Simplify using $f_s = 1/T_s$.**

Since $f_s = 1/T_s$, the constant $\dfrac{1}{2\pi T_s}$ becomes $\dfrac{f_s}{2\pi}$. **However**, the standard signal-processing convention uses the symmetric Fourier pair where this reduces elegantly to:

$$\boxed{\,X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)\,}$$

**Step 10 — Interpret the result.**

The original spectrum $X(f)$ is **replicated** at every integer multiple of $f_s$, with amplitude scaled by $f_s$. Adjacent replicas are separated by $f_s$. **No overlap** iff $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — completing the proof of the sampling theorem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Natural Sampling (Chopper Sampling)

In **natural sampling**, the sampling pulses are finite-width rectangular pulses of width $\tau$ and period $T_s$, but the **top of each pulse follows the analog signal** during the pulse interval.

**Step 1 — Express the natural sampling pulse train.**

Let the pulse amplitude be $A$ and pulse width be $\tau$:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Write the natural-sampled signal.**

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the Fourier series of the rectangular pulse train.**

A periodic rectangular pulse train has the Fourier series:

$$p(t) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{k\tau}{T_s}\right) e^{j2\pi k f_s t}$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

**Step 4 — Multiply $x(t)$ by $p(t)$ and take the Fourier Transform.**

Multiplication in time-domain corresponds to convolution in the frequency domain. The result:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{k\tau}{T_s}\right) X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the spectrum.**

- The baseband replica is **scaled** by $\dfrac{A\tau}{T_s}$ (the average duty cycle).
- Higher replicas are **attenuated** by the sinc envelope $\text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{k\tau}{T_s}\right)$.
- As $\tau \to 0$, the sinc envelope approaches 1 for all $k$, and we recover the ideal impulse-sampling case.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold)

In **flat-top sampling**, the sampled value is **held constant** for the entire pulse duration $\tau$. This is what real-world **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuits** do.

**Step 1 — Express the held sample mathematically.**

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the structure.**

This is a **convolution** of an impulse-sampled signal $x_\delta(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$ with a rectangular pulse $h(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$:

$$x_s(t) = x_\delta(t) * h(t)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Fourier Transform.**

By the convolution theorem:

$$X_s(f) = X_\delta(f) \cdot H(f)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $X_\delta(f)$ from our earlier derivation.**

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) \cdot H(f)$$

**Step 5 — The Fourier Transform of the rectangle.**

The Fourier transform of a rectangle of width $\tau$ and unit height is:

$$H(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression.**

$$\boxed{\,X_s(f) = f_s \tau \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(f\tau)\, X(f - k f_s)\,}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result — The Aperture Effect.**

> [!NOTE]
> The sinc factor $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ multiplies **every** spectral replica. This means the baseband $X(f)$ is **distorted** — high frequencies inside the baseband are attenuated more than low frequencies. This is the **Aperture Effect**, and it is corrected at the receiver using an **equalization filter** $E(f) = 1/\text{sinc}(f\tau)$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reconstruction via Ideal LPF

Once $x_s(t)$ is available at the receiver, the original $x(t)$ is recovered.

**Step 1 — Pass $x_s(t)$ through an ideal LPF.**

The transfer function:

$$H_{LPF}(f) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > f_m \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Filter the spectrum.**

$$Y(f) = X_s(f) \cdot H_{LPF}(f) = f_s X(f)$$

The LPF picks out the $k = 0$ replica.

**Step 3 — Recover $x(t)$.**

$$y(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{f_s X(f)\} = f_s\, x(t)$$

The factor $f_s$ is just a constant amplitude scaling, easily removed by a gain stage. The **shape of $x(t)$ is exactly preserved** — confirming the theorem.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Aliasing Frequency Calculation

When $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas **overlap**, and the original signal becomes irrecoverable. The aliased frequency is:

$$f_{\text{alias}} = \left\vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f}{f_s}\right) \right\vert$$

**Worked example:** A signal at $f = 7$ kHz is sampled at $f_s = 4$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Compute $f/f_s$.**

$$\frac{f}{f_s} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75$$

**Step 2 — Round to nearest integer.**

$$\text{round}(1.75) = 2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the alias.**

$$f_{\text{alias}} = \vert 7 - 2 \cdot 4 \vert = \vert 7 - 8 \vert = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

**Interpretation:** A 7 kHz tone, when undersampled at 4 kHz, **folds down** to 1 kHz — an irrecoverable distortion.

# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Digital Communication Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Analog Source x of t] --> aaf[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    aaf --> smp[Sampler and Hold Circuit]
    smp --> quan[Quantizer]
    quan --> enc[Binary Encoder]
    enc --> chx[Channel Encoder]
    chx --> mod[Modulator]
    mod --> chan[Noisy Channel]
    chan --> dem[Demodulator]
    dem --> chd[Channel Decoder]
    chd --> dec[Binary Decoder]
    dec --> rcn[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    rcn --> dst[Recovered Signal y of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** sits *before* the sampler, and the **Reconstruction LPF** sits *after* the decoder. The sampling theorem is implemented in the AAF → Sampler → Reconstruction LPF trio.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — The Sampling Theorem as a Five-Stage Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Maximum Frequency f sub m] --> B[Step 2: Apply Anti Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[Step 3: Choose Sampling Frequency f sub s greater than or equal to 2 f sub m]
    C --> D[Step 4: Generate Pulse Train p of t and Multiply with x of t]
    D --> E{Step 5: Is f sub s greater than or equal to 2 f sub m?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Replicas do NOT overlap: Pass through LPF to recover x of t]
    E -- No --> G[Replicas OVERLAP: Aliasing occurs, x of t is IRRECOVERABLE]
```

---

## 4.3 Time-Domain and Frequency-Domain View of Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Time Domain]
        A1[Continuous x of t] --> A2[Sampled x sub s of t: weighted impulses]
    end
    subgraph FD[Frequency Domain]
        B1[Baseband X of f: single lobe] --> B2[Replicated X sub s of f: lobes at k f sub s]
    end
    A1 -. Fourier .-> B1
    A2 -. Fourier .-> B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most important visualization for the KTU exam. In the **time domain**, sampling turns a continuous waveform into a *sparse* set of impulses. In the **frequency domain**, sampling *replicates* the baseband spectrum. The Nyquist criterion keeps the replicas apart.

---

## 4.4 Sampling Theorem Decision Tree — Sampling Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Choose Sampling Method] --> S1{Is hardware cost critical?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Natural Sampling: simple chopper transistor switch]
    S1 -- No --> S3[Is high accuracy required at receiver?]
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Flat Top Sample and Hold: needs equalization filter at receiver]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Impulse Sampling: theoretical ideal used in math derivations]
```

---

## 4.5 Aliasing Visualization — Spectral Overlap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Good[Adequate Sampling f sub s greater than 2 f sub m]
        G1[X of f at origin] -. gap .-> G2[X of f at f sub s]
    end
    subgraph Bad[Undersampling f sub s less than 2 f sub m]
        B1[X of f at origin] ==overlap== B2[X of f at f sub s]
    end
```

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**State and explain the sampling theorem for low-pass signals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** *A band-limited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz can be completely recovered from its samples taken at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ samples/second.*

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- The signal is sampled by multiplying with an impulse train $p(t) = \sum \delta(t - nT_s)$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$.
- The resulting spectrum is $X_s(f) = f_s \sum X(f - k f_s)$, which contains infinitely many replicas of $X(f)$ spaced $f_s$ apart.
- If $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the replicas do not overlap; passing $x_s(t)$ through an ideal LPF of bandwidth $f_m$ recovers the original signal $x(t)$ exactly.
- $f_s = 2 f_m$ is the **Nyquist rate**; $T_s = 1/(2 f_m)$ is the **Nyquist interval**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *"sampling frequency must be greater than maximum frequency"* — that is **wrong**. It must be *at least twice* the maximum frequency. Full 3 marks lost for this single-word mistake.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**What is aliasing? How is it prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which **high-frequency components of a signal appear as false low-frequency components** in the sampled version, caused by **overlap of spectral replicas** when $f_s < 2 f_m$.

**Prevention (2 marks):**
1. **Pre-sampling Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):** A sharp low-pass filter placed *before* the sampler to band-limit the signal to $f_m$.
2. **Adequate Sampling Rate:** Choose $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (in practice, $2.5$ to $4$ times $f_m$).
3. **Oversampling** combined with digital filtering for better stop-band attenuation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students say *"increase the sampling rate"* without mentioning the *anti-aliasing filter*. The AAF is the **first line of defense** — without it, no sampling rate is safe from out-of-band noise folding in. Deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question provides an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below, both **Question A** and **Question B** are designed to be at par with university standard.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum \delta(t - nT_s)$. Show clearly that the original signal can be recovered if $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An analog signal $x(t)$ has a maximum frequency of 4 kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 10$ kHz. Determine (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) the Nyquist interval, and (iii) whether the chosen sampling rate satisfies the Nyquist criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the impulse train.**

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2 — Sampled signal in time domain.**

$$x_s(t) = x(t)\, p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Fourier transform using the shift property.**

$$X_s(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, e^{-j2\pi f n T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Fourier transform of the impulse train.**

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the convolution theorem.**

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\, X(f) * P(f) = \frac{f_s}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) * \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 6 — Sifting property of convolution.**

$$\boxed{\,X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)\,}$$

**Step 7 — Reconstruction condition.**

The replicas are centered at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$, each of width $2 f_m$. **No overlap** requires the gap between replica edges to be non-negative:

$$f_s - 2f_m \geq 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

A low-pass filter of bandwidth $f_m$ then extracts the central replica and recovers $x(t)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 10$ kHz.

**(i) Nyquist rate:**

$$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Nyquist interval:**

$$T_N = \frac{1}{f_N} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125\;\mu\text{s}$$

**(iii) Criterion check:**

$$f_s = 10 \text{ kHz} \;\geq\; f_N = 8 \text{ kHz} \;\checkmark$$

The chosen sampling rate **satisfies** the Nyquist criterion, with a guard band of $10 - 8 = 2$ kHz on each side, ensuring no aliasing.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** Explain the difference between **natural sampling** and **flat-top sampling** with neat spectrum diagrams. Derive the spectrum expression for flat-top sampling and discuss the **aperture effect**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal with maximum frequency 6 kHz is passed through an anti-aliasing filter and sampled at 5 kHz. Determine (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) whether the system aliases, and (iii) if yes, calculate the alias frequency of a 7 kHz component. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Natural Sampling | Flat-Top Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse shape | Top follows $x(t)$ | Top held at $x(nT_s)$ |
| Sample value | Time-varying during $\tau$ | Constant during $\tau$ |
| Implementation | Chopper / FET switch | Sample-and-Hold (S/H) IC |
| Mathematical model | $x(t) \cdot p(t)$ | $\sum x(nT_s) \text{rect}((t - nT_s)/\tau)$ |
| Aperture effect | Absent (sinc envelope in spectrum) | Present (causes amplitude distortion) |
| Reconstruction | Direct LPF | LPF + equalization filter |

**Derivation of Flat-Top Spectrum (3 marks):**

The flat-top signal is a convolution of impulse-sampled signal with a rectangular pulse $h(t)$:

$$x_{FT}(t) = x_\delta(t) * h(t), \quad h(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$$

Taking the Fourier transform:

$$X_{FT}(f) = X_\delta(f) \cdot H(f) = f_s \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) \sum_{k} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Aperture Effect (1 mark):**

The factor $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ attenuates high frequencies inside the baseband. It is corrected at the receiver with an equalization filter $E(f) = 1/\text{sinc}(f\tau)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f_m = 6$ kHz, $f_s = 5$ kHz, test tone $f = 7$ kHz.

**(i) Nyquist rate:**

$$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Aliasing check:**

$$f_s = 5 \text{ kHz} \;<\; f_N = 12 \text{ kHz} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Aliasing WILL occur.}$$

**(iii) Alias frequency of the 7 kHz tone:**

Using the alias formula $f_a = \vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f/f_s) \vert$:

$$\frac{f}{f_s} = \frac{7}{5} = 1.4, \quad \text{round}(1.4) = 1$$

$$f_a = \vert 7 - 1 \cdot 5 \vert = \vert 2 \mid = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

The 7 kHz tone is **irrecoverably folded** to 2 kHz.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings — High-Frequency Loss Zones ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places where KTU examiners routinely deduct marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $f_s$** in the spectrum derivation $X_s(f) = f_s \sum X(f - k f_s)$ — write the full expression, not just the summation.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist rate with sampling rate** — $f_N = 2 f_m$ is the *minimum*; the *chosen* $f_s$ can be (and usually is) higher.
> 3. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter** in block diagrams — it is a **mandatory** block for full marks.
> 4. **Writing $|x|$ in a markdown table** — use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking the table layout in KTU's online portal.
> 5. **Failing to mention the aperture effect** in flat-top sampling — it is a frequently asked 2-mark follow-up question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist ✅

| # | Concept to Remember | One-Line Takeaway |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is the master rule. |
| 2 | **Nyquist Rate** | $f_N = 2 f_m$ — the minimum legal $f_s$. |
| 3 | **Nyquist Interval** | $T_N = 1/(2 f_m)$ — the maximum legal $T_s$. |
| 4 | **Impulse Sampling** | Pure math model; produces weighted impulses. |
| 5 | **Natural Sampling** | Pulse top tracks signal; simple but variable amplitude. |
| 6 | **Flat-Top Sampling** | Value held constant for $\tau$; introduces aperture effect. |
| 7 | **Aperture Effect** | High-frequency roll-off $\propto \text{sinc}(f\tau)$; fix with equalizer. |
| 8 | **Aliasing** | $f_s < 2 f_m \Rightarrow$ spectral overlap $\Rightarrow$ irrecoverable distortion. |
| 9 | **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | Sharp LPF placed *before* the sampler; the first line of defense. |
| 10 | **Reconstruction** | Ideal LPF of bandwidth $f_m$ after the digital-to-analog stage. |
| 11 | **Spectral Replication** | $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k} X(f - k f_s)$ — central equation. |
| 12 | **Guard Band** | Practical $f_s = 2.5$–$4 f_m$ to leave headroom. |
| 13 | **Alias Formula** | $f_a = \vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f/f_s) \vert$. |
| 14 | **Convolution Theorem** | Time-domain multiplication $\Leftrightarrow$ frequency-domain convolution. |
