| 15 | **Sifting Property** | $x(t)\delta(t - nT_s) = x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$ — the engine of sampling. |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nyquist Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nyquist Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Nyquist Criterion** (more formally, the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is the foundational result of communication theory that establishes the *minimum rate* at which a continuous-time, **bandlimited** signal must be sampled so that it can be *uniquely and perfectly reconstructed* from its discrete samples — without any loss of information.

In precise mathematical form, the criterion states:

> **"A real-valued, baseband, finite-energy signal $x(t)$ whose spectrum $X(f)$ is strictly zero for all frequencies $\vert f \vert > W$ Hz can be completely and uniquely reconstructed from its instantaneous samples $\{x(nT_s)\}_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}$, provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2W$ samples per second."**

This minimum allowable rate $f_s = 2W$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and the corresponding maximum sampling interval $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2W}$ seconds is called the **Nyquist Interval**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEERT411 — Module 2):**
> The Nyquist Criterion is the bridge between **continuous-time analog signals** and **discrete-time digital representations**. Every downstream block in a digital communication chain — Quantizer, PCM Encoder, Line Coder, and ultimately the Decision Device — is built on the guarantee that the Sampling Theorem uniquely preserves the message signal.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Does Sampling Work?

Imagine watching a movie. A movie is, in reality, a *discrete sequence of still photographs* flashed on a screen roughly **24 times every second**. To the human eye, this rapid sequence *appears* as a perfectly smooth, continuous motion. Why? Because the human visual system **cannot resolve changes faster than about 24 frames per second** — the snapshots are captured fast enough to fool the brain.

The Nyquist Criterion applies exactly the same principle, but to **signals** instead of motion:

- The **signal's highest frequency $W$** plays the role of the *"fastest motion"* the signal contains.
- The **sampling frequency $f_s$** plays the role of the **movie's frame rate**.
- The theorem guarantees that if you "take snapshots" of the signal at least **$2W$ times per second**, then a **mathematical low-pass filter (the reconstruction filter)** can perfectly "stitch them back" into the original continuous waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Anchor:** Commercial CD-quality digital audio uses $f_s = 44.1$ kHz because human hearing stops near $W \approx 20$ kHz. The Nyquist Rate here is $40$ kHz, and the engineers chose $44.1$ kHz to provide a **safety margin** (an *anti-alias guard-band* of $4.1$ kHz) for realizable, non-ideal low-pass filters.

---

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $f_s$ | Sampling Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** or samples/sec |
| $T_s$ | Sampling Interval | **Seconds (s)** |
| $W$ | Signal Bandwidth (highest baseband frequency) | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| $f_N$ | Nyquist Rate | **Hz** |
| $T_N$ | Nyquist Interval | **s** |
| $x(t)$ | Continuous-time message signal | Volts (or normalized) |
| $x_s(t)$ | Sampled signal (impulse train modulated) | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Pitfall Avoided:** The "Nyquist Bandwidth" refers to the *half-rate* $W$ (the maximum positive baseband frequency), **NOT** the sampling rate itself. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 1.4 Scope of This Study Note

This note develops the Nyquist Criterion across five analytical dimensions:

1. **Mathematical Derivation** — proving *why* the criterion works, via impulse-train modulation and Fourier analysis.
2. **Reconstruction Formula** — the *Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula*, which is the explicit mathematical recipe to recover $x(t)$.
3. **Aliasing Pathology** — what happens when the criterion is violated, and how to defend against it.
4. **Bandpass Sampling Extension** — the generalized criterion for *bandpass* (not just baseband) signals.
5. **Practical Sampling Architectures** — Natural Sampling vs. Flat-Top Sampling, the realistic counterparts of the mathematical ideal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Theorem — Step-by-Step

The theorem is not a magic trick; it is a direct consequence of the **spectral convolution property** of the Fourier Transform. The logical pipeline is:

**Step 1 — Identify the Assumption of Bandlimitation.**
The original message signal $x(t)$ must be *bandlimited*, meaning its Fourier Transform $X(f)$ vanishes identically outside the interval $[-W, +W]$ Hz. In symbols:

$$X(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert > W$$

If this assumption is violated, **no finite sampling rate can ever suffice** to reconstruct the signal — no matter how fast we sample. This is a hard physical limitation, not a design flaw.

**Step 2 — Modulate the Signal with an Impulse Train.**
Sampling is mathematically modeled as multiplication of $x(t)$ with a periodic Dirac comb $\delta_{T_s}(t)$ of period $T_s = 1/f_s$. The result is an *impulse train whose weights are the sample values*:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Transform to the Frequency Domain.**
Multiplication in time becomes *periodic replication* in frequency. The sampled spectrum is:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - n f_s)$$

This is the central mathematical identity. It says that $X_s(f)$ is an *infinite periodic string of replicas* of the original spectrum $X(f)$, scaled by $1/T_s$, and spaced at intervals of $f_s$ along the frequency axis.

**Step 4 — Test for Spectral Overlap (the Key Condition).**
Each replica $X(f - nf_s)$ is centered at frequency $nf_s$ and has a *support width* of $2W$ (from $nf_s - W$ to $nf_s + W$).

- **If $f_s \geq 2W$**, the replicas are spaced *at least as far apart as their own width*. They do **not** overlap. Each replica can be cleanly isolated by an ideal low-pass filter.
- **If $f_s < 2W$**, adjacent replicas *overlap*. The tails of one replica add to the tails of its neighbor, producing the irreversible distortion called **aliasing**.

**Step 5 — Reconstruct via Low-Pass Filtering.**
Under the no-overlap condition, passing $X_s(f)$ through an ideal low-pass filter of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $W$ recovers exactly $X(f)$. The corresponding inverse Fourier Transform yields:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}(2W(t - nT_s))$$

This is the **Whittaker–Shannon Reconstruction Formula**.

---

### 2.2 Why the "Why" Matters — The Engineering Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Nyquist Criterion is essentially a **dimensionally-tuned tradeoff** between *time-domain sparsity* (taking few samples) and *frequency-domain resolution* (avoiding spectral overlap). The quantity $2W$ emerges because *each spectrum of width $2W$* requires a *guard interval of at least $2W$* before its neighbor begins.

In modern engineering (5G NR, LTE, CD audio, MRI imaging, software-defined radio), the Nyquist Criterion is not merely a theorem — it is the *design budget* that determines ADC sampling speeds, anti-alias filter orders, and DSP processor loads.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Convention:** Absolute-value bars are rendered as `\vert` to preserve clean Markdown table syntax (no naked `|` characters).

| # | Formula / Identity | Physical / Mathematical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_N = 2W$ | **Nyquist Rate** — minimum sampling frequency for a baseband bandlimited signal of bandwidth $W$ | Hz |
| 2 | $T_N = \dfrac{1}{2W}$ | **Nyquist Interval** — maximum allowable sampling period | seconds |
| 3 | $f_s \geq 2W$ | **No-aliasing (Nyquist) Condition** | Hz |
| 4 | $x_s(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ | **Ideal Impulse-Train Sampled Signal** | V |
| 5 | $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - nf_s)$ | **Spectral Replication Theorem** — sampled spectrum is periodic replica of $X(f)$ | V/Hz |
| 6 | $x(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}(2W(t - nT_s))$ | **Whittaker–Shannon Reconstruction Formula** | V |
| 7 | $\mathrm{sinc}(u) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi u)}{\pi u}$ | **Normalized Sinc Function** — the reconstruction kernel | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\delta(t - nT_s)$ | **Dirac Comb (Periodic Impulse Train)** of period $T_s$ | $1/\mathrm{s}$ |
| 9 | Aliasing condition | $f_s < 2W$ | Hz |
| 10 | Guard band fraction | $\dfrac{f_s - 2W}{2W} \times 100\%$ | % |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Nyquist Criterion |
|---|---|
| **Digital Audio (CD, MP3)** | $W = 20$ kHz $\Rightarrow f_s = 44.1$ kHz |
| **Telephony (8 kHz PCM)** | $W = 3.4$ kHz $\Rightarrow f_s = 8$ kHz |
| **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT)** | Determines ADC sampling speed and k-space trajectory |
| **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)** | Sets the ADC sampling rate at the RF front-end |
| **5G NR Baseband** | Baseband bandwidth up to 400 MHz $\Rightarrow f_s \geq 800$ MHz |
| **Vibration / Seismic Analysis** | Earthquake sensors must sample faster than ground-motion frequencies |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spectral Form of the Sampled Signal

**Goal:** Derive $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}X(f - nf_s)$.

**Starting Expression — Sampled Signal as Modulated Impulse Train:**

$$x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot\delta_{T_s}(t)$$

**Express the Dirac Comb as an Equivalent Fourier Series:**

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Convert to its Complex Exponential Fourier Series:**
The Dirac comb is a periodic function with period $T_s$. Its complex Fourier series coefficients $C_k$ are all equal to $\dfrac{1}{T_s}$, because:

$$C_k = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{-T_s/2}^{+T_s/2}\delta_{T_s}(t)\,e^{-j2\pi k f_s t}\,dt = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{j2\pi k f_s t}$$

**Substitute Back into the Sampled Signal:**

$$x_s(t) = \frac{x(t)}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{j2\pi k f_s t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,e^{j2\pi k f_s t}$$

**Apply the Frequency-Shift Property of the Fourier Transform:**
Recall the standard identity: $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)e^{j2\pi f_0 t}\} = X(f - f_0)$.

Taking the Fourier Transform of both sides:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Conclusion:** The spectrum of the sampled signal is the original spectrum $X(f)$ replicated at every integer multiple of the sampling frequency $f_s$, with amplitude scaled by $1/T_s$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reconstruction Formula via Ideal Low-Pass Filtering

**Goal:** Derive $x(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}(2W(t - nT_s))$.

**Assume No Aliasing** ($f_s \geq 2W$).

**Step 1 — Pass the Sampled Spectrum Through an Ideal LPF.**
The ideal reconstruction filter has transfer function:

$$H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq W \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > W \end{cases}$$

The filter isolates only the $k = 0$ replica from $X_s(f)$:

$$Y(f) = X_s(f)\cdot H(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\cdot X(f)\cdot T_s = X(f)$$

This recovers the *exact original spectrum*, confirming reconstruction is theoretically perfect.

**Step 2 — Inverse Fourier Transform of $X(f)$.**

$$x(t) = \int_{-W}^{+W}X(f)\,e^{j2\pi f t}\,df$$

**Step 3 — Express $X(f)$ Using the Poisson Summation Formula.**
Substituting the sampled-signal spectrum back and isolating $k = 0$:

$$X(f) = T_s\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,e^{-j2\pi f n T_s} \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq W$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Into the Inverse Transform:**

$$x(t) = T_s\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\int_{-W}^{+W} e^{j2\pi f (t - nT_s)}\,df$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Inner Integral:**

$$\int_{-W}^{+W} e^{j2\pi f (t - nT_s)}\,df = \frac{e^{j2\pi W(t - nT_s)} - e^{-j2\pi W(t - nT_s)}}{j2\pi(t - nT_s)} = \frac{\sin(2\pi W(t - nT_s))}{\pi(t - nT_s)}$$

$$= 2W\cdot\mathrm{sinc}(2W(t - nT_s))$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $T_s$ and Simplify Using $T_s = 1/(2W)$:**

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\cdot \mathrm{sinc}\!\big(2W(t - nT_s)\big) \qquad \blacksquare$$

This identity expresses the continuous signal as an **infinite weighted sum of shifted sinc pulses**, each centered at a sampling instant $nT_s$ and weighted by the corresponding sample value.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aliasing Distortion When $f_s < 2W$

**Setup:** Consider a single-tone signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ sampled at $f_s$.

The sampled spectrum consists of impulses at $f = \pm f_m + kf_s$ for all integers $k$.

**Case 1: $f_m < f_s/2$ (No Aliasing).**
The lowest two impulses are at $\pm f_m$, which lie *inside* the principal Nyquist band $[-f_s/2, +f_s/2]$. The LPF isolates them cleanly, recovering $x(t)$ exactly.

**Case 2: $f_m > f_s/2$ (Aliasing).**
The impulse that *should* have been at $f_m$ is *folded back* into the principal band. The folded image appears at:

$$f_{\text{alias}} = f_s - f_m$$

If $f_s < 2f_m$, the tone is reconstructed at the *wrong* frequency $f_{\text{alias}}$ instead of $f_m$. This irreversible spectral contamination is **aliasing**.

**Example (Wheel-Wagon Effect):**
In old Western movies, stagecoach wheels appear to rotate *backwards* because the camera frame rate ($f_s$) is less than twice the rotation frequency ($2f_m$). The brain perceives the alias frequency $f_s - f_m$ as the true rotational speed.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sampling Theorem for Bandpass Signals

For a **bandpass signal** with spectrum confined to $[f_L, f_L + B]$ and $f_L \gg B$, the sampling rate need *not* be $2f_{\max}$. The generalized Nyquist Criterion for bandpass signals is:

$$f_s \geq 2B$$

**Strict Condition (to avoid guard-band complications):**

$$\frac{2 f_L + B}{n+1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2 f_L - B}{n} \quad \text{for some integer } n$$

where $n$ is chosen such that $1 \leq n \leq \lfloor f_L / B \rfloor$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Trick:** For a *narrowband* signal ($B \ll f_L$), the minimum sampling rate approaches $2B$, not $2f_L$. This is exploited heavily in **Software Defined Radio (SDR)** and **IF sampling** architectures.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Types of Sampling

| Sampling Type | Pulse Shape | Mathematical Form | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal (Impulse) Sampling** | Dirac delta | $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$ | Theoretical benchmark |
| **Natural Sampling (Chopper)** | Rectangular pulse of width $\tau$, top follows $x(t)$ | $x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot p(t)$ | First-stage samplers |
| **Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold)** | Rectangular pulse of constant amplitude $x(nT_s)$ | $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\,p(t - nT_s)$ | All real-world ADCs |
| **Bandpass Sampling** | Impulse train applied to bandpass signal | $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\sum X(f - nf_s)$ | SDR, IF sampling |

**Key Engineering Consequence of Flat-Top Sampling — Aperture Effect:**
The finite pulse width $\tau$ in a Sample-and-Hold introduces a multiplicative distortion $\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)$ on the spectrum. This is corrected by an **equalizing filter** with response $1/\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)$ in the reconstruction stage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Sampling and Reconstruction System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Time Signal x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing LPF]
    B --> C[Sampler Multiplier with Impulse Train]
    C --> D[Sampled Signal x sub s of t]
    D --> E[Transmission Channel]
    E --> F[Reconstruction LPF]
    F --> G[Reconstructed Signal y of t]
    style A fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **Anti-Aliasing LPF** (in front of the sampler) limits the message bandwidth to $W$ before sampling, ensuring the Nyquist Condition can be met by the chosen $f_s$. The **Reconstruction LPF** (after the channel) isolates the baseband replica from the periodic spectrum.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Spectral View of Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPECTRUM_INPUT["Original Spectrum X of f"]
        SP1[Triangular shape confined to minus W to plus W]
    end
    subgraph SPECTRUM_SAMPLED["Sampled Spectrum X sub s of f"]
        SP2[Periodic replicas at every n times f sub s with amplitude one over T sub s]
    end
    subgraph RECONSTRUCTION["After Ideal LPF with cutoff W and gain T sub s"]
        SP3[Recovered X of f exactly equals original]
    end
    SPECTRUM_INPUT -->|Sampling Theorem| SPECTRUM_SAMPLED
    SPECTRUM_SAMPLED -->|Ideal LPF isolates k equals 0 replica| RECONSTRUCTION
    style SP1 fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#333
    style SP2 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333
    style SP3 fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#333
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Aliasing Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UNDER_SAMPLED["When f sub s less than 2W"]
        A1[Spectrum X of f with bandwidth W]
        A2[Adjacent replica overlaps with original]
        A3[Sum of overlapping spectra corrupted]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4[Cannot recover x of t via LPF]
    style A1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#333
    style A2 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reconstruction via Sinc Interpolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sample at n T sub s equals zero] --> I1[Sinc centered at zero]
    S2[Sample at n T sub s equals T sub s] --> I2[Sinc centered at T sub s]
    S3[Sample at n T sub s equals 2 T sub s] --> I3[Sinc centered at 2 T sub s]
    S4[Sample at n T sub s equals 3 T sub s] --> I4[Sinc centered at 3 T sub s]
    I1 --> SUM[Weighted Sum]
    I2 --> SUM
    I3 --> SUM
    I4 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Continuous signal x of t]
    style SUM fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each $\mathrm{sinc}(2W(t - nT_s))$ function equals $1$ at its center sample instant $t = nT_s$ and crosses zero at every other sample instant. This orthogonality-like property ensures each sample "contributes only to itself" at the sample point — a key reason the Whittaker–Shannon formula achieves perfect reconstruction in theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1, 3 marks]**
> **Q.** Define **Nyquist Rate** and **Nyquist Interval**. A telephone channel has a message bandwidth of $3.4$ kHz. Compute the minimum sampling rate and the maximum sampling interval for distortion-free reconstruction.

**Model Answer:**

- **Nyquist Rate** is the minimum sampling frequency $f_N = 2W$ that permits perfect reconstruction of a bandlimited signal of bandwidth $W$ Hz.
- **Nyquist Interval** is the maximum allowable sampling period $T_N = 1/(2W)$ seconds.

**Computation:** Given $W = 3.4$ kHz $= 3400$ Hz:

$$f_N = 2W = 2 \times 3400 = 6800\ \text{Hz} = 6.8\ \text{kHz}$$

$$T_N = \frac{1}{2W} = \frac{1}{6800} \approx 147.06\ \mu\text{s}$$

Hence, the minimum sampling rate is **$6.8$ kHz** and the maximum sampling interval is **$\approx 147\ \mu$s**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2, 3 marks]**
> **Q.** What is **aliasing**? State two methods to prevent it in a practical digital communication system.

**Model Answer:**

**Aliasing** is the *irreversible spectral overlap* that occurs when a bandlimited signal is sampled at a rate $f_s$ below the Nyquist Rate $2W$. High-frequency components fold back into the baseband as *false low-frequency images*, distorting the reconstructed signal.

**Two Methods of Prevention:**

1. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** Insert an analog LPF with cutoff $W$ at the sampler input to *strictly bandlimit* the message before sampling. This guarantees $W_{\text{in}} \leq f_s/2$.

2. **Oversampling:** Operate the ADC at a sampling rate substantially higher than $2W$ (e.g., $44.1$ kHz for $20$ kHz audio) so that realizable filters with gentle roll-off can sufficiently attenuate alias components.

*(Other acceptable answers: increasing $f_s$ to satisfy Nyquist condition, using bandpass sampling for narrowband signals.)*

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Question Paper, 14 Marks]**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** **State and prove the Sampling Theorem for low-pass bandlimited signals. Clearly mention all assumptions and the condition under which the theorem holds.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:**
> *A real, finite-energy, baseband bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with no frequency components above $W$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its instantaneous samples taken uniformly at a rate $f_s \geq 2W$ samples per second.*

**Assumptions:**

1. $x(t)$ is **bandlimited**: $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > W$.
2. $x(t)$ is of **finite energy**: $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt < \infty$.
3. Sampling is **uniform** with period $T_s = 1/f_s$.
4. The reconstruction filter is **ideal** (brick-wall LPF of cutoff $W$).

**Proof (Summary of Detailed Derivation in Section 3):**

**Step 1:** Sample using impulse train:

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Fourier Transform and use the modulation property:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

**Step 3:** Since $f_s \geq 2W$, the spectral replicas do not overlap. The principal replica $X(f)$ can be isolated by an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $W$.

**Step 4:** Apply the inverse Fourier Transform to obtain the Whittaker–Shannon reconstruction:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\big(2W(t - nT_s)\big)$$

This proves the theorem. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** **An audio signal contains frequency components up to $15$ kHz. It is sampled at $25$ kHz. Determine whether aliasing will occur. If yes, calculate the alias frequency for a tone at $18$ kHz. What minimum sampling rate would you recommend?**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $W = 15$ kHz, $f_s = 25$ kHz, $f_m = 18$ kHz.

**Check Nyquist Condition:**
$$2W = 30\ \text{kHz} > f_s = 25\ \text{kHz}$$

Since $f_s < 2W$, **aliasing will occur**.

**Compute Alias Frequency:**
A tone at $f_m = 18$ kHz will fold into the principal band at:

$$f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_s - f_m \vert = \vert 25 - 18 \vert = 7\ \text{kHz}$$

**Recommended Minimum Sampling Rate:**

$$f_{s,\min} = 2W = 2 \times 15 = 30\ \text{kHz}$$

(With a *practical safety margin*, $f_s = 32$ kHz or $44.1$ kHz would be preferred.)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** **Explain the Sampling Theorem for bandpass signals. Derive the condition on the sampling frequency to avoid aliasing, mentioning the role of the spectral placement parameter $n$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (Bandpass Sampling Theorem):**
> *A bandpass signal $x(t)$ with spectrum confined to $[f_L, f_L + B]$, where $f_L \gg B$, can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ is chosen such that no spectral replicas overlap.*

**Derivation of the Sampling Condition:**

Each replica of $X(f)$ in $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\displaystyle\sum X(f - kf_s)$ has width $B$. For *no overlap*, we need:

$$f_s \geq 2B$$

**Strict Anti-Alias Condition:**
Let $n$ be the largest integer satisfying $n \leq \lfloor f_L/B \rfloor$. The permissible sampling frequencies form the *guard bands*:

$$\frac{2f_L + B}{n + 1} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2f_L - B}{n} \quad \text{for } n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

The integer $n$ denotes the *number of full spectrum copies* that fit between $0$ and $f_L$.

**Example:**
For $f_L = 100$ kHz, $B = 10$ kHz, $n_{\max} = 10$. For $n = 4$:

$$\frac{2(100) + 10}{5} \leq f_s \leq \frac{2(100) - 10}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 42 \leq f_s \leq 47.5\ \text{kHz}$$

Any $f_s$ in this range avoids aliasing — much lower than $f_s = 200$ kHz one might naively use.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** **With neat block diagrams, distinguish between Natural Sampling and Flat-Top Sampling. Mention the aperture effect and how it is corrected.**

**Model Solution:**

**Natural Sampling:**
The sampling switch closes for a brief duration $\tau$ during each interval, and the *top of the rectangular pulse follows the instantaneous value* of $x(t)$ within that window.

$$x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot p(t), \quad p(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & nT_s \leq t \leq nT_s + \tau \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Spectral Form:**

$$X_s(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n X(f - nf_s), \quad C_n = \frac{\tau}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}(nf_s\tau)$$

**Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold) Sampling:**
The sample value $x(nT_s)$ is held *constant* over the entire pulse duration $\tau$:

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,p(t - nT_s)$$

**Spectral Form:**

$$X_s(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)\,\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - nf_s)$$

**Aperture Effect:**
The factor $\dfrac{\tau}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)$ in the Flat-Top spectrum is a multiplicative distortion that attenuates higher frequencies within the message band — a high-frequency roll-off.

**Correction:**
An **equalizing filter** with response $H_{\text{eq}}(f) = \dfrac{T_s}{\tau}\,\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)}$ is inserted in the reconstruction chain to flatten the spectrum and undo the aperture distortion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Anti-Alias Filter:** Students often draw a sampler directly from the source signal. A *correct* KTU answer must include an **Anti-Aliasing LPF** before the sampler. *(Penalty: ~2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Sampling Frequency:** The Nyquist Rate is the *minimum*, not the *chosen* $f_s$. Always write "minimum sampling rate" or "$f_s \geq 2W$" in definitions.
>
> 3. **Missing the $1/T_s$ Scaling in the Spectral Formula:** $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\sum X(f - nf_s)$, **not** $\sum X(f - nf_s)$. *(Penalty: ~1 mark)*
>
> 4. **Using Non-Normalized Sinc:** KTU expects the normalized form $\mathrm{sinc}(u) = \sin(\pi u)/(\pi u)$. Using the unnormalized $\sin(x)/x$ without clarification is *acceptable but ambiguous*; prefer the normalized form.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Bandlimited Assumption:** Without this, the theorem has no validity. State it explicitly.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Practical Margins:** Examiners expect you to mention that *real* systems oversample (e.g., $f_s = 44.1$ kHz vs. theoretical $40$ kHz) to allow non-ideal filters.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Nyquist Criterion** states $f_s \geq 2W$ for unique reconstruction of a baseband bandlimited signal.
- [x] **Nyquist Rate** $f_N = 2W$; **Nyquist Interval** $T_N = 1/(2W)$.
- [x] Sampled spectrum is a **periodic replica** of $X(f)$ scaled by $1/T_s$ at every multiple of $f_s$.
- [x] **Reconstruction** uses ideal LPF of cutoff $W$ and gain $T_s$.
- [x] **Whittaker–Shannon Formula:** $x(t) = \sum x(nT_s)\mathrm{sinc}(2W(t - nT_s))$.
- [x] **Aliasing** occurs when $f_s < 2W$; high-frequency content folds back as false low-frequency images.
- [x] **Anti-Aliasing LPF** before sampler is *mandatory* in real systems.
- [x] **Practical safeguard:** Oversample by $10\%-20\%$ above $2W$.
- [x] **Bandpass Sampling:** $f_s \geq 2B$ (when $B \ll f_L$), with guard-band formula $\dfrac{2f_L + B}{n + 1} \leq f_s \leq \dfrac{2f_L - B}{n}$.
- [x] **Natural Sampling** — pulse top follows signal; **Flat-Top Sampling** — sample held constant.
- [x] **Aperture Effect** — flat-top introduces $\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)$ distortion; corrected by an equalizer $H_{\text{eq}}(f) = 1/\mathrm{sinc}(f\tau)$.
- [x] **Real-world anchors:** CD audio $44.1$ kHz, telephony $8$ kHz, 5G NR up to $400$ MHz baseband.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quantization** is the process of mapping a continuous range of analog signal amplitudes (an infinite set of values) into a finite set of discrete amplitude levels. In the context of **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)**, quantization is the second fundamental operation (preceded by sampling and followed by encoding) that converts a continuous-amplitude **sampled signal** into a **discrete-amplitude**, **discrete-time** digital signal suitable for binary encoding.

Mathematically, a quantizer `Q(x)` is a many-to-one mapping function defined as:

$$Q: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_L\}$$

where `x` is the input continuous-amplitude sample, and `{y_1, y_2, …, y_L}` are the `L = 2^n` discrete reconstruction levels corresponding to an `n`-bit encoder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** Quantization is an **irreversible (lossy)** and **non-linear** operation. The original analog amplitude **cannot be recovered exactly** because infinite input values are folded into a single output level. This inherent irreversibility introduces **Quantization Error (Quantization Noise)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Staircase Approximation

Imagine you are standing on a **smooth ramp** (your continuous analog signal) and you must descend using only **discrete stairs of fixed height** (your quantization levels). You cannot step continuously; you must land on the nearest stair tread.

- The **ramp's smooth slope** represents the original continuous signal `x(t)`.
- Each **stair tread's height** represents a quantization level `y_i`.
- The **vertical gap** between where you actually wanted to step (true ramp position) and the stair tread you actually stepped on is the **quantization error** `e(t)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Sampling operates in the **time domain** (continuous-time → discrete-time). Quantization operates in the **amplitude domain** (continuous-amplitude → discrete-amplitude). Together, they convert an **Analog Signal** into a **Digital Signal**.

## 1.3 Quantization Step Size and Resolution

The **step size** (or **resolution**) `Δ` of a uniform quantizer spanning the dynamic range `[−V_max, +V_max]` is given by:

$$\Delta = \frac{2 V_{max}}{L} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$$

where:
- `L = 2^n` = total number of quantization levels
- `n` = number of bits per sample
- `V_pp` = peak-to-peak voltage range
- `V_max` = peak amplitude of the input signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For an **8-bit PCM system**, the quantizer produces `L = 2^8 = 256` discrete levels. For a telephone-grade signal with `V_max = 4.096 V`, the step size evaluates to `Δ = 8.192/256 = 32 mV`.

## 1.4 Types of Quantizers (Classification Overview)

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Uniform (Linear) Quantizer** | Equal step size `Δ` across entire range | High-fidelity audio, instrumentation |
| **Non-Uniform Quantizer** | Variable step size (finer for small signals, coarser for large signals) | Speech telephony (PCM) |
| **Mid-Rise Quantizer** | Origin lies at the **midpoint of a step** (no level at zero) | Symmetric bipolar signals |
| **Mid-Tread Quantizer** | Origin lies at the **center of a tread** (one level at zero) | General-purpose ADC systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Quantization Process

A quantizer operates through the following structured logic:

1. **Range Partitioning:** The total amplitude range `[−V_max, +V_max]` is divided into `L = 2^n` equal sub-intervals (for uniform quantization), each of width `Δ`.
2. **Decision Thresholds:** Boundary points `x_k` are defined such that the `k`-th interval is `[x_k, x_{k+1}]`.
3. **Mapping Rule:** Any input sample `x` falling within the `k`-th interval is mapped (rounded) to a single representative output level `y_k` (the reconstruction value).
4. **Encoding:** The index `k` is then binary-encoded into an `n`-bit codeword.

> [!NOTE]
> **Round-off vs. Truncation:** Most practical quantizers use **mid-tread rounding** (round to nearest level). The standard convention assigns the reconstruction level at the **midpoint of each interval**, which minimizes the maximum quantization error to `±Δ/2`.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Mid-Tread Placement

If the reconstruction level `y_k` is placed at the **center** of the `k`-th quantization interval, then any input `x` in that interval produces a maximum error magnitude:

$$\vert e \vert_{max} = \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Placing `y_k` at either edge would allow errors up to `Δ`, doubling the worst-case error and the resulting noise power. The mid-tread (or mid-rise) placement is therefore the **optimal uniform quantizer design** that minimizes mean-squared error under the assumption of uniform input distribution.

## 2.3 Quantization Error (Quantization Noise)

The quantization error `e(t)` is defined as the difference between the quantizer output and the input:

$$e(t) = x_q(t) - x(t)$$

For a uniform mid-tread quantizer with input range spanning a single step, the error lies in the interval:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

### 2.3.1 Assumptions for Statistical Analysis (KTU Standard)

For analytical tractability, the following standard assumptions are adopted:

1. The quantization error `e` is **uniformly distributed** over `[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]`.
2. The error `e` is **statistically independent** of the input signal `x(t)`.
3. The error sequence is **uncorrelated** (white noise-like spectrum).
4. The signal is **fine-quantized** (small `Δ`, large `n`) so that the uniform distribution assumption holds.

### 2.3.2 Quantization Noise Power (Mean-Square Error)

For an error uniformly distributed in `[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]`, the mean is zero (no DC bias) and the variance (noise power) is:

$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot (\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this result:** The **quantization noise power** of a uniform quantizer is `σ_e² = Δ² / 12`, and it is **independent of the input signal's statistical properties** (under the uniform-distribution assumption). This is one of the most heavily tested derivations in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)

The **SQNR** is the figure of merit that quantifies quantizer performance. It is the ratio of input signal power to quantization noise power.

### 2.4.1 General Form

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{12 \, \sigma_x^2}{\Delta^2}$$

### 2.4.2 In Decibels

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{12 \, \sigma_x^2}{\Delta^2} \right) \text{ dB}$$

### 2.4.3 Full-Scale Sinusoidal Input (Most Tested Case)

For a **full-amplitude sinusoid** of peak `V_max`, the input power is `σ_x² = V_max² / 2`. Substituting `Δ = 2V_max / 2^n`:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{12 \cdot (V_{max}^2 / 2)}{(2V_{max}/2^n)^2} = \frac{6 \cdot V_{max}^2 \cdot 2^{2n}}{4 V_{max}^2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}$$

Converting to decibels:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \, n \text{ dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Famous 6 dB-per-bit Rule:** Each additional bit in the quantizer improves the SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB** (exactly 4.77 dB per additional bit for the noise power ratio, or 6.02 dB per bit for the amplitude SNR). This rule is the cornerstone of all KTU quantization problems.

### 2.4.4 For a Uniformly Distributed Input (Speech-Like)

For an input uniformly distributed in `[−V_max, +V_max]`, the signal power is `σ_x² = V_max² / 3`:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{12 \cdot (V_{max}^2/3)}{\Delta^2} = \frac{4 \cdot V_{max}^2 \cdot 2^{2n}}{4 V_{max}^2} = 2^{2n}$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, n \text{ dB}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Number of levels | `L = 2^n` | dimensionless |
| 2 | Step size (uniform) | `Δ = 2V_max / L = V_pp / 2^n` | Volts |
| 3 | Maximum error | `e_max = ±Δ/2` | Volts |
| 4 | Quantization noise power | `σ_e² = Δ² / 12` | Watts |
| 5 | SQNR (general) | `SQNR = 12 σ_x² / Δ²` | ratio |
| 6 | SQNR (full-scale sinusoid) | `SQNR_dB = 1.76 + 6.02n` | dB |
| 7 | SQNR (uniform distribution) | `SQNR_dB = 6.02n` | dB |
| 8 | Per-bit SQNR improvement | `ΔSQNR ≈ 6.02 dB/bit` | dB |
| 9 | Output bit rate | `R_b = n \cdot f_s` | bits/sec |
| 10 | Channel bandwidth (Nyquist) | `BW = R_b / 2` | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Inside all tables, absolute values or magnitude bars **MUST** be written as `\vert x \vert` — never as `|x|` — to prevent breaking the Markdown table parser.

## 2.6 Why Non-Uniform Quantization? The Companding Solution

For **speech signals**, the probability density is **Laplacian** (peaked near zero, exponential tails). A uniform quantizer wastes resolution on rarely-visited large amplitudes. **Non-uniform quantization** uses **coarser steps for large signals** and **finer steps for small signals**, providing near-constant SQNR across a wide dynamic range.

**Companding** (COMPressing + exPANDING) achieves this non-uniform behavior by:
1. Applying a **compressor** characteristic (e.g., μ-law or A-law) at the **transmitter** to logarithmically squash large amplitudes.
2. Quantizing uniformly on the compressed signal.
3. Applying an **expander** (inverse of compressor) at the **receiver** to restore linearity.

### 2.6.1 μ-Law Companding (North American / Japanese Standard)

$$F(x) = V \cdot \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln\left(1 + \mu \frac{\vert x \vert}{V}\right)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$$

where `V` = peak amplitude, `\text{sgn}(x)` = sign function, and `\mu` = compression parameter (typically **μ = 255** in North America).

### 2.6.2 A-Law Companding (European / ITU-T Standard)

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \cdot \vert x \vert / V}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x) & \text{if } 0 \leq \vert x \vert / V \leq \dfrac{1}{A} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1 + \ln\left(A \cdot \vert x \vert / V\right)}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x) & \text{if } \dfrac{1}{A} < \vert x \vert / V \leq 1 \end{cases}$$

with typical `A = 87.6`.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Telephony (G.711 Standard):** Uses **8-bit PCM with μ-law (μ=255) or A-law (A=87.6)** companding at a sampling rate of **8 kSamples/sec**, producing a **64 kbps** bitstream — the fundamental building block of the PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network).
- **CD Audio (Red Book Standard):** Uses **16-bit linear uniform quantization** at 44.1 kSamples/sec, achieving a theoretical SQNR of approximately **98 dB**.
- **VoIP & Modern Codecs:** Builds on PCM fundamentals with adaptive quantization (e.g., ADPCM in G.726) for bandwidth compression.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantization Noise Power `σ_e² = Δ²/12`

**Given:** A uniform mid-tread quantizer with step size `Δ`. The quantization error `e` is uniformly distributed over `[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]`.

**To Prove:** The mean-square quantization error is `σ_e² = Δ²/12`.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Define the Probability Density Function (PDF):**

Since `e` is uniformly distributed over an interval of total width `Δ`, the PDF `p(e)` is constant:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for } -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Mean (DC Component):**

The mean of the error is:

$$\mu_e = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e \cdot p(e) \, de = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de$$

$$\mu_e = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e^2}{2} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta^2}{8} - \frac{\Delta^2}{8} \right) = 0$$

**Interpretation:** The quantization error has **zero mean**, confirming that the quantizer introduces no DC bias — a desirable symmetry property of the mid-tread design.

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean-Square Value (Noise Power):**

$$\sigma_e^2 = E[e^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:**

$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{2 (\Delta/2)^3}{3}$$

$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{2 \Delta^3}{24} = \frac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Final Substitution:**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoidal Input

**Given:** An `n`-bit uniform quantizer with input range `[−V_max, +V_max]`. The input is a full-amplitude sinusoid `x(t) = V_max \cos(2\pi f t)`.

**To Derive:** `SQNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Step Size:**

For `L = 2^n` levels spanning `2V_max`:

$$\Delta = \frac{2V_{max}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Quantization Noise Power:**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(2V_{max}/2^n)^2}{12} = \frac{4 V_{max}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2n}} = \frac{V_{max}^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Input Signal Power:**

For a sinusoid `x(t) = V_max \cos(\omega t)`, the average power is:

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{V_{max}^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SQNR Ratio:**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{V_{max}^2 / 2}{V_{max}^2 / (3 \cdot 2^{2n})} = \frac{V_{max}^2}{2} \cdot \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{V_{max}^2}$$

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Decibels:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2n})$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2n})$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \cdot (0.1761) + 2n \cdot 10 \log_{10}(2)$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.761 + 2n \cdot (3.0103)$$

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \, n \text{ dB} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 1: KTU Standard Pattern

> **Problem:** A PCM system uses a uniform quantizer with `n = 8` bits and a full-scale range of `±5 V`. The input is a full-amplitude sinusoid. Calculate:
> (a) The step size `Δ`
> (b) The quantization noise power `σ_e²`
> (c) The SQNR in dB
> (d) The output bit rate if the sampling rate is `f_s = 8 kHz`

### Solution

**Part (a) — Step Size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{2 V_{max}}{2^n} = \frac{2 \times 5}{2^8} = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta \approx 39.06 \text{ mV}$$

**Part (b) — Quantization Noise Power:**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(0.0390625)^2}{12} = \frac{1.5259 \times 10^{-3}}{12}$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = 1.2715 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}^2 \approx 127.15 \text{ }\mu\text{V}^2$$

**Part (c) — SQNR in dB:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times n = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Part (d) — Output Bit Rate:**

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

This is precisely the **G.711 PCM standard** used in classical digital telephony!

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2: Effect of Increasing Bits

> **Problem:** A system currently uses `n = 6` bits and achieves an SQNR of `37.88 dB`. How many additional bits are required to achieve an SQNR of at least `50 dB`?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Current SQNR Verification:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 6 = 1.76 + 36.12 = 37.88 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Set Up the Target Equation:**

Let `n_new` be the new bit count:

$$1.76 + 6.02 \cdot n_{new} \geq 50$$

**Step 3 — Solve for `n_new`:**

$$6.02 \cdot n_{new} \geq 48.24$$

$$n_{new} \geq \frac{48.24}{6.02} = 8.013$$

**Step 4 — Round Up to Integer:**

$$n_{new} = 9 \text{ bits (minimum integer value)}$$

**Step 5 — Additional Bits Required:**

$$\Delta n = 9 - 6 = 3 \text{ additional bits}$$

**Verification:** `1.76 + 6.02(9) = 1.76 + 54.18 = 55.94 dB ≥ 50 dB` ✓

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 3: Companded PCM Comparison

> **Problem:** An 8-bit PCM system is used for a speech signal with `σ_x² = 0.1 V²` and `V_max = 4 V`. Compare the SQNR of (i) uniform quantization and (ii) μ-law companding with `μ = 255`.

### Solution

**Part (i) — Uniform Quantization:**

Step size:
$$\Delta = \frac{2 \times 4}{2^8} = \frac{8}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V}$$

Noise power:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(0.03125)^2}{12} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 8.138 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}^2$$

SQNR:
$$\text{SQNR}_{uniform} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{0.1}{8.138 \times 10^{-5}} = 1228.8$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{uniform,\,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1228.8) = 30.89 \text{ dB}$$

**Part (ii) — μ-Law Companded:**

For μ-law, the effective SQNR for a sinusoidal input is approximated by the empirical formula:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02n + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}(\ln(1+\mu))$$

Substituting `μ = 255`:

$$\ln(1 + 255) = \ln(256) = 5.545$$

$$20 \log_{10}(5.545) = 20 \times 0.7440 = 14.88 \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{\mu-law,\,dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02(8) + 4.77 - 14.88$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{\mu-law,\,dB} \approx 1.76 + 48.16 + 4.77 - 14.88 = 39.81 \text{ dB}$$

**Comparison:** Companding yields an **~8.92 dB improvement** for small-signal speech — a dramatic perceptual quality gain in telephony.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Quantization Scheme

| Signal Characteristic | Recommended Quantizer | Justification |
|------------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Uniform PDF (e.g., instrumentation sensors) | **Uniform / Linear** | Matches signal distribution; minimum total MSE |
| Laplacian PDF (e.g., speech) | **Non-Uniform / Companded (μ-law or A-law)** | Concentrates levels where signal dwells |
| High dynamic range (e.g., audio) | **Non-uniform or floating-point** | Preserves low-amplitude details |
| High fidelity (e.g., CD audio) | **Uniform, high `n` (16-bit)** | Avoids companding distortion; high SQNR |
| Power-constrained, voice-grade (e.g., VoIP) | **A-law, 8-bit, 8 kHz** | G.711 standard; bandwidth-efficient |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PCM Transmitter with Quantization Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigA["Analog Signal x(t)"] --> lpfA["Anti-Aliasing LPF"]
    lpfA --> samp["Sampler f_s >= 2 f_max"]
    samp --> quant["Uniform Quantizer Q(x)"]
    quant --> enc["Binary Encoder n bits"]
    enc --> chan["Channel Coder / Line Coder"]
    chan --> txLine["Transmission Channel"]

    quant -.->.-> errA["Quantization Error e(t) = x_q - x"]
    samp -.->.-> errA
```

**Narrative Description of the Block Diagram:**

1. **Analog Signal `x(t)`** — the original continuous-time, continuous-amplitude source.
2. **Anti-Aliasing LPF** — bandlimits the signal to `f_max` before sampling (Nyquist criterion).
3. **Sampler** — converts continuous-time to discrete-time, producing samples `x(nT_s)`.
4. **Uniform Quantizer `Q(x)`** — the focus topic; maps each sample to the nearest of `L = 2^n` discrete levels.
5. **Binary Encoder** — converts the quantizer index into an `n`-bit binary codeword.
6. **Channel Coder** — applies line coding (e.g., NRZ, Manchester) for spectral shaping and clock recovery.
7. **Transmission Channel** — physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless).

The dashed arrow indicates the **irreversible loss** introduced by the quantizer — the difference between the sampled (but still continuous-amplitude) signal and the quantized (discrete-amplitude) signal.

## 4.2 Mid-Rise vs Mid-Tread Quantizer Transfer Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MR["Mid-Rise Quantizer (No Level at Zero)"]
        direction LR
        mrIn["Input x"] --> mrBlock["Step Function<br/>Origin at step boundary"]
        mrBlock --> mrOut["Output y_k<br/>y = +-Delta/2, +-3Delta/2, ..."]
    end

    subgraph MT["Mid-Tread Quantizer (One Level at Zero)"]
        direction LR
        mtIn["Input x"] --> mtBlock["Step Function<br/>Origin at tread center"]
        mtBlock --> mtOut["Output y_k<br/>y = 0, +-Delta, +-2Delta, ..."]
    end

    mrOut -.-> note1["Odd number of levels L is typical"]
    mtOut -.-> note2["Even number of levels L is typical"]
```

**Key Distinctions:**

- **Mid-Rise:** Has `L` levels if `L` is odd; reconstruction levels are `..., −3Δ/2, −Δ/2, +Δ/2, +3Δ/2, ...` (no level at zero). The transfer characteristic crosses the vertical axis at a step boundary.
- **Mid-Tread:** Has `L` levels if `L` is even; reconstruction levels are `..., −2Δ, −Δ, 0, +Δ, +2Δ, ...`. The transfer characteristic has a flat tread centered at zero — preferred when the signal frequently passes through zero.

## 4.3 Quantization Error Waveform Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Input Signal x(t)"]
        aNode["Smooth continuous waveform"]
    end

    subgraph B["Quantized Output x_q(t)"]
        bNode["Staircase waveform"]
    end

    subgraph C["Quantization Error e(t) = x_q - x"]
        cNode["Sawtooth-like residual<br/>Bounded by +-Delta/2"]
    end

    A -->|"Rounded to nearest level"| B
    B -->|"Subtract x from x_q"| C

    C --> p1["Mean = 0"]
    C --> p2["Uniform PDF in [-Delta/2, +Delta/2]"]
    C --> p3["Variance = Delta^2 / 12"]
```

## 4.4 Companding System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX["Transmitter"]
        xIn["Analog x(t)"] --> compressor["Compressor<br/>mu-law or A-law"]
        compressor --> lpcm["Linear PCM<br/>(uniform quantization)"]
    end

    TX --> channel["Channel<br/>64 kbps typical"]

    subgraph RX["Receiver"]
        channel --> lpcmRx["Linear PCM samples"]
        lpcmRx --> expander["Expander<br/>Inverse of compressor"]
        expander --> xOut["Reconstructed x_hat(t)"]
    end

    compressor -.->.-> noteGain["Large amplitudes compressed<br/>Small amplitudes expanded"]
    expander -.->.-> noteGain
```

**Why Companding Works:** By compressing the dynamic range at the transmitter, the signal is forced to spend more time in regions where the quantizer has finer resolution. The receiver's expander restores the original dynamics. The net effect is a near-constant SQNR over a wide input amplitude range.

## 4.5 Quantization Error vs. Input Amplitude (Granular vs. Overload)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Amplitude"]
        inSmall["Small x: e < Delta/2"] --> granular["Granular Region<br/>e uniformly distributed"]
        inLarge["Large x > V_max"] --> overload["Overload Region<br/>e grows linearly, severe distortion"]
    end

    granular --> granNote["S_QNR is well-behaved"]
    overload --> ovNote["Saturates quantizer; clipping distortion"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Granular Noise vs. Overload Noise:** For inputs within `[−V_max, +V_max]`, the error is bounded (`±Δ/2`) and is called **granular noise**. For inputs exceeding `V_max`, the quantizer **saturates**, and the error grows without bound — this is **overload noise** (or clipping distortion) and is far more objectionable perceptually.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define quantization. Why is it considered an irreversible operation in PCM?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Quantization is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude (analog) signal into a finite set of discrete amplitude levels. In a PCM system, it follows sampling and precedes binary encoding.

**Irreversibility (2 marks):** Quantization is irreversible because the mapping is **many-to-one** — infinitely many distinct input values that fall within a single quantization interval are collapsed into a single output level. Since the original distinction between these inputs is permanently lost, the original analog amplitude **cannot be uniquely recovered** at the receiver. The maximum recoverable information is limited to the index of the quantization level (`n` bits), introducing a permanent loss quantified as **quantization error** with power `σ_e² = Δ²/12`.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a uniform quantizer with a full-scale sinusoidal input. State the 6 dB-per-bit rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** SQNR is the ratio of the input signal power to the quantization noise power. For a full-scale sinusoid of peak amplitude `V_max` and an `n`-bit uniform quantizer, the input power is `V_max²/2` and the quantization noise power is `Δ²/12 = V_max²/(3·2^{2n})`. The ratio is `SQNR = 1.5·2^{2n}`, which in decibels gives:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n \text{ dB}$$

**6 dB-per-bit rule (1.5 marks):** This rule states that **each additional bit** used in the quantizer increases the SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**. Equivalently, it increases the signal-to-noise amplitude ratio by a factor of 2 per added bit. This is the fundamental driver for choosing higher bit depths in high-fidelity digital audio (e.g., 16-bit CD audio vs. 8-bit telephony).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. You are required to answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a uniform mid-tread quantizer. Derive the expression for quantization noise power assuming uniform distribution of error. (7 marks)**

**(b) A PCM system uses 10 bits per sample with a full-scale range of ±6 V. The input is a full-amplitude sinusoid. Calculate: (i) step size, (ii) quantization noise power, (iii) SQNR in dB, and (iv) the output bit rate if `f_s = 16 kHz`. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
[A mid-tread quantizer has `L = 2^n` evenly spaced reconstruction levels with one level centered at zero. Each input `x` in the interval `[x_k − Δ/2, x_k + Δ/2]` is mapped to the output `y_k`.]

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- The full-scale range `[−V_max, +V_max]` is divided into `L = 2^n` equal sub-intervals of width `Δ = 2V_max/L`.
- The decision thresholds are at `..., −3Δ/2, −Δ/2, +Δ/2, +3Δ/2, ...` (no thresholds at zero for mid-tread).
- Reconstruction levels are at `..., −Δ, 0, +Δ, +2Δ, ...` (one level at zero).
- The error `e = y_k − x` is bounded by `±Δ/2`.

**Derivation of Noise Power (3 marks):**
Assuming `e` is uniformly distributed in `[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]` with PDF `p(e) = 1/Δ`:

$$\sigma_e^2 = E[e^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** `n = 10`, `V_max = 6 V`, full-scale sinusoid, `f_s = 16 kHz`.

**(i) Step Size (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta = \frac{2 V_{max}}{2^n} = \frac{2 \times 6}{2^{10}} = \frac{12}{1024} = 0.011719 \text{ V} = 11.72 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Quantization Noise Power (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(0.011719)^2}{12} = \frac{1.373 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 1.144 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}^2$$

**(iii) SQNR in dB (2 marks):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 10 = 1.76 + 60.20 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Output Bit Rate (2 marks):**

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 10 \times 16{,}000 = 160{,}000 \text{ bps} = 160 \text{ kbps}$$

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the concept of companding in PCM. Derive the expression for SQNR improvement of μ-law companding over linear PCM for a sinusoidal input. (7 marks)**

**(b) An 8-bit PCM system is used for a speech signal with full-scale range ±3 V. Compare the SQNR (in dB) of uniform quantization and μ-law companding with μ = 255. Assume full-scale sinusoidal input. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Concept of Companding (2.5 marks):**
- Companding = **COM**pression + ex**PAND**ing.
- At the **transmitter**, a non-linear compressor (μ-law or A-law) squashes large amplitudes and expands small amplitudes before uniform quantization.
- At the **receiver**, the inverse expander restores the original dynamic range.
- This effectively implements a **non-uniform quantizer** with finer resolution for small signals and coarser resolution for large signals.

**Why Companding is Used (1.5 marks):**
Speech signals have a **Laplacian PDF** — most samples have small amplitudes, but a few are large. Uniform quantization is inefficient. Companding matches the quantizer step size to the signal statistics, improving the **perceptual SQNR** by 6–8 dB.

**SQNR Improvement (3 marks):**
For a full-scale sinusoid with μ-law companding, the empirical SQNR formula is:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\mu,\,dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}[\ln(1 + \mu)]$$

The improvement over uniform PCM is:

$$\Delta \text{SQNR} = 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}[\ln(1 + \mu)]$$

For `μ = 255`: `ln(256) = 5.545`, so `20 log₁₀(5.545) = 14.88 dB`. Improvement `= 4.77 − 14.88 = −10.11 dB` for full-scale sinusoid, but for small-signal speech (e.g., −20 dB below full scale), the improvement becomes a net gain of ~6–8 dB.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** `n = 8`, `V_max = 3 V`, sinusoidal input, μ-law with `μ = 255`.

**Uniform PCM:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{uniform,\,dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**μ-Law PCM (full-scale sinusoid):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\mu,\,dB} = 1.76 + 6.02(8) + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}[\ln(256)]$$

$$= 1.76 + 48.16 + 4.77 - 14.88 = 39.81 \text{ dB}$$

**Comparison (1.5 marks):**

For a **full-scale** sinusoid, uniform PCM is **better by ~10 dB** because the signal is already at peak amplitude where companding is least beneficial.

For a **small-amplitude** (e.g., −20 dBFS) signal, the relationship reverses: companding gives a substantial SQNR advantage (often 20+ dB), making it the preferred choice for **speech telephony** where the signal rarely reaches full scale.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `1.76` constant** — Students often write only `6.02n` and lose 1 mark. Always include the `1.76` for sinusoidal inputs.
> 2. **Confusing `V_max` with `V_pp`** — In step-size formulas, the range is `2V_max` (peak-to-peak), not `V_max`. Substituting `V_max` instead of `2V_max` produces a 6 dB error.
> 3. **Mixing companding formulas** — The 4.77 dB term and the `−20 log₁₀[ln(1+μ)]` correction are for **sinusoidal** inputs with μ-law. For uniform-distribution inputs, the constant differs.
> 4. **Not stating uniform-distribution assumption** — KTU examiners deduct marks if the noise power derivation doesn't explicitly state the assumption of uniform PDF for the error.
> 5. **Wrong dB factor** — `20 log₁₀` for amplitude ratios and `10 log₁₀` for power ratios. SQNR is a **power ratio**, so always use `10 log₁₀`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quantization (Module 2)**

### Conceptual Mastery
- ☐ Quantization = continuous-amplitude → discrete-amplitude mapping (irreversible, lossy).
- ☐ Sampling is in **time**; quantization is in **amplitude**.
- ☐ `L = 2^n` levels, `Δ = 2V_max / 2^n` step size.
- ☐ Mid-tread = one level at zero (even `L`); mid-rise = no level at zero (odd `L`).
- ☐ Max quantization error = `±Δ/2` for optimal mid-tread placement.

### Formula Arsenal
- ☐ Quantization noise power: `σ_e² = Δ²/12` (memorize derivation).
- ☐ SQNR (sinusoid): `1.76 + 6.02n` dB.
- ☐ SQNR (uniform PDF): `6.02n` dB.
- ☐ 6 dB-per-bit rule is the single most-tested relationship.
- ☐ Output bit rate: `R_b = n·f_s`.

### Companding Essentials
- ☐ Companding = compression + expansion; implements non-uniform quantization.
- ☐ μ-law: `μ = 255` (North America/Japan); A-law: `A = 87.6` (Europe/ITU-T).
- ☐ G.711 PCM: 8-bit, 8 kHz sampling, 64 kbps — foundation of digital telephony.
- ☐ Companding improves SQNR for **small-amplitude** signals (speech), not for full-scale sinusoids.

### Examiner Watch-List
- ☐ Always state the **uniform error distribution assumption** in derivations.
- ☐ Distinguish **granular noise** (`±Δ/2` bounded) from **overload/clipping noise** (unbounded).
- ☐ Use `10 log₁₀` for power ratios (SQNR), `20 log₁₀` for amplitude ratios.
- ☐ For `V_max` vs `V_pp`: remember step size uses `2V_max` (full peak-to-peak range).
- ☐ In KTU answer sheets, **draw the staircase transfer characteristic** of the quantizer — it earns 1–2 easy marks.

### Real-World Anchor
- ☐ CD audio: 16-bit uniform, 44.1 kHz → ~98 dB SQNR.
- ☐ Telephony: 8-bit μ-law/A-law, 8 kHz → 64 kbps, ~38–40 dB SQNR at full scale.
- ☐ VoIP/ADPCM: builds on PCM foundations, reduces bit rate using adaptive quantization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoding Techniques - Unipolar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Encoding Techniques - Unipolar ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Unipolar Encoding** is a **line coding** (also called **baseband signaling**) scheme in which only **one polarity of voltage** (either entirely positive or entirely negative, with respect to a common ground reference) is used to represent the binary data stream. By the KTU 2024 Scheme definition: - A binary **'1'** is represented by a **non-zero DC pulse** of amplitude $+A$ volts (positive direction). - A binary **'0'** is represented by **zero voltage** ($0\text{ V}$), i.e., the absence of a pulse. The signal voltage therefore swings between **two levels: $0\text{ V}$ and $+A\text{ V}$** (or equivalently $0\text{ V}$ and $-A\text{ V}$ depending on polarity convention). Because only one polarity is used, it is termed *uni-polar* (single pole). It is the simplest member of the **NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero)** family of baseband transmission codes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** *Unipolar signaling is a binary transmission technique in which one logic state is represented by a non-zero voltage level and the other logic state is represented by zero voltage, thereby utilizing only a single polarity of the signal.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a **light switch in a room**: - When the switch is **ON**, the bulb glows at full brightness (+A volts, binary '1'). - When the switch is **OFF**, the bulb is dark (0 volts, binary '0'). Now imagine blinking the switch following a secret Morse-code pattern. The bulb is either **lit or dark** — there is no concept of "dim" or "negative brightness." This *single-polarity, on-off nature* is exactly what defines **Unipolar encoding**. The bulb glows in only one direction (positive), never in the opposite direction. A second analogy: think of a **digital clock that flashes once for 'Yes' and stays silent for 'No'**. The flash always has the same brightness and the same color (single polarity). You never see a "negative flash." > [!NOTE] > **One-line takeaway for KTU viva:** *"Unipolar means only one pole — only one non-zero voltage level exists; the other binary state is plain zero."* ### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics | Metric | Value / Property | |---|---| | Signal levels | **2** (binary: '0' and '1') | | Voltage levels used | **2** ($0\text{ V}$ and $+A\text{ V}$) | | Polarities | **1** (only positive OR only negative) | | DC component | **Present (non-zero average value)** | | Synchronization | **Poor** for long runs of identical bits | | Implementation complexity | **Very Low** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theory 1. **Input:** A binary data sequence $\lbrace b_k \rbrace$ where each $b_k \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$. 2. **Mapping rule:** - If $b_k = 1$, transmit a rectangular pulse of amplitude $+A$ volts for the entire bit duration $T_b$. - If $b_k = 0$, transmit $0$ volts for the entire bit duration $T_b$. 3. **Mathematical representation** of the unipolar signal $s(t)$: $$ s(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b) $$ where $a_k = A$ when $b_k = 1$ and $a_k = 0$ when $b_k = 0$, and $p(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of unit amplitude and width $T_b$. 4. **"Non-Return-to-Zero" property:** Once the signal level is set at the start of a bit interval, it **does NOT return to zero** within that interval — it stays constant throughout the full bit period $T_b$. 5. **Two major sub-variants:** - **Unipolar NRZ** — signal stays at $+A$ for the full $T_b$ when bit = 1. - **Unipolar RZ** — signal returns to zero halfway through $T_b$ (i.e., pulse width $T_b/2$). 6. **DC Component Generation:** Because the long-term average of a random binary stream (with equal probability of 0s and 1s) equals $A/2 \neq 0$, the signal carries a **non-zero DC component**. This DC component cannot traverse AC-coupled channels (transformers, capacitive coupling), making unipolar signaling unsuitable for many real-world channels. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Intuition Behind the Logic | Question | Answer | |---|---| | Why does the signal stay flat during the bit? | Because NRZ = "Non-Return-to-Zero" — the amplitude holds for the full $T_b$. | | Why does a DC component appear? | Because the signal is always ≥ 0; its average is $+A/2$ for equiprobable bits, never zero. | | Why is synchronization poor? | A long run of 0s gives a flat zero line; the receiver loses bit-timing edges. | | Why is it simple? | Only one voltage source (e.g., +5V) and one switching transistor are needed. | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Binary '1' amplitude | $+A$ | Volts (V) | | 2 | Binary '0' amplitude | $0$ | Volts (V) | | 3 | Mean (DC) value (equiprobable) | $V_{DC} = A/2$ | V | | 4 | RMS value (equiprobable) | $V_{rms} = A/\sqrt{2}$ | V | | 5 | Bit duration | $T_b = 1/R_b$ | seconds (s) | | 6 | Bit rate | $R_b$ | bits/second (bps) | | 7 | Minimum Nyquist bandwidth (NRZ) | $B_{min} = R_b/2$ | Hz | | 8 | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | $S(f) = \dfrac{A^{2}T_b}{4}\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b) + \dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)$ | W/Hz | | 9 | Synchronization | $\text{Poor}$ (no transitions for long same-bit strings) | qualitative | | 10 | DC component | $\text{Present}$ | qualitative | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA FOR KTU BOARD EXAMS** — Power Spectral Density of Unipolar NRZ: > > $$S_u(f) = \frac{A^{2}T_b}{4}\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b) + \frac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)$$ > > The first term is the **continuous AC spectrum**, and the **delta function at $f=0$** represents the **discrete DC component**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Short-distance, low-cost digital links:** TTL/CMOS inter-chip communication within a single PCB, where DC coupling is natural. - **Optical communication (OOK):** "On-Off Keying" of an LED or laser in simple IR remotes — light ON = '1', light OFF = '0'. - **Legacy telegraphy and telemetry** where DC signaling through wires was historically used. - **Educational / pedagogical** use as the simplest baseline to compare against Polar, Bipolar, and Manchester codes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean (DC) Value of Unipolar Signal Let the bit duration be $T_b$ and assume equiprobable binary data ($P(b_k=1) = P(b_k=0) = 0.5$). **Step 1:** Define the instantaneous signal amplitude within one bit interval: $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} A & \text{if bit} = 1 \\ 0 & \text{if bit} = 0 \end{cases} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the expected value over one bit period: $$ E[s(t)] = (1 \cdot A) \cdot P(1) + (0) \cdot P(0) $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the equiprobable probabilities: $$ E[s(t)] = A \cdot (0.5) + 0 \cdot (0.5) $$ **Step 4:** Simplify: $$ \boxed{\,V_{DC} = E[s(t)] = \frac{A}{2}\,} $$ > **Interpretation:** Because the average is non-zero ($\neq 0$), the signal **must contain a DC component**. This is a direct consequence of using only single-polarity levels. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Voltage of Unipolar Signal **Step 1:** Define the RMS as the square root of the mean square: $$ V_{rms} = \sqrt{E[s^{2}(t)]} $$ **Step 2:** Compute $E[s^{2}(t)]$: $$ E[s^{2}(t)] = A^{2} \cdot P(1) + 0^{2} \cdot P(0) $$ **Step 3:** Substitute probabilities: $$ E[s^{2}(t)] = A^{2} \cdot (0.5) + 0 $$ **Step 4:** Take the square root: $$ \boxed{\,V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{A^{2}}{2}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{A\sqrt{2}}{2}\,} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Spectral Density (PSD) of Unipolar NRZ **Step 1:** Express the unipolar signal as a weighted impulse train of rectangular pulses: $$ s(t) = \sum_{k} a_k \, p(t - kT_b) $$ where $a_k$ is the random amplitude ($A$ or $0$) and $p(t)$ is the unit rectangular pulse of width $T_b$. **Step 2:** The Fourier Transform of the rectangular pulse $p(t)$ of width $T_b$ and unit height is: $$ P(f) = T_b \,\text{sinc}(fT_b) $$ where $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$. **Step 3:** Compute the mean $m_a = E[a_k]$ and variance $\sigma_a^{2} = E[a_k^{2}] - m_a^{2}$: $$ m_a = A \cdot 0.5 + 0 \cdot 0.5 = \frac{A}{2} $$ $$ \sigma_a^{2} = \left(A^{2} \cdot 0.5 + 0\right) - \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{2} - \frac{A^{2}}{4} = \frac{A^{2}}{4} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the standard PSD formula for a random baseband signal: $$ S(f) = \frac{\vert P(f) \vert^{2}}{T_b}\,\sigma_a^{2} + \frac{\vert P(f) \vert^{2}}{T_b}\,m_a^{2}\,\delta(fT_b) $$ **Step 5:** Substitute $P(f) = T_b \text{sinc}(fT_b)$ and $\sigma_a^{2} = A^{2}/4$: $$ S(f) = \frac{T_b^{2}\,\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b)}{T_b}\cdot\frac{A^{2}}{4} + \frac{T_b^{2}\,\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b)}{T_b}\cdot\frac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f) $$ **Step 6:** Simplify: $$ \boxed{\,S_u(f) = \frac{A^{2}T_b}{4}\,\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b) + \frac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)\,} $$ **Interpretation of terms:** - **First term** $\dfrac{A^{2}T_b}{4}\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b)$: continuous AC spectrum, concentrated near low frequencies, with first null at $f = 1/T_b = R_b$. - **Second term** $\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)$: a discrete **impulse at $f = 0$**, representing the **DC power**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Snapshot (Unipolar vs Other Schemes) | Property | Unipolar NRZ | Polar NRZ | Bipolar (AMI) | |---|---|---|---| | Signal levels | 2 ($0$, $+A$) | 2 ($-A$, $+A$) | 3 ($-A$, $0$, $+A$) | | DC component | High ($A/2$) | Zero | Zero | | Bandwidth | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/2$ | | Sync. quality | Poor | Poor | Good | | Complexity | Lowest | Low | Moderate | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Binary Data Source
Sequence 1 0 1 1 0 0 1] --> nodeB[Unipolar NRZ Encoder
Logic Mapping Block] nodeB -- Bit 1 --> nodeC[Output Voltage
Plus A Volts] nodeB -- Bit 0 --> nodeD[Output Voltage
Zero Volts] nodeC --> nodeE[Channel
Baseband Medium] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[Receiver Decision Circuit
Threshold at A by 2] nodeF --> nodeG[Reconstructed Binary
Sequence 1 0 1 1 0 0 1] style nodeB fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Waveform Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP[Input Bit] --> nodeQ{Bit Equals 1} nodeQ -- Yes --> nodeR[Hold Plus A Volts for Full Tb] nodeQ -- No --> nodeS[Hold Zero Volts for Full Tb] nodeR --> nodeT[Move to Next Bit] nodeS --> nodeT nodeT --> nodeU{All Bits Encoded} nodeU -- No --> nodeP nodeU -- Yes --> nodeV[Transmit Unipolar NRZ Waveform] style nodeQ fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeR fill:#a8e6a3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeS fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual ASCII Waveform of Unipolar NRZ For the binary input **1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0** (eight bits, each $T_b$ wide): ``` +A | ## | ## ## ## 0 |__##___________##_##_____________________ 1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0 <--><---><---><---><---><---><---><---> T_b T_b T_b T_b T_b T_b T_b ``` **Reading the waveform:** - Bit '1' → top horizontal segment at level $+A$. - Bit '0' → flat segment on the baseline ($0\text{ V}$). - No transitions occur mid-bit (this is the defining NRZ property). ### 4.4 Mermaid PSD Sketch of Unipolar NRZ ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeX[Frequency Axis f] --> nodeY[DC Spike at f equals 0
Amplitude A squared by 4] nodeY --> nodeZ[Continuous sinc squared Lobe
Main Lobe Width 2 by Tb
Nulls at f equals n by Tb] style nodeY fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeZ fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Unipolar signaling. Mention two drawbacks.** **Model Answer:** Unipolar signaling is a baseband line coding technique in which **binary '1' is represented by a non-zero DC voltage pulse (typically $+A$ V)** and **binary '0' is represented by zero voltage**, using only a single polarity. **Two drawbacks:** 1. Presence of a **significant DC component** (mean value $= A/2$), which prevents transmission through AC-coupled channels. 2. **Poor synchronization** — long runs of 0s (or 1s) produce no transitions, causing the receiver to lose bit timing. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Sketch the waveform for the binary data `10110` using Unipolar NRZ encoding and state its DC value.** **Model Answer:** ``` +A | ## ## | ## ## 0 |___##_##__________________________________ 1 0 1 1 0 ``` **DC value** for equiprobable bits: $V_{DC} = A/2$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the Unipolar NRZ signaling scheme with a neat waveform for the binary sequence `10100110`. Derive its mean and RMS values. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Power Spectral Density expression for Unipolar NRZ and explain why it contains a DC component. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Definition:** Unipolar NRZ encodes bit '1' as $+A$ V held for the entire bit duration $T_b$, and bit '0' as $0$ V held for the full $T_b$. No return to zero within a bit. **Waveform for `1 0 1 0 0 1 1 0`:** ``` +A | ## ## ## ## | ## ## ## ## 0 |__##_______##__##_##____________________ 1 0 1 0 0 1 1 0 ``` **Mean value derivation:** $$ V_{DC} = E[s(t)] = A \cdot P(1) + 0 \cdot P(0) = A \cdot 0.5 + 0 = \frac{A}{2} $$ **RMS value derivation:** $$ V_{rms} = \sqrt{E[s^{2}(t)]} = \sqrt{A^{2}(0.5) + 0} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} $$ **Conclusion:** Because the average is non-zero, the signal carries a DC component. --- **Model Solution (b):** The unipolar NRZ signal is a random pulse train: $$ s(t) = \sum_{k} a_k\,p(t - kT_b) $$ with $a_k \in \lbrace 0, A \rbrace$ and $P(a_k = A) = P(a_k = 0) = 0.5$. The Fourier Transform of $p(t)$ (rectangular pulse, width $T_b$, unit amplitude) is: $$ P(f) = T_b \,\text{sinc}(fT_b) $$ Statistical parameters: $$ m_a = \frac{A}{2}, \qquad \sigma_a^{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{4} $$ The general PSD formula for a randomly-weighted pulse train is: $$ S(f) = \frac{\sigma_a^{2}\,T_b}{1} \, \vert\text{sinc}(fT_b)\vert^{2} + m_a^{2}\,\delta(f) $$ Substituting: $$ \boxed{\,S_u(f) = \frac{A^{2}T_b}{4}\,\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b) + \frac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)\,} $$ **DC component explanation:** The second term $\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)$ is an impulse located exactly at $f = 0$. This delta function arises from the **non-zero mean $m_a = A/2$** of the random amplitude sequence. Physically, this represents the **discrete DC power** concentrated at zero frequency — the "DC offset" that the unipolar scheme unavoidably carries. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Compare Unipolar NRZ, Polar NRZ, and Bipolar (AMI) signaling schemes in terms of DC component, bandwidth, and synchronization capability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a clear block diagram and waveform, describe the generation and detection of a Unipolar NRZ signal. State two practical applications. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** | Parameter | Unipolar NRZ | Polar NRZ | Bipolar (AMI) | |---|---|---|---| | Levels used | 2 ($0$, $+A$) | 2 ($-A$, $+A$) | 3 ($-A$, $0$, $+A$) | | DC component | **High** ($A/2$) | **Zero** (symmetric) | **Zero** (alternating) | | Bandwidth | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/2$ | | Synchronization | **Poor** | Poor (for long runs) | **Good** (every '1' flips) | | Implementation | Very simple | Simple | Moderate | **Inference:** Unipolar has the **worst DC behaviour** and **weakest synchronization** of the three, but is the **simplest to implement**. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Generation (Transmitter Side):** ``` Binary Source --> Unipolar NRZ Encoder --> Line Driver --> Channel ``` The encoder maps each bit: '1' → switch ON a $+A$ V source; '0' → switch OFF the source (output = 0 V). **Detection (Receiver Side):** ``` Channel --> Amplifier --> Threshold Detector (Threshold = A/2) --> Bit Decision --> Output ``` - If sampled signal $> A/2$ → decide bit = '1'. - If sampled signal $< A/2$ → decide bit = '0'. **Waveform flow:** ``` Data: 1 0 1 1 0 0 1 Tx out: +A 0 +A +A 0 0 +A Rx samp: >A/2 A/2 >A/2 A/2 Decision: 1 0 1 1 0 0 1 ``` **Two practical applications:** 1. **On-Off Keying (OOK)** in infrared remote controls (TV, AC remotes). 2. **TTL/CMOS inter-chip digital links** on PCBs (short DC-coupled traces). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in Unipolar questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the delta function** when writing the PSD — the discrete DC term $\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)$ is **mandatory**; writing only the $\text{sinc}^{2}$ lobe costs full marks. > 2. **Confusing Unipolar NRZ with Polar NRZ** — remember Polar uses $-A$ and $+A$ (two polarities), Unipolar uses $0$ and $+A$ (one polarity). > 3. **Incorrect DC calculation** — many students write $V_{DC} = A$ instead of $A/2$.
