Always remember the $0.5$ probability factor. > 4. **Saying "no DC component"** for unipolar — this is the *most common trap answer* and is **strictly wrong**; unipolar always carries DC. > 5. **Skipping the threshold value** at the receiver — always state the decision threshold $= A/2$ for unipolar signaling. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Unipolar encoding uses **one polarity only**; bit '1' = $+A$ V, bit '0' = $0$ V. - **Variants:** Unipolar NRZ (full-width pulses) and Unipolar RZ (half-width pulses). - **DC value:** $V_{DC} = A/2$ (for equiprobable bits). - **RMS value:** $V_{rms} = A/\sqrt{2}$. - **PSD:** $S_u(f) = \dfrac{A^{2}T_b}{4}\,\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b) + \dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\,\delta(f)$. - **Bandwidth (NRZ):** $B_{min} = R_b/2$ (first null bandwidth). - **Advantages:** Simplest to implement, single voltage source, ideal for short DC-coupled links. - **Disadvantages:** Strong DC component, poor synchronization for long same-bit runs, baseline wander. - **Applications:** OOK in IR remotes, TTL/CMOS inter-chip signaling, legacy telemetry. - **Comparison hook:** Unipolar is the **baseline** against which Polar and Bipolar codes are benchmarked for DC performance and synchronization.

topics.title: Bipolar and Manchester, Pulse Modulation Techniques - Sampling Process - PAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bipolar and Manchester, Pulse Modulation Techniques - Sampling Process - PAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Line Coding: Bipolar (AMI) and Manchester Formats

### 1.1.1 Bipolar (Alternate Mark Inversion – AMI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Bipolar line coding**, also known as **Alternate Mark Inversion (AMI)**, is a ternary signaling scheme in which the binary symbol **'1' (Mark)** is alternately represented by **positive and negative voltage pulses**, while the binary symbol **'0' (Space)** is represented by **zero voltage** (no pulse). The polarity alternation occurs for every successive '1' to ensure a zero DC component on the line.

- **Number of signal levels:** 3 levels → $\{+V, 0, -V\}$
- **Synonyms:** AMI, Bipolar Return-to-Zero (BRZ variant), Pseudo-ternary (when '0' is pulsed — *not* AMI)
- **DC Component:** Ideally **zero** (because +V and −V balance out over time)
- **Bandwidth:** $B_{AMI} = R_b$ (equal to the bit rate)
- **Standard Frequency Band:** Baseband, occupying from **0 Hz to Rb Hz** for the power spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Balanced March":**
> Imagine a long line of soldiers (the data stream '1', '1', '1', ...) marching in place. To prevent the entire line from drifting either forward or backward (which would be a DC offset), the commander gives the order: *every time you step, alternate the foot used.* Left foot (+V), right foot (−V), left foot (+V)… The '0' soldiers simply stand still (0 V). Because equal numbers of left and right steps are taken, the *average* position of the line remains centered — the **DC component is zero**.

### 1.1.2 Manchester (Biphase-L) Encoding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Manchester coding** is a **biphase, self-clocking line code** in which a **mandatory transition always occurs at the middle of every bit interval**. A logical '1' is represented by a transition from **HIGH to LOW**, and a logical '0' is represented by a transition from **LOW to HIGH** (IEEE 802.3 convention).

- **Number of signal levels:** 2 levels → High and Low transitions
- **Synonyms:** Biphase-L, Manchester (G.E. Thomas convention uses opposite polarity)
- **DC Component:** **Zero** (every bit has equal + and − area, balanced)
- **Bandwidth:** $B_{MAN} = 2R_b$ (doubled compared to NRZ schemes)
- **Application:** **10Base-T Ethernet**, RFID, NFC physical layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Always-Clocking Pendulum":**
> Picture a pendulum (the clock signal) swinging from left to right at a constant rate. Every second, the pendulum *must* swing — it never sits still. If the data is a '1', the swing is from LEFT to RIGHT at the half-second mark; if the data is a '0', the swing is from RIGHT to LEFT. Because the pendulum *always* moves in the middle of each second, the receiver always knows where the bit boundaries are — hence **"self-clocking."**

---

## 1.2 Pulse Modulation Techniques — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Pulse Modulation** is a class of modulation techniques in which a **periodic train of pulses** acts as the carrier, and one of its parameters (amplitude, width, position, or quantized value) is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal $m(t)$.

The four primary analog pulse modulation techniques are:

| Technique | Parameter Varied | Abbreviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pulse Amplitude Modulation | Amplitude of pulse | **PAM** |
| Pulse Width (Duration) Modulation | Width/Time of pulse | **PWM / PDM / PTM** |
| Pulse Position Modulation | Position of pulse | **PPM** |
| Pulse Code Modulation | Quantized amplitude in binary | **PCM** |
| Delta Modulation | 1-bit differential coding | **DM** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Flashlight Morse Code":**
> Imagine a flashlight in a dark room acting as a **carrier pulse train**. The information can be encoded by:
> - **PAM:** varying the *brightness* (amplitude)
> - **PWM:** varying the *duration* the bulb stays ON (width)
> - **PPM:** varying the *position* of the flash within the period
> - **PCM:** sending a numeric code for each brightness level

---

## 1.3 Sampling Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition — Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:**
> A **band-limited message signal** $m(t)$ with **maximum frequency $f_m$** (Hz) can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its samples provided that the **sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$**. The minimum sampling rate $f_{Nyquist} = 2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s = 2f_m$
- **Aliasing:** Distortion caused when $f_s < 2f_m$ (high frequencies fold onto low frequencies)
- **Anti-aliasing Filter:** Low-pass filter placed *before* the sampler to band-limit $m(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Stroboscopic Wheel":**
> Imagine a spinning wheel with a mark on it. If you photograph it slowly (low $f_s$), the wheel may appear stationary or spinning *backwards* (aliasing). Photograph it *fast enough* (at least twice per revolution), and you can reconstruct the true motion exactly. **The Nyquist rate is the "twice-per-revolution" rule.**

---

## 1.4 Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> In **Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)**, the amplitude of each pulse in a periodic pulse train is **modulated in accordance with the instantaneous sample value** of the message signal. The pulse train has a constant period $T_s = 1/f_s$ and a constant pulse width $\tau$.

- **Two main types:** **Natural PAM** (aperture effect, rounded pulses) and **Flat-top PAM** (sample-and-hold, rectangular pulses).
- **Mathematical form:** $s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s)$
- **Bandwidth:** $B_{PAM} = 1/\tau$, but practically $B_{PAM} \approx 2W$ (twice message BW)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bipolar (AMI) — Operational Rules

- **Bit '0':** Output level = 0 V (no pulse — *true* AMI convention).
- **Bit '1':** Output level alternates between $+V$ and $-V$ for successive '1's.
- **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** Has a null at DC ($f = 0$) and at $f = R_b$ — useful for AC-coupled channels.
- **Synchronization Issue:** A long string of '0's produces no transitions, leading to **clock recovery loss**. Solutions: **B8ZS** (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution) and **HDB3** (High-Density Bipolar-3).
- **Error Detection:** A bipolar violation (two consecutive same-polarity pulses) indicates an error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Alternate"?** The alternation enforces spectral shaping — the spectrum has no DC and no low-frequency content, making it suitable for transformer-coupled or AC-coupled transmission lines.

## 2.2 Manchester — Operational Rules

- **Bit '0':** First half of bit period = LOW, second half = HIGH (transition LOW → HIGH at center).
- **Bit '1':** First half = HIGH, second half = LOW (transition HIGH → LOW at center).
- **Self-clocking property:** Transition at bit center provides a clock edge every bit period $T_b$.
- **Differential Manchester:** Transition at the *start* of the bit interval indicates '1'; no transition indicates '0'. Transition at center always present.

## 2.3 Sampling Process — Theoretical Framework

- **Impulse Sampling Model:** $s(t) = m(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$
- **Reconstruction:** Low-pass filtering of the sampled signal extracts the original message (sinc interpolation).
- **Anti-aliasing requirement:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ with a **guard band** of 10–20% in practice.

## 2.4 PAM — Detailed Operation

- **Natural PAM:** Each pulse has the natural shape of the sampled signal segment. The aperture effect causes signal distortion.
- **Flat-top PAM (Sample-and-Hold):** A zero-order hold (ZOH) maintains the sample value for duration $\tau$. Easier to decode but introduces **aperture distortion** $H_{ap}(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau)$.
- **Demodulation:** Low-pass filter with bandwidth $W$ recovers the message.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Cheat Sheet — KTU Exam Ready**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | AMI Bandwidth | $B_{AMI} = R_b$ | Hz |
| 2 | Manchester Bandwidth | $B_{MAN} = 2R_b$ | Hz |
| 3 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 4 | Nyquist Rate | $f_{Nyq} = 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 5 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 6 | Aliasing Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - f_s \vert$ | Hz |
| 7 | PAM Signal (General) | $s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n} m(nT_s)\,p(t - nT_s)$ | Volts |
| 8 | PAM Bandwidth (Flat-top) | $B_{PAM} = 1/\tau$ | Hz |
| 9 | Aperture Distortion | $H_a(f) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$ | unitless |
| 10 | Natural PAM Spectrum | $S(f) = M(f) \cdot P(f)$ | V/Hz |
| 11 | Pulse Train Spectrum | $P(f) = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau)\, \delta(f - k f_s)$ | V/Hz |
| 12 | PAM SNR | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{PAM} = \dfrac{\overline{m^2(t)}}{N_0 B}$ | dB (unitless ratio) |
| 13 | Bit Rate (Bipolar) | $R_b = 2B \cdot \log_2(M)$ for M-ary; for binary $R_b = B$ | bps |
| 14 | Reconstruction Filter | $G(f) = T_s$ for $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$; 0 otherwise | unitless |
| 15 | HDB3 Substitution | Replaces 4 consecutive 0s with B00V (B=AMI pulse, V=violation) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:**
> - **AMI** is used in **T1 (1.544 Mbps)** digital telephony trunks and ISDN PRI.
> - **Manchester** powers **10Base-T Ethernet** and **RFID/NFC** air interfaces.
> - **PAM-4** (4-level PAM) is used in **100G/400G Ethernet** and **5G NR** uplink.
> - **Sampling Theorem** underpins every **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter) in the world.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Spectral Density of Bipolar (AMI) Signal

Let an AMI signal $s(t)$ consist of alternating $+V$ and $-V$ pulses of width $T_b/2$ (Return-to-Zero) for bit '1' and zero for bit '0'.

**Step 1 — Define a basic positive pulse:**
$$p(t) = \begin{cases} V, & 0 \leq t \leq T_b/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Define negative pulse for alternation:**
$$p_{-}(t) = -p(t)$$

**Step 3 — Model the AMI bit stream:**
Let $a_n \in \{+1, -1, 0\}$ be the ternary symbol at bit $n$ such that $a_n = 0$ when bit = 0, and $a_n$ alternates in sign for consecutive '1's.

**Step 4 — Compute the auto-correlation:**
For independent equiprobable bits, $\Pr(0) = \Pr(1) = 1/2$:

$$R_s(\tau) = E[s(t)\,s(t+\tau)] = \frac{1}{4} \cdot R_{pulse}(\tau) \cdot \big[2\delta(\tau - nT_b) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{alternation}\big]$$

For an alternating stream, the ACF is computed using the polarity sequence. A standard result from KTU textbooks (Lathi's *Modern Digital and Analog Communication Systems*):

$$P_{AMI}(f) = \frac{V^2 \tau^2}{T_b}\,\sin^2(\pi f \tau)\,\frac{\sin^2(\pi f T_b)}{\sin^2(\pi f T_b)} \cdot \big[1 - \cos(2\pi f T_b)\big]$$

**Step 5 — Spectral characteristics:**
$$\lim_{f \to 0} P_{AMI}(f) \to 0 \quad \text{(no DC component)}$$

This confirms the **DC-free property** of AMI.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Theorem Reconstruction

**Step 1 — Impulse train sampler:**
Let $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$ be the ideal impulse train. The sampled signal is:
$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform:**
Using the multiplication property:
$$S(f) = M(f) * P(f)$$

The Fourier Transform of the impulse train is another impulse train:
$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 3 — Convolution result:**
$$S(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

This shows the spectrum of $m(t)$ is **replicated** at every multiple of $f_s$.

**Step 4 — Reconstruction condition:**
For non-overlapping spectral replicas:
$$f_s - f_m > f_m \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_s > 2f_m$$

**Step 5 — Reconstruction filter:**
An ideal low-pass filter with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_m$:
$$G(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Time-domain interpolation (Whittaker-Shannon):**
$$m(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

This is the **sinc interpolation formula** — every KTU exam favorite.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Flat-Top PAM Signal and Aperture Effect

**Step 1 — Sample-and-hold (ZOH) model:**
The flat-top PAM signal holds the sample value $m(nT_s)$ for duration $\tau$:
$$s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot h(t - nT_s)$$

where $h(t) = 1$ for $0 \leq t \leq \tau$, and 0 otherwise.

**Step 2 — Fourier transform of rectangular pulse:**
$$H(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) \cdot e^{-j\pi f \tau}$$

The magnitude:
$$\vert H(f) \vert = \tau \left\vert \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau} \right\vert$$

**Step 3 — Spectrum of flat-top PAM:**
$$S_{PAM}(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot H(f) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Aperture distortion $H_a(f)$:**
$$H_a(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau) = \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Compensation filter at receiver:**
A filter with transfer function $1/H_a(f)$ (an equalizer) is placed at the receiver to flatten the aperture distortion, within the message bandwidth $W$, so that $\tau \ll T_s$ minimizes distortion.

**Step 6 — Practical bandwidth:**
Since $H_a(f)$ rolls off after $f = 1/\tau$, the effective PAM bandwidth is:
$$B_{PAM} = \frac{1}{\tau} \quad \text{(to preserve pulse shape)}$$

When $\tau \to 0$ (ideal impulses), $B_{PAM} \to \infty$ but signal energy vanishes.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Natural vs. Flat-Top PAM

| Property | Natural PAM | Flat-Top PAM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pulse Shape | Follows message shape (rounded) | Rectangular (held) |
| Aperture Effect | None | Present ($H_a(f) = \text{sinc}$) |
| Circuit Complexity | Lower | Higher (sample-and-hold) |
| Decoding Difficulty | Higher | Lower (constant top) |
| Used in | Theoretical analysis | Practical transmitters |
| Distortion | None intrinsic | Requires equalizer |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Bandwidth Comparison of Line Codes

| Code | Levels $M$ | Bit Rate $R_b$ vs. Bandwidth $B$ | DC Component | Clock Recovery |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| NRZ-L | 2 | $R_b = 2B$ | Yes | Poor |
| NRZ-M | 2 | $R_b = 2B$ | Yes | Good |
| RZ | 2 | $R_b = B$ | Yes | Good |
| **Bipolar (AMI)** | 3 | $R_b = B$ | **No** | **Poor (needs HDB3)** |
| **Manchester** | 2 | $R_b = 2B$ | **No** | **Excellent** |
| MLT-3 | 3 | $R_b = 2B$ | No | Moderate |
| 2B1Q | 4 | $R_b = 4B$ | Yes | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** Manchester trades **double bandwidth** for **perfect clocking**. AMI saves bandwidth but needs scrambling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Line Coding Waveforms (Bipolar vs Manchester)

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Manchester Encoder State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StartBit
    StartBit --> FirstHalf: Read bit b
    FirstHalf --> MidTransition: At t = nTb + Tb over 2
    MidTransition --> SecondHalf: Apply XOR with clock
    SecondHalf --> NextBit: At t = nTb + Tb
    NextBit --> FirstHalf: n = n + 1
    SecondHalf --> [*]: Stream ends

    note right of MidTransition
        If b = 1: HIGH to LOW
        If b = 0: LOW to HIGH
        (IEEE 802.3)
    end note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The block diagrams above represent the *functional architecture* of PAM, sampling, and line coding systems. The Manchester state machine illustrates the timing-critical nature of the biphase encoding.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Bipolar (AMI) line coding. Mention its two main advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Bipolar (Alternate Mark Inversion)** is a ternary line coding scheme in which the binary '1' is represented by alternating positive and negative voltage pulses, while binary '0' is represented by zero voltage.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. **No DC component** in the spectrum, enabling AC coupling and transformer isolation.
> 2. **Single-bit error detection** via bipolar violation (two consecutive '1' pulses of same polarity).
>
> **Disadvantage:**
> Long strings of '0's cause **loss of clock synchronization** at the receiver. This is mitigated using **B8ZS** or **HDB3** scrambling. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**
> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal $m(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
>
> **Aliasing:** When $f_s < 2f_m$, high-frequency components fold back into the message band, creating irreversible distortion. The aliased frequency is $f_{alias} = \vert f - f_s \vert$.
>
> **Prevention:** An **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (with cutoff $f_m$) is placed before the sampler, and $f_s$ is chosen with a **10–20% guard band** above $2f_m$. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain Manchester line coding with waveform. Compare it with NRZ-L on the basis of bandwidth, DC component, and clock recovery.

**b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **flat-top PAM** system with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the spectrum of flat-top PAM signal and explain the **aperture effect**.

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Manchester Encoding — Explanation:**

In Manchester coding, each bit interval $T_b$ has a **mandatory transition at the center**. Using the **IEEE 802.3 convention**:
- **Bit '0'** → transition from **LOW to HIGH** at bit center.
- **Bit '1'** → transition from **HIGH to LOW** at bit center.

**Waveform Sketch (description):** For bit stream `1 0 1 0`, the waveform shows a square-wave clock XORed with the data. A '1' is +V for the first half and 0 for the second half; a '0' is the opposite.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | NRZ-L | Manchester |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Bandwidth | $B = R_b/2$ | $B = R_b$ |
| DC Component | Present | **Zero** (always balanced) |
| Clock Recovery | Poor (no transitions for long 0/1) | **Excellent** (mid-bit transition) |
| Bit-rate to BW | High | Half (less efficient) |
| Application | Serial RS-232 | 10Base-T Ethernet |

**Conclusion:** Manchester is preferred where robust synchronization is critical, despite the doubled bandwidth. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Flat-Top PAM System Block Diagram:**

```
m(t) → [Anti-Aliasing LPF] → [Sampler] → [Sample-and-Hold (ZOH)] → s_PAM(t) → [Channel] → [LPF + Equalizer] → m̂(t)
       ↑                       ↑               ↑                                         ↑
   fm cutoff             fs pulses         Hold τ sec                                  1/H_a(f)
```

**Step 1 — Define flat-top PAM mathematically:**
$$s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot h(t - nT_s)$$

where $h(t) = 1$ for $0 \leq t \leq \tau$ (rectangular hold pulse).

**Step 2 — Fourier transform of the rectangular pulse:**
$$H(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) \cdot e^{-j\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 3 — Spectrum of flat-top PAM:**
By Poisson Summation:
$$S_{PAM}(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot H(f) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Aperture effect explanation:**
The factor $H(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ distorts the message spectrum. This distortion is called the **aperture effect**, given by:
$$H_a(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau) = \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Mitigation:**
- Use $\tau \ll T_s$ to minimize distortion.
- Insert an **equalizer** with transfer function $1/H_a(f)$ after the channel LPF at the receiver.

**Conclusion:** Flat-top PAM is the practical choice for transmission; aperture distortion is corrected via equalization. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **natural sampling** of bandlimited signals. Derive the spectrum of naturally sampled PAM signal and discuss the conditions for distortionless recovery.

**b) [7 Marks]** A message signal $m(t)$ has maximum frequency **$W = 4$ kHz**. Determine:
  (i) Minimum Nyquist sampling rate.
  (ii) The sampled signal frequency if sampling at 6 kHz — check for aliasing.
  (iii) If a 3 kHz tone is present in $m(t)$ and $f_s = 5$ kHz, find the apparent frequency at baseband.

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Natural Sampling Block Diagram:**

```
m(t) → [x] ← [Pulse Train p(t) at fs] → s(t) = m(t)·p(t) → [LPF at fm] → m̂(t)
```

The natural sampler multiplies the message $m(t)$ with a train of pulses of width $\tau$ and period $T_s$.

**Step 1 — Pulse train representation:**
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \Pi\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Sampled signal:**
$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$$

**Step 3 — Fourier transform of pulse train:**
$$P(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \cdot \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Spectrum (convolution):**
$$S(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \cdot M(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Distortionless recovery condition:**
- The replicas at $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ must not overlap with the baseband replica (at $k = 0$).
- This requires: $f_s - W > W$, i.e., $\boxed{f_s \geq 2W}$.
- An ideal LPF with gain $T_s/\tau$ and cutoff $W$ recovers $m(t)$ exactly.

**Conclusion:** Natural sampling preserves message shape, but its spectrum has weighted replicas requiring precise LPF cutoff. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $W = 4$ kHz.

**(i) Minimum Nyquist sampling rate:**
$$f_{Nyq} = 2W = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Sampling at $f_s = 6$ kHz:**
Check: $f_s = 6$ kHz $< 2W = 8$ kHz.

Since $f_s < 2W$, **aliasing occurs**.

The folded (apparent) frequency of a tone at $f = W = 4$ kHz would be:
$$f_{alias} = \vert f - f_s \vert = \vert 4 - 6 \vert = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

So a 4 kHz tone would appear as a **2 kHz tone** in the reconstructed signal. **Severe distortion.**

**(iii) 3 kHz tone with $f_s = 5$ kHz:**
$$f_{alias} = \vert 3 - 5 \vert = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

The 3 kHz tone appears as **2 kHz** in baseband. **Aliasing confirmed.** **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing AMI with Pseudo-ternary:** AMI pulses on '1'; Pseudo-ternary pulses on '0'. Mixing these loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the reconstruction LPF in PAM block diagrams:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the low-pass filter at the receiver. Missing it costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $f_s = 2f_m$ without the "$\geq$" sign:** KTU expects $f_s \geq 2f_m$. Writing equality loses half a mark.
> 4. **Writing bandwidth of PAM as $2W$ without justification:** Always derive from $B = 1/\tau$ and state the assumption $\tau \ll T_s$.
> 5. **Skipping the aperture effect in flat-top PAM:** KTU's favorite 14-mark question *always* includes aperture effect. Omit it and lose 3–4 marks.
> 6. **Conflating Manchester with Differential Manchester:** Differential Manchester has a *start-of-bit* transition indicator; standard Manchester has only a mid-bit transition. Misnaming loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Aliasing calculation sign errors:** Use $\vert f - k f_s \vert$ (absolute value) and pick the smallest $k$ that brings the alias into $[0, W]$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **Bipolar (AMI):** 3 levels, '1' alternates $\pm V$, '0' is 0V, no DC, BW = $R_b$, needs HDB3 for long zero strings.
- **Manchester:** 2 levels, mid-bit transition mandatory, self-clocking, no DC, BW = $2R_b$, used in 10Base-T Ethernet.
- **Pulse Modulation Family:** PAM, PWM, PPM, PCM, DM — all use a periodic pulse train as carrier.
- **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (Nyquist rate = $2f_m$).
- **Aliasing:** Occurs if $f_s < 2f_m$; prevented by anti-aliasing LPF + guard band.
- **Sinc Interpolation:** $m(t) = \sum m(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}[(t - nT_s)/T_s]$.
- **Natural PAM:** No aperture effect; harder to decode; replicas weighted by sinc.
- **Flat-top PAM:** Uses sample-and-hold; aperture distortion $H_a(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau)$; needs equalizer.
- **PAM Bandwidth:** $B_{PAM} = 1/\tau$ (practical); with $\tau \ll T_s$, signal energy is small.
- **PAM SNR:** $\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) = \frac{\overline{m^2(t)}}{N_0 B}$.
- **PAM-4:** Multi-level PAM used in 100G/400G Ethernet and 5G NR.
- **Error Detection in AMI:** Bipolar violation = error.
- **Reconstruction:** Always use LPF of bandwidth $W$ at the receiver.
- **Memory hook:** "AMI saves bandwidth; Manchester saves clocking; PAM saves cost."
- **Most-tested KTU fact:** Sampling theorem + aliasing numerical problems (worth 7–8 marks regularly).
- **Spectral memory trick:** AMI spectrum → null at DC and at $f = R_b$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PWM and PPM Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PWM and PPM Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**, also known as **Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM)** or **Pulse Length Modulation (PLM)**, is a modulation technique in which the **amplitude** and **position** of the pulses are kept **constant**, while the **width (duration)** of each pulse is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating (message) signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> "PWM is a class of analog modulation schemes in which the width of the carrier pulse is varied in accordance with the amplitude of the modulating signal, while keeping other parameters (amplitude, period) fixed."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Intuition

Imagine a water tap that is switched **ON and OFF** periodically. The **ON-duration** (how long the tap stays open in each cycle) is **fixed** under normal conditions. Now, suppose you want to control how much water flows through in a given cycle:

- If the modulating signal is **large** (loud voice, strong voltage), you keep the tap open **for a longer time** within each cycle.
- If the modulating signal is **small** (whisper, weak voltage), you keep the tap open **for a shorter time** within each cycle.
- The **rate of switching** (frequency) and the **maximum flow rate** (amplitude) are kept the same — only the **duration** changes.

That is **PWM in a single picture**: **constant rate, constant peak flow, but variable open-time**.

---

## 1.2 Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)

**Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)** is a modulation technique in which the **amplitude** and **width** of the pulses are kept **constant**, while the **relative position (time shift)** of each pulse with respect to a reference time instant is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> PPM is essentially derived from PWM — once a PWM signal is formed, the **trailing (or leading) edge** of each pulse triggers a **narrow fixed-width pulse**. This makes the **timing of the narrow pulse** carry the message information. Therefore, **PPM is a "derivative" of PWM** in practice.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Schedule" Intuition

Imagine a train that is scheduled to depart every hour on the hour (**constant period**), and the train is always the **same length** (**constant pulse width**). However, a dispatcher can **delay** the train's departure by a small amount (say, 0 to 10 minutes late) to encode information:

- **Large message value** → train is delayed by a **larger amount** → pulse position is shifted **more to the right**.
- **Small message value** → train leaves **closer to the scheduled time** → pulse position is shifted **less**.

The train length, the train's size (amplitude), and the 1-hour schedule period are **all fixed**; only the **lateness** (position) changes. **That is PPM.**

---

## 1.3 Key Parameters — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Parameters Used in KTU Problems:**
>
> - **$A$** = Pulse amplitude (constant) — unit: **V (Volts)**
> - **$T_s$** = Sampling period / pulse repetition period — unit: **seconds**
> - **$f_s = 1/T_s$** = Sampling frequency — unit: **Hz** (must be ≥ **2W**, where W = message bandwidth, per **Nyquist Criterion**)
> - **$\tau(t)$** = Instantaneous pulse width — unit: **seconds**
> - **$\tau_p$** = Width of narrow pulse in PPM (constant) — unit: **seconds**
> - **$t_p(t)$** = Instantaneous pulse position shift — unit: **seconds**
> - **$k_p$** = Position sensitivity constant — unit: **seconds/volt**
> - **$D = \tau/T_s$** = Duty cycle (dimensionless, $0 \le D \le 1$)

---

## 1.4 PWM vs. PPM — At a Glance

| **Property** | **PWM** | **PPM** |
|---|---|---|
| Amplitude | Constant | Constant |
| Pulse Width | **Varies (information carrier)** | Constant (fixed narrow pulse) |
| Pulse Position | Constant | **Varies (information carrier)** |
| Power Efficiency | Lower (longer pulses) | **Higher (narrow pulses)** | 
| Noise Immunity | Moderate | **Better** |
| Synchronization | Not strictly required at receiver | **Requires strict synchronization** |
| Generation Complexity | **Simpler** | More complex (needs PWM + pulse generator) |
| Bandwidth | Higher (depends on max pulse width) | **Slightly less** (narrow pulses) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PWM — Generation Principle (Natural Sampling Method)

A PWM signal is generated by comparing the **modulating signal** $m(t)$ with a **sawtooth (or ramp) carrier** $c(t)$ using a **comparator** (a 1-bit quantizer).

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1 — Carrier Generation:** A periodic **sawtooth waveform** $c(t)$ of period $T_s$ is generated locally.
- **Step 2 — Comparison:** The instantaneous value of $m(t)$ is continuously compared with $c(t)$.
  - If $m(t) > c(t)$ → comparator output is **HIGH** (pulse ON).
  - If $m(t) < c(t)$ → comparator output is **LOW** (pulse OFF).
- **Step 3 — Pulse Formation:** Since $c(t)$ rises linearly with time, the duration for which $m(t) > c(t)$ is **proportional to $m(t)$**. This duration is the **pulse width** $\tau(t)$.
- **Step 4 — Result:** A train of pulses with **constant amplitude $A$** and **variable width $\tau(t)$** is obtained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note — Types of PWM:**
> - **Leading-Edge Modulation:** Only the **leading (rising) edge** of the pulse is fixed; the trailing edge moves with $m(t)$.
> - **Trailing-Edge Modulation:** Only the **trailing (falling) edge** is fixed; the leading edge moves with $m(t)$. *(This is the type most commonly derived into PPM.)*

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of PWM

Let the modulating signal be bounded such that $\vert m(t) \vert \le m_{max}$. The instantaneous pulse width is:

$$\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w \, m(t)$$

where:
- $\tau_0$ = nominal (unmodulated) pulse width — usually $\tau_0 = T_s / 2$ for **50% duty cycle at rest**.
- $k_w$ = pulse-width sensitivity constant — unit: **seconds/volt**.

The full PWM signal can be expressed as a **gating function**:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = A \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \Pi\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s - T_s/2}{\tau(t)}\right)$$

where $\Pi(x)$ is the **unit rectangular (gate) function** defined as $\Pi(x) = 1$ for $\vert x \vert \le 1/2$ and $0$ otherwise.

---

## 2.3 PPM — Generation Principle from PWM

**PPM is generated from PWM** using a **monostable multivibrator** triggered by the **trailing edge** of each PWM pulse.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1 — Take PWM Input:** The PWM signal from the previous stage is fed as the input.
- **Step 2 — Edge Detection:** A **differentiator + full-wave rectifier** converts each edge of the PWM pulse into a sharp **spike**.
- **Step 3 — Monostable Trigger:** The monostable is triggered by the **trailing edge spike** of each PWM pulse. It produces a **fixed-width narrow pulse** of width $\tau_p$ after a small delay.
- **Step 4 — Pulse Shifting:** The position of the narrow output pulse shifts **in proportion to the original PWM pulse width**, because the trailing edge itself shifted with $m(t)$.

The instantaneous position shift is:

$$t_p(t) = k_p \, m(t)$$

where $k_p$ is the **position sensitivity constant** — unit: **seconds/volt**.

The PPM signal can be written as:

$$s_{PPM}(t) = A \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \Pi\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s - \tau_0 - t_p(t)}{\tau_p}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Units / Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| $f_s \ge 2W$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| $\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w m(t)$ | PWM instantaneous width | seconds |
| $D = \tau(t)/T_s$ | Duty cycle | dimensionless, $0 \le D \le 1$ |
| $t_p(t) = k_p m(t)$ | PPM instantaneous shift | seconds |
| $P_{PWM} = A^2 \cdot D_{avg}$ | Average power of PWM | Watts |
| $P_{PPM} = A^2 \cdot (\tau_p / T_s)$ | Average power of PPM | Watts (lower if $\tau_p \ll \tau$) |
| $BW_{PWM} \ge 2W$ | Approx. transmission BW of PWM | Hz |
| $BW_{PPM} \ge 2W$ | Approx. transmission BW of PPM | Hz |
| $\text{SNR}_{PWM}$ vs $\text{SNR}_{PPM}$ | PPM has ~ **3 dB** advantage at high SNR | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Formula Insight:**
> When $\tau_p$ (PPM pulse width) is much smaller than $\tau_{avg}$ (average PWM pulse width), the **average transmitted power of PPM is much lower** than PWM — but the **information is preserved in timing** rather than energy. This is why PPM is preferred in **power-critical applications** like **space telemetry** and **optical communication**.

---

## 2.5 Why PWM and PPM Matter — Real-World Utility

- **PWM is the backbone of:** motor speed control, class-D audio amplifiers, switched-mode power supplies (SMPS), and servo motor drive circuits.
- **PPM is the backbone of:** remote control (RC) systems for drones and aircraft, optical communication (Li-Fi), and deep-space telemetry where every milliwatt of power matters.
- Both are **digital-friendly analog modulation** — they can be easily interfaced with microcontrollers, FPGAs, and DSP chips, making them highly relevant in **IoT, embedded systems, and 5G/6G waveform design**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Width $\tau(t)$ as a Linear Function of $m(t)$

**Given:** A sawtooth carrier $c(t)$ with linear rise from $0$ to $A_{max}$ in time $T_s$, and modulating signal $m(t)$ with $\vert m(t) \vert \le A_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Equation of the rising edge of the sawtooth:**

The sawtooth starts at $0$ at $t = nT_s$ and rises linearly to $A_{max}$ at $t = (n+1)T_s$. So the slope is $A_{max}/T_s$.

$$c(t) = \frac{A_{max}}{T_s} \, (t - nT_s), \quad \text{for } nT_s \le t \le (n+1)T_s$$

**Step 2 — Comparator condition for HIGH output (pulse ON):**

The comparator output is HIGH when $m(t) \ge c(t)$:

$$m(t) \ge \frac{A_{max}}{T_s} \, (t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the time interval of the pulse:**

The pulse begins at $t_{start} = nT_s$ and ends at $t_{end}$ when $m(t) = c(t)$. Solving for $t_{end}$:

$$t_{end} = nT_s + \frac{T_s \cdot m(t)}{A_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pulse width:**

The pulse width is the duration for which the output is HIGH:

$$\tau(t) = t_{end} - t_{start} = nT_s + \frac{T_s \cdot m(t)}{A_{max}} - nT_s$$

$$\tau(t) = \frac{T_s}{A_{max}} \cdot m(t)$$

This proves the **linear relationship** between the message signal and the pulse width. The proportionality constant is $k_w = T_s / A_{max}$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **non-zero resting width** (so that negative excursions of $m(t)$ still produce a positive width), we add the DC bias $\tau_0$:

$$\boxed{\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w \, m(t), \quad k_w = \frac{T_s}{A_{max}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power of a PWM Signal

**Given:** A PWM signal $s_{PWM}(t)$ with constant amplitude $A$ and varying width $\tau(t)$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power:**

For a pulse of amplitude $A$ (assumed across a **1 Ω** load for normalization), the instantaneous power during the pulse is:

$$p_{inst}(t) = \frac{A^2}{R} = A^2 \quad (\text{taking } R = 1 \,\Omega)$$

**Step 2 — Average power over one period:**

The signal is ON for time $\tau(t)$ and OFF for the rest of the period $T_s$. Hence, the average over one period is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{T_s} \vert s_{PWM}(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot A^2 \cdot \tau(t)$$

**Step 3 — Recognizing duty cycle:**

By definition, $D = \tau(t)/T_s$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{PWM} = A^2 \cdot D = A^2 \cdot \frac{\tau(t)}{T_s}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Since $D$ is **time-varying** in PWM (it carries the message), the **instantaneous power varies** with the message signal — this is one of the **drawbacks** of PWM compared to PPM (where $D$ is constant).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Advantage of PPM over PWM

**Step 1 — Power of PPM:**

For PPM, the pulse width is **fixed** at $\tau_p$ and the period is $T_s$:

$$P_{PPM} = A^2 \cdot \frac{\tau_p}{T_s}$$

**Step 2 — Power of PWM (worst-case, with max width $\tau_{max}$):**

$$P_{PWM} = A^2 \cdot \frac{\tau_{max}}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of powers:**

$$\frac{P_{PPM}}{P_{PWM}} = \frac{\tau_p}{\tau_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since $\tau_p \ll \tau_{max}$ in typical design (e.g., $\tau_p = 0.1 \, \tau_{max}$), we have $P_{PPM} \approx 0.1 \cdot P_{PWM}$ — a **90% power saving**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** gain of PPM with the **power** gain. PPM has a SNR advantage of about **3 dB** at high SNR over PWM, but its **average power is dramatically lower** because the pulses are narrower — these are two different properties!

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem:** A message signal $m(t) = 2 \sin(2000\pi t)$ volts is used to PWM a pulse train with sampling frequency $f_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$. The pulse amplitude is $A = 5\,\text{V}$ and the unmodulated pulse width is $\tau_0 = 50\,\mu\text{s}$. The carrier peak is $A_{max} = 5\,\text{V}$. Calculate:

1. The instantaneous pulse width $\tau(t)$.
2. The maximum and minimum pulse width.
3. The average power of the PWM signal.

**Step 1 — Compute $k_w$:**

$$k_w = \frac{T_s}{A_{max}} = \frac{1/f_s}{A_{max}} = \frac{1/8000}{5} = \frac{125 \times 10^{-6}}{5} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s/V}$$

**Step 2 — Write $\tau(t)$:**

$$\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w \, m(t) = 50 \times 10^{-6} + 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2\sin(2000\pi t)$$

$$\boxed{\tau(t) = 50 + 50\sin(2000\pi t) \, \mu\text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum and minimum widths:**

- When $\sin(2000\pi t) = +1$: $\tau_{max} = 50 + 50 = 100 \, \mu\text{s}$
- When $\sin(2000\pi t) = -1$: $\tau_{min} = 50 - 50 = 0 \, \mu\text{s}$ (edge case — pulses vanish at negative peaks)

**Step 4 — Average power:**

Average duty cycle $\langle D \rangle = \tau_0 / T_s = (50 \times 10^{-6}) / (125 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.4$

$$\boxed{P_{PWM} = A^2 \cdot \langle D \rangle = 5^2 \cdot 0.4 = 25 \cdot 0.4 = 10 \, \text{W (per 1 }\Omega\text{)}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PWM Generation — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> C[Summing Comparator]
    B[Sawtooth Carrier c of t] --> C
    C --> D[PWM Output s PWM of t]
    E[DC Bias tau 0] --> C
    C -.->|Modulated Width| F[Pulse Train with Variable tau]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#fce4ec
    style F fill:#f3e5f5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **message signal** and the **sawtooth carrier** are the two inputs to the **summing comparator**. A small DC bias $\tau_0$ ensures the resting pulse width is non-zero. The output is the **PWM signal** whose pulse widths track $m(t)$.

---

## 4.2 PPM Generation from PWM — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PWM Input s PWM of t] --> B[Differentiator]
    B --> C[Full Wave Rectifier]
    C --> D[Monostable Multivibrator]
    D --> E[PPM Output s PPM of t]
    F[External Trigger Pulse tau p] --> D
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#fce4ec
    style E fill:#f3e5f5
    style F fill:#fff9c4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The PWM input is **differentiated** to extract edges, **rectified** to obtain unipolar spikes, and the **trailing-edge spike** triggers a **monostable multivibrator** that produces a fixed-width pulse $\tau_p$ whose **position** encodes the message. The result is the **PPM signal**.

---

## 4.3 Waveform Relationship — PWM → PPM Conversion Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Modulating Signal m of t"]
    A1[Sinusoidal Waveform]
    end
    subgraph B["PWM Signal"]
    B1[Pulses with Variable Width tracking m of t]
    end
    subgraph C["PPM Signal"]
    C1[Narrow Pulses with Variable Position]
    end
    A1 -->|Modulates Width| B1
    B1 -->|Edge Triggers Monostable| C1
    style A1 fill:#bbdefb
    style B1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style C1 fill:#ffccbc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the elegant transformation — the **width information** of PWM is **converted** into **position information** in PPM. The **envelope shape of the original message** can be recovered from either signal at the receiver.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison of Information Carriers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Modulation Family] --> B[Pulse Analog Modulation]
    B --> C[PAM - Varies Amplitude]
    B --> D[PWM - Varies Width]
    B --> E[PPM - Varies Position]
    B --> F[PTM - Varies Time of occurrence]
    D -->|Derived via Edge Trigger| E
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#fff3e0
    style C fill:#ffebee
    style D fill:#e8f5e9
    style E fill:#f3e5f5
    style F fill:#fce4ec
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM). Mention any two advantages of PWM over Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM).**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a modulation technique in which the **width of each pulse** in a uniform pulse train is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating (message) signal, while the **amplitude** and **position** of the pulses are kept **constant**.
>
> **Two Advantages of PWM over PAM:**
>
> 1. **Constant pulse amplitude** → the transmitted signal is less affected by **noise** that adds amplitude fluctuations; noise primarily affects pulse edges (timing), which are easier to filter.
> 2. **Higher power efficiency** → the pulse amplitude is at its maximum value whenever a pulse exists, leading to better utilization of the available transmission power compared to PAM where amplitude varies.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)? Why is PPM preferred over PWM in certain applications?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Pulse Position Modulation (PPM) is a modulation technique in which the **position (time of occurrence)** of each narrow pulse is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating signal, while the **amplitude** and **width** of the pulses are kept **constant**.
>
> **Reasons PPM is Preferred:**
>
> 1. **Lower average power consumption** — because the pulses are narrow and of fixed width, the average transmitted power is much less than PWM, which is critical for **battery-powered** and **space telemetry** applications.
> 2. **Better noise immunity** — the information is carried in **timing (position)**, which is less susceptible to additive amplitude noise; PPM provides an SNR improvement of approximately **3 dB** over PWM at high SNR.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the generation of a PWM signal using a comparator with a sawtooth carrier. Draw the waveforms of the message signal, sawtooth carrier, and the resulting PWM output. Derive the relationship between the instantaneous pulse width $\tau(t)$ and the modulating signal $m(t)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Generation Principle:**

A PWM signal is generated by feeding the **modulating signal** $m(t)$ and a **sawtooth (or ramp) carrier** $c(t)$ into a **voltage comparator** (a high-gain op-amp without feedback). The comparator behaves as a **1-bit quantizer**.

- When $m(t) > c(t)$ → comparator output = **HIGH** (= $A$)
- When $m(t) < c(t)$ → comparator output = **LOW** (= $0$)

**Waveform Sketch (described in words):**

| **Waveform** | **Description** |
|---|---|
| $m(t)$ | Sinusoidal, $\pm A_{max}$ |
| $c(t)$ | Sawtooth, ramp from $0$ to $A_{max}$ every $T_s$ |
| $s_{PWM}(t)$ | Pulses of constant height $A$, with width = duration for which $m(t) > c(t)$ |

**Derivation:**

The sawtooth rises linearly: $c(t) = (A_{max}/T_s)(t - nT_s)$ during the $n$-th period.

The pulse begins at $t_{start} = nT_s$ and ends when $m(t) = c(t)$:

$$m(t) = \frac{A_{max}}{T_s}\,(t_{end} - nT_s) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{end} = nT_s + \frac{T_s \, m(t)}{A_{max}}$$

The pulse width:

$$\tau(t) = t_{end} - t_{start} = \frac{T_s}{A_{max}} \, m(t)$$

Including the DC bias $\tau_0$:

$$\boxed{\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w \, m(t), \quad \text{where } k_w = \frac{T_s}{A_{max}}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A message signal $m(t) = 3\cos(1000\pi t)$ V is used to PWM a pulse train. The sampling frequency is $f_s = 10\,\text{kHz}$, pulse amplitude is $A = 10\,\text{V}$, and the resting (unmodulated) pulse width is $\tau_0 = 40\,\mu\text{s}$. The peak of the sawtooth carrier is $5\,\text{V}$. Calculate the maximum and minimum pulse width and the average power of the PWM signal.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute sensitivity constant $k_w$:**

$$k_w = \frac{T_s}{A_{max}} = \frac{1/10{,}000}{5} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{5} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s/V}$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous pulse width:**

$$\tau(t) = 40 \times 10^{-6} + 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 3\cos(1000\pi t)$$

$$\tau(t) = 40 + 60\cos(1000\pi t) \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum and minimum widths:**

- $\tau_{max}$ at $\cos(1000\pi t) = +1$: $\tau_{max} = 40 + 60 = 100 \, \mu\text{s}$
- $\tau_{min}$ at $\cos(1000\pi t) = -1$: $\tau_{min} = 40 - 60 = -20 \, \mu\text{s}$

> Since $\tau$ cannot be negative, the actual minimum is **clipped to 0** $\mu$s (this is a **design limitation** — it causes distortion known as **slope overload**).

**Step 4 — Average power:**

Average duty cycle:

$$\langle D \rangle = \frac{\tau_0}{T_s} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.4$$

Average power (across 1 Ω):

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = A^2 \cdot \langle D \rangle = 10^2 \cdot 0.4 = 40 \, \text{W}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (7-mark sub-question):**
> - Students often forget to **add the DC bias $\tau_0$** when computing $\tau(t)$. Always write the **full equation** $\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w m(t)$, not just $k_w m(t)$.
> - Do not forget the **units** (μs vs ms). KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency.
> - If $\tau_{min}$ comes out negative, **state explicitly** that it is clipped to zero and mention the term **"slope overload distortion"** for full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a block diagram, explain how PPM signals are generated from PWM signals. Derive an expression for the instantaneous position shift of the PPM pulse in terms of the modulating signal.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (in prose):**

1. **PWM Input Stage:** A PWM signal $s_{PWM}(t)$ (with variable pulse width $\tau(t)$) is generated from the message signal using a comparator and sawtooth carrier.
2. **Differentiator:** The PWM signal is passed through a **differentiator** (op-amp with capacitor input), which produces positive and negative **spikes** at the leading and trailing edges of each PWM pulse.
3. **Full-Wave Rectifier:** The differentiated output is rectified to produce **unipolar spikes**, all of the same polarity, marking every edge.
4. **Monostable Multivibrator:** The monostable is configured to be triggered by the **trailing-edge spike** only (via a diode gating arrangement). It produces a **narrow fixed-width pulse** of duration $\tau_p$ immediately after being triggered.
5. **PPM Output:** The output of the monostable is the **PPM signal** $s_{PPM}(t)$ with constant amplitude $A$, constant width $\tau_p$, and **variable position** $t_p(t)$.

**Derivation of Position Shift:**

The trailing edge of the $n$-th PWM pulse occurs at time:

$$t_{tr}^{(n)} = nT_s + \tau(t) = nT_s + \tau_0 + k_w m(t)$$

In the **unmodulated case** (i.e., $m(t) = 0$), the trailing edge would be at $nT_s + \tau_0$. The **position shift** is the deviation from the nominal:

$$t_p(t) = t_{tr}^{(n)} - (nT_s + \tau_0) = k_w \, m(t)$$

If we define the position sensitivity $k_p = k_w$, then:

$$\boxed{t_p(t) = k_p \, m(t)}$$

This shows that the **position of the PPM pulse is linearly proportional to the message signal** — hence information is faithfully transferred from PWM (width) to PPM (position).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Compare PWM and PPM systems based on (i) information-carrying parameter, (ii) bandwidth requirement, (iii) noise immunity, (iv) power efficiency, and (v) synchronization requirement. State two applications of each.**

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **PWM** | **PPM** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Information parameter | **Pulse width** $\tau(t)$ | **Pulse position** $t_p(t)$ |
| (ii) Bandwidth | Higher (depends on $\tau_{max}$) | Slightly less (narrow pulses) |
| (iii) Noise immunity | Moderate (noise affects edges) | **Better** (~3 dB SNR gain at high SNR) |
| (iv) Power efficiency | Lower (longer pulses) | **Higher** (narrow $\tau_p$) |
| (v) Synchronization | Not strictly required at receiver | **Strictly required** (position cannot be recovered without a reference) |

**Applications of PWM:**

1. **DC motor speed control** — duty cycle controls average voltage, hence speed.
2. **Class-D audio amplifiers** — efficient switching amplification of audio signals.

**Applications of PPM:**

1. **Optical communication systems** (Li-Fi, IR remote controls) — low-power narrow pulses.
2. **Space telemetry** (e.g., ISRO / NASA deep-space links) — minimal power consumption critical.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (7-mark comparison sub-question):**
> - Do **not** give vague statements like *"PPM is better"*. You must justify each comparison with a **quantitative** or **engineering** reason (e.g., *"$P_{PPM} = A^2 \tau_p/T_s$, which is $\tau_p / \tau_{max}$ times the PWM power"*).
> - Always give **two real applications** — naming "*PWM is used in motors*" without specifying **DC or servo** loses a mark. Use specific examples.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **PWM = variable width, constant amplitude, constant position.**
- [x] **PPM = variable position, constant amplitude, constant width.**
- [x] The core PWM equation: $\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_w m(t)$, with $k_w = T_s / A_{max}$.
- [x] The core PPM equation: $t_p(t) = k_p m(t)$, derived from the trailing edge of PWM.
- [x] **PPM is generated from PWM** using a **differentiator → rectifier → monostable** chain.
- [x] PWM power: $P_{PWM} = A^2 \cdot D$ (where $D$ is duty cycle, time-varying).
- [x] PPM power: $P_{PPM} = A^2 \cdot \tau_p / T_s$ (constant, much smaller if $\tau_p$ is small).
- [x] **Nyquist criterion** must be satisfied: $f_s \ge 2W$ for the sampling process to be lossless.
- [x] **Slope overload distortion** occurs in PWM if the message changes too fast for the pulse width to track it.
- [x] PWM receivers are **simpler** (peak detector of pulse width); PPM receivers **require a precise time reference** (synchronization clock).
- [x] PPM has **better noise immunity** (~3 dB SNR gain over PWM at high SNR) — but **requires synchronization**.
- [x] **KTU favourite trick question:** *"Why can't we use PAM for high-noise environments?"* — because additive noise directly corrupts the amplitude, whereas in PWM/PPM the noise must shift an edge to cause error.
- [x] **Applications to remember:** PWM → motor control, SMPS, class-D amplifiers. PPM → optical comms, space telemetry, RC drones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of PCM Encoder and Decoder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of PCM Encoder and Decoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the most fundamental form of **digital pulse modulation** in which an analog message signal is represented in the digital domain through three sequential operations — **Sampling, Quantization, and Encoding** — and then transmitted as a serial stream of binary pulses over a communication channel.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, the **Block Diagram of a PCM Encoder** refers to the complete transmitter-side architecture that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude (analog) baseband signal `m(t)` into a serial binary Pulse Code Modulated waveform suitable for transmission. The corresponding **PCM Decoder** is the receiver-side architecture that reconstructs the original analog signal from the received binary stream.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Typewriter of Communication"

Imagine you are dictating a smooth, flowing musical tune to a friend over a **crackling telephone line**. The continuous tone is hard to transmit without noise. Now imagine instead you hire an assistant who:
1. **Listens** to your tune and takes a *snapshot* of its amplitude at every tiny moment (this is **sampling**).
2. **Rounds off** each snapshot to the nearest number on a pre-decided ruler, because you cannot write infinite decimal values (this is **quantization**).
3. **Writes down** each rounded number using only the digits **0 and 1** (binary), like a typewriter that only has two keys (this is **encoding**).
4. Sends this clean typewritten page across the noisy line.

At the receiving end, your friend **reads the 0s and 1s**, **converts them back to the nearest ruler number** (decoding), and **smooths out** the staircase output through a low-pass filter to recreate the original tune.

That entire pipeline of operations is what the **PCM Encoder and Decoder Block Diagram** describes.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **bold** values are the universal reference numbers for every PCM numerical problem in KTU examinations:
> - **Sampling Theorem: fs ≥ 2fm** (Nyquist Rate, where **fm** is the highest frequency component of the message signal)
> - **Standard Voice-grade Telephony: fm = 3.4 kHz, fs = 8 kHz**
> - **Standard CD-quality Audio: fm = 20 kHz, fs = 44.1 kHz**
> - **Quantization Levels: L = 2ⁿ**, where **n** = number of bits per sample
> - **Quantization Step (Resolution): Δ = (Vmax − Vmin) / L**

### 1.4 Operational Overview of the Complete PCM System

A complete PCM communication system is divided into three macro-blocks:
1. **PCM Transmitter (Encoder)** — converts analog `m(t)` to binary PCM waveform.
2. **Communication Channel** with **Regenerative Repeaters** — cleans and re-amplifies the binary pulses.
3. **PCM Receiver (Decoder)** — converts the binary PCM waveform back to analog `m(t)`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-by-Block Analysis of the PCM Encoder (Transmitter)

The PCM encoder consists of **five cascaded functional blocks**, each performing a mathematically distinct transformation on the signal:

#### Block 1 — Anti-Aliasing Low Pass Filter (Pre-filter)
- **Why it is needed:** Real-world signals like speech or music contain frequency components well beyond the audible band (e.g., high-frequency noise above **20 kHz**). If we sample such signals directly, these out-of-band components **fold back** into our baseband via a phenomenon called **aliasing**, permanently corrupting the signal.
- **How it works:** A sharp **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency **fm** removes all spectral content above `fm`, ensuring the **sampling theorem** is not violated.
- **Engineering Utility:** This is the **first line of defense** in any digital communication system. It is also called the **pre-alias filter**.

#### Block 2 — Sampler
- **Why it is needed:** A digital system can only process *discrete-time* signals; it cannot directly store or transmit a continuous-time waveform.
- **How it works:** The filtered analog signal is multiplied by a periodic train of **narrow rectangular pulses** of period `Ts = 1/fs`. The output is a **PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulated) signal** `m_s(t)`, which holds the amplitude of `m(t)` at instants `t = nTs`.
- **Sampling Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** `fs ≥ 2fm`. If `fs < 2fm`, **aliasing distortion** occurs and the original signal **cannot be recovered**.

#### Block 3 — Quantizer
- **Why it is needed:** The PAM signal has continuous amplitudes (infinite precision), which would require an *infinite* number of binary codes to represent exactly. This is practically impossible.
- **How it works:** The continuous amplitude range `[−Vmax, +Vmax]` is partitioned into **L = 2ⁿ discrete levels** with step size **Δ**. Each sample amplitude is **rounded off** to the nearest quantization level, producing a **finite-precision** signal.
- **Side Effect:** This rounding introduces an **unavoidable error** called **Quantization Error** `e_q(t) = m(t) − m_q(t)`, with maximum magnitude `|e_q|max = Δ/2`.
- **Two Types of Quantization:**
  - **Uniform Quantization** — all levels are equally spaced (Δ is constant). Used in basic PCM.
  - **Non-Uniform Quantization** — step size is small for small signals and large for large signals (e.g., **µ-law** in North America, **A-law** in Europe). Companding is used to implement this.

#### Block 4 — Encoder
- **Why it is needed:** The quantized levels (which are still decimal numbers) must be converted into binary words so they can be transmitted as robust **on-off voltage pulses**.
- **How it works:** Each quantization level is assigned a unique **n-bit binary codeword**. For example, with `n = 8`, there are **L = 256 levels** and each sample becomes an 8-bit word.
- **Common Encoding Schemes:** **Natural Binary Code (NBC)**, **Gray Code**, **Folded Binary Code**.

#### Block 5 — Parallel-to-Serial Converter (Shift Register)
- **Why it is needed:** The encoder produces all `n` bits of a codeword **simultaneously in parallel** on `n` separate wires. A single communication channel, however, can carry data only **one bit at a time** (serially).
- **How it works:** A high-speed shift register clocks out the `n` bits sequentially at the **bit rate `Rb = n·fs`**, forming a serial binary waveform `s(t)` of 0s and +V volts.

### 2.2 Block-by-Block Analysis of the PCM Decoder (Receiver)

The decoder is essentially the **time-reversed** counterpart of the encoder:

#### Block 1 — Serial-to-Parallel Converter & Frame Synchronizer
- Groups the incoming serial bits back into **n-bit codewords** and aligns them using a **frame synchronizer** so that the decoder knows where each codeword begins.

#### Block 2 — Decoder (Binary-to-Level Converter)
- Each `n-bit` binary word is converted back to its corresponding discrete **quantization level** voltage. This is the inverse operation of the encoder.
- The output is a **staircase PAM signal** `m_q(t)`.

#### Block 3 — Reconstruction (Low-Pass) Filter
- **Why it is needed:** The staircase output contains the original baseband signal **plus high-frequency spectral images** (replicas) centered around multiples of `fs`.
- **How it works:** A low-pass filter with cutoff frequency **fm** removes the spectral replicas, leaving behind a **smooth, continuous-time approximation** `m'(t)` of the original message `m(t)`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Numerical Question on PCM.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Key Condition |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Hz | Minimum rate to avoid aliasing |
| 2 | Sampling Theorem (General) | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz | Strict inequality for practical systems |
| 3 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | (dimensionless) | n = bits per sample |
| 4 | Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L}$ | Volts | Uniform quantization |
| 5 | Maximum Quantization Error | $\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts | Rounding quantizer |
| 6 | Output Bit Rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/sec (bps) | Serial transmission rate |
| 7 | Transmission Bandwidth (min) | $B_T = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ | Hz | For NRZ polar line coding |
| 8 | SQNR (Uniform Quantizer) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$ | dB | Sinusoidal input assumption |
| 9 | Quantization Noise Power | $\sigma_q^{2} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$ | W (normalized) | Uniform distribution of error |
| 10 | Channel Capacity (ideal) | $C = 2B \log_2(L)$ | bps | Hartley-Shannon for noiseless channel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The formula $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ is the **bridge** between the analog world (fm) and the digital world (n, fs, Rb). The bandwidth efficiency of PCM is its biggest engineering trade-off — every extra bit of resolution (`n`) **doubles the levels** but also **doubles the bit rate** and hence the required transmission bandwidth.

### 2.4 Why PCM? — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Noise Immunity:** Binary 0s and 1s can be perfectly regenerated by **repeaters** along a long-haul link, completely eliminating accumulated noise. This is the single greatest advantage over AM/FM analog systems.
- **Encryption & Security:** Digital bitstreams can be encrypted, compressed, and error-corrected with sophisticated algorithms.
- **Multiplexing:** Multiple PCM channels (e.g., 32 voice channels in **E1**, 24 in **T1**) can be **Time Division Multiplexed (TDM)** into a single high-speed digital trunk.
- **Storage:** Digital formats (WAV, MP3, FLAC) are the universal storage standard — all are based on PCM or its derivatives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

The SQNR is the **single most important formula** in PCM theory. It quantifies the **fidelity** of the reconstructed signal relative to the unavoidable quantization noise. Let us derive it from first principles for a **uniform mid-rise quantizer** with a **sinusoidal input**.

**Step 1 — Set up the input and quantizer step.**
Let the message signal be a sinusoid of peak amplitude `Am`. The quantizer has `L = 2^n` levels spanning `−Am` to `+Am`, so the total voltage range is `2Am`. The step size is:

$$
\Delta = \frac{2 A_m}{L} = \frac{2 A_m}{2^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Model the quantization error as uniformly distributed noise.**
The quantization error `e_q(t)` lies in the range `[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]`. If the signal is varying smoothly (no clipping), the error is well-modeled as a **uniform random variable** with zero mean.

**Step 3 — Compute the mean-square (power) of the quantization noise.**
For a uniform distribution on `[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]`, the variance (normalized power) is:

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^{3}}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} \right]
$$

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta^{3}}{8}
$$

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average signal power.**
For a sinusoidal input `m(t) = Am · sin(2πfmt)`, the average power delivered to a **1 Ω** load is:

$$
P_s = \frac{A_m^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Form the SQNR ratio.**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right) = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^{2}} = \frac{A_m^{2} / 2}{\Delta^{2} / 12} = \frac{6 A_m^{2}}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the expression for Δ in terms of n.**

$$
\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{6 A_m^{2}}{(2 A_m / 2^n)^{2}} = \frac{6 A_m^{2} \cdot 2^{2n}}{4 A_m^{2}} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 4^{n}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to decibels.**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(4^{n})
$$

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 1.76 + 10 n \log_{10}(4)
$$

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \quad \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The famous "6 dB-per-bit rule":** Every additional bit of quantization (`n`) improves the SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**. This is the **fidelity-vs-bandwidth trade-off** at the heart of all digital audio design (telephony uses 8 bits, CDs use 16 bits, studio masters use 24 bits).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Output Bit Rate and Transmission Bandwidth

**Step 1 — A single sample yields n bits.**
Each sampled value is encoded into an `n-bit` binary word.

**Step 2 — Samples are generated at the sampling rate fs.**
In `T` seconds, the number of samples produced is `fs · T`.

**Step 3 — Total bits generated in T seconds.**

$$
\text{Total bits} = n \cdot f_s \cdot T
$$

**Step 4 — Bit rate (bits per second).**

$$
R_b = \frac{n \cdot f_s \cdot T}{T} = n \cdot f_s \quad \text{bits/second (bps)}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum transmission bandwidth (Nyquist Criterion for binary signaling).**
For a baseband binary signal using **polar NRZ** line coding, the minimum bandwidth required to transmit a bit stream of rate `Rb` without ISI is:

$$
B_T = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{n \cdot f_s}{2} \quad \text{Hz}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Block-by-Block Summary

| Stage | Block Name | Input Signal Type | Output Signal Type | Mathematical Operation | KTU Exam Weightage |
|-------|------------|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Anti-alias LPF | Continuous-time, analog | Continuous-time, band-limited analog | Convolution with filter impulse response | Low (definition) |
| 2 | Sampler | Continuous-time, band-limited | Discrete-time, continuous-amplitude (PAM) | $m_s(nT_s) = m(nT_s)$ | Very High |
| 3 | Quantizer | Discrete-time, continuous-amplitude | Discrete-time, discrete-amplitude | $m_q = \Delta \cdot \text{round}(m_s / \Delta)$ | Very High |
| 4 | Encoder | Discrete amplitude levels | n-bit binary word (parallel) | Codebook mapping | Medium |
| 5 | P/S Converter | n-bit parallel word | Serial bit stream | Time-division multiplexing of bits | Low |
| 6 | Channel + Repeaters | Serial binary waveform | Cleaned serial binary waveform | Regeneration, amplification, timing | Medium |
| 7 | S/P Converter | Serial bit stream | n-bit parallel word | Demultiplexing, framing | Low |
| 8 | Decoder | n-bit binary word | Discrete quantization level | Inverse codebook mapping | Medium |
| 9 | Reconstruction LPF | Staircase PAM | Smooth analog approximation | Low-pass filtering | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete PCM Communication System — Block-Level Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **complete end-to-end PCM link**, from the analog source `m(t)` at the transmitter to the reconstructed analog output `m'(t)` at the receiver, including the channel and regenerative repeaters.

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[Analog Source m of t]
    lpf1[Anti Aliasing LPF]
    samp[Sampler]
    quant[Quantizer L equals 2 to the n]
    enc[Encoder n bit Binary]
    psconv[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    chan[Communication Channel with Noise]
    rep1[Regenerative Repeater 1]
    rep2[Regenerative Repeater 2]
    spconv[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    sync[Frame Synchronizer]
    dec[Decoder]
    rlpf[Reconstruction LPF]
    out1[Reconstructed Signal m prime of t]

    src1 --> lpf1
    lpf1 --> samp
    samp --> quant
    quant --> enc
    enc --> psconv
    psconv --> chan
    chan --> rep1
    rep1 --> rep2
    rep2 --> spconv
    spconv --> sync
    sync --> dec
    dec --> rlpf
    rlpf --> out1
```

### 4.2 PCM Encoder (Transmitter) — Detailed View

```mermaid
graph LR
    a[Input m of t] --> b[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter cutoff fm]
    b --> c[Sampler fs greater than or equal to 2fm]
    c --> d[PAM Signal m sub s of t]
    d --> e[Quantizer L levels step Delta]
    e --> f[Quantized Signal m sub q]
    f --> g[Encoder n bit Codeword]
    g --> h[Parallel n bit Word]
    h --> i[Parallel to Serial Shift Register]
    i --> j[Serial PCM Waveform s of t]
```

### 4.3 PCM Decoder (Receiver) — Detailed View

```mermaid
graph LR
    p[Received Serial PCM] --> q[Regenerative Repeater and Amplifier]
    q --> r[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    r --> s[Frame Synchronizer]
    s --> t[n bit Parallel Codeword]
    t --> u[Decoder Binary to Level]
    u --> v[Staircase PAM m sub q hat of t]
    v --> w[Reconstruction LPF cutoff fm]
    w --> x[Reconstructed Analog m prime of t]
```

### 4.4 Signal Waveform Evolution Across the PCM Encoder

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Stage 1: Original Analog m of t - smooth sinusoid] --> B[Stage 2: Anti aliased m of t - high frequencies removed]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Sampled PAM m sub s - amplitude held at nTs]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Quantized m sub q - staircase approximation]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Binary PCM s of t - sequence of 0 and 1 voltage levels]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Trace the signal path **left to right** in every diagram. Notice how the signal character changes progressively from a **smooth continuous wave** at the source to a **digital pulse train** at the encoder output, and back to a **smooth wave** at the decoder output. This transformation is the *entire essence* of PCM and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List and briefly explain the functional blocks of a PCM encoder.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A PCM encoder consists of **five cascaded blocks**:
1. **Anti-aliasing Low Pass Filter** (1 mark) — limits the message bandwidth to `fm` to prevent aliasing during sampling.
2. **Sampler** (0.5 mark) — produces a discrete-time PAM signal `m_s(t)` at instants `t = nTs` with `fs ≥ 2fm`.
3. **Quantizer** (0.5 mark) — discretizes the amplitudes into `L = 2^n` levels with step size `Δ`.
4. **Encoder** (0.5 mark) — maps each quantized level to a unique `n-bit` binary codeword.
5. **Parallel-to-Serial Converter** (0.5 mark) — transmits the `n` bits sequentially at bit rate `Rb = n·fs`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is an anti-aliasing filter essential before the sampler in a PCM system? State the related theorem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Real-world message signals (like speech) contain frequency components far beyond the message bandwidth `fm` (1 mark). If these are sampled directly, the high-frequency components will **fold back** into the baseband, producing **aliasing distortion** that permanently corrupts the signal and cannot be removed later (1 mark). The **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** with cutoff `fm` removes these out-of-band components before sampling. The **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** states that the sampling frequency must satisfy `fs ≥ 2fm` to ensure lossless reconstruction (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

(a) Draw the **block diagram of a PCM encoder** and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

(b) For a **sinusoidal signal** `m(t) = 5·sin(2000πt)` quantized using a **uniform 8-bit quantizer**, calculate:
- (i) The sampling rate `fs` (using Nyquist criterion)
- (ii) The number of quantization levels `L`
- (iii) The quantization step size `Δ`
- (iv) The output bit rate `Rb`
- (v) The Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio in dB **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The block diagram is:

```
m(t) → [Anti-alias LPF] → [Sampler] → [Quantizer] → [Encoder] → [P/S Converter] → s(t)
```

| Block | Function | Marks |
|-------|----------|-------|
| Anti-alias LPF | Removes frequency components above `fm` to prevent aliasing | 1.5 |
| Sampler | Samples the bandlimited signal at `fs ≥ 2fm`, producing PAM | 1.5 |
| Quantizer | Rounds sample amplitudes to nearest of `L = 2^n` discrete levels | 1.5 |
| Encoder | Assigns a unique `n-bit` binary codeword to each level | 1.5 |
| P/S Converter | Sends the `n` bits one-by-one over a single channel at bit rate `Rb = n·fs` | 1.0 |

**Part (b) — 7 marks (1.4 marks per sub-part)**

**Step 1 — Identify the message frequency.**
Comparing `m(t) = 5·sin(2000πt)` with `Am·sin(2πfmt)`:
`2πfm = 2000π` ⟹ `fm = 1000 Hz = 1 kHz`.

**Step 2 (i) Sampling rate using Nyquist criterion.**
`fs = 2fm = 2 × 1000 = 2000 Hz = 2 kHz`.

**Step 3 (ii) Number of quantization levels.**
Given `n = 8 bits` per sample:
`L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256` levels.

**Step 4 (iii) Quantization step size.**
With peak amplitude `Am = 5 V`, the total range is `2Am = 10 V`:
`Δ = 2Am / L = 10 / 256 = 0.03906 V = 39.06 mV`.

**Step 5 (iv) Output bit rate.**
`Rb = n · fs = 8 × 2000 = 16,000 bps = 16 kbps`.

**Step 6 (v) Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio.**
`SQNR = 1.76 + 6.02·n = 1.76 + 6.02·8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 dB`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

(a) Draw the **block diagram of a PCM decoder** and explain how the original analog signal is reconstructed from the received binary PCM waveform. **(7 marks)**

(b) A **bandlimited message signal** has `fm = 4 kHz`. It is sampled at **20% above the Nyquist rate** and quantized using a **10-bit uniform quantizer**. Calculate:
- (i) The Nyquist rate and the actual sampling rate `fs`
- (ii) The number of quantization levels `L`
- (iii) The maximum quantization error
- (iv) The output bit rate `Rb` and minimum transmission bandwidth `BT`
- (v) The SQNR in dB **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The block diagram is:

```
Received s(t) → [S/P Converter + Frame Sync] → [Decoder] → [Reconstruction LPF] → m'(t)
```

| Block | Function | Marks |
|-------|----------|-------|
| S/P Converter & Frame Sync | Groups incoming serial bits into `n-bit` codewords and aligns them in time | 2.0 |
| Decoder (Binary-to-Level) | Converts each `n-bit` codeword back to its corresponding discrete quantization voltage (inverse of encoder) | 2.0 |
| Reconstruction LPF | A low-pass filter with cutoff `fm` smooths the staircase PAM output, removing spectral replicas and yielding a continuous-time approximation of `m(t)` | 2.0 |
| Mention of Regenerative Repeaters in channel for noise immunity | 1.0 |

**Part (b) — 7 marks (1.4 marks per sub-part)**

**Step 1 — Nyquist rate.**
`f_Nyquist = 2fm = 2 × 4 kHz = 8 kHz`.

**Step 2 (i) Actual sampling rate.**
`fs = f_Nyquist × 1.20 = 8 × 1.20 = 9.6 kHz`.

**Step 3 (ii) Number of levels.**
`L = 2^n = 2^10 = 1024` levels.

**Step 4 (iii) Maximum quantization error.**
Maximum quantization error `= Δ/2`. For the step size:
Without specified amplitude, express in terms of `2Am`:
`Δ = 2Am / 1024` ⟹ `|e_q|max = Am / 1024`.
(If the student assumes `Am = 1 V` for normalized case, then `|e_q|max = 1/1024 ≈ 0.977 mV`.)

**Step 5 (iv) Bit rate and bandwidth.**
`Rb = n · fs = 10 × 9600 = 96,000 bps = 96 kbps`.
`BT = Rb / 2 = 48 kHz` (for polar NRZ line coding).

**Step 6 (v) SQNR.**
`SQNR = 1.76 + 6.02·n = 1.76 + 6.02·10 = 1.76 + 60.2 = 61.96 dB`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on PCM Block Diagram questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Anti-Aliasing Filter** — Many students jump directly to the sampler. This is a guaranteed 1–1.5 mark deduction. Always show the **LPF first**.
> 2. **Confusing `fs` and `fm`** — `fm` is the *message* bandwidth; `fs` is the *sampling* rate. The Nyquist criterion is `fs ≥ 2fm`, **not** `fm ≥ 2fs`.
> 3. **Wrong order of Decoder blocks** — S/P conversion must come **before** decoding, because the decoder operates on `n-bit` parallel words, not on individual bits.
> 4. **Skipping the Reconstruction Filter** — The decoder output is a *staircase*. Without the LPF, you do **not** have an analog signal — you only have quantized levels.
> 5. **Units mismatch in bit rate** — `n` is in **bits/sample**, `fs` is in **samples/second**, so `Rb = n·fs` is in **bits/second (bps)**. Always include the unit.
> 6. **SQNR formula sign error** — The formula is `1.76 + 6.02n`, **not** `6.02n − 1.76`. The `1.76 dB` is added.
> 7. **Not drawing arrows** in the block diagram — Each block must have a clear directional arrow showing signal flow from input to output.
