### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Diagram of PCM Encoder and Decoder**

- ☐ A **PCM Encoder** has **5 blocks** in this strict order: **LPF → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → P/S Converter**.
- ☐ A **PCM Decoder** has **3 core blocks** in this strict order: **S/P Converter (with sync) → Decoder → Reconstruction LPF**.
- ☐ The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** comes **before** the sampler and prevents **aliasing distortion**.
- ☐ The **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** is the foundation: **`fs ≥ 2fm`**.
- ☐ **Quantization** introduces **irreversible error** bounded by **`Δ/2`**. There is **no quantization error** in pure sampling.
- ☐ **Encoding** converts each quantized level to a unique `n-bit` binary word.
- ☐ **Bit rate** is the **most commonly tested numerical**: `Rb = n·fs` bps.
- ☐ **Minimum transmission bandwidth** for polar NRZ: `BT = Rb / 2 = n·fs / 2`.
- ☐ **SQNR formula** to memorize: **`(S/Nq)_dB = 1.76 + 6.02n`** — the **6 dB-per-bit rule**.
- ☐ **Regenerative Repeaters** in the channel can completely eliminate accumulated noise, making PCM the **most noise-immune** analog-to-digital modulation scheme.
- ☐ The output of the decoder is a **staircase PAM**; it is **not yet** the original analog signal — the **Reconstruction LPF** is mandatory.
- ☐ **Voice telephony standard:** `fm = 3.4 kHz, fs = 8 kHz, n = 8 bits` ⟹ `Rb = 64 kbps` (this is the **G.711 PCM standard**).
- ☐ The complete signal evolution is: **Analog → Bandlimited → Sampled (PAM) → Quantized → Binary → Channel → Binary → Quantized → Smoothed Analog**.
- ☐ **Companding (µ-law / A-law)** is used in practice to improve SQNR for small signals, but the basic KTU problems assume **uniform quantization**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Satellite Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Satellite Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Satellite Communication** is a specialized branch of wireless telecommunication in which artificial satellites stationed in specific orbits around the Earth act as **repeaters, relays, or active transponders** to receive radio signals from a ground station (Earth Station), amplify and frequency-translate them, and retransmit them to another ground station located at a distant geographical location.

The fundamental architecture is a **bent-pipe** (or transparent) relay system in which the satellite does not perform baseband processing in conventional analog systems; it merely shifts the carrier from the **uplink frequency** $f_{U}$ to the **downlink frequency** $f_{D}$ and rebroadcasts the modulated signal back to Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Satellite communication is a method of transmitting information from one place to another using a satellite stationed in a specified orbit around the Earth as a radio repeater or relay. The communication link consists of an uplink (Earth-to-Satellite), a transponder (on the satellite), and a downlink (Satellite-to-Earth)."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a beach in Mumbai and want to talk to a friend standing on a beach in Kochi. The Earth's curvature (≈ 1,500 km apart) blocks the direct line-of-sight. You cannot simply use a long-distance Wi-Fi router.

**The Analogy — "The Floating Lighthouse":**
- A **lighthouse** on a tall tower can be seen by ships that are far away on the curved horizon.
- Now, place an **invisible, electronic lighthouse** at an altitude of 36,000 km above the equator. It can "see" almost an entire hemisphere of the Earth at once.
- A satellite is exactly that: a **giant electronic mirror in the sky** that catches your voice from one side of the continent and bounces it down to the other side.

Just like a tower amplifies your mobile signal for a small cell, the satellite amplifies the signal for an **entire continent**. The cost of building a very tall tower (≈ 36,000 km) is paid once, and it serves millions of users.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants (Must Memorize)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mu = GM$ | Earth's Geocentric Gravitational Constant | $3.986 \times 10^{5}$ | km³/s² |
| $R_E$ | Mean Equatorial Radius of Earth | **6,378** | km |
| $h_{GEO}$ | Geostationary Altitude Above Sea Level | **35,786** | km |
| $r_{GEO}$ | Geostationary Orbital Radius | **42,164** | km |
| $T_{sid}$ | Sidereal Day | 86,164 | seconds |
| $c$ | Speed of Light in Free Space | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| $g_{0}$ | Standard Gravity at Sea Level | 9.80665 | m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> The number **42,164 km** is the single most important constant in satellite communication. It is the radius of the geostationary orbit, derived from Kepler's Third Law. Almost every KTU numerical problem revolves around this value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step by Step Logic

The end-to-end signal journey in a satellite link follows a strict six-stage pipeline:

1. **Information Generation (Source)**: The user creates a baseband signal (voice, video, data) at the originating Earth Station.
2. **Modulation & Up-conversion**: The baseband signal modulates an RF carrier at the **uplink frequency** $f_U$ (typically in the 6/4 GHz, 14/12 GHz, or 30/20 GHz bands).
3. **Uplink Transmission**: A High-Power Amplifier (HPA) — usually a Travelling Wave Tube (TWT) or a Solid-State Power Amplifier (SSPA) — feeds a large parabolic antenna (e.g., 9 m to 30 m dish) that radiates a tightly focused beam toward the satellite.
4. **Onboard Reception & Transponding**: The satellite antenna intercepts the uplink beam. The signal passes through a **Receiver-Transmitter (Transponder)** which:
   - Low-Noise Amplifies (LNA) the weak incoming signal.
   - Down-converts it from $f_U$ to the **downlink frequency** $f_D$ (to avoid interference with the uplink).
   - High-Power Amplifies it before retransmission.
5. **Downlink Transmission**: The onboard antenna (a shaped-beam or global horn) broadcasts the amplified signal over a wide geographic region known as the **footprint**.
6. **Ground Reception**: The destination Earth Station receives the weak downlink signal (often below −100 dBm) using a low-noise parabolic dish and recovers the original information.

## 2.2 Why is Frequency Translation Necessary on the Satellite?

If the satellite simply amplified and rebroadcasted at the same frequency $f_U$, the powerful transmitter on the satellite and the powerful transmitter at the ground station would **jam each other** through the near-end feedback path. Shifting to $f_D$ (with $f_D \neq f_U$) creates a clean separation — a principle called the **outbound/inbound frequency plan**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Numbers — $f_U > f_D$:**
> Uplink always uses a **higher** frequency than downlink (e.g., uplink 6 GHz, downlink 4 GHz). This is because the **free-space path loss** $FSPL \propto f^{2}$ is higher at higher frequencies. By using the higher frequency on the uplink (where the ground station has a massive, powerful transmitter), we balance the link budget, while the lower downlink frequency experiences less attenuation on its way back to Earth.

## 2.3 Kepler's Three Laws of Planetary Motion (The Theoretical Foundation)

All satellite orbital mechanics are governed by these three laws:

- **Kepler's First Law (The Shape):** The orbit of a satellite is an **ellipse** with the center of the Earth located at one of the two foci.
- **Kepler's Second Law (The Speed):** The line joining the satellite and the Earth sweeps out **equal areas in equal time intervals**. This means the satellite moves *fastest* at perigee (closest to Earth) and *slowest* at apogee (farthest).
- **Kepler's Third Law (The Timing):** The square of the orbital period is directly proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of the orbit.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-weightage formula bank** for KTU 2024 ESE Module 3. Memorize every cell.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Kepler's Third Law | $T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}}{\mu} a^{3}$ | $T$ in s, $a$ in km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{5}$ km³/s² |
| 2 | Circular Orbit Period | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{a^{3}}{\mu}}$ | Used when orbit is circular ($a = r$) |
| 3 | Geostationary Radius | $r_{GEO} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\mu T_{sid}^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}}$ | $T_{sid} = 86164$ s, $r_{GEO} \approx 42164$ km |
| 4 | Geostationary Altitude | $h_{GEO} = r_{GEO} - R_{E}$ | $h_{GEO} \approx 35786$ km |
| 5 | Slant Range (Range Eq.) | $d = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r \cos\theta}$ | $R_E = 6378$ km, $r$ = orbital radius, $\theta$ = central angle |
| 6 | Max Slant Range (Horizon) | $d_{max} = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r \cos\gamma}$ | $\gamma$ = half-angle of Earth central coverage |
| 7 | Coverage Half-Angle | $\gamma = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{R_{E}}{r}\right)$ | For GEO: $\gamma \approx 81.3^{\circ}$ |
| 8 | Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $d$ in m, $\lambda$ in m, dimensionless ratio or dB |
| 9 | One-Way Propagation Delay | $t_{d} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | $d$ in km, $c = 3 \times 10^{5}$ km/s |
| 10 | Round-Trip Delay (GEO) | $t_{RT} = \dfrac{2 \times 42164}{c} \approx 0.28$ s | Critical for voice calls (echo issue) |
| 11 | Orbital Velocity (Circular) | $v_{orb} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r}}$ | km/s, for GEO: $v_{orb} \approx 3.07$ km/s |
| 12 | Look Angle Elevation | $\sin E = \dfrac{r \cos\gamma - R_{E}}{d}$ | $E$ in degrees, $0^{\circ} \le E \le 90^{\circ}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Engineering Role of Satellite Communication |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Direct-to-Home (DTH) TV** | TATA Sky, Dish TV, DD Direct — use Ku-band GEO satellites (e.g., GSAT-15). |
| **Long-Distance Telephony** | Inmarsat and Iridium handle maritime, aeronautical, and rural mobile backhaul. |
| **Internet Backbone** | Undersea cable backup; rural Wi-Fi hotspots via VSAT terminals. |
| **Navigation** | GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), IRNSS/NavIC (India), Galileo (EU) — all use MEO satellites. |
| **Remote Sensing** | Earth observation, weather forecasting (INSAT-3D), disaster monitoring. |
| **Military & Defense** | Secure encrypted beyond-line-of-sight communication for armed forces. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Geostationary Orbital Radius from Kepler's Third Law

**Goal:** Prove that $r_{GEO} = 42{,}164$ km for a satellite with an orbital period equal to one sidereal day.

### Step 1 — State Kepler's Third Law

For any celestial body in a closed orbit around Earth, the square of the period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.

$$T^{2} = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{\mu} a^{3}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Circular Orbit Assumption

For a geostationary satellite, the orbit is **circular** (constant altitude, constant velocity). For a circle, the semi-major axis equals the radius.

$$a = r_{GEO}$$

Substitute $a$ with $r$ in the equation:

$$T^{2} = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{\mu} r^{3}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Orbital Radius $r$

Isolate $r$ by multiplying both sides by $\mu$ and dividing by $4\pi^{2}$:

$$\frac{\mu T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}} = r^{3}$$

Take the cube root of both sides:

$$r = \sqrt[3]{\frac{\mu T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

Use $T = T_{sid} = 86{,}164$ seconds and $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{5}$ km³/s².

$$r = \sqrt[3]{\frac{(3.986 \times 10^{5}) \times (86{,}164)^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}}$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Numerator

$$(86{,}164)^{2} = 7.4242 \times 10^{9} \text{ s}^{2}$$

$$3.986 \times 10^{5} \times 7.4242 \times 10^{9} = 2.9593 \times 10^{15} \text{ km}^{3}\!/\!\text{s}$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$4\pi^{2} = 39.478 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

### Step 7 — Divide and Take the Cube Root

$$r^{3} = \frac{2.9593 \times 10^{15}}{39.478} = 7.496 \times 10^{13} \text{ km}^{3}$$

$$r = \sqrt[3]{7.496 \times 10^{13}} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$$

### Step 8 — Derive the Altitude Above Sea Level

$$h_{GEO} = r_{GEO} - R_{E} = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU 2024:** If a question gives you an orbital period (e.g., "a satellite orbits Earth every 12 hours"), you use this exact derivation to find $r$, then $h$, then verify if it is LEO/MEO/GEO. The examiner expects the algebraic steps, not a direct formula substitution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slant Range Between Earth Station and Satellite

**Goal:** Derive the straight-line distance $d$ from a point $P$ on Earth's surface to a satellite $S$ in a circular orbit.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry

Let $O$ be the center of the Earth, $P$ be the Earth Station on the surface, and $S$ be the satellite. The triangle $OPS$ has sides:

- $OP = R_E$ (Earth's radius)
- $OS = r$ (orbital radius)
- $PS = d$ (slant range, what we want)
- The angle at $O$ between $OP$ and $OS$ is the **central (earth-station) angle** $\theta$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Law of Cosines

For any triangle, the square of one side equals the sum of squares of the other two minus twice their product times the cosine of the included angle.

$$d^{2} = R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r \cos\theta$$

### Step 3 — Take the Square Root

$$d = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r \cos\theta}$$

### Step 4 — Special Case: Earth Station at the Sub-Satellite Point

When the Earth Station lies exactly on the equator directly below the satellite, $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ and $\cos\theta = 1$.

$$d_{min} = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r} = \sqrt{(r - R_{E})^{2}} = r - R_{E} = h$$

This is simply the **vertical altitude** of the satellite above the ground station.

### Step 5 — Special Case: Earth Station at the Geometric Horizon

When the Earth Station is just barely visible at the edge of the satellite's footprint, the line $PS$ is tangent to the Earth's surface. By the tangent-radius theorem, the angle at $P$ is $90^{\circ}$, so the angle at $O$ becomes:

$$\theta_{max} = \arccos\!\left(\frac{R_{E}}{r}\right)$$

Substituting this $\theta_{max}$ into the slant range equation:

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r \cdot \frac{R_{E}}{r}}$$

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E}^{2}} = \sqrt{r^{2} - R_{E}^{2}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Orbital Classification

| Parameter | **LEO** (Low Earth Orbit) | **MEO** (Medium Earth Orbit) | **GEO** (Geostationary Orbit) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Altitude Range | 160 km – 2,000 km | 2,000 km – 35,786 km | **35,786 km** (exact) |
| Orbital Period | 88 min – 127 min | 2 hr – 24 hr | **24 hr** (sidereal) |
| Orbital Radius | 6,540 km – 8,378 km | 8,378 km – 42,164 km | **42,164 km** |
| Round-Trip Delay | 4 ms – 40 ms | 40 ms – 120 ms | **≈ 240 ms** |
| Beam Footprint | Small (city-wide) | Continental | **≈ 42% of Earth** |
| Satellites Needed for Global Coverage | **40 – 80** (Iridium: 66) | **10 – 12** (GPS: 24) | **3 (triad)** |
| Examples | Starlink, ISS, Iridium | GPS, GLONASS, Galileo | **INSAT, GSAT, Inmarsat** |
| KTU Mnemonic | **"L**ow **E**arth **O**rbit = **L**ow delay" | **"M**id = **M**edium **P**eriod" | **"G**eo = **G**round-station-**S**tationary" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — End-to-End Satellite Link

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a complete bent-pipe satellite communication chain, including both Earth stations and the onboard transponder. Each block is named with strict alphanumeric identifiers and clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[VOICE VIDEO DATA SOURCE]
    enc1[BASEBAND ENCODER AND MODULATOR]
    upa1[HIGH POWER AMPLIFIER TWT OR SSPA]
    tx1[EARTH STATION ANTENNA UPLINK]
    srx[SATELLITE RECEIVE ANTENNA]
    lna1[LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER LNA]
    dcn[DOWN CONVERTER FU TO FD]
    hpa2[HIGH POWER AMPLIFIER ONBOARD]
    stx[SATELLITE TRANSMIT ANTENNA]
    rx1[EARTH STATION ANTENNA DOWNLINK]
    lna2[LOW NOISE RECEIVER LNA]
    dec1[DEMODULATOR AND DECODER]
    dst1[USER DESTINATION]

    src1 --> enc1 --> upa1 --> tx1
    tx1 -. UPLINK FU .-> srx
    srx --> lna1 --> dcn --> hpa2 --> stx
    stx -. DOWNLINK FD .-> rx1
    rx1 --> lna2 --> dec1 --> dst1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The dotted arrows labeled `UPLINK FU` and `DOWNLINK FD` represent the wireless radio-frequency propagation path through free space. The solid arrows represent guided (wired) signal flow inside the Earth station or onboard the satellite. Notice that the only blocks on the satellite itself are the antenna, LNA, down-converter, and HPA — the satellite is essentially a *frequency-shifting amplifier*.

## 4.2 Onboard Satellite Subsystem Architecture

The satellite itself is a complex spacecraft with multiple subsystems beyond the communication payload. The diagram below shows all critical subsystems and their inter-relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    payload[COMMUNICATION PAYLOAD TRANSPONDERS]
    ttcm[TT AND C SUBSYSTEM TELEMETRY TRACKING COMMAND]
    power[POWER SUBSYSTEM SOLAR ARRAYS AND BATTERIES]
    prop[PROPULSION SUBSYSTEM STATION KEEPING]
    therm[THERMAL CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
    struc[STRUCTURE AND MECHANICAL FRAME]
    attitude[ATTITUDE CONTROL SUBSYSTEM AOCS]

    payload --- ttcm
    payload --- power
    payload --- prop
    payload --- therm
    payload --- struc
    payload --- attitude
    ttcm --- power
    attitude --- prop
    therm --- power
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mapping Hint:** The "Communication Payload" is the only part that interacts with the user's signal. The other five subsystems (TT&C, Power, Propulsion, Thermal, AOCS) are **housekeeping** subsystems that keep the satellite alive. A typical 14-mark question asks you to "Explain the various subsystems of a communication satellite" — this diagram covers all six in one figure.

## 4.3 Frequency Band Allocation Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[L BAND 1 TO 2 GHZ MOBILE SATELLITE]
    S[S BAND 2 TO 4 GHZ RADAR NASA]
    C[C BAND 4 TO 8 GHZ FIXED SATELLITE TRADITIONAL]
    X[X BAND 8 TO 12 GHZ MILITARY]
    Ku[KU BAND 12 TO 18 GHZ DTH BROADCAST]
    K[K BAND 18 TO 27 GHZ UPLINK FEEDER]
    Ka[KA BAND 27 TO 40 GHZ HIGH THROUGHPUT]
    V[V BAND 40 TO 75 GHZ FUTURE 5G]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The C-band (6/4 GHz) and Ku-band (14/12 GHz) are the two workhorse bands in KTU board problems. The classic uplink-downlink pair is **uplink 6 GHz / downlink 4 GHz** (C-band) with a frequency offset of 2.32 GHz (the **band-center difference**).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define a satellite communication system. List any four advantages of satellite communication over terrestrial communication.**

**Model Answer:**

A satellite communication system is a method of transmitting information between two or more Earth stations using an artificial satellite stationed in a designated orbit as a radio repeater. The link consists of an **uplink** (Earth-to-Satellite), an **onboard transponder**, and a **downlink** (Satellite-to-Earth).

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Wide Geographic Coverage:** A single GEO satellite covers ≈ 42% of Earth's surface, eliminating the need for multiple terrestrial repeater towers.
2. **Independence from Terrain:** Signals travel via line-of-sight through space, bypassing mountains, oceans, deserts, and dense forests that obstruct ground links.
3. **Broadcast Capability:** Satellites inherently support point-to-multipoint distribution, ideal for TV, radio, and software updates to distributed terminals.
4. **Cost-Effective for Long Distances:** For intercontinental links, satellite is cheaper than laying thousands of kilometers of fiber or erecting chain of microwave towers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — 1 Mark Lost Here:** Students often write "satellite is faster than fiber" or "satellite has no delay." Both are **wrong**. The round-trip delay in GEO is **240 ms**, much higher than fiber. Do not confuse *reach* with *latency*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a geostationary orbit? Why is the altitude fixed at 35,786 km?**

**Model Answer:**

A **geostationary orbit (GEO)** is a unique circular orbit in the Earth's equatorial plane at an altitude of **35,786 km** above mean sea level, where a satellite's orbital period exactly matches Earth's sidereal rotation period of **23 hours 56 minutes 4 seconds**. As a result, the satellite appears **stationary** to an observer on the ground.

**Why the Altitude is Fixed at 35,786 km:**
- From Kepler's Third Law, $T^{2} \propto a^{3}$, the orbital radius is uniquely determined by the period.
- Setting $T = 86{,}164$ s (one sidereal day) and solving $r = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\mu T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}}$ yields $r = 42{,}164$ km.
- Subtracting the Earth's radius $R_E = 6{,}378$ km gives $h = 35{,}786$ km.
- This is the **only** altitude at which the satellite's angular velocity matches Earth's rotation; at any other altitude, the satellite drifts east or west relative to the ground.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** For Module 3, the examiner provides an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different subsystems of a communication satellite with a neat block diagram. List the functions of the TT&C subsystem.**

**Model Solution:**

A communication satellite is composed of two major functional groups: the **communication payload** and the **bus** (housekeeping subsystems). The complete architecture is shown below:

| # | Subsystem | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Communication Payload (Transponder)** | Receives, amplifies, frequency-shifts, and retransmits user signals. |
| 2 | **Telemetry, Tracking & Command (TT&C)** | Reports satellite health to ground; receives commands; tracks orbital position. |
| 3 | **Power Subsystem** | Solar arrays generate DC; Nickel-Hydrogen batteries store energy for eclipse periods. |
| 4 | **Attitude & Orbit Control (AOCS)** | Keeps antenna pointed at Earth using reaction wheels, gyros, and Earth/Sun sensors. |
| 5 | **Propulsion Subsystem** | Hydrazine thrusters for station-keeping (NSSK) and orbit raising maneuvers. |
| 6 | **Thermal Control** | Maintains onboard temperature using radiators, multilayer insulation, and heaters. |
| 7 | **Structure** | Aluminum honeycomb frame supporting all subsystems during launch vibration. |

**Functions of the TT&C Subsystem (Detail for 7 marks):**
- **Telemetry:** Continuously measures satellite parameters (temperatures, voltages, currents, pressures) and transmits them to a ground control station via a beacon signal.
- **Tracking:** Determines the satellite's position and velocity in orbit using range and range-rate measurements.
- **Commanding:** Receives coded instructions from the ground (e.g., "switch to redundant transponder") and executes them to switch operating modes or correct anomalies.
- **Housekeeping:** Critical for the **operational lifetime** of the satellite (typically 15 years for modern GEO birds).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — 2 Marks Lost Here:** Many students write "TT&C sends user data." This is **wrong**. The TT&C link uses a **separate frequency** (e.g., S-band) dedicated solely to satellite housekeeping, never for user traffic.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the orbital period of a satellite in a circular orbit using Kepler's Third Law. Calculate the orbital period of a GPS satellite at an altitude of 20,200 km.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Law:**
For a circular orbit, $T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}}{\mu} r^{3}$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $T$:**
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^{3}}{\mu}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the orbital radius for GPS:**
$$r = R_E + h = 6{,}378 + 20{,}200 = 26{,}578 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(26{,}578)^{3}}{3.986 \times 10^{5}}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the cube:**
$$(26{,}578)^{3} = 1.877 \times 10^{13} \text{ km}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Divide and square-root:**
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{1.877 \times 10^{13}}{3.986 \times 10^{5}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{4.710 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 6.864 \times 10^{3} = 43{,}127 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hours:**
$$T = \frac{43{,}127}{3600} \approx 11.98 \text{ hours} \approx 12 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** The GPS satellite completes one revolution in approximately **12 hours**, completing two orbits per sidereal day. This matches the published GPS constellation specification.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define the terms (i) Uplink, (ii) Downlink, (iii) Transponder, (iv) Footprint, (v) EIRP, (vi) G/T ratio, (vii) SFD. State the typical uplink and downlink frequencies for C-band and Ku-band satellite communication.**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | **Uplink** | The radio link from the Earth Station to the satellite, carrying the modulated carrier. |
| (ii) | **Downlink** | The radio link from the satellite to the Earth Station, carrying the rebroadcast signal. |
| (iii) | **Transponder** | The onboard electronic package that amplifies, frequency-translates, and retransmits the signal. A typical satellite carries 12–24 transponders. |
| (iv) | **Footprint** | The geographic area on Earth's surface within which the satellite's downlink signal can be received with adequate strength. |
| (v) | **EIRP** | Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power = $P_{TX} \times G_{TX}$ (in dBW). Indicates effective power radiated by antenna in the desired direction. |
| (vi) | **G/T Ratio** | Figure of Merit of receiving Earth Station = $G_{RX} \setminus T_{sys}$ (in dB/K). Higher G/T means better reception of weak signals. |
| (vii) | **SFD** | Saturation Flux Density — the input power flux density at the satellite antenna required to saturate the transponder's HPA. |

**Frequency Bands:**

| Band | Uplink Frequency | Downlink Frequency | Primary Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **C-Band** | **5.925 – 6.425 GHz** | **3.700 – 4.200 GHz** | Fixed Satellite, VSAT, TV distribution |
| **Ku-Band** | **14.0 – 14.5 GHz** | **11.7 – 12.2 GHz** | DTH TV (TATA Sky), VSAT |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — 1 Mark Lost Here:** A common mistake is writing "uplink = 4 GHz, downlink = 6 GHz." This is **reversed**. Always remember: **Uplink Frequency > Downlink Frequency** (e.g., 6 GHz up, 4 GHz down).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a geostationary satellite, calculate the (i) orbital radius, (ii) altitude above Earth's surface, and (iii) round-trip propagation delay for an Earth Station located at the sub-satellite point. Take $R_E = 6{,}378$ km and $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{5}$ km³/s².**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**
- Orbital period $T = T_{sid} = 86{,}164$ s
- $R_E = 6{,}378$ km
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{5}$ km³/s²

**Step 2 — Compute the orbital radius $r$:**
$$r = \sqrt[3]{\frac{\mu T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}}$$

$$r = \sqrt[3]{\frac{(3.986 \times 10^{5}) \times (86{,}164)^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}}$$

$$r = \sqrt[3]{7.496 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$r = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the altitude $h$:**
$$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the slant range for sub-satellite point:**
At the sub-satellite point, the slant range equals the altitude:
$$d = h = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the round-trip delay:**
$$t_{RT} = \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{2 \times 35{,}786}{3 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$t_{RT} = \frac{71{,}572}{300{,}000} = 0.2386 \text{ s} \approx 239 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** A signal sent from the sub-satellite Earth Station to the GEO satellite and back experiences a **one-way delay of ≈ 120 ms** and a **round-trip delay of ≈ 239 ms**. This is why voice calls over GEO satellite require **echo cancellers**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Introduction to Satellite Communication**

- [ ] **The Big 5 Numbers:** $r_{GEO} = 42{,}164$ km; $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786$ km; $T_{sid} = 86{,}164$ s; $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{5}$ km³/s²; $R_E = 6{,}378$ km.
- [ ] **Three Kepler's Laws:** Ellipse, Equal Areas, $T^{2} \propto a^{3}$.
- [ ] **Frequency Plan:** Uplink > Downlink (e.g., 6/4 GHz C-band, 14/12 GHz Ku-band).
- [ ] **Orbit Classification:** LEO < 2,000 km | MEO 2,000–35,786 km | GEO = 35,786 km.
- [ ] **Round-Trip Delay of GEO:** ≈ 240 ms (echo cancellers mandatory for voice).
- [ ] **Six Subsystems of a Satellite:** Payload, TT&C, Power, AOCS, Propulsion, Thermal (+ Structure).
- [ ] **TT&C vs Payload:** TT&C uses S-band for housekeeping only; payload uses C/Ku/Ka for user traffic.
- [ ] **Slant Range Formula:** $d = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_{E} r \cos\theta}$ — appears in 14-mark derivations.
- [ ] **Coverage Half-Angle for GEO:** $\gamma \approx 81.3^{\circ}$, covering ≈ 42% of Earth.
- [ ] **Bent-Pipe Definition:** Satellite is a transparent repeater — no baseband demodulation on traditional bent-pipe transponders.
- [ ] **EIRP Formula:** $EIRP_{dBW} = P_{TX,dBW} + G_{TX,dBi}$.
- [ ] **G/T Ratio:** $G/T = G_{RX,dBi} - 10 \log_{10} T_{sys}$ (in dB/K).
- [ ] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never claim "satellite has no delay" — GEO has 240 ms round-trip, which is the **biggest disadvantage**.
- [ ] **Application Examples:** INSAT/GSAT (India DTH), Inmarsat (maritime), Iridium (LEO telephony), GPS (MEO navigation), Starlink (LEO broadband).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Satellite Orbits, Space Segment - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Satellite Orbit?

A **satellite orbit** is the gravitationally curved path followed by an artificial satellite around the Earth, governed primarily by the balance between the satellite's tangential velocity and the Earth's gravitational pull. For a stable circular orbit, the centripetal force required to keep the satellite in its path is exactly provided by the gravitational attraction between the Earth and the satellite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A satellite orbit is a closed elliptical (or in the ideal case, circular) trajectory around a central body (Earth) in which a body repeatedly traces the same path under the influence of gravitational force and centripetal acceleration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble on a String" Intuition

Imagine you tie a small marble to a string and swing it in a horizontal circle above your head.

- **The string** = Earth's gravity (pulls the marble inward)
- **The swing (tangential motion)** = The satellite's orbital velocity (pushes the marble outward)
- **If you swing fast** → the circle becomes larger (higher orbit)
- **If you swing slow** → the circle collapses (the satellite falls back to Earth)
- **If you let go of the string** → the marble flies off in a straight line (escape velocity)

This is exactly what a satellite does — it is perpetually "falling" toward Earth but moving fast enough tangentially that it continuously misses the Earth's surface, tracing a curved path known as an **orbit**.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Satellite Orbits

Satellite orbits are classified based on two key parameters:
1. **Altitude** (distance from Earth's surface)
2. **Inclination** (angle between the orbital plane and the Earth's equatorial plane)

| Orbit Type | Standard Altitude (km) | Inclination | Example Application |
|------------|------------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **LEO** — Low Earth Orbit | 160 – 2,000 | Variable (often polar) | Earth observation, Starlink, ISS |
| **MEO** — Medium Earth Orbit | 5,000 – 25,000 | Variable | GPS (20,200 km), GLONASS |
| **GEO** — Geostationary Earth Orbit | **35,786** | **0° (equatorial)** | DBS TV, weather satellites |
| **HEO** — Highly Elliptical Orbit | Perigee ~500, Apogee ~40,000 | Highly inclined (~63.4°) | Molniya, Tundra (for polar coverage) |
| **Polar / Sun-Synchronous Orbit** | 600 – 800 | ~98° – 99° | Remote sensing, spy satellites |

## 1.4 The Space Segment — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> The **Space Segment** of a satellite communication system refers collectively to the satellite (or constellation of satellites) itself and the associated ground-based facilities that control and monitor it (Telemetry, Tracking, Command and Monitoring — TTC&M). The satellite is essentially a sophisticated RF repeater placed in orbit.

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of the Space Segment as a **giant mirror suspended in the sky**. It catches the faint signal thrown up from Earth (uplink), amplifies it, changes its frequency, and throws it back down to a wider area on Earth (downlink). The mirror itself is moving, so engineers had to choose its path (orbit) very carefully so that it stays "still" relative to users on the ground.

## 1.5 The Five Pillars of the Space Segment

1. **The Satellite (Bus + Payload)**
2. **Ground Control Station (TT&C)**
3. **Launch Vehicle Compatibility**
4. **Orbit Position and Frequency Allocation**
5. **Inter-Satellite Links (in modern constellations)**

The most critical standard metric in orbit selection is the **orbital period (T)**, which for a Geostationary satellite is exactly **86,400 seconds (one sidereal day)**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Different Orbits Exist — The Engineering Trade-off

Every orbit is a compromise between four engineering constraints:

- **Coverage Area** → Higher orbits cover larger ground areas but have weaker signals
- **Signal Delay** → Lower orbits have lower delays (good for voice/data), GEO has ~250 ms one-way
- **Path Loss** → Higher altitudes mean greater free-space path loss, requiring larger antennas
- **Launch Cost** → Higher orbits cost exponentially more to reach (Δv to GEO ≈ 11 km/s from LEO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why can't we use a single LEO satellite for global TV broadcasting?"* The expected answer: LEO satellites have small footprints, short visibility windows, and would require an impractically large constellation to provide 24/7 global coverage.

## 2.2 Detailed Characteristics of Each Orbit

### 2.2.1 LEO (Low Earth Orbit)
- Altitude: **160 km – 2,000 km**
- Orbital Period: ~88 – 127 minutes
- Signal Round-Trip Delay: **~5 – 20 ms** (excellent for real-time communication)
- Visibility: ~15 – 20 minutes per pass over a ground station
- Doppler shift: Significant (must be compensated)
- Examples: Iridium constellation (780 km), Starlink (550 km)

### 2.2.2 MEO (Medium Earth Orbit)
- Altitude: **5,000 km – 25,000 km**
- Orbital Period: ~2 – 12 hours
- Examples: GPS satellites at **20,200 km** (Period ≈ 11h 58m), GLONASS at 19,100 km
- Advantage over LEO: Fewer satellites needed for global coverage (24–30 satellites suffice for global navigation)
- Advantage over GEO: Lower path loss, lower delay, no inclined orbit issues for global coverage

### 2.2.3 GEO (Geostationary Earth Orbit)
- Altitude: **35,786 km** above equator
- Orbital Period: **23 hours 56 minutes 4 seconds (sidereal day)** — exactly matches Earth's rotation
- Inclination: 0° (must be in equatorial plane)
- Footprint: Covers **42.5% of Earth's surface** (one satellite can see ~1/3 of globe)
- Round-trip delay: ~240 ms (one-way ~120 ms) — too high for voice without echo cancellation
- Examples: INSAT, INTELSAT, Hughes, AsiaSat

### 2.2.4 HEO (Highly Elliptical Orbit)
- Highly elliptical with perigee ~500 km, apogee ~40,000 km
- Inclination ~63.4° (Molniya orbit — apogee dwells over high latitudes)
- Used to provide long coverage of polar regions where GEO fails
- Molniya orbit period: 12 hours, with ~8 hours of useful dwell time over high latitudes

### 2.2.5 Polar / Sun-Synchronous Orbit
- Inclination ~98° – 99° (near-polar)
- Passes over (or very near) both poles on every revolution
- Earth rotates beneath the orbit, allowing the satellite to scan the entire Earth surface over time
- Used for remote sensing (Landsat, NOAA, SPOT)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Newton's Gravitational Law | $F = \dfrac{GMm}{R^{2}}$ | Force between Earth (M) and satellite (m) at distance R from Earth's center |
| 2 | Equating Centripetal & Gravitational Force | $\dfrac{mv^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{GMm}{R^{2}}$ | Governing equation of circular orbital motion |
| 3 | **Orbital Velocity** | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{R}}$ | Tangential speed needed for circular orbit at radius R |
| 4 | **Orbital Period (Kepler's 3rd Law)** | $T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}}{GM}R^{3}$ | Universal law valid for all closed orbits |
| 5 | **Angular Velocity of Satellite** | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{R^{3}}}$ | Equal to Earth's rotation only for GEO |
| 6 | **Geostationary Radius** | $R_{GEO} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$ | Distance from Earth's center (~35,786 km from surface) |
| 7 | **Geostationary Altitude** | $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$ | Altitude above mean sea level |
| 8 | Free-Space Path Loss | $L_{FS} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}$ | dB form: $L_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ |
| 9 | **Round-Trip Time (GEO)** | $t_{rt} \approx 240 \text{ ms}$ | For uplink + downlink at GEO altitude |
| 10 | Satellite Footprint Radius (nadir angle θ) | $r_{foot} = R_{E} \sin \theta$ | Earth radius $R_{E} \approx 6{,}378$ km |
| 11 | Footprint Coverage Angle | $\cos \theta = \dfrac{R_{E}}{R_{E} + h}$ | Maximum half-angle of Earth seen from satellite |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use standard SI units. **GM (Earth's gravitational parameter) = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²** is a critical constant to memorize for KTU derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **GEO orbits** power the Direct-to-Home (DTH) television industry worth hundreds of billions of dollars globally (e.g., Tata Sky, Dish TV).
- **MEO orbits** form the backbone of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) — GPS, GLONASS, IRNSS/NavIC, Galileo.
- **LEO constellations** (Starlink, OneWeb, Project Kuiper) are revolutionizing global broadband with low-latency internet.
- **HEO/Molniya** orbits were historically used by Russia (Soviet Union) to cover its high-latitude territory where GEO satellites offer poor elevation angles.
- **Polar orbits** are essential for climate monitoring, weather forecasting, and military reconnaissance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Orbital Velocity of a Satellite

**Step 1: Identify the Two Forces**

For a satellite of mass $m$ moving in a circular orbit of radius $R$ around the Earth (mass $M$), two forces must be balanced:

- **Gravitational force (inward):** $F_{g} = \dfrac{GMm}{R^{2}}$
- **Centripetal force (inward, required for circular motion):** $F_{c} = \dfrac{mv^{2}}{R}$

**Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition**

For the satellite to maintain a stable circular orbit, these two forces must be exactly equal:

$$\dfrac{mv^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{GMm}{R^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the Equation**

The mass of the satellite $m$ cancels from both sides:

$$\dfrac{v^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{GM}{R^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $v$**

Multiply both sides by $R$:

$$v^{2} = \dfrac{GM}{R}$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{R}}$$

This is the **orbital velocity** of the satellite. Notice that heavier satellites do not need to move faster — the required velocity depends only on the orbit radius and Earth's mass.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kepler's Third Law (Orbital Period)

**Step 1: Use the Relationship Between Velocity and Period**

For a circular orbit, the satellite travels one full circumference $2\pi R$ in time $T$ (the orbital period):

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi R}{T}$$

**Step 2: Equate With the Orbital Velocity Formula**

$$\dfrac{2\pi R}{T} = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{R}}$$

**Step 3: Square Both Sides**

$$\dfrac{4\pi^{2} R^{2}}{T^{2}} = \dfrac{GM}{R}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T^{2}$**

$$T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} R^{2} \cdot R}{GM} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} R^{3}}{GM}$$

**Step 5: Final Form**

$$\boxed{T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}}{GM} \cdot R^{3}}$$

This is **Kepler's Third Law** of planetary motion, which holds for all gravitationally bound orbits in our solar system and beyond.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Geostationary Orbit Radius

**Step 1: Apply the GEO Condition**

A geostationary satellite must have an orbital period equal to Earth's sidereal rotation period:

$$T_{GEO} = 86{,}164 \text{ seconds} \approx 86{,}400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Into Kepler's Third Law**

$$T_{GEO}^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} R_{GEO}^{3}}{GM}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_{GEO}$**

$$R_{GEO}^{3} = \dfrac{GM \cdot T_{GEO}^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}$$

$$R_{GEO} = \left(\dfrac{GM \cdot T_{GEO}^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values**

- $GM = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$
- $T_{GEO} = 86{,}164 \text{ s}$
- $4\pi^{2} \approx 39.478$

$$R_{GEO}^{3} = \dfrac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (86{,}164)^{2}}{39.478}$$

$$R_{GEO}^{3} = \dfrac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (7.424 \times 10^{9})}{39.478}$$

$$R_{GEO}^{3} = \dfrac{2.959 \times 10^{24}}{39.478} = 7.495 \times 10^{22} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 5: Take Cube Root**

$$R_{GEO} = (7.495 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} \approx 4.2164 \times 10^{7} \text{ m} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Altitude Above Earth's Surface**

$$h_{GEO} = R_{GEO} - R_{E} = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Round-Trip Signal Delay to GEO

**Step 1: Geometry of the Uplink/Downlink**

The signal travels a one-way distance of $d = R_{GEO} - R_{E} = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$ at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 2: Calculate One-Way Delay**

$$t_{one-way} = \dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{35{,}786 \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.1193 \text{ s} \approx 119 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Round-Trip Delay**

For a complete round trip (uplink + downlink), the signal traverses the distance twice:

$$t_{rt} = 2 \cdot t_{one-way} = 2 \times 119 \text{ ms} \approx 240 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 240 ms delay is the fundamental reason why GEO satellites are unsuitable for real-time voice communication without sophisticated **echo cancellation** algorithms.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Free-Space Path Loss at GEO for a 6 GHz Uplink

**Step 1: Use the Free-Space Path Loss Formula (dB form)**

$$L_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

**Step 2: Substitute $d = 35{,}786$ km and $f = 6{,}000$ MHz**

$$L_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(35{,}786) + 20\log_{10}(6{,}000)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate Logarithms**

- $20 \log_{10}(35{,}786) = 20 \times 4.5536 = 91.07 \text{ dB}$
- $20 \log_{10}(6{,}000) = 20 \times 3.7782 = 75.56 \text{ dB}$

**Step 4: Sum the Components**

$$L_{FS} = 32.45 + 91.07 + 75.56 = 199.08 \text{ dB}$$

This is an enormous path loss — nearly **200 dB** — which is why satellite ground stations need high-power transmitters and very sensitive receivers with large parabolic antennas.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Orbit Types

| Parameter | LEO | MEO | GEO | HEO (Molniya) |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|----------------|
| **Altitude (km)** | 160 – 2,000 | 5,000 – 25,000 | 35,786 | 500 – 40,000 |
| **Orbital Period** | 88 – 127 min | 2 – 12 h | 23h 56m 4s | ~12 h |
| **Footprint (diameter)** | ~100 – 1,000 km | ~3,000 – 8,000 km | ~17,000 km | Variable |
| **Round-Trip Delay** | 5 – 20 ms | 50 – 100 ms | ~240 ms | Variable |
| **Free-Space Path Loss (4 GHz)** | ~135 – 155 dB | ~165 – 180 dB | ~196 dB | Variable |
| **Satellites for Global Coverage** | 40 – 800+ | 10 – 30 | 3 | 3 |
| **Doppler Shift** | High | Moderate | Negligible | Moderate-High |
| **Orbital Inclination Required** | Variable | Variable | **Must be 0°** | ~63.4° |
| **Launch Cost (relative)** | 1× | 2× – 3× | 5× – 10× | 3× – 5× |
| **Best Application** | Broadband LEO, Earth observation | GNSS / Navigation | DTH TV, broadcasting | Polar-region coverage |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a geostationary satellite orbit. List any two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO)** is a circular, equatorial orbit at an altitude of approximately **35,786 km** above the Earth's surface, in which a satellite has an orbital period equal to the Earth's sidereal rotation period (**23 hours, 56 minutes, 4 seconds**). As a result, the satellite appears to remain stationary relative to a fixed point on Earth.

**Key characteristics (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Orbital period equals Earth's rotation period**, making the satellite appear stationary to a ground observer.
2. **Orbital inclination must be exactly 0°** (it lies in the equatorial plane).
3. **Footprint covers ~42.5% of Earth's surface**, enabling one satellite to serve nearly an entire hemisphere.
4. **Round-trip signal delay is ~240 ms**, requiring echo cancellation for voice services.
