## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, derive the expression for the orbital period of a satellite orbiting in a circular path around the Earth. Hence calculate the orbital period for a satellite at an altitude equal to the Earth's radius. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the three main classifications of satellite orbits (LEO, MEO, GEO) and list two practical applications for each. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the equilibrium condition. For a satellite of mass $m$ at distance $R$ from Earth's center, gravitational force equals centripetal force:

$$\dfrac{GMm}{R^{2}} = \dfrac{mv^{2}}{R}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Solve for orbital velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{R}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Express velocity in terms of orbital period $T$ (distance = circumference / time):

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi R}{T}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Equate and solve for $T$:

$$\dfrac{2\pi R}{T} = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{R}} \implies T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} R^{3}}{GM}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R^{3}}{GM}}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Numerical calculation. Given altitude $h = R_{E} = 6378$ km, orbital radius $R = R_{E} + h = 2R_{E} = 12{,}756 \text{ km} = 1.2756 \times 10^{7}$ m.

Substitute $GM = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{(1.2756 \times 10^{7})^{3}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2.076 \times 10^{21}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{5.208 \times 10^{6}} = 2\pi \times 2282.1$$

$$T \approx 14{,}340 \text{ s} \approx 3 \text{ hours } 59 \text{ minutes}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**1 mark each for describing the three orbits (3 marks total):**

- **LEO (Low Earth Orbit):** Altitude 160–2,000 km. Low propagation delay (~5–20 ms), small footprint, short orbital period. **Examples:** ISS (400 km), Iridium (780 km), Starlink (550 km).

- **MEO (Medium Earth Orbit):** Altitude 5,000–25,000 km. Compromise between LEO and GEO. **Examples:** GPS (20,200 km), GLONASS (19,100 km), Galileo (23,222 km).

- **GEO (Geostationary Earth Orbit):** Altitude 35,786 km in equatorial plane, period equal to Earth's rotation. **Examples:** INSAT, INTELSAT, AsiaSat.

**0.5 marks each for two applications per orbit (4 marks total):**

| Orbit | Applications (Any two) |
|-------|------------------------|
| LEO | Earth imaging, broadband internet constellations (Starlink), mobile satellite services (Iridium) |
| MEO | GPS navigation, GLONASS positioning, satellite-based time synchronization |
| GEO | Direct-to-Home TV, weather forecasting, long-distance telecommunications |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Define the term "Space Segment" in satellite communication. With the help of a block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a communication satellite. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the orbital radius of a geostationary satellite. Hence find the round-trip propagation delay for a voice signal transmitted through a GEO satellite. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**

The **Space Segment** of a satellite communication system comprises the satellite(s) in orbit and the associated ground-based control facilities. The satellite itself is a complex platform consisting of a **bus** (support subsystems) and a **payload** (communication transponders). The space segment works in conjunction with the **Ground Segment** (user terminals and gateways) and the **Control Segment** (TT&C stations).

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** See the Mermaid architecture diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Major Subsystems (3 marks — 0.5 marks each):**

1. **Power Subsystem** — Solar arrays (GaAs or silicon cells) generate electricity, with NiH₂ or Li-ion batteries providing power during eclipse.
2. **Antenna Subsystem** — Horn antennas, parabolic reflectors, beam-shaping networks for transmit/receive.
3. **Transponder** — The communication payload: receives the uplink signal, amplifies (LNA + TWTA), down-converts the frequency, amplifies again, and re-radiates as downlink.
4. **TT&C Subsystem** — Telemetry (health monitoring), Tracking (orbit determination), Command (instructions from ground).
5. **Thermal Control** — Maintains operating temperature using passive (insulation, radiators) and active (heaters) techniques.
6. **Attitude & Orbit Control (AOCS)** — Stabilizes the satellite (spin-stabilized or 3-axis stabilized) and maintains correct pointing using thrusters and reaction wheels.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Derivation of orbital radius (4 marks):**

A geostationary satellite must have an orbital period equal to Earth's sidereal rotation:

$$T = 86{,}164 \text{ s}$$

By Kepler's Third Law:

$$T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} R^{3}}{GM}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \left(\dfrac{GM T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Substitution (1.5 marks):**

$$R = \left(\dfrac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times (86{,}164)^{2}}{39.478}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$R = \left(7.495 \times 10^{22}\right)^{1/3} \approx 4.216 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}$$

$$R_{GEO} \approx 42{,}164 \text{ km from Earth's center}$$

$$h_{GEO} = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km above surface}$$

**Round-trip delay calculation (1.5 marks):**

One-way distance from Earth's surface to GEO satellite ≈ 35,786 km.

$$t_{one-way} = \dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{35{,}786 \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.119 \text{ s} = 119 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_{round-trip} = 2 \times 119 = 238 \approx 240 \text{ ms}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing radius vs. altitude:** Examiners often deduct **1 full mark** if a student writes the orbital radius as the altitude. Always distinguish: $R$ = distance from Earth's **center**, $h$ = distance from Earth's **surface**.
> 2. **Sidereal vs. solar day:** Using the 24-hour solar day instead of 23h 56m 4s sidereal day will give the wrong altitude by about 35 km. Examiners expect the **sidereal** value for true geostationary.
> 3. **Unit slip-ups:** Forgetting to convert km to m (or vice versa) in Kepler's law calculations is the single most common cause of numerical errors. Always cross-check by writing units in every step.
> 4. **Path loss confusion:** Examiners commonly see students writing 199 dB as the answer for GEO but failing to specify the frequency (6 GHz). Always state the operating frequency in such questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LEO** is closest to Earth (160–2,000 km) — best for low-latency broadband and imaging.
- **MEO** is the middle ground (5,000–25,000 km) — home of all GNSS systems (GPS at 20,200 km).
- **GEO** is the highest practical orbit (35,786 km) — only 3 satellites can cover the entire Earth.
- **HEO (Molniya)** uses 63.4° inclination to provide high-latitude coverage.
- **Polar/SSO** orbits (~98° inclination) are essential for Earth observation and remote sensing.
- A geostationary satellite must have **zero inclination**, **zero eccentricity**, and an **orbital period of 23h 56m 4s**.
- The fundamental balance equation $\dfrac{mv^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{GMm}{R^{2}}$ is the basis of all orbital mechanics derivations.
- **Kepler's Third Law** ($T^{2} \propto R^{3}$) is the most frequently tested relationship in KTU examinations.
- The round-trip delay of **~240 ms** is the defining limitation of GEO systems for voice traffic.
- Free-space path loss at GEO at 6 GHz is approximately **~199 dB** — driving the need for high-gain antennas and high-power amplifiers.
- The **Space Segment** consists of a satellite with subsystems: Power, Thermal, AOCS, TT&C, Propulsion, Antenna, and the **Transponder payload**.
- **Memorize the value:** $GM = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$ — it is the only constant you will need for nearly all numerical KTU problems.
- **Earth's radius** for orbital calculations = **6,378 km** (mean sea level).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Supply** is an electronic subsystem that converts the available raw electrical energy from a source (typically the **230 V, 50 Hz** AC mains) into a stable, regulated form of DC voltage/current suitable for energising electronic circuits, communication transceivers, and signal-processing stages.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, a *DC Power Supply* is the cascade of four functional blocks: **Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator**. The combined assembly is formally termed a **Linear Regulated DC Power Supply (LRPS)** when the regulation element is a dissipative (series-pass) device, in contrast to a **Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS)** which uses high-frequency chopping for energy conversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (PEERT411 – Module 3):** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically demands study of *rectifier circuits (HWR, FWR, Bridge), passive filters, discrete and IC-based voltage regulators, and the qualitative treatment of SMPS.* All sub-topics below are mapped to this anchor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a power supply as a **water treatment plant** for your electronics:

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | Electronic Function |
|---|---|---|
| Mains AC inlet | Muddy river water | Raw **230 V AC, 50 Hz** supply |
| Transformer | Coarse strainer | Steps voltage down to required level and isolates the load |
| Rectifier | One-way valve | Allows current to flow in only one direction (AC → pulsating DC) |
| Filter | Settling tank | Smooths the pulsations to a near-steady DC level |
| Regulator | Pressure controller | Holds the DC output *constant* against load/input changes |

After the regulator stage, you get a *clean, steady stream* — a DC voltage that your radio receiver, op-amp, or microcontroller can safely drink.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are **assumed standard** in KTU numerical problems unless explicitly stated otherwise:

- **Mains RMS Voltage: V\_RMS = 230 V**
- **Mains Frequency: f = 50 Hz**
- **Peak Mains Voltage: V\_m = √2 × 230 ≈ 325.27 V**
- **Diode forward drop (Silicon): V\_D ≈ 0.7 V**
- **Standard IC regulator outputs: +5 V, +6 V, +9 V, +12 V, +15 V, −5 V, −12 V, −15 V**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For numerical problems involving rectifiers, KTU examiners expect students to **neglect the diode drop** unless the question explicitly states "with diode drop of 0.7 V". Always read the question stem carefully.

## 1.4 Functional Block Diagram of a Linear DC Power Supply

A regulated DC power supply consists of the following cascaded stages, each performing a distinct energy-conversion function:

**Mains AC (230 V, 50 Hz) → Step-Down Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Voltage Regulator → Regulated DC Output (V\_o)**

This block-level chain is the **single most important diagram** for the KTU Module 3 question bank. A detailed Mermaid schematic is provided in Section 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Step-Down Transformer

The **step-down transformer** reduces the mains AC voltage to the level required by the rectifier. It provides:
- **Galvanic isolation** between the mains and the load (safety).
- Voltage transformation ratio: **N₂/N₁ = V₂/V₁** (ideal transformer).

The secondary voltage is denoted **V\_s = V\_m sin(ωt)**, where **V\_m** is the peak secondary voltage.

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Rectifier Circuits

A rectifier converts bidirectional AC into unidirectional pulsating DC. The three KTU-relevant topologies are:

### 2.2.1 Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)
- Conducts during **only the positive half-cycle** of the input.
- One diode, one transformer secondary.
- Frequency of output ripple = **f** (line frequency).

### 2.2.2 Full-Wave Centre-Tapped Rectifier (FWR-CT)
- Uses **two diodes** and a centre-tapped transformer.
- Each diode conducts on alternate half-cycles.
- Frequency of output ripple = **2f**.
- Each diode must block **2 V\_m** during the off half-cycle.

### 2.2.3 Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (FWR-Bridge)
- Uses **four diodes** in a bridge configuration.
- No centre-tap required — full secondary voltage is utilised.
- Frequency of output ripple = **2f**.
- Each diode must block **V\_m** only (lower PIV requirement).

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Filters

A filter removes the AC ripple component from the rectified output, leaving a smooth DC. The KTU syllabus focuses on:

- **Capacitor filter** (most common in low-current supplies)
- **Inductor filter** (used in high-current supplies)
- **LC (L-section) filter** and **CLC (π-section) filter** for better ripple attenuation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A capacitor filter is placed in **parallel** with the load (it stores charge and supplies the load when the rectifier output falls). An inductor filter is placed in **series** with the load (it opposes changes in current).

## 2.4 Stage 4 — Voltage Regulators

A regulator maintains **V\_out constant** despite variations in:
- Input voltage (line regulation)
- Load current (load regulation)

KTU-relevant types:
- **Zener diode shunt regulator** (discrete, low-power)
- **Series-pass regulator** (discrete, using BJT/MOSFET)
- **Three-terminal IC regulators (78xx, 79xx, LM317)**
- **SMPS** (qualitative — switching at high frequency, 20 kHz – 1 MHz)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this table:** All quantities below refer to a *resistive load R\_L* with an *ideal diode* (V\_D = 0) unless stated otherwise. The symbol **V\_m** denotes the peak secondary voltage of the transformer, and **f** is the line frequency (50 Hz in India).

| # | Parameter | Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR) | Full-Wave Rectifier (FWR) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC output voltage, V\_DC | $\frac{V\_m}{\pi}$ | $\frac{2V\_m}{\pi}$ | V |
| 2 | RMS output voltage, V\_RMS | $\frac{V\_m}{2}$ | $\frac{V\_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | V |
| 3 | RMS AC ripple voltage, V\_r(ac) | $\sqrt{V\_RMS^2 - V\_DC^2}$ | same expression | V |
| 4 | Ripple factor, $\gamma$ | **1.21** | **0.482** | unitless |
| 5 | Rectification efficiency, $\eta$ | **40.6 %** | **81.2 %** | % |
| 6 | Transformer Utilisation Factor (TUF) | 0.287 | 0.693 (CT) / 0.810 (Bridge) | unitless |
| 7 | Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) | V\_m | 2 V\_m (CT) \vert V\_m (Bridge) | V |
| 8 | Output ripple frequency | f | 2f | Hz |
| 9 | Number of diodes | 1 | 2 (CT) \vert 4 (Bridge) | — |
| 10 | Form factor, F | 1.57 | 1.11 | unitless |
| 11 | Peak factor | 2 | √2 | unitless |

### 2.5.1 Filter & Regulator Key Equations

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V\_{DC} \approx V\_m - \dfrac{I\_{DC}}{2fC}$ | DC voltage with capacitor filter (FWR) |
| 2 | $V\_{r(pp)} = \dfrac{I\_{DC}}{fC}$ | Peak-to-peak ripple voltage (FWR) |
| 3 | $\% \text{Regulation} = \dfrac{V\_{NL} - V\_{FL}}{V\_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation percentage |
| 4 | $I\_L = I\_S - I\_Z$ | KCL at Zener regulator node |
| 5 | $V\_o = V\_{ref}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I\_{adj} R\_2$ | LM317 adjustable regulator output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ripple factor **γ = 1.21** for HWR and **0.482** for FWR are *fixed numerical values* and should be memorised. KTU short-answer questions frequently test these directly for **3 marks**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Communication Engineering:** The local oscillator, mixer, and IF stages of a superheterodyne receiver all need a noise-free DC rail. A poorly designed power supply introduces **hum** (50 Hz/100 Hz buzz) that corrupts the audio output.
- **Industrial applications:** SMPS units power computers, LED drivers, and DC motors where efficiency > 80 % is mandatory.
- **Battery chargers:** A regulated DC supply with constant-current/constant-voltage (CC/CV) profile charges Li-ion cells safely.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the *complete, exhaustive* mathematical derivations for the key KTU Module 3 formulas. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: DC Output Voltage of a Half-Wave Rectifier

**Given:** Input to rectifier: $v\_i(t) = V\_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$, and zero for $\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$.

**Step 1 — Definition of average (DC) value:**

The average value over one full period T is the area under one rectified cycle divided by the period.

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v\_o(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} V\_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 2 — Split the integral (conducting + non-conducting half):**

Since the diode conducts only for $0 \le \theta \le \pi$ and the output is zero for $\pi \le \theta \le 2\pi$:

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} V\_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} 0\, d\theta\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the non-zero integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_{0}^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) = -(-1) + 1 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[V\_m \times 2\right] = \frac{V\_m}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\;V\_{DC,\,HWR} = \frac{V\_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\, V\_m\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of a Half-Wave Rectifier

**Step 1 — Definition of RMS:**

$$V\_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v\_o^2(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the half-wave output:**

$$V\_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V\_m^2 \sin^2(\theta)\, d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:**

$$V\_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{V\_m^2}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}\, d\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}\, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\left[\theta - \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\pi - 0 - 0 + 0\right] = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$V\_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{V\_m^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{V\_m^2}{4}} = \frac{V\_m}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;V\_{RMS,\,HWR} = \frac{V\_m}{2}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ripple Factor of a Half-Wave Rectifier

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Ripple factor is the ratio of the RMS value of the AC (ripple) component to the DC value:

$$\gamma = \frac{V\_{r(ac)}}{V\_{DC}}$$

**Step 2 — RMS ripple voltage from the identity $V\_{RMS}^2 = V\_{DC}^2 + V\_{r(ac)}^2$:**

$$V\_{r(ac)} = \sqrt{V\_{RMS}^2 - V\_{DC}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the HWR results from Sections 3.1 and 3.2:**

$$V\_{r(ac)} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V\_m}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{V\_m}{\pi}\right)^2} = V\_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{\pi^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerically:**

$$\frac{1}{4} = 0.2500, \quad \frac{1}{\pi^2} = \frac{1}{9.8696} \approx 0.1013$$

$$V\_{r(ac)} = V\_m \sqrt{0.2500 - 0.1013} = V\_m \sqrt{0.1487} \approx 0.3854\, V\_m$$

**Step 5 — Compute ripple factor:**

$$\gamma = \frac{V\_{r(ac)}}{V\_{DC}} = \frac{0.3854\, V\_m}{0.3183\, V\_m} \approx 1.211$$

$$\boxed{\;\gamma\_{HWR} \approx 1.21\;}$$

This confirms the value in the formula table. The ripple factor of an HWR is **1.21**, meaning the AC ripple content is **121 % of the DC level** — a very poor supply quality.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: DC Output Voltage of a Full-Wave Rectifier

**Step 1 — Output of FWR (rectified on both half-cycles):**

$$v\_o(t) = V\_m \sin(\theta) \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le \theta \le \pi$$

$$v\_o(t) = V\_m \sin(\theta - \pi) \cdot (-1) = V\_m \vert\sin(\theta)\vert \quad \text{over the full period}$$

**Step 2 — Average over the full period (or equivalently, over half a period multiplied by 2):**

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V\_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = 2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{1}{\pi} \times V\_m \times 2 = \frac{2 V\_m}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\;V\_{DC,\,FWR} = \frac{2V\_m}{\pi} \approx 0.636\, V\_m\;}$$

Note: This is **exactly double** the HWR value — a direct consequence of using both half-cycles.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Rectification Efficiency of FWR

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Rectification efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of DC output power to AC input power (both delivered to a resistive load $R\_L$).

$$\eta = \frac{P\_{DC}}{P\_{AC}} = \frac{V\_{DC}^2 / R\_L}{V\_{RMS}^2 / R\_L} = \left(\frac{V\_{DC}}{V\_{RMS}}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the FWR values:**

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{2V\_m}{\pi}, \qquad V\_{RMS} = \frac{V\_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\eta = \left(\frac{2V\_m / \pi}{V\_m / \sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\right)^2 = \frac{8}{\pi^2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical value:**

$$\eta = \frac{8}{9.8696} \approx 0.8104 = 81.04\,\% \approx 81.2\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta\_{FWR} \approx 81.2\,\%\;}$$

This is the **theoretical maximum** for an ideal linear rectifier with resistive load.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Ripple Voltage Across a Capacitor Filter (Full-Wave)

**Scenario:** A full-wave rectifier with a capacitor C feeds a DC load current I\_DC. The capacitor charges to V\_m on each peak and discharges through R\_L between peaks.

**Step 1 — Time between successive peaks:**

For a full-wave rectifier, the ripple frequency is 2f, so the time between charging pulses is:

$$T' = \frac{1}{2f}$$

**Step 2 — Charge lost by capacitor during discharge:**

The charge delivered to the load during one discharge interval is:

$$\Delta Q = I\_{DC} \times T' = \frac{I\_{DC}}{2f}$$

**Step 3 — Corresponding drop in capacitor voltage:**

Since $Q = CV$, the voltage drop is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{\Delta Q}{C} = \frac{I\_{DC}}{2fC}$$

**Step 4 — This drop is the peak-to-peak ripple voltage:**

$$V\_{r(pp)} = \frac{I\_{DC}}{2fC}$$

$$\boxed{\;V\_{r(pp)} = \frac{I\_{DC}}{2fC}\; \text{ (Full-Wave, Capacitor Filter)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a half-wave rectifier, the discharge interval is the full period T = 1/f, so the ripple is **twice as large**: $V\_{r(pp)} = \frac{I\_{DC}}{fC}$. This is yet another reason why FWRs are preferred in practice.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Rectifier Topologies

| Performance Criterion | Half-Wave | Full-Wave (CT) | Full-Wave (Bridge) | Best Topology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rectification efficiency | 40.6 % | 81.2 % | 81.2 % | FWR |
| Ripple factor | 1.21 | 0.48 | 0.48 | FWR |
| TUF | 0.287 | 0.693 | **0.810** | Bridge |
| PIV per diode | V\_m | 2 V\_m | V\_m | HWR / Bridge |
| Transformer utilisation | Poor (only half the AC cycle used) | Better | Best (no CT needed) | Bridge |
| Cost & complexity | Lowest | Medium (needs CT) | Highest (4 diodes) | HWR (cheapest) |
| Output DC voltage | 0.318 V\_m | 0.636 V\_m | 0.636 V\_m | FWR |
| Output ripple frequency | 50 Hz | 100 Hz | 100 Hz | FWR (easier to filter) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **modern commercial DC adapters**, the **bridge rectifier is almost universal** because it needs no centre-tap transformer, has the highest TUF (0.81), and uses diodes with a lower PIV rating. Centre-tapped transformers are now rare in low-voltage supplies.

---

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A full-wave bridge rectifier is supplied from a 230 V, 50 Hz mains through a step-down transformer with turns ratio 10:1. The load resistance is R\_L = 100 Ω. Compute (a) V\_DC, (b) V\_RMS, (c) ripple factor, and (d) rectification efficiency. Assume ideal diodes.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute secondary RMS voltage:**

$$V\_{s(RMS)} = \frac{230}{10} = 23\text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute peak secondary voltage:**

$$V\_m = \sqrt{2} \times 23 \approx 32.527\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — DC output voltage:**

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{2 V\_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 32.527}{3.1416} \approx 20.71\text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — RMS output voltage:**

$$V\_{RMS} = \frac{V\_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{32.527}{1.4142} \approx 23.00\text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Ripple factor (using identity):**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V\_{RMS}}{V\_{DC}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{23.00}{20.71}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.2338 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2338} \approx 0.483$$

**Step 6 — Rectification efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{V\_{DC}^2}{V\_{RMS}^2} \times 100 = \left(\frac{20.71}{23.00}\right)^2 \times 100 \approx 81.0\,\%$$

All four answers match the theoretical predictions for an ideal FWR.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid** block diagrams. The block-level architecture maps every functional interaction of a regulated DC power supply, from the AC mains to the regulated DC output. This is the **canonical KTU Module 3 figure** that examiners expect students to reproduce.

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Regulated Linear DC Power Supply

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mainsIn["AC Mains 230V 50Hz"]:::mains
    stepXformer["Step Down Transformer"]:::xform
    rectBlock["Rectifier Stage HWR or FWR or Bridge"]:::rect
    filtBlock["Filter Stage C or L or LC or Pi"]:::filt
    regBlock["Voltage Regulator Zener or Series or 78xx IC"]:::reg
    dcOut["Regulated DC Output to Load RL"]:::load
    loadDev["Electronic Load Receiver or Amplifier"]:::dev

    mainsIn -- "V in 230V RMS" --> stepXformer
    stepXformer -- "V s reduced AC" --> rectBlock
    rectBlock -- "Pulsating DC" --> filtBlock
    filtBlock -- "Rippled DC" --> regBlock
    regBlock -- "V o constant DC" --> dcOut
    dcOut --> loadDev

    classDef mains fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef xform fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rect fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef filt fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The colour-coded stages are:
> - **Beige** — AC source
> - **Light blue** — Transformer (isolation + level shift)
> - **Pink** — Rectifier (AC → pulsating DC)
> - **Light green** — Filter (pulsating DC → smooth DC)
> - **Plum** — Regulator (smooth DC → regulated DC)
> - **Khaki** — Output node
> - **Lavender** — Load device

## 4.2 Bridge Rectifier — Diode Conduction Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    acTop["AC Input Top"]:::node
    acBot["AC Input Bottom"]:::node
    d1["D1 Conducts Positive Half"]:::diodeOn
    d2["D2 Conducts Positive Half"]:::diodeOn
    d3["D3 Conducts Negative Half"]:::diodeOff
    d4["D4 Conducts Negative Half"]:::diodeOff
    posOut["DC Positive Output"]:::posOut
    negOut["DC Negative Output"]:::negOut
    rl["Load Resistor RL"]:::load

    acTop -- "during +ve half" --> d1
    d1 --> posOut
    posOut --> rl
    rl --> negOut
    negOut -- "during +ve half" --> d3
    d3 --> acBot

    acTop -- "during -ve half" --> d4
    d4 --> negOut
    negOut --> rl
    rl --> posOut
    posOut -- "during -ve half" --> d2
    d2 --> acBot

    classDef node fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    classDef diodeOn fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef diodeOff fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef posOut fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    classDef negOut fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:3px
    classDef load fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **bridge rectifier**:
> - **D1 and D2** conduct during the positive half-cycle (highlighted in green).
> - **D3 and D4** conduct during the negative half-cycle (highlighted in pink).
> - The DC output appears across the bridge diagonal; polarity is fixed regardless of input polarity.

## 4.3 Zener Diode Shunt Regulator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vinNode["Vin Unregulated DC from Filter"]:::inNode
    rsNode["Series Resistor Rs Limits Current"]:::seriesR
    zenerNode["Zener Diode Dz in Reverse Bias Vz Constant"]:::zener
    voutNode["Vout Regulated DC equals Vz"]:::outNode
    rlNode["Load Resistor RL"]:::loadR

    vinNode -- "I s flows through Rs" --> rsNode
    rsNode -- "I s splits" --> zenerNode
    rsNode -- "I s splits" --> voutNode
    zenerNode -. "I z through Zener" .-> voutNode
    voutNode --> rlNode
    rlNode -. "I L to load" .-> voutNode

    classDef inNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef seriesR fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef zener fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef loadR fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px
```

**Node behaviour summary:**

| Node ID | Function | KCL/KVL relation |
|---|---|---|
| vinNode | Unregulated DC input from filter | — |
| rsNode | Series current-limiting resistor | $V\_{Rs} = I\_s R\_s$ |
| zenerNode | Zener diode (operates in breakdown) | $V\_{out} = V\_Z$ |
| voutNode | Regulated output node | $V\_{out} = V\_{in} - I\_s R\_s$ |
| rlNode | Load | $I\_L = V\_{out} / R\_L$ |

**Critical KCL at the output node:**

$$I\_s = I\_Z + I\_L$$

This single equation is the basis of **all Zener regulator design** problems in KTU exams.

## 4.4 Three-Terminal IC Regulator (78xx) — Pin Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pin1["Pin 1 INPUT Unregulated DC from Filter"]:::inPin
    pin2["Pin 2 GROUND Common Return"]:::gndPin
    pin3["Pin 3 OUTPUT Regulated Vout e.g. 5V 12V 15V"]:::outPin
    cap1["Cin 0.33uF Bypass at Input"]:::capin
    cap2["Cout 0.1uF at Output for Stability"]:::capout

    pin1 -- "Vin" --> cap1
    cap1 --> pin1
    pin3 -- "Vout" --> cap2
    cap2 --> pin3

    classDef inPin fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gndPin fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outPin fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef capin fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef capout fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **78xx** family is the *fixed positive* regulator. The "**xx**" denotes the output voltage: **7805 → +5 V**, **7812 → +12 V**, **7815 → +15 V**. The **79xx** family provides *fixed negative* outputs (**7905 → −5 V**, **7912 → −12 V**). The **LM317** is an *adjustable* positive regulator whose output is set by an external resistor divider.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section contains exam-pattern questions modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** End-Semester Examination (ESE) format. Every question is tagged with a simulated past-year marker.

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define ripple factor. State the values of ripple factor for a half-wave and a full-wave rectifier.**

**Model Answer:**

Ripple factor is the quantitative measure of the residual AC (ripple) content present in the rectified DC output, defined as the ratio of the RMS value of the AC component to the DC (average) component.

$$\gamma = \frac{V\_{r(ac)\,(rms)}}{V\_{DC}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V\_{RMS}}{V\_{DC}}\right)^2 - 1}$$

Standard values:
- Half-wave rectifier: **γ = 1.21**
- Full-wave rectifier: **γ = 0.482**

A lower ripple factor indicates a smoother DC output and hence a better-quality power supply.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List the functional blocks of a regulated DC power supply. State the purpose of each block.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Block | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step-down Transformer | Reduces 230 V AC to required lower AC level; provides isolation |
| 2 | Rectifier | Converts AC to pulsating DC (HWR/FWR/Bridge) |
| 3 | Filter | Smooths pulsating DC by reducing AC ripple |
| 4 | Voltage Regulator | Maintains constant DC output against line and load variations |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (ESE Module Choice):** The examiner will pose a 14-mark question with internal choice. Below are **two independent alternatives (A and B)** to be attempted; each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks across two sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a full-wave **bridge rectifier**. Draw the input and output waveforms. Derive expressions for V\_DC, V\_RMS, and ripple factor.

**(b)** A bridge rectifier is fed from a transformer with secondary RMS voltage of **30 V** at **50 Hz**. It supplies a load of R\_L = **500 Ω** with a smoothing capacitor of **C = 1000 µF**. Calculate the **DC output voltage** and the **peak-to-peak ripple voltage**. Assume ideal diodes.

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Circuit description (2 marks):**

A bridge rectifier consists of **four diodes** (D1, D2, D3, D4) connected in a closed-loop bridge. The AC input is applied across one diagonal of the bridge, and the DC output is taken across the other diagonal. A load resistor R\_L is connected across the output.

**Working (3 marks):**

- **Positive half-cycle:** D1 and D2 are forward-biased and conduct; D3 and D4 are reverse-biased. Current flows from the top of the secondary → D1 → R\_L (top to bottom) → D2 → back to the bottom of the secondary. The output across R\_L is positive.
- **Negative half-cycle:** D3 and D4 are forward-biased and conduct; D1 and D2 are reverse-biased. Current flows from the bottom of the secondary → D3 → R\_L (top to bottom) → D4 → back to the top of the secondary. The output across R\_L is again positive.
- **Net effect:** Current through R\_L is unidirectional in both half-cycles — a full-wave rectified output.

**Waveforms (1 mark):**

- Input: sinusoidal AC v\_s = V\_m sin(ωt)
- Output: full-wave rectified |\sin(ωt)| — unidirectional pulsating DC

**Derivations (1 mark):**

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{2V\_m}{\pi}, \quad V\_{RMS} = \frac{V\_m}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \gamma = 0.482$$

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute V\_m (1 mark):**

$$V\_m = \sqrt{2} \times 30 = 42.43\text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — DC output voltage with capacitor filter (2 marks):**

$$V\_{DC} \approx V\_m - \frac{I\_{DC}}{2fC}$$

We need the DC load current. Approximating $I\_{DC} = V\_{DC} / R\_L$:

$$V\_{DC} = V\_m - \frac{V\_{DC}}{2fC R\_L}$$

$$V\_{DC}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2fC R\_L}\right) = V\_m$$

**Step 3 — Compute 2fCR\_L (1 mark):**

$$2fC R\_L = 2 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 = 50$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V\_DC (2 marks):**

$$V\_{DC}\left(1 + \frac{1}{50}\right) = 42.43$$

$$V\_{DC} = \frac{42.43}{1.02} \approx 41.6\text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute ripple voltage (1 mark):**

$$V\_{r(pp)} = \frac{I\_{DC}}{2fC} = \frac{V\_{DC}/R\_L}{2fC} = \frac{41.6/500}{2 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.0832}{0.1} = 0.832\text{ V}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$V\_{DC} \approx 41.6\text{ V}, \quad V\_{r(pp)} \approx 0.83\text{ V}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **Zener diode shunt voltage regulator** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the **series resistance R\_S** required to maintain regulation.

**(b)** A Zener diode with V\_Z = **12 V** and I\_Z(min) = **5 mA**, I\_Z(max) = **50 mA** is used to regulate the output of a bridge rectifier. The unregulated input varies between **V\_in(min) = 15 V** and **V\_in(max) = 20 V**. The load current varies between **I\_L(min) = 10 mA** and **I\_L(max) = 60 mA**. Determine the value of the series resistor R\_S.

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Circuit description (1 mark):**

A Zener diode is connected in **reverse bias** across the load. A series resistor R\_S is connected between the unregulated DC input V\_in and the output node V\_out. The Zener operates in its breakdown region, holding V\_out = V\_Z constant.

**KCL at the output node (2 marks):**

The current through R\_S splits into the Zener current I\_Z and the load current I\_L:

$$I\_S = I\_Z + I\_L$$

**KVL around the input loop (2 marks):**

$$V\_{in} = I\_S R\_S + V\_Z$$

**Derivation of R\_S (2 marks):**

$$I\_S = \frac{V\_{in} - V\_Z}{R\_S}$$

To ensure regulation under **all** operating conditions, R\_S must be chosen such that the Zener stays in breakdown even when I\_L is maximum and V\_in is minimum:

$$R\_{S(max)} = \frac{V\_{in(min)} - V\_Z}{I\_{Z(min)} + I\_{L(max)}}$$

And R\_S must not be so small that I\_Z exceeds I\_Z(max) when I\_L is minimum and V\_in is maximum:

$$R\_{S(min)} = \frac{V\_{in(max)} - V\_Z}{I\_{Z(max)} + I\_{L(min)}}$$

A value of R\_S between R\_S(min) and R\_S(max) ensures proper regulation.

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Given data (1 mark):**

$V\_Z = 12\text{ V}$, $I\_{Z(min)} = 5\text{ mA}$, $I\_{Z(max)} = 50\text{ mA}$, $V\_{in(min)} = 15\text{ V}$, $V\_{in(max)} = 20\text{ V}$, $I\_{L(min)} = 10\text{ mA}$, $I\_{L(max)} = 60\text{ mA}$.

**Step 1 — Compute R\_S(max) (3 marks):**

$$R\_{S(max)} = \frac{V\_{in(min)} - V\_Z}{I\_{Z(min)} + I\_{L(max)}} = \frac{15 - 12}{(5 + 60)\text{ mA}} = \frac{3}{0.065} = 46.15\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute R\_S(min) (3 marks):**

$$R\_{S(min)} = \frac{V\_{in(max)} - V\_Z}{I\_{Z(max)} + I\_{L(min)}} = \frac{20 - 12}{(50 + 10)\text{ mA}} = \frac{8}{0.060} = 133.33\,\Omega$$

**Final Answer:**

For guaranteed regulation under all conditions, R\_S must satisfy:

$$46.15\,\Omega \le R\_S \le 133.33\,\Omega$$

A standard preferred value of **R\_S = 100 Ω, 1 W** is typically selected as a safe, practical choice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Power Supply questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert V\_RMS to V\_m** — Most transformer secondary voltages are given in RMS, but rectifier formulas use V\_m. Multiplying by 1.414 first is the most common step missed.
> 2. **Confusing PIV for FWR centre-tapped vs bridge** — Centre-tapped needs 2V\_m per diode; bridge needs only V\_m. Mixing these up costs the full 2–3 marks allocated to PIV.
> 3. **Skipping the diode drop** — If the question says "silicon diodes with V\_D = 0.7 V", you *must* subtract 2V\_D = 1.4 V from V\_m for bridge (two diodes conduct in series) or 1V\_D = 0.7 V for HWR. Not doing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong ripple frequency** — HWR ripple = f (50 Hz); FWR ripple = 2f (100 Hz). This is critical for filter calculations.
> 5. **No units / no formula** — Always write the formula *before* substituting numbers. Examiners award partial marks for the correct formula even if arithmetic is wrong.
> 6. **No waveform sketches** — In a 7-mark question, drawing the input and output waveforms is compulsory (typically 1–2 marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **linear regulated DC power supply** is the cascade: **Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator**.
- The **ripple factor** of an HWR is **1.21** and of an FWR is **0.482** — memorise these.
- The **rectification efficiency** is **40.6 %** for HWR and **81.2 %** for FWR.
- **PIV** = V\_m (HWR), 2V\_m (FWR-CT), V\_m (FWR-Bridge).
- **TUF** is highest for the **bridge rectifier (0.81)** → preferred in modern supplies.
- A **capacitor filter** charges to V\_m and discharges through R\_L; ripple voltage for FWR is $V\_{r(pp)} = I\_{DC}/(2fC)$.
- An **inductor filter** opposes changes in current; placed in series with the load.
- **Zener regulator** holds V\_out = V\_Z by carrying the excess current: $I\_S = I\_Z + I\_L$.
- The **78xx / 79xx** IC regulators are three-terminal fixed regulators; **LM317** is adjustable.
- **SMPS** uses high-frequency switching (20 kHz – 1 MHz) → smaller transformer, higher efficiency (> 80 %), used in computer/phone chargers.
- **Performance metrics to remember:** % Regulation, Ripple factor, Rectification efficiency, TUF, PIV.
- **Diode drop:** Ideal diode → V\_D = 0; Silicon → V\_D = 0.7 V. For bridge, *two* diodes conduct in series → total drop = 1.4 V.
- For waveform questions, **always sketch** v\_i(t), the rectified output, and the filtered output on the same time axis with **labelled amplitudes and time periods**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS) — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuition

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

The **Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS)** is a critical satellite subsystem responsible for **acquiring, maintaining, and modifying** the orientation (attitude) and trajectory (orbit) of a spacecraft throughout its operational lifetime. The AOCS operates as a closed-loop feedback control system that continuously measures the satellite's kinematic state using an array of **sensors**, processes the data through onboard **control algorithms**, and generates corrective commands to drive **actuators** that physically adjust the spacecraft's attitude and orbital parameters.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Communication Engineering - PEERT411), the AOCS is studied as a foundational subsystem block in satellite transponder and payload engineering, since a poorly pointed antenna link can result in link-budget losses of several decibels, making AOCS a **link-integrity enabling** subsystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AOCS is conventionally assessed as a "subsystem-level" question, often combined with **TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking, and Command)** and **Power Subsystem** questions. Expect 7 to 14 mark questions linking AOCS to antenna pointing accuracy, EIRP loss, and G/T degradation.

## 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle** while holding a glass of water upright. To prevent the water from spilling, you must:
1. **Sense** the tilt of the glass (your *eyes and inner ear* — analogous to *attitude sensors*).
2. **Decide** how much to correct (your *brain* — analogous to the *onboard computer / control law*).
3. **Act** by steering and shifting weight (your *hands and body* — analogous to *reaction wheels and thrusters*).

A satellite experiences an analogous challenge in the **harsh, frictionless, micro-gravity space environment**, except that:
- There is **no air** to dampen disturbances.
- **Gravity gradient**, **solar radiation pressure**, and **magnetic field torques** constantly nudge the satellite.
- The required pointing accuracy for a geostationary communication antenna is on the order of **0.1° to 0.3°** (typically ±0.05° for Ka-band payloads).

## 🌌 Standard Reference Frames Used in AOCS

| Frame | Abbreviation | Defining Axes | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earth-Centered Inertial** | ECI | X→Vernal Equinox, Z→Celestial North Pole | Orbit determination (Kepler) |
| **Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed** | ECEF | Co-rotates with Earth | Ground tracking & antenna pointing |
| **Orbital Reference Frame** | ORF | Radial, Along-Track, Cross-Track | Local attitude reference |
| **Body-Fixed Frame** | BF | Roll (X), Pitch (Y), Yaw (Z) | Spacecraft-mounted sensor/actuator measurements |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Attitude* is described in the **Body Frame** with respect to the **Orbital Reference Frame**. *Orbit* is described in the **ECI** or **ECEF** frame with respect to Earth's center.

## 🧩 Primary Functions of an AOCS

1. **Attitude Acquisition (Detumbling):** Damp out initial tip-off rates after launch vehicle separation.
2. **Three-Axis Stabilization:** Maintain antenna payload pointing within ±0.1° to ±0.5°.
3. **Orbit Insertion Correction:** Trim the launcher dispersion error using liquid or electric thrusters.
4. **Station-Keeping:** Counteract East-West and North-South drift for GEO satellites (periodically every 2-4 weeks).
5. **Momentum Management:** Unload accumulated angular momentum from reaction wheels to avoid saturation.
6. **Attitude Maneuvering:** Execute slews for mission objectives (e.g., inter-satellite link acquisition).
7. **Safe-Hold Mode:** Re-orient and stabilize in contingency scenarios (e.g., Earth-pointing safe mode).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🏗️ Architectural Layers of the AOCS

The AOCS is a hierarchical control system, conceptually divided into three layers:

1. **Sensor Layer** — measures physical state (Sun direction, Earth limb, magnetic field, angular rate).
2. **Estimation & Control Layer** — fuses sensor data (e.g., via **Extended Kalman Filter**), computes error signal, and generates torque commands.
3. **Actuation Layer** — converts commands into physical torque/force (wheels, thrusters, magnetic torquers).

## 🧮 1. Attitude Kinematics — Euler's Rotation & Quaternions

The orientation of the satellite Body Frame with respect to the Reference Frame is mathematically expressed using:
- **Euler Angles** (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$)
- **Quaternions** $q = [q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3]$ — preferred for onboard implementation as they avoid gimbal lock.
- **Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** — a 3×3 orthogonal matrix.

The **kinematic equation** relates the time derivative of the quaternion to the body angular velocity vector $\boldsymbol{\omega}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{\omega})\,\mathbf{q}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Omega}(\boldsymbol{\omega})$ is the 4×4 skew-symmetric matrix built from the body rates.

## 🧮 2. Attitude Dynamics — Euler's Equation for a Rigid Body

For a rigid spacecraft rotating with body angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3]^T$ and inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$, Euler's rotational equation of motion is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}$ is the total external torque acting on the spacecraft.

In principal-axis form, where $\mathbf{I} = \text{diag}(I_1, I_2, I_3)$, the scalar component equations are:

$$
\tau_1 = I_1\dot{\omega}_1 + (I_3 - I_2)\omega_2\omega_3
$$

$$
\tau_2 = I_2\dot{\omega}_2 + (I_1 - I_3)\omega_3\omega_1
$$

$$
\tau_3 = I_3\dot{\omega}_3 + (I_2 - I_1)\omega_1\omega_2
$$

## 🧮 3. Torque-Free Motion (Euler's Torque-Free Solution)

In the absence of external torque, the **angular momentum** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is conserved. The body traces a closed curve around the angular momentum vector. The kinetic energy $T$ is also conserved.

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}^T\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega} = \text{const}
$$

$$
\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega} = \text{const}
$$

## 🧮 4. Reaction Wheel Dynamics

A reaction wheel is a rotating inertial mass whose spin-rate change creates a *reaction torque* on the spacecraft body (Newton's third law). The momentum exchange is governed by:

$$
\mathbf{H}_{total} = \mathbf{H}_{spacecraft} + \mathbf{H}_{wheels} = \text{const (no external torque)}
$$

For a single wheel with moment of inertia $J_w$ and spin rate $\Omega_w$:

$$
\tau_{reaction} = -J_w \dot{\Omega}_w
$$

The wheel can store a finite angular momentum $H_{max} = J_w \Omega_{max}$, after which it must be **desaturated** using external torquers (e.g., magnetic torquers or thrusters).

## 🧮 5. Magnetic Torquer Interaction

A current-carrying coil of area $A$ and $N$ turns in Earth's magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ produces a controllable dipole moment $\mathbf{m} = NIA\hat{\mathbf{n}}$. The torque experienced is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{mag} = \mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}
$$

This is the principal non-propulsive actuation method for **Low Earth Orbit (LEO)** satellites.

## 🧮 6. Thruster-Based Control

A thruster ejects propellant at exhaust velocity $v_e$ with mass flow rate $\dot{m}$, producing thrust:

$$
F_{thrust} = \dot{m}\, v_e
$$

The corresponding **delta-V** imparted to a spacecraft of mass $m$ is given by the **Tsiolkovsky rocket equation**:

$$
\Delta v = v_e \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)
$$

where $m_0$ is the initial mass and $m_f$ is the final (dry) mass.

## 🧮 7. Disturbance Torques

| Disturbance Torque | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude (LEO, 500 kg) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gravity Gradient** | Differential gravity across extended body | $\sim 10^{-3}$ N·m |
| **Solar Radiation Pressure** | Photon momentum transfer | $\sim 10^{-5}$ N·m |
| **Aerodynamic Drag Torque** | Residual atmosphere at LEO altitudes | $\sim 10^{-4}$ N·m |
| **Magnetic Dipole Torque** | Interaction of residual spacecraft dipole with $\mathbf{B}$ | $\sim 10^{-4}$ N·m |

The **gravity-gradient torque** for a body with principal moments of inertia $I_y, I_z$ and an offset $\theta$ from the local vertical is:

$$
\tau_{gg} = \tfrac{3}{2} n^2 (I_z - I_y)\sin(2\theta)
$$

where $n = \sqrt{\mu / r^3}$ is the orbital mean motion and $\mu$ is Earth's gravitational parameter $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2$.

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Equation / Symbol | Expression | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Euler's Rotational Eqn. | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ | Rigid-body rotational dynamics |
| Angular Momentum | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | Conservation in torque-free case |
| Kinetic Energy | $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}^T\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | Constant for torque-free motion |
| Reaction Wheel Torque | $\tau_{rw} = -J_w \dot{\Omega}_w$ | Momentum exchange principle |
| Magnetic Torque | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{mag} = \mathbf{m}\times\mathbf{B}$ | LEO actuation via torquer rods |
| Thrust Equation | $F = \dot{m}\,v_e$ | Cold gas / chemical thrusters |
| Tsiolkovsky (Delta-V) | $\Delta v = v_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$ | Orbit-maneuver propellant budget |
| Gravity-Gradient Torque | $\tau_{gg} = \tfrac{3}{2}n^2(I_z - I_y)\sin(2\theta)$ | Natural stabilization torque |
| Mean Motion | $n = \sqrt{\mu/r^3}$ | Orbital angular rate |
| Kepler's Third Law | $T^2 = \tfrac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$ | Orbital period vs. semi-major axis |
| Pointing Loss (Antenna) | $L_{pt} = 12\,(e/\theta_{3dB})^2$ (dB) | For $e \ll \theta_{3dB}$ (Gaussian beam) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The **pointing loss** formula above is critical in link-budget design. For a GEO satellite with $\theta_{3dB} = 0.3°$ and pointing error $e = 0.1°$, the loss is approximately $L_{pt} = 12(0.1/0.3)^2 \approx 1.33$ dB. This is why AOCS accuracy *directly* determines the achievable EIRP and G/T of the satellite link.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧾 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 Derivation 1: Euler's Rotational Equation of Motion (From Newton's Second Law for Rotation)

### Step 1 — Start from the linear momentum principle
For a system of $N$ particles, the time rate of change of total angular momentum equals the sum of external torques:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext} = \frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{r}_i \times m_i \mathbf{v}_i
$$

### Step 2 — Express the position vector in the rotating (body) frame
For a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ relative to the inertial frame, the time derivative of any body-fixed vector $\mathbf{r}_i$ observed from the inertial frame is:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{r}_i}{dt}\bigg|_{inertial} = \frac{d\mathbf{r}_i}{dt}\bigg|_{body} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_i
$$

Since the body is rigid, $\left.\frac{d\mathbf{r}_i}{dt}\right|_{body} = \mathbf{0}$. Hence:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_i
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the angular momentum expression

$$
\mathbf{H} = \sum_{i} m_i \mathbf{r}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_i)
$$

Using the vector triple product identity $\mathbf{A}\times(\mathbf{B}\times\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{A}) - \mathbf{A}(\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{B})$, the cross product becomes:

$$
\mathbf{r}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_i) = \boldsymbol{\omega}\,(\mathbf{r}_i \cdot \mathbf{r}_i) - \mathbf{r}_i\,(\mathbf{r}_i \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega})
$$

### Step 4 — Form the inertia tensor
Recognizing that $\sum m_i (\mathbf{r}_i \cdot \mathbf{r}_i)\mathbf{I} - m_i \mathbf{r}_i \mathbf{r}_i^T$ defines the inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$, we obtain:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}
$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate H to obtain Euler's equation
The time derivative of $\mathbf{H}$ in the inertial frame, expressed in body coordinates, picks up an additional rotational term $\boldsymbol{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext} = \frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt}\bigg|_{inertial} = \frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt}\bigg|_{body} + \boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{H}
$$

Substituting $\mathbf{H}=\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})
$$

This is the **fundamental equation of attitude dynamics**, valid in the body frame for a rigid spacecraft.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2: Reaction-Wheel Momentum Exchange

### Step 1 — Consider an isolated two-body system (spacecraft + wheel)
The total angular momentum is conserved:

$$
\mathbf{H}_{total} = \mathbf{H}_{sc} + \mathbf{H}_{w} = \text{const}
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain the coupling
If the only coupling is internal, no external torque acts, so:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{H}_{sc}}{dt} = -\frac{d\mathbf{H}_w}{dt}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the wheel's angular momentum
If the wheel spins about a single body axis $\hat{\mathbf{e}}$ with spin rate $\Omega_w$:

$$
\mathbf{H}_w = J_w \Omega_w \hat{\mathbf{e}}
$$

where $J_w$ is the wheel moment of inertia about its spin axis.

### Step 4 — Compute the reaction torque on the spacecraft
The torque on the spacecraft is therefore:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{sc} = -\frac{d\mathbf{H}_w}{dt} = -J_w \dot{\Omega}_w \hat{\mathbf{e}}
$$

The **magnitude** of the reaction torque is $\vert \tau_{sc} \vert = J_w \vert \dot{\Omega}_w \vert$, and it acts **opposite** to the wheel's angular acceleration. This is the principle used in zero-momentum-bias and bias-momentum three-axis stabilized spacecraft.

---

## 📘 Derivation 3: Delta-V Budget for Station-Keeping (GEO Satellite)

### Step 1 — Estimate the principal perturbations
For a geostationary satellite, the dominant orbit perturbations are:
- **Lunar/Solar gravitational perturbations** causing an inclination drift $\Delta i \approx 0.85°/\text{year}$ (North-South station-keeping needed).
- **Earth's triaxiality ($J_{22}$ term)** causing an East-West longitude drift.

### Step 2 — Compute the $\Delta V$ required per year
Empirically, the total annual $\Delta V$ budget for a GEO communication satellite is:

$$
\Delta V_{annual} \approx 50 \text{ to } 60 \text{ m/s per year}
$$

Broken down approximately as:
- North-South (inclination control): $\Delta V_{N/S} \approx 45$ m/s/yr
- East-West (longitude control): $\Delta V_{E/W} \approx 5$ m/s/yr
- Wheel desaturation: $\Delta V_{desat} \approx 5$ m/s/yr

### Step 3 — Compute the propellant mass via Tsiolkovsky
For a bipropellant thruster with $v_e = 2900$ m/s and spacecraft dry mass $m_{dry} = 1500$ kg:

$$
\Delta V = v_e \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_{dry}}\right)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{m_0}{m_{dry}} = e^{\Delta V / v_e} = e^{55/2900} \approx e^{0.01897} \approx 1.01915
$$

Therefore the propellant mass is:

$$
m_{prop} = m_0 - m_{dry} = 1500 \times 0.01915 \approx 28.7 \text{ kg}
$$

This 28–30 kg of propellant (typically hydrazine or bipropellant) supports a 15-year station-keeping life.

---

## 📘 Derivation 4: Antenna Pointing Loss

### Step 1 — Antenna pattern approximation
For a parabolic antenna, the gain near the boresight follows approximately a Gaussian shape. The 3-dB beamwidth $\theta_{3dB}$ relates to the antenna diameter $D$ and wavelength $\lambda$:

$$
\theta_{3dB} \approx 70\,\frac{\lambda}{D} \quad (\text{degrees})
$$

### Step 2 — Off-boresight loss expression
For a small off-boresight error $e$ (in degrees), the gain loss in dB is approximately:

$$
L_{pt} \approx 12 \left(\frac{e}{\theta_{3dB}}\right)^2 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical example
For a Ku-band antenna with $D = 1.5$ m, $\lambda = 0.025$ m, and $e = 0.1°$:

$$
\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \times \frac{0.025}{1.5} \approx 1.17°
$$

$$
L_{pt} \approx 12 \times \left(\frac{0.1}{1.17}\right)^2 \approx 0.088 \text{ dB}
$$

This 0.088 dB loss is a *direct* link-budget penalty due to AOCS pointing inaccuracy, illustrating the engineering coupling between AOCS and RF performance.

---

## 📊 KTU Analytical Matrix — Control Mode vs. Actuator Selection

| Mission Orbit | Recommended Stabilization | Primary Actuator | Secondary Actuator | Sensor Suite |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GEO (36,000 km)** | Three-axis (zero momentum bias) | Bipropellant thrusters | Reaction wheels | Earth sensor, Sun sensor, star tracker, gyros |
| **MEO (20,000 km)** | Three-axis | Hydrazine thrusters | Reaction wheels | Sun sensor, Earth sensor, gyros |
| **LEO (500–1500 km)** | Three-axis (gravity-gradient optional) | Magnetic torquers | Reaction wheels | Sun sensor, magnetometer, GPS, gyros |
| **LEO (small satellites)** | Spin / gravity-gradient | Magnetic torquers | Cold-gas thrusters | Sun sensors, magnetometer |
| **Deep Space** | Three-axis (bias-momentum) | Reaction wheels + CMG | Thrusters | Star trackers, inertial measurement unit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1: High-Level AOCS Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[External Disturbance Torques] --> B1[Sensed Spacecraft State]
    A2[Target Attitude / Orbit Reference] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Attitude and Orbit Sensors]
    C1 -->|Sun direction| S1[Sun Sensor]
    C1 -->|Earth limb| S2[Earth Horizon Sensor]
    C1 -->|Star positions| S3[Star Tracker]
    C1 -->|Angular rate| S4[Gyroscope IMU]
    C1 -->|Magnetic field| S5[Magnetometer]
    C1 -->|Position and velocity| S6[GPS Receiver]
    S1 --> D1[Sensor Data Fusion EKF]
    S2 --> D1
    S3 --> D1
    S4 --> D1
    S5 --> D1
    S6 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[State Estimate q, omega, r, v]
    E1 --> F1[Control Law PID and LQR]
    E1 --> F2[Momentum Management Algorithm]
    F1 --> G1[Actuator Command Dispatcher]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Reaction Wheels]
    G1 --> H2[Control Moment Gyroscopes]
    G1 --> H3[Thrusters Cold Gas and Chemical]
    G1 --> H4[Magnetic Torquer Rods]
    H1 --> I1[Corrected Attitude]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I2[Corrected Orbit]
    H4 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Antenna Payload Pointing]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Communication Link Integrity]
```

## 📊 Diagram 2: Closed-Loop Attitude Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Attitude q_ref] --> SUM((Summation Junction))
    SENS[Attitude Sensors] --> EST[Extended Kalman Filter Estimator]
    EST --> MEAS[Estimated State q_hat and omega_hat]
    MEAS --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Attitude Error e]
    ERR --> CTRL[Control Law PID]
    CTRL --> CMD[Actuator Command u]
    CMD --> ACT[Actuator Reaction Wheel or Thruster]
    ACT --> DYN[Spacecraft Dynamics Euler Equation]
    DYN --> OUT[Actual Attitude q and omega]
    OUT --> SENS
    DYN --> DIST[Disturbance Torques d]
    DIST --> DYN
```

## 📊 Diagram 3: Three-Axis Stabilization Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SC[Satellite Body] --> P1[Pitch Axis Y Pointing Along Orbit Normal]
    SC --> P2[Roll Axis X Along Velocity Vector]
    SC --> P3[Yaw Axis Z Toward Earth Nadir]
    P1 --> A1[Earth Sensor Measures Pitch Error]
    P2 --> A2[Sun Sensor Measures Roll Reference]
    P3 --> A3[Star Tracker or Gyro Measures Yaw Drift]
    A1 --> CTRL[Onboard Control Computer]
    A2 --> CTRL
    A3 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> RW[Reaction Wheels Apply Torque on Three Axes]
    CTRL --> THR[Thrusters for Orbit Maintenance]
    RW --> SC
    THR --> SC
```

## 📊 Diagram 4: Torque-Free Motion of a Rigid Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Angular Momentum Vector H Constant] --> POLH[Polhode Curve on Inertia Ellipsoid]
    POLH --> HER[Herpolhode Curve on Invariable Plane]
    POLH --> NOTE1[Body axes trace polhode]
    HER --> NOTE2[Angular momentum vector traces herpolhode in inertial space]
    NOTE1 --> CON[Energy and Momentum Conserved]
    NOTE2 --> CON
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation of Torque-Free Motion:** A perfectly symmetric satellite (all principal moments equal) spinning about a body axis will remain oriented indefinitely, since no external torques exist. In real satellites, residual magnetic dipole moments, gravity-gradient coupling, and solar radiation pressure provide the external disturbances that the AOCS must continually counteract.
