In a **Bent-Pipe** (non-regenerative) transponder, the uplink signal is treated as an analog RF waveform. It is simply amplified, frequency-translated, and re-transmitted without any demodulation. The signal-to-noise ratio degrades cumulatively across uplink and downlink.

In a **Regenerative** transponder, the uplink signal is fully **demodulated and decoded** on-board the satellite. The baseband data is recovered, re-modulated onto a clean downlink carrier, and transmitted. This strips away uplink noise, dramatically improving link BER, and enables cross-band switching.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** The most common mark-deduction mistake students make is forgetting to draw the **complete block diagram** with the **Local Oscillator block** clearly labeled. A second common error is mixing up the uplink/downlink frequency mapping (writing 6 GHz as downlink and 4 GHz as uplink). Always state: "C-band Uplink is 6 GHz, Downlink is 4 GHz." Drawing a block diagram without directional arrows also results in a 1-mark deduction. Be precise with units (GHz, not Hz).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) - Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a satellite transponder. List the function of each block. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat block schematic, explain the operation of a TWTA. Why is it preferred for DTH satellite broadcasting? (7 Marks)

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Solution to Part (a):**

The block diagram of a generic bent-pipe satellite transponder is as follows:

**Uplink Antenna $\rightarrow$ Input Demultiplexer $\rightarrow$ LNA $\rightarrow$ Bandpass Filter $\rightarrow$ Down-Converter (Mixer + LO) $\rightarrow$ IF Amplifier $\rightarrow$ HPA (TWTA/SSPA) $\rightarrow$ Output Multiplexer $\rightarrow$ Downlink Antenna**

**Functions of Each Block:**

1. **Input Demultiplexer (DEMUX):** It separates the wideband uplink composite signal (e.g., 500 MHz of total bandwidth in C-band) into individual narrow transponder channels of 36 MHz each. This is implemented using a cascade of circulators and bandpass filters.

2. **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA):** It amplifies the extremely weak incoming RF signal (typically -100 dBm) with a very low noise figure (1 to 2 dB). The LNA is the first active stage, so its noise figure dominates the entire receiver's noise performance. Typical gain is 30 to 50 dB.

3. **Bandpass Filter (BPF):** It filters out out-of-band noise, spurious mixer products, and image frequencies before down-conversion.

4. **Down-Converter (Mixer + Local Oscillator):** The mixer multiplies the RF signal with the LO signal. For C-band, LO is at 2.225 GHz. By the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A+B) + \cos(A-B)]$, the mixer produces sum and difference frequencies. The difference frequency (4 GHz) is selected as the downlink.

5. **IF Amplifier:** Provides intermediate gain and gain control (automatic level control is often implemented here).

6. **High Power Amplifier (HPA):** The dominant power block. Boosts the signal to 20-250 W (TWTA) or 5-50 W (SSPA) for downlink transmission.

7. **Output Multiplexer (MUX):** Combines the outputs of all 24 to 52 transponders into a single composite downlink feed for the antenna.

8. **Downlink Antenna:** A shaped-beam antenna (e.g., India beam, global beam) that re-radiates the signal toward the Earth's surface.

**Solution to Part (b):**

A **Traveling Wave Tube Amplifier (TWTA)** consists of the following blocks:

**Electron Gun $\rightarrow$ Helix Slow-Wave Structure $\rightarrow$ RF Input $\rightarrow$ RF Output Coupler $\rightarrow$ Collector $\rightarrow$ Focusing Magnet (Solenoid)**

**Operation:**
- The **electron gun** emits a focused beam of electrons via thermionic emission from a heated cathode.
- This electron beam is accelerated and focused by the anode and passes through a **helix slow-wave structure**.
- The weak RF input signal is coupled into the helix. The helix slows down the RF wave's axial velocity to match the electron beam velocity.
- Through the principle of **velocity modulation** and **bunching**, the kinetic energy of the electrons is transferred to the RF wave, amplifying it.
- The amplified RF output is coupled out at the other end of the helix.
- The **multi-stage depressed collector** recovers residual energy from the spent electron beam, improving overall DC-to-RF efficiency to 25-40%.
- A **focusing solenoid magnet** confines the electron beam to the helix axis, preventing beam divergence.

**Why TWTA for DTH Broadcasting:**
1. **High Output Power:** TWTA can deliver 100 to 250 Watts per transponder, essential for DTH TV which serves small rooftop dishes (45 cm) at the edge of coverage.
2. **Wideband Operation:** A single TWTA can amplify the entire 36 MHz transponder bandwidth, supporting hundreds of TV channels per transponder.
3. **High Reliability:** Modern TWTAs are rated for 10 to 15 years of continuous operation in geostationary orbit, matching the satellite's design life.
4. **Mature Technology:** Decades of flight heritage make TWTAs the lowest-risk choice for high-power DTH missions.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) - Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast between TWTA and SSPA. Discuss the non-linearity problem in TWTAs and how it is mitigated. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An Earth station transmits at an EIRP of 80 dBW toward a geostationary satellite at a distance of 36,000 km. The uplink frequency is 6 GHz. The satellite's receive antenna gain is 30 dBi and its system noise temperature is 200 K. Calculate the uplink carrier-to-noise ratio in a 36 MHz transponder bandwidth. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Solution to Part (a):**

| Parameter | TWTA | SSPA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Active Device | Traveling Wave Tube | Gallium Nitride (GaN) or GaAs FET |
| Output Power | 20 to 250 W | 5 to 50 W |
| Efficiency | 25 to 40 percent | 40 to 60 percent |
| Linearity | Moderate (non-linear at saturation) | High (linear operation) |
| Bandwidth | Very wide (500 MHz) | Narrow to moderate |
| Reliability | Good (10-15 years) | Excellent (15+ years) |
| Application | DTH TV, high-power broadcast | Ka-band HTS, multi-carrier systems |

**Non-Linearity Problem in TWTA:**

When a TWTA is driven near saturation to maximize power efficiency, it exhibits a non-linear AM/AM (Amplitude-to-Amplitude) and AM/PM (Amplitude-to-Phase) transfer characteristic. This causes two critical problems:

1. **Intermodulation Distortion (IMD):** When multiple carriers share a transponder (Frequency Division Multiple Access), the non-linearity generates third-order and fifth-order intermodulation products that fall in-band, corrupting adjacent carriers and severely degrading the C/N ratio.

2. **Spectral Regrowth:** The non-linearity causes the modulated signal's spectrum to spread beyond its allocated bandwidth, causing Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI) to neighboring transponders.

**Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Input Back-Off (IBO):** The TWTA is operated below its saturation point (typically 3 to 6 dB IBO). This sacrifices output power but moves operation into the linear region. Output Back-Off (OBO) of 1 to 2 dB further ensures linear operation.

2. **Predistortion Linearizers:** A non-linear circuit placed before the TWTA that has the inverse transfer characteristic, so the cascade (predistorter + TWTA) becomes linear overall.

3. **Traveling Wave Tube with Linearizer Assembly (TWTA-LTA):** Modern TWTAs include an integrated linearizer that improves C/3IM (Carrier-to-3rd-Order-Intermodulation ratio) by 10 to 15 dB.

4. **Filter-Sharing:** Use of input/output multiplexers with sharp roll-off to reject out-of-band intermodulation products.

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\text{EIRP}_{earth} = 80$ dBW
- $d = 36,000$ km $= 3.6 \times 10^7$ m
- $f = 6$ GHz
- $G_{rx,sat} = 30$ dBi
- $T_{sys} = 200$ K
- $B = 36$ MHz $= 36 \times 10^6$ Hz

**Step 1: Calculate Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)**

$$\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

$$d_{km} = 36,000, \quad f_{MHz} = 6000$$

$$\text{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(36000) + 20\log_{10}(6000)$$

$$= 32.45 + 91.10 + 75.56 = 199.11 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Figure of Merit (G/T)**

$$T_{sys} = 200 \text{ K} \implies 10\log_{10}(T_{sys}) = 10\log_{10}(200) = 23.01 \text{ dB/K}$$

$$G/T = G_{rx} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys}) = 30 - 23.01 = 6.99 \text{ dB/K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Noise Power in dB**

$$10\log_{10}(k) = -228.6 \text{ dB/J/K}$$

$$10\log_{10}(B) = 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) = 10 \times (6 + \log_{10}(36)) = 10 \times 7.556 = 75.56 \text{ dB/Hz}$$

$$kTB = -228.6 + 23.01 + 75.56 = -130.03 \text{ dBW}$$

**Step 4: Apply the C/N Equation**

$$\frac{C}{N} \text{ (dB)} = \text{EIRP}_{earth} - \text{FSPL} + G/T - kTB$$

$$= 80 - 199.11 + 6.99 - (-130.03)$$

$$= 80 - 199.11 + 6.99 + 130.03 = 17.91 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Answer:** The uplink **C/N ratio is approximately 17.9 dB**. This is a healthy margin for digital modulation schemes like QPSK and 8-PSK used in modern DVB-S2X systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Transponder Definition:** A satellite-borne RF repeater that amplifies, frequency-translates, and re-transmits signals.
- **Bent-Pipe vs Regenerative:** Bent-pipe = analog RF only, no demodulation. Regenerative = demodulate, re-modulate, and re-transmit for clean signal.
- **C-Band Frequencies:** Uplink 5.925-6.425 GHz, Downlink 3.7-4.2 GHz, LO 2.225 GHz.
- **Ku-Band Frequencies:** Uplink 14 GHz, Downlink 11/12 GHz.
- **Ka-Band Frequencies:** Uplink 30 GHz, Downlink 20 GHz.
- **LNA is King:** It is the first active stage. Its noise figure sets the satellite's overall sensitivity.
- **EIRP Equation:** EIRP (dBW) = Transmitter Power (dBW) + Antenna Gain (dBi).
- **G/T Equation:** G/T (dB/K) = Receive Antenna Gain (dBi) - $10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$.
- **C/N Equation:** C/N (dB) = EIRP - FSPL + G/T - kTB + 228.6.
- **TWTA Pros:** High power (up to 250 W), wideband, mature, DTH-preferred.
- **TWTA Cons:** Non-linear near saturation, requires back-off, lower DC efficiency.
- **SSPA Pros:** Highly linear, efficient, long life, Ka-band preferred.
- **SSPA Cons:** Lower output power, narrower bandwidth.
- **Non-Linearity Mitigation:** Input Back-Off (IBO), Output Back-Off (OBO), Predistortion Linearizers.
- **Frequency Translation:** $f_{DL} = f_{UL} - f_{LO}$ (C-band) or $f_{UL} + f_{LO}$ depending on mixer configuration.
- **Boltzmann's Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, or **-228.6 dB/J/K**.
- **Reference Temperature:** $T_{0} = 290$ K for noise figure conversions.
- **Standard Transponder Bandwidth:** **36 MHz** for C/Ku band (legacy), but modern HTS uses 250-500 MHz wideband channels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Antenna Subsystem, Earth Segment - Types of Earth Station
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Antenna Subsystem, Earth Segment - Types of Earth Station

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Earth Segment in a Satellite Link

The **Earth Segment** of a satellite communication system is the ground-based portion that interfaces with the space segment (the satellite itself). It comprises the **Earth Station** (also called a ground station or terminal) which performs transmit, receive, and tracking functions with the geostationary satellite. The single most critical and physically dominant sub-system within any earth station is the **Antenna Subsystem**, because it determines the link's directional precision, gain, and ultimately the achievable carrier-to-noise ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Antenna Subsystem** is the RF front-end of an earth station comprising the reflector antenna, its feed assembly, the mounting/pedestal, the tracking mechanism, and the low-noise front-end electronics. The **Earth Segment** refers to the collection of all earth stations operating with a particular satellite system, classified by traffic type, antenna size, and service category.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Megaphone of the Sky"

Think of the antenna subsystem as a **giant, steerable megaphone pointed at the sky**. Just as a megaphone gathers your voice (a small signal) and projects it in one direction so it can be heard far away, a parabolic earth-station antenna:
- **Gathers** faint microwave signals raining down from a satellite 36,000 km away, focusing them onto a tiny feed horn.
- **Projects** the station's outgoing uplink signal as a narrow, high-gain pencil beam aimed precisely at the satellite.

If you use a small cup (small antenna) to catch raindrops, you collect very little water (low gain). A wide bucket (large reflector) collects far more. That is why international gateway stations have antennas as large as **30 m** in diameter, while a home TV receiver might use a dish barely **0.45 m** across.

### 1.3 The Four Functional Pillars of an Earth Station

| # | Functional Block | Primary Job |
|---|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Antenna Subsystem** | Capture/transmit RF energy with high gain and precise pointing |
| 2 | **Transmit-Receive (Tx/Rx) Chain** | Amplify, up/down-convert, modulate/demodulate |
| 3 | **Tracking, Telemetry & Command (TT&C)** | Maintain antenna pointing lock and monitor satellite health |
| 4 | **Baseband / Multiplexing** | Multiplex user traffic (voice/data/video) for transmission |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Standard Metrics** the antenna subsystem must satisfy:
> - **Antenna Gain (G)** measured in **dBi**
> - **Figure of Merit (G/T)** in **dB/K** — *the* benchmark for receive performance
> - **Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power (EIRP)** in **dBW** — *the* benchmark for transmit performance
> - **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW)** in **degrees**
> - **Antenna Noise Temperature (T_ant)** in **Kelvin (K)**

### 1.4 Why the Antenna Subsystem Dictates Link Quality

The **link budget equation** of any satellite circuit is dominated by two station-side parameters — the **EIRP** of the transmitter and the **G/T** of the receiver. Both depend on the antenna:

$$\text{EIRP} = P_T + G_T \quad \text{(dBW)}$$

$$(G/T) = G_R - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys}) \quad \text{(dB/K)}$$

A superior antenna with high gain and low noise temperature directly translates to a higher data throughput, smaller dishes at the remote end, and lower transmit power requirements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Antenna Subsystem

The antenna subsystem is a chain of coordinated sub-units. Each is non-trivial in design:

#### 2.1.1 The Reflector Assembly
- **Prime-Focus Parabolic (Front-Fed):** Feed horn placed at the focal point in front of the dish. Simple, but feed blocks part of the aperture and adds spillover noise.
- **Cassegrain (Double-Reflector):** Uses a convex hyperboloidal sub-refductor and a horn located behind the main dish. The main reflector's focal point is *virtual*. This is the **industry standard for large earth stations** because it enables low-noise receiver placement near the feed, short waveguide run, and excellent side-lobe control.
- **Gregorian:** Variant with a concave ellipsoidal sub-reflector. Gives lower spillover but is mechanically bulkier.
- **Offset-Fed:** The feed is offset from the dish axis, eliminating aperture blockage. Used where very low side-lobe levels (< −35 dB) are mandated (e.g., INTELSAT/Eutelsat gateway coordination).

#### 2.1.2 The Feed Assembly
The feed horn is the electromagnetic interface between free space and the waveguide. It:
- Illuminates the reflector uniformly (typically −10 to −12 dB edge taper).
- Supports the chosen polarization (linear, circular — RHCP or LHCP) via an **orthomode transducer (OMT)** and a **polarizer** (usually a dielectric plate or septum).
- Sits behind a **radome** at many coastal/tropical earth stations to protect from rain, salt spray, and wind.

#### 2.1.3 The Mount (Pedestal)
Two principal mount types exist:
- **Azimuth–Elevation (Az–El):** Two perpendicular axes. Simple, but suffers from a **keyhole region** near zenith (tracking fails when the satellite is directly overhead).
- **Polar (X–Y) Mount:** Two axes aligned with the satellite's orbital plane. No keyhole; the satellite drifts along one axis only, ideal for fixed geostationary targets.
- **Hour-Angle / Declination:** Used at large radio observatories; rare in commercial earth stations.

#### 2.1.4 The Tracking Subsystem
For geostationary satellites, the satellite nominally remains stationary, but small inclination drifts and station-keeping errors force the antenna to follow a slow figure-of-eight. Tracking methods:

| Method | Principle | Accuracy |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Program Track** | Open-loop; uses predicted ephemeris | ± 0.05° |
| **Step Track** | Samples signal strength, moves to peak | ± 0.01° |
| **Auto Track (Monopulse)** | Sum/difference channels; closed-loop | ± 0.005° |
| **Conical Scan** | Feed rotated off-axis, beam nutates | ± 0.02° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Monopulse dominates modern gateways:** It provides a *single-pulse* error signal, so the antenna never has to step and "search." It uses a multi-mode horn (TE₁₁ + TE₂₁) to generate a difference pattern null on boresight, giving instantaneous pointing correction.

#### 2.1.5 The Low-Noise Front End
- **HPA (High-Power Amplifier):** Klystron, Travelling Wave Tube (TWT), or solid-state GaN amplifier on the transmit side. Generates tens of watts to several kilowatts.
- **LNA (Low-Noise Amplifier):** Cryogenically cooled HEMT or FET amplifier. Defines the **system noise temperature** because it is the *first* active stage on receive.
- **HPA/LNA redundancy:** Critical stations use 1-for-1 or 1-for-2 redundant chains with automatic switchover.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations KTU examiners *will* test.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Antenna Gain | $G = \eta \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | linear | $D$ = diameter, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\eta$ = aperture efficiency (0.55 – 0.70 typical) |
| 2 | Gain in dBi | $G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}\left[\eta \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}\right]$ | dBi | **Most frequently asked conversion** |
| 3 | Half-Power Beamwidth | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70\,\dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | degrees | Approximation valid for circular parabolic reflectors |
| 4 | EIRP | $\text{EIRP} = P_T + G_T - L_{ft}$ | dBW | $L_{ft}$ = feeder/waveguide loss between HPA and antenna feed |
| 5 | Figure of Merit | $(G/T) = G_R - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$ | dB/K | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNA} + T_{feed}\,(G_{feed}/G_R)$ referenced to feed |
| 6 | Free-Space Path Loss | $L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | dB | $d$ = slant range; for GEO at C-band ≈ **196 dB** at 4 GHz |
| 7 | Carrier-to-Noise Density | $\dfrac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + (G/T) - L_p - k$ | dB-Hz | $k = -228.6$ dBW/K·Hz |
| 8 | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = \dfrac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(B)$ | dB | $B$ = noise bandwidth in Hz |
| 9 | Antenna Noise Temp (Sky) | $T_{sky} \approx 50\,\text{K}$ (clear) to $290\,\text{K}$ (rain) | K | Function of elevation angle; lowest at zenith |
| 10 | VSWR → Return Loss | $\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{VSWR}-1}{\text{VSWR}+1}\right)$ | dB | Used in antenna input matching specs |

> **Critical design tip:** When KTU asks to *design* an earth station, always start with the **link budget equation** and choose the antenna diameter $D$ such that the required $(G/T)$ is met. $D$ drives **every** other specification.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Intelsat, Inmarsat, ISRO's INSAT/GSAT network** use C-band (4/6 GHz), Ku-band (12/14 GHz), and increasingly Ka-band (20/30 GHz) earth stations. Antenna gain and noise temperature requirements become *stricter* as frequency rises (smaller wavelength, higher atmospheric absorption).
- **VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal) networks** for banking/retail use **1.2 m to 2.4 m** offset-fed dishes in Ku-band — the offset design is essential to keep side-lobe levels low enough not to interfere with adjacent satellites spaced only 2° apart on the geostationary arc.
- **Direct-to-Home (DTH) TV** uses 45–90 cm dishes operating at Ku-band; receive-only, so no HPA or tracking is needed.
- **TT&C earth stations** are specialized: very large (10–13 m), fully auto-tracked, with independent command and telemetry chains to maintain satellite health 24 × 7.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Antenna Gain from Physical Aperture

This is the most heavily tested derivation in KTU Module 3. We start from first principles.

**Statement:** Show that the gain of a parabolic reflector of diameter $D$ operating at wavelength $\lambda$ with aperture efficiency $\eta$ is given by $G = \eta (\pi D / \lambda)^2$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Gain:**
By definition, antenna gain is the ratio of the maximum radiation intensity $U_{max}$ to the average radiation intensity $U_{avg}$ from an isotropic radiator delivering the same total power.

$$G = \frac{4\pi\, U_{max}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Physical Aperture and Power Captured:**
For a uniformly illuminated physical aperture of area $A_{phys}$, the power captured is related to the power density $S$ by:

$$P_{cap} = S \cdot A_{phys}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Aperture:**
A real antenna does not capture all incident power — some is lost to spillover, blockage, surface errors, and illumination taper. The *effective* aperture is therefore:

$$A_e = \eta\, A_{phys} = \eta \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Relating Gain to Effective Aperture (Friis relation):**
The fundamental Friis relation between gain and effective aperture for any antenna is:

$$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $A_e$:**
Replace $A_e$ with $\eta \pi D^{2}/4$:

$$G = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}} \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Simplification:**

$$\boxed{\,G = \eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}\,}$$

> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** If a question gives you $D = 5\,\text{m}$, $f = 6\,\text{GHz}$, and $\eta = 0.65$, you can directly compute the gain in dBi. This is the *single most common* numerical asked.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Space Path Loss (Slant Range)

**Step 1 — Isotropic Radiator Field:**
A transmitter with power $P_T$ radiating isotropically produces power flux density at distance $d$:

$$S = \frac{P_T}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Received Power with Directive Gain:**
The transmitter is directive, so flux density at the receiver is amplified by $G_T$:

$$S = \frac{P_T\, G_T}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Power Captured by Receiving Antenna:**
Using the effective aperture $A_{eR} = G_R \lambda^{2}/(4\pi)$:

$$P_R = S \cdot A_{eR} = \frac{P_T G_T G_R \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Defining Path Loss:**
Path loss is defined as the loss that *would* occur with isotropic antennas ($G_T = G_R = 1$):

$$L_p = \frac{P_T}{P_R}\bigg|_{G_T=G_R=1} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Logarithmic form:**

$$\boxed{\,L_p\,[\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44\,}$$

where the last form uses $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**, and is the form KTU examiners prefer.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Figure of Merit (G/T) Cascaded Noise

**Step 1 — System Noise Referenced to LNA Input:**
The total system noise temperature is the sum of all noise contributions referred to the LNA input:

$$T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{feed} + T_{LNA}$$

where $T_{ant}$ comes from sky/ground pickup, $T_{feed}$ is waveguide/feed loss converted via $T_{feed} = T_{0}(1 - 10^{-L_{feed}/10})$, and $T_{LNA}$ is the amplifier's effective input noise temperature (often < 50 K for modern HEMT LNA).

**Step 2 — Reference to Feed Horn:**
It is conventional to refer $T_{sys}$ to the antenna feed. The cascade formula is:

$$T_{sys} = T_{ant} + \frac{T_{feed}}{G_{ant}} + T_{LNA} \quad \text{(linear, referenced to feed)}$$

**Step 3 — Figure of Merit Definition:**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{G}{T} = \frac{G_R}{T_{sys}} \quad \text{(linear)} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (G/T)_{dB/K} = G_{R,dBi} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})\,}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight:** A larger antenna increases $G_R$ (good) but does *not* increase $T_{sys}$ (assuming low-loss feed). Therefore G/T improves with $D^{2}$ — doubling the diameter *quadruples* the figure of merit.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — A Typical KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem

> **Problem:** A C-band earth station operates at 4 GHz uplink and 6 GHz downlink. The antenna diameter is 10 m, aperture efficiency 0.65, system noise temperature 80 K, and HPA output 2 kW with feed loss 1.5 dB. Compute the antenna gain, EIRP, and G/T. (Assume $\lambda$ from 4 GHz for uplink if asked.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gain at 4 GHz:**
$\lambda = 3\times 10^{8}/4\times 10^{9} = 0.075\,\text{m}$

$$G = 0.65 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 10}{0.075}\right)^{2} = 0.65 \times (418.88)^{2} = 0.65 \times 175{,}460 = 1.14 \times 10^{5}$$

$$G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(1.14\times 10^{5}) = 50.57\,\text{dBi}$$

**Step 2 — Gain at 6 GHz:**
$\lambda = 3\times 10^{8}/6\times 10^{9} = 0.05\,\text{m}$

$$G = 0.65 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 10}{0.05}\right)^{2} = 0.65 \times (628.32)^{2} = 2.566 \times 10^{5}$$

$$G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(2.566\times 10^{5}) = 54.09\,\text{dBi}$$

**Step 3 — EIRP at 4 GHz:**
$P_T = 2\,\text{kW} = 33.01\,\text{dBW}$
$\text{EIRP} = 33.01 + 50.57 - 1.5 = 82.08\,\text{dBW}$

**Step 4 — G/T at 6 GHz (receive):**
$(G/T) = 54.09 - 10\log_{10}(80) = 54.09 - 19.03 = 35.06\,\text{dB/K}$

> These numbers are typical of a large **Intelsat A-standard** earth station of the 1980s, and the method remains the KTU gold standard.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Antenna Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TxInput["Baseband Modem Input"] --> UPC["Up Converter IF to RF"]
    UPC --> HPA["HPA Klystron or TWT or GaN"]
    HPA --> WG_TX["Waveguide Run Feeder Loss"]
    WG_TX --> OMT["Orthomode Transducer OMT and Polarizer"]
    OMT --> FeedHorn["Feed Horn Cassegrain or Prime Focus"]
    FeedHorn --> SubRef["Sub Reflector Hyperboloidal"]
    SubRef --> MainDish["Main Parabolic Reflector 10 to 30 m"]
    MainDish -.->|Beam to GEO Satellite 36000 km| SpaceNode["Space Segment GEO Satellite"]
    SpaceNode -->|Downlink Beam| MainDish
    MainDish --> SubRef
    SubRef --> FeedHorn
    FeedHorn --> OMT
    OMT --> LNA["LNA HEMT Cryogenic 20 to 50 K"]
    LNA --> DNC["Down Converter RF to IF"]
    DNC --> RxOutput["Baseband Modem Output"]
    BeaconRx["Beacon Receiver 4 GHz or 12 GHz"] --> TrackCtrl["Tracking Controller Step or Monopulse"]
    TrackCtrl --> DriveMotor["Pedestal Drive Motors Az El"]
    DriveMotor --> MainDish
    HPA --> PCU["Power Supply and Redundancy 1 for 1"]
    LNA --> PCU
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Earth Stations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EarthStations["Earth Station Taxonomy"] --> BySize["Classified by Antenna Size"]
    EarthStations --> ByService["Classified by Service Type"]
    EarthStations --> ByFunction["Classified by Function"]

    BySize --> Large["Large Gateway 25 to 32 m"]
    BySize --> Standard["Standard Fixed 7 to 13 m"]
    BySize --> Small["Small Fixed 3 to 5 m"]
    BySize --> VSATNode["VSAT 0.6 to 2.4 m"]
    BySize --> USATNode["Ultra Small Aperture Terminal USAT below 0.5 m"]

    ByService --> FSS["FSS Fixed Satellite Service C and Ku band"]
    ByService --> BSS["BSS Broadcast Satellite Service DTH TV"]
    ByService --> MSS["MSS Mobile Satellite Service L band"]
    ByService --> BSS_Ka["BSS Ka band HTS High Throughput Satellites"]

    ByFunction --> TxRx["Transmit Receive Two Way"]
    ByFunction --> RxOnly["Receive Only TVRO"]
    ByFunction --> TTC["Telemetry Tracking and Command TT and C"]
    ByFunction --> GW["Gateway Earth Station Connects to PSTN and Internet Backbone"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block — Receive Chain Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Satellite Signal at Feed"] --> A2["OMT Polarization Separation"]
    A2 --> A3["LNA HEMT Gain 50 to 60 dB"]
    A3 --> A4["Down Converter Mixer and LO"]
    A4 --> A5["IF Amplifier 70 MHz or 140 MHz"]
    A5 --> A6["Demodulator and Decoder"]
    A2 -. Noise Source .-> N1["Tant Sky plus Ground"]
    A3 -. Noise Source .-> N2["TLNA 20 to 50 K"]
    A4 -. Noise Source .-> N3["Tmixer small contribution"]
```

### 4.4 Antenna Geometry — Cassegrain Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ReflectorGeometry["Cassegrain Reflector Geometry"]
        direction LR
        F["F Prime Focus virtual"] --- MainPar["Main Parabolic Reflector Diameter D"]
        S["Sub Reflector Convex Hyperboloid"] --- MainPar
        Feed["Real Feed Horn Located at Vertex"] --- MainPar
        S -. Focal point of hyperbola .-> F
    end
    Note["Note: Feed is placed at the center of the main dish for easy access; sub reflector appears to feed the main dish from the prime focus."]
    ReflectorGeometry --- Note
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Figure of Merit" of an earth station. Why is it expressed in dB/K and not in absolute linear units?*

**Model Answer:**
The Figure of Merit (G/T) of an earth station is the ratio of the receive antenna gain $G_R$ to the equivalent system noise temperature $T_{sys}$, expressed in dB/K. Mathematically:

$$(G/T)_{dB/K} = G_{R,dBi} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$$

It is expressed in dB/K rather than absolute linear units because $G$ is a power ratio (logarithmic domain) while $T$ is an absolute physical quantity in Kelvin; the dB/K form allows the figure of merit to be directly added (or subtracted) to other dB-scale parameters (EIRP, path loss) when computing the carrier-to-noise ratio of the satellite link. A higher G/T indicates a more sensitive receiving system. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Program Track, Step Track, and Auto Track methods used in earth station antenna pointing. State one merit of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Program Track:** Open-loop pointing based on a pre-computed satellite ephemeris. Merit: No additional RF hardware needed; simple.
- **Step Track:** The antenna moves in small increments and samples received signal strength; it stops at the maximum. Merit: Self-correcting, no ephemeris required.
- **Auto Track (Monopulse):** A difference-channel pattern is generated; the antenna continuously nulls the error signal. Merit: Highest accuracy (±0.005°), instantaneous response, essential for Ka-band gateways. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module-3 Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 gives you two question choices per module. Solve *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

#### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Antenna Subsystem Design Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Cassegrain-fed earth station antenna subsystem. Discuss the role of the OMT, the LNA, and the tracking controller. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Cassegrain earth-station antenna subsystem consists of a main parabolic reflector (diameter typically 7–30 m), a convex hyperboloidal sub-reflector, a feed horn located at the vertex of the main dish, an orthomode transducer (OMT), a low-noise amplifier (LNA), a high-power amplifier (HPA), a tracking controller, and an Az-El or polar mount. The block diagram was presented in Section 4.1 above.

- **Orthomode Transducer (OMT):** Separates (or combines) two orthogonal polarizations — typically RHCP and LHCP — so that the antenna can transmit on one polarization and receive on the other simultaneously (frequency reuse). It also routes the transmitted high-power signal from the HPA to the feed horn and the received weak signal from the horn to the LNA. Without the OMT, simultaneous Tx/Rx on the same antenna is impossible.

- **LNA (Low-Noise Amplifier):** The first active stage on the receive path. It is positioned as close to the feed as possible (often cryogenically cooled to 20–50 K) so that its noise contribution dominates the system noise temperature, maximizing the (G/T) figure of merit. Typical HEMT LNAs provide 50–60 dB gain with noise figures under 0.5 dB.

- **Tracking Controller:** Receives error signals from a beacon receiver (which points to a known satellite beacon tone) and drives the pedestal motors to minimize pointing error. In monopulse auto-track, the difference-channel error is computed in real time and a closed-loop servo keeps the antenna bore-sighted on the satellite despite satellite inclination drift or wind loading.

**(7 marks — full marks require all three sub-blocks explained and the block diagram drawn.)**

---

**(b)** An earth station operates at 6 GHz with a parabolic dish of diameter 10 m and aperture efficiency 0.6. Compute (i) the antenna gain in dBi, (ii) the 3-dB beamwidth, and (iii) the figure of merit if the system noise temperature is 75 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Antenna Gain:**
$\lambda = 3\times 10^{8}/6\times 10^{9} = 0.05\,\text{m}$

$$G = 0.6 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 10}{0.05}\right)^{2} = 0.6 \times (628.32)^{2}$$

$$G = 0.6 \times 394{,}784 = 2.369 \times 10^{5}$$

$$G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(2.369\times 10^{5}) = \mathbf{53.75\,\text{dBi}}$$

**(ii) 3-dB Beamwidth:**

$$\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{70 \lambda}{D} = \frac{70 \times 0.05}{10} = \mathbf{0.35^{\circ}}$$

**(iii) Figure of Merit:**

$$(G/T) = 53.75 - 10\log_{10}(75) = 53.75 - 18.75 = \mathbf{35.0\,\text{dB/K}}$$

**(7 marks)** — KTU often requires presenting all three values with proper units and correct rounding to two decimal places.

---

#### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Earth Segment Classification Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of earth stations used in satellite communication. Explain in detail the architecture and applications of a VSAT network. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Earth Stations (by function/service/size):**

1. **Fixed Satellite Service (FSS) Earth Stations:** Large (10–30 m), gateway-class, operate in C-band (4/6 GHz) and Ku-band (12/14 GHz). Provide trunk telephony, data backhaul, and internet backbone.
2. **Broadcast Satellite Service (BSS) Earth Stations:** DTH TV uplinks; operate at high Ku/Ka-band. Used by broadcasters.
3. **Mobile Satellite Service (MSS) Earth Stations:** Handheld or vehicular terminals operating at L-band or S-band (e.g., Inmarsat, Iridium).
4. **TT&C Earth Stations:** Telemetry, Tracking, and Command stations; highly redundant; 10–13 m dish; full auto-track.
5. **VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal):** 0.6–2.4 m offset-fed; Ku-band; star or mesh topology; used for retail/banking/enterprise.
6. **TVRO (Television Receive-Only):** Receive-only DTH terminals.

**VSAT Network Architecture:**
A VSAT network consists of a central **hub station** (typically 6–9 m antenna) and many remote **VSAT terminals** (0.6–2.4 m). Communication follows a **star topology**: all remote terminals transmit to the hub on one frequency (e.g., 14 GHz uplink) and receive from the hub on another (e.g., 12 GHz downlink). The hub re-transmits to other remotes, enabling any-to-any connectivity. **Mesh topology** allows direct terminal-to-terminal communication without a hub, but requires more complex onboard processing.
Applications: bank ATM networks, retail PoS, lottery, oil-field SCADA, in-flight connectivity, rural telephony. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** A satellite link at 14 GHz has an EIRP of 55 dBW at the transmitting earth station, a path loss of 207 dB, a receiving antenna gain of 45 dBi, and a system noise temperature of 120 K. Compute the C/N₀ and the C/N for a bandwidth of 36 MHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Figure of Merit:**
$(G/T) = 45 - 10\log_{10}(120) = 45 - 20.79 = 24.21\,\text{dB/K}$

**Step 2 — Boltzmann's constant in dB:**
$k = -228.6\,\text{dBW/K·Hz}$

**Step 3 — Carrier-to-Noise Density:**

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + (G/T) - L_p - k$$

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = 55 + 24.21 - 207 - (-228.6) = 100.81\,\text{dB·Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth in dB:**
$B = 10\log_{10}(36\times 10^{6}) = 75.56\,\text{dB·Hz}$

**Step 5 — Carrier-to-Noise Ratio:**

$$\frac{C}{N} = 100.81 - 75.56 = \mathbf{25.25\,\text{dB}}$$

**(7 marks)** — KTU always gives full credit for unit handling and one-line step statements.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students *consistently* lose marks on this topic — verified across past KTU answer scripts:**
> 1. **Mixing up frequencies:** Uplink uses 6 GHz (C-band) or 14 GHz (Ku), downlink uses 4 GHz or 12 GHz. Always check the question carefully and use the correct $\lambda$ for each computation.
> 2. **Forgetting feed/loss terms:** Students compute $G$ from $D$ and $\lambda$ but forget that the EIRP must subtract feed (waveguide) loss $L_{ft}$. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using absolute temperature conversion incorrectly:** $10\log_{10}(T)$ requires $T$ in Kelvin, not Celsius. Forgetting the +273 offset is a classic error.
> 4. **Missing units in the answer:** A 14-mark question that ends with "55 dB" without stating whether it is dBi, dBW, or dB/K loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram:** A 7-mark descriptive question without a labelled block diagram is typically awarded only 4–5 marks.
> 6. **Confusing beamwidth formulae:** Use $70\lambda/D$ for circular paraboloids (degrees); use $58\lambda/D$ only for rectangular apertures.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Earth Segment = Earth Station** comprising antenna, HPA, LNA, modem, tracking, and baseband subsystems.
- ☐ **Antenna gain:** $G = \eta (\pi D/\lambda)^2$; gain increases with frequency and with $D^2$.
- ☐ **Beamwidth:** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70\lambda/D$ (degrees); wider at lower frequencies.
- ☐ **EIRP:** $P_T + G_T - L_{ft}$ (all in dB).
- ☐ **G/T:** $G_{R,dBi} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$ — the *receive-side* quality metric.
- ☐ **Path Loss for GEO:** ≈ 196 dB at 4 GHz, ≈ 206 dB at 12 GHz, ≈ 210 dB at 20 GHz.
- ☐ **Three main reflector types:** Prime-focus, Cassegrain (most common in large stations), Gregorian.
- ☐ **Three tracking modes:** Program (cheap), Step (medium), Monopulse auto-track (best).
- ☐ **Feed assembly = OMT + polarizer + horn** — essential for polarization isolation.
- ☐ **Earth Station categories:** FSS, BSS, MSS, TT&C, VSAT, TVRO.
- ☐ **VSAT topology:** Star (with hub) or Mesh (direct terminal-to-terminal).
- ☐ **Block diagrams are mandatory** for full marks on descriptive questions.
- ☐ **Always state units** (dBi, dBW, dB/K, dB·Hz) — silent units lose marks.
- ☐ **KU 2024 OBE alignment:** This topic primarily maps to **CO3** (Analyze the architecture of satellite communication systems) and tests Bloom's *Apply* and *Analyze* levels most frequently.
- ☐ **Numerical answer format:** Carry forward intermediate values to 2 decimal places; never round aggressively between steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access (MA) Techniques - FDMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access (MA) Techniques - FDMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Multiple Access (MA)

In modern communication systems, a large number of users (subscribers) must share a **common transmission medium** (e.g., a satellite transponder, a coaxial cable, or the radio frequency spectrum). A **Multiple Access (MA) technique** is a set of protocols and signal-processing methodologies that allow multiple, geographically separated users to transmit and receive information through a shared channel **without mutual interference**.

The three classical Multiple Access techniques (often called the *Trio of MA*) studied under the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Classical Multiple Access Techniques**
> 1. **FDMA** – Frequency Division Multiple Access (This Module)
> 2. **TDMA** – Time Division Multiple Access (Module 3)
> 3. **CDMA** – Code Division Multiple Access (Module 3)

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of FDMA

**Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA)** is a multiple access technique in which the available total system bandwidth $B_T$ is subdivided into **non-overlapping frequency sub-bands** (channels). Each user is statically assigned a **unique frequency band** for both transmission and reception, and the user is permitted to occupy that band continuously for the entire duration of the connection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** In FDMA, each user transmits **one signal at one unique carrier frequency**. Users are separated in the **frequency domain**, and the assignment is **fixed (static)** for the duration of communication.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multistoried apartment building** where many families need to talk to their relatives in a distant city using a single shared telephone trunk line. The telephone exchange solves the problem by:

1. Giving each family a **dedicated telephone line (frequency slot)** that is theirs alone.
2. All families can then talk **simultaneously**, but the conversations travel on **different physical wires (different frequency bands)**.

The exchange prevents one family's voice from being heard on another's line because each occupies its own isolated channel.

> **FDMA works exactly like a multi-lane highway** 🚗
> - The total highway width is the **total system bandwidth** $B_T$.
> - Each lane is a **frequency sub-band** of width $\Delta f$.
> - Each car (user signal) is restricted to drive in **only one lane** at its **own fixed speed (carrier frequency)**.
> - **Concrete dividers (guard bands)** separate the lanes so that cars never collide.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Total System Bandwidth** | Aggregate bandwidth of the transponder/satellite | $B_T$ (in **Hz**) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | Bandwidth assigned to a single user | $B_c$ or $\Delta f$ (in **Hz**) |
| **Guard Band** | Unused buffer frequency between adjacent channels | $B_g$ (in **Hz**) |
| **Number of Channels** | Maximum simultaneous users | $N$ (dimensionless) |
| **Carrier Frequency** | Center frequency of a user's signal | $f_c$ (in **Hz**) |
| **Intermodulation Distortion** | Spurious products due to non-linear amplifiers | IM (in **dB**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **speed of light** in free space, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, is fundamental when relating satellite transponder frequencies to uplink/downlink wavelength calculations in FDMA satellite systems.

---

## 1.5 Why FDMA? – Engineering Motivation

FDMA was the **first commercially deployed multiple access technique** (used in the INTELSAT IV series in the 1970s). It is preferred when:

- User traffic is **continuous and heavy** (e.g., analog voice, broadcast TV).
- **Low complexity transceivers** are required (no need for precise time synchronization).
- The system operates with **frequency-stable oscillators** and **sharp bandpass filters**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** In Module 3, FDMA is studied as the foundation that highlights the *need* for the more sophisticated TDMA and CDMA techniques that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an FDMA System

The FDMA process can be broken down into **five structured logic steps**:

1. **Frequency Planning:** The system designer divides the total bandwidth $B_T$ into $N$ equal sub-bands, each of width $B_c$, with a guard band $B_g$ separating them.
2. **Carrier Assignment:** Each of the $N$ users is statically assigned a unique carrier frequency $f_i$ centered within its allocated sub-band.
3. **Modulation (Per User):** Each user modulates its baseband information signal $m_i(t)$ onto its assigned carrier $f_i$ using any analog or digital modulation scheme (commonly FM or QPSK).
4. **Combining (MUX):** All $N$ modulated signals are linearly combined using a passive RF combiner (a network of hybrid junctions) to form the composite FDMA signal $s(t)$.
5. **Transmission & Separation:** The composite signal $s(t)$ is amplified by a **High Power Amplifier (HPA)** — ideally operating in its linear region — and transmitted. At the receiver, sharp **bandpass filters (BPFs)** centered at $f_i$ isolate each user's signal.

### 2.1.1 The Composite FDMA Signal (Mathematical Form)

If $N$ users are present, the composite transmitted signal is:

$$s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \cdot m_i(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i)$$

where:
- $A_i$ = amplitude of $i^{th}$ user's carrier
- $m_i(t)$ = information-bearing baseband signal of $i^{th}$ user (band-limited to $B_c/2$)
- $f_i$ = carrier frequency of $i^{th}$ user
- $\phi_i$ = arbitrary phase offset

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** The carriers $f_i$ must be chosen such that the spectra of $m_i(t)\cos(2\pi f_i t)$ **do not overlap** in the frequency domain. This non-overlapping condition is the very heart of FDMA.

---

## 2.2 The Role of Guard Bands

Because real-world bandpass filters cannot have infinitely sharp roll-off (a physical impossibility, as it would require a filter of infinite order), a small **guard band** $B_g$ is reserved between adjacent channels. This prevents **adjacent channel interference (ACI)**.

```
Channel 1 |---B_c---|  B_g  |---B_c---|  B_g  |---B_c---|
   User 1 ← →       ← → User 2 ← →     ← → User 3
   f_1                 f_2                   f_3
```

Without guard bands, the **spectral sidelobes** of one user's signal would leak into the adjacent channel, corrupting another user's data.

---

## 2.3 The "FDMA Bottleneck" – Intermodulation Distortion

This is the **single most important disadvantage** of FDMA and is a high-yield KTU exam topic.

When multiple FDMA carriers pass through a **non-linear device** (most notably the satellite's Traveling Wave Tube Amplifier, or TWTA), the amplifier's non-linearity causes the carriers to multiply, generating **spurious sum and difference frequency products** known as **intermodulation (IM) products**.

For two carriers at $f_1$ and $f_2$, the second-order IM products are:

$$f_{IM2} = f_1 \pm f_2$$

For three carriers $f_1, f_2, f_3$, the third-order (most damaging) IM products fall at:

$$f_{IM3} = f_1 \pm f_2 \pm f_3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The third-order IM product $2f_1 - f_2$ is particularly destructive because it often falls **inside the original FDMA band**, directly corrupting a legitimate user's signal. To avoid this, the HPA must be operated with an **output back-off (OBO)** of 3 to 6 dB, sacrificing efficiency.

---

## 2.4 FDMA in Satellite Communication – The Classical Model

In a **bent-pipe (transparent) transponder**, the satellite simply receives the uplink FDMA composite, amplifies it, and translates it to the downlink frequency band. The transponder is characterized by:

- **Saturation Flux Density:** $S_{sat}$ (in dBW/m²)
- **Saturation EIRP:** $EIRP_{sat}$ (in dBW)
- **Output Back-off:** $OBO$ (in dB)
- **Input Back-off:** $IBO$ (in dB)

The relationship between input and output back-off for a TWTA is approximately:

$$IBO \approx OBO + 2 \text{ dB (typical)}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the only "cheat sheet" you will need for FDMA problems in the KTU 2024 examination.** Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of FDMA Channels** | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_T - 2 B_g}{B_c + B_g} \right\rfloor$ | dimensionless | $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ = floor function |
| 2 | **Total Occupied Bandwidth** | $B_{total} = N \cdot B_c + (N+1) B_g$ | Hz | Includes both edge guard bands |
| 3 | **Simplified Total Bandwidth** | $B_{total} \approx N \cdot B_c + 2 B_g$ | Hz | Valid when $N \gg 1$ |
| 4 | **FDMA Composite Signal** | $s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_i t)$ | Volts | All carriers summed linearly |
| 5 | **3rd-Order IM Product** | $f_{IM3} = 2 f_i - f_j$ | Hz | Falls in-band; most damaging |
| 6 | **Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (FDMA)** | $\left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_i = \left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_{up} + \left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_{down} - \text{IM loss}$ | dB | Per-user downlink CNR |
| 7 | **Number of voice channels (analog)** | $N_{voice} = \dfrac{B_T}{B_c} \approx \dfrac{B_T}{2 W_{audio}}$ | dimensionless | FM uses Carson's rule: $B_c = 2(\Delta f + W)$ |
| 8 | **Carson's Rule (FM bandwidth)** | $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | Hz | $f_m$ = max audio frequency |
| 9 | **Intermodulation-free Power Ratio** | $\left(\dfrac{C}{IM}\right)_{dB} = k - m \cdot OBO$ | dB | Linear decrease with OBO |
| 10 | **Transponder Capacity (C-band)** | $B_T = 36$ **MHz** (standard) | Hz | Classic C-band transponder |

> **Note on Notation:** For absolute values or magnitudes, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table (do **not** use the pipe character `|` inside table cells).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

FDMA is the workhorse of:
- **Analog FM cellular systems** (1G AMPS – Advanced Mobile Phone System).
- **VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal)** networks for corporate data.
- **Satellite TV broadcast** (e.g., traditional C-band and Ku-band DTH uplinks).
- **HF/VHF radio communications** for aviation and maritime services.

It is **not** preferred for:
- **Bursty data traffic** (where TDMA wins).
- **Mobile cellular 2G+** (where TDMA/CDMA win).
- **Spread-spectrum military radios** (where CDMA wins).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Number of FDMA Channels

This is the **single most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 3 exam papers.

### Statement
A satellite transponder has a usable bandwidth of $B_T$ Hz. Each user is allocated a channel of bandwidth $B_c$ Hz, and a guard band of $B_g$ Hz is placed between adjacent channels (and at the two extreme ends of the spectrum). Derive the maximum number of FDMA users $N$ that the transponder can support.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 – Visualize the Spectrum Layout**

Place $N$ channels and $N+1$ guard bands side by side along the frequency axis. The two outer guard bands (one at the lower edge, one at the upper edge) prevent out-of-band leakage to/from neighboring transponders.

```
← B_g →← B_c →← B_g →← B_c →       ...       →← B_c →← B_g →
   1      1      2      2                       N      N
```

**Step 2 – Count the Components**

From the diagram, we can see that there are:
- Exactly $N$ channels of width $B_c$ each.
- Exactly $N + 1$ guard bands of width $B_g$ each.

**Step 3 – Express the Total Bandwidth**

The sum of all channels and guard bands must equal the total usable bandwidth $B_T$:

$$B_T = N \cdot B_c + (N + 1) \cdot B_g$$

**Step 4 – Isolate $N$**

Expand the right-hand side:

$$B_T = N \cdot B_c + N \cdot B_g + B_g$$

Group the $N$ terms:

$$B_T = N (B_c + B_g) + B_g$$

Subtract $B_g$ from both sides:

$$B_T - B_g = N (B_c + B_g)$$

Divide both sides by $(B_c + B_g)$:

$$N = \frac{B_T - B_g}{B_c + B_g}$$

**Step 5 – Apply a More Conservative Formula (KTU Standard Form)**

Many KTU textbooks use a slightly different form that reserves a guard band on **both** ends of the spectrum (so the two edge guard bands are counted separately from the inter-channel guard bands). For an exam, the **most commonly accepted KTU formula** is:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_T - 2 B_g}{B_c} \right\rfloor$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Convention:** Always cross-check the wording of the question. If the problem says *"a guard band of $B_g$ is placed at each end of the total bandwidth"*, use the $B_T - 2B_g$ form. If it says *"guard bands separate adjacent channels"*, use the $B_T - B_g$ form derived above.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

### Problem
A satellite transponder has a usable bandwidth of $36$ **MHz**. Each voice channel occupies $30$ **kHz** after FM modulation, and a $10$ **kHz** guard band is provided between adjacent channels. How many voice channels can the transponder support using FDMA?

### Solution

**Step 1 – Identify the given parameters**

$$B_T = 36 \text{ MHz} = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$$
$$B_c = 30 \text{ kHz} = 30 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$$
$$B_g = 10 \text{ kHz} = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the FDMA channel-counting formula**

Using the conservative KTU form (guard band on each end):

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_T - 2 B_g}{B_c} \right\rfloor$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{36 \times 10^6 - 2 \times 10 \times 10^3}{30 \times 10^3} \right\rfloor$$

Evaluate the numerator:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{36{,}000{,}000 - 20{,}000}{30{,}000} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{35{,}980{,}000}{30{,}000} \right\rfloor$$

$$N = \left\lfloor 1199.33 \right\rfloor = 1199 \text{ channels}$$

**Step 3 – Verification (using the alternate formula)**

Using the alternate form $N = \frac{B_T - B_g}{B_c + B_g}$:

$$N = \frac{36 \times 10^6 - 10 \times 10^3}{30 \times 10^3 + 10 \times 10^3} = \frac{35{,}990{,}000}{40{,}000} = 899.75 \approx 899 \text{ channels}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two formulas give different answers because they assume different physical layouts of the guard bands. **Always state your assumption clearly in the exam** to earn full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Intermodulation Product Frequencies

### Statement
Show that the third-order intermodulation product of two carriers at $f_1$ and $f_2$ falls at $2 f_1 - f_2$, and explain why it is problematic in FDMA.

### Derivation

A non-linear amplifier can be modelled by a Taylor series expansion of its input-output characteristic:

$$v_{out}(t) = a_1 v_{in}(t) + a_2 v_{in}^2(t) + a_3 v_{in}^3(t) + \dots$$

Consider two equal-amplitude carriers applied to this non-linearity:

$$v_{in}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

The cubic term produces:

$$a_3 v_{in}^3(t) = a_3 \left[ A \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A \cos(2\pi f_2 t) \right]^3$$

Expanding the cube and applying trigonometric identities, the third-order products (i.e., products with one cubed term and two linear terms, or three cross-terms) yield frequencies of the form $\pm 2 f_1 \pm f_2$ and $\pm f_1 \pm 2 f_2$. The most damaging in-band product is:

$$f_{IM3} = 2 f_1 - f_2 \quad \text{(and its mirror } 2 f_2 - f_1\text{)}$$

This product **falls within the original FDMA band** (close to $f_1$ and $f_2$) and cannot be filtered out without removing the desired signal itself.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – FDMA vs TDMA vs CDMA (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | **FDMA** | **TDMA** | **CDMA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain of Separation** | Frequency | Time | Code |
| **Channel Allocation** | Static | Dynamic (Bursts) | Dynamic (Pseudo-random) |
| **Synchronization** | Loose (frequency only) | Strict (frame timing) | Very strict (chip-level) |
| **Guard Requirement** | Frequency guard bands | Time guard intervals | Power control |
| **HPA Non-linearity** | **Highly susceptible (IM distortion)** | Less sensitive | Robust (spread spectrum) |
| **Typical Use** | Analog voice, broadcast TV | GSM (2G) digital voice | 3G/4G/5G mobile data |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Moderate | Higher | Highest under heavy load |
| **Complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Susceptibility to Jamming** | High | High | Very low (spread spectrum) |

---

## 3.5 KTU 2024 Concept-Mapping Matrix (Module 3 to Course Outcomes)

| Concept Discussed | Mapped Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| FDMA channel-counting formula | CO3 – Analyze multiple access techniques | Apply / Analyze |
| Intermodulation distortion | CO3 – Identify non-linear impairments | Analyze |
| Comparison FDMA/TDMA/CDMA | CO4 – Compare MA techniques | Evaluate |
| FDMA in satellite transponders | CO3 – Apply to satellite links | Apply |
| Guard band necessity | CO3 – Justify design parameters | Understand / Evaluate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDMA Transmitter & Receiver Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a generic FDMA communication system (uplink transmitter + downlink receiver), as adapted for KTU 2024 Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX["UPLINK TRANSMITTER EARTH STATION"]
        U1["USER 1\nBaseband Signal m1 of t"]:::user
        U2["USER 2\nBaseband Signal m2 of t"]:::user
        UN["USER N\nBaseband Signal mN of t"]:::user
        M1["MODULATOR 1\nCarrier f1"]:::mod
        M2["MODULATOR 2\nCarrier f2"]:::mod
        MN["MODULATOR N\nCarrier fN"]:::mod
        COMB["RF COMBINER\nHybrid Junction Network"]:::comb
        HPA["HIGH POWER AMPLIFIER\nTWTA with OBO"]:::amp
    end

    subgraph CH["TRANSMISSION CHANNEL"]
        SAT["SATELLITE TRANSPONDER\nUplink to Downlink Frequency Translation"]:::sat
    end

    subgraph RX["DOWNLINK RECEIVER EARTH STATION"]
        LNA["LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER\nFront End"]:::lna
        DEMUX["FREQUENCY DEMULTIPLEXER\nBank of Bandpass Filters"]:::demux
        BPF1["BPF Centered at f1"]:::bpf
        BPF2["BPF Centered at f2"]:::bpf
        BPFN["BPF Centered at fN"]:::bpf
        D1["DEMODULATOR 1"]:::demod
        D2["DEMODULATOR 2"]:::demod
        DN["DEMODULATOR N"]:::demod
        O1["USER 1 OUTPUT\nRecovered m1 of t"]:::out
        O2["USER 2 OUTPUT\nRecovered m2 of t"]:::out
        ON["USER N OUTPUT\nRecovered mN of t"]:::out
    end

    U1 --> M1
    U2 --> M2
    UN --> MN
    M1 --> COMB
    M2 --> COMB
    MN --> COMB
    COMB --> HPA
    HPA --> SAT
    SAT --> LNA
    LNA --> DEMUX
    DEMUX --> BPF1
    DEMUX --> BPF2
    DEMUX --> BPFN
    BPF1 --> D1 --> O1
    BPF2 --> D2 --> O2
    BPFN --> DN --> ON

    classDef user fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mod fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comb fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#f08080,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sat fill:#98fb98,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lna fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#bdb76b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef demux fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#8b4789,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bpf fill:#afeeee,stroke:#5f9ea0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef demod fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 FDMA Frequency Spectrum Allocation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FREQ["FREQUENCY AXIS - Total Bandwidth BT"]
        direction LR
        G1["GUARD BAND Bg"]:::gb
        C1["CHANNEL 1\nBandwidth Bc\nCarrier f1"]:::ch
        G2["GUARD BAND Bg"]:::gb
        C2["CHANNEL 2\nBandwidth Bc\nCarrier f2"]:::ch
        G3["GUARD BAND Bg"]:::gb
        C3["CHANNEL 3\nBandwidth Bc\nCarrier f3"]:::ch
        G4["..."]:::gb
        CN["CHANNEL N\nBandwidth Bc\nCarrier fN"]:::ch
        G5["GUARD BAND Bg"]:::gb
    end

    G1 --- C1 --- G2 --- C2 --- G3 --- C3 --- G4 --- CN --- G5

    classDef gb fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow of an FDMA Channel Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Total Bandwidth BT Available"]:::start
    B["STEP 1: Choose Channel Bandwidth Bc\nBased on User Information Rate and Modulation Scheme"]:::step
    C["STEP 2: Choose Guard Band Bg\nBased on Filter Roll Off Characteristics"]:::step
    D["STEP 3: Calculate Number of Channels\nN equals floor of BT minus 2Bg over Bc"]:::step
    E["STEP 4: Assign Unique Carrier Frequency fi\nto each of the N users"]:::step
    F["STEP 5: Check HPA Back Off Requirement\nto Avoid Intermodulation Distortion"]:::step
    G["END: FDMA System Ready for Transmission"]:::end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G

    classDef start fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram Interpretation Notes for KTU 2024

- The **RF Combiner** in the transmitter is typically a *passive* device (lossy hybrid). The composite FDMA signal entering the HPA has a high **peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR)**, which is why the HPA must be backed off.
- The **Demultiplexer** at the receiver is a bank of sharp, narrow bandpass filters — one per FDMA channel. This is the most hardware-intensive part of the receiver.
- The **guard bands** in the spectrum diagram are *not optional*; they are an engineering necessity dictated by the *finite roll-off* of real filters.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are 2-mark short-answer items (often accompanied by a 1-mark follow-up) that test direct recall. The model answers below are intentionally concise to match the KTU valuation key.

---

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA). State any two advantages of FDMA over TDMA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
FDMA is a multiple access technique in which the available transmission bandwidth is divided into several non-overlapping frequency bands, and each band is assigned to a different user for the entire duration of communication.

**Advantages over TDMA (2 Marks – 1 each):**

1. **Simpler transceiver design:** FDMA does not require precise time synchronization between users and the base station; it only needs stable frequency oscillators. TDMA, in contrast, requires strict frame and slot timing.
2. **No need for high-speed switching:** Since each user transmits continuously on its own frequency, the transmitter does not need to rapidly switch between transmit and receive modes (as TDMA does), reducing hardware complexity.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is intermodulation distortion in an FDMA system? How is it mitigated in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
When multiple FDMA carriers pass through the non-linear High Power Amplifier (HPA) of a satellite transponder, the amplifier's non-linearity causes the carriers to multiply, generating spurious sum and difference frequency products. These unwanted products are called **intermodulation (IM) products**, and the resulting corruption of the signal is called **intermodulation distortion**. The third-order product $2f_1 - f_2$ is particularly harmful because it falls within the FDMA band.

**Mitigation (1 Mark):**
The HPA is operated with an **output back-off (OBO)** of several dB below its saturation point, keeping it in a more linear region. This reduces IM products at the cost of amplifier efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of a choice of two. Step-by-step model solutions are provided below.

---

### Question B.A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an FDMA system used in satellite communication. Clearly identify the function of the RF combiner, the High Power Amplifier (HPA), and the frequency demultiplexer at the receiver. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A satellite transponder in the C-band has a usable bandwidth of **36 MHz**. Each voice channel is allocated **30 kHz** and a guard band of **10 kHz** is provided between adjacent channels (and at both edges of the band). Calculate the maximum number of voice channels that the transponder can support using FDMA. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B.A(a)

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
[Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — must be redrawn cleanly in the answer book.]

**Working Explanation (4 Marks – distribute as below):**

1. **Transmitter side (1.5 Marks):** Each of the $N$ users modulates its baseband signal onto a unique carrier frequency $f_i$. The modulated signals are then linearly summed using an **RF combiner** (a passive hybrid junction network). The function of the RF combiner is to add all the modulated signals into a single composite signal without introducing intermodulation, because it is a passive linear device.

2. **High Power Amplifier (1.5 Marks):** The composite FDMA signal is amplified by an **HPA (typically a TWTA in the satellite, or a Klystron on the ground)**. The HPA must be operated in its **linear region with an output back-off** of typically 3–6 dB to avoid generating intermodulation products that would corrupt other users' signals.

3. **Receiver side (1 Mark):** At the receiving earth station, the composite signal is first amplified by an **LNA** and then passed through a **frequency demultiplexer**, which is a bank of sharp bandpass filters. Each filter passes only the bandwidth of one user and rejects all others, allowing the demodulators to recover the original baseband information.

---

#### Model Solution to B.A(b)

**Given (1 Mark):**
$$B_T = 36 \text{ MHz} = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad B_c = 30 \text{ kHz} = 30 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}, \quad B_g = 10 \text{ kHz} = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 1 – Choose the correct formula (1 Mark):**

Since guard bands are provided at both edges of the band as well as between channels, we use:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_T - 2 B_g}{B_c} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 2 – Substitute numerical values (2 Marks):**

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{36 \times 10^6 - 2 \times 10 \times 10^3}{30 \times 10^3} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3 – Evaluate the numerator (1 Mark):**

$$B_T - 2 B_g = 36{,}000{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 35{,}980{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 – Perform the division (1 Mark):**

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{35{,}980{,}000}{30{,}000} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1199.33 \right\rfloor = 1199$$

**Step 5 – State the final answer with units (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{N = 1199 \text{ voice channels}}$$

---

### Question B.B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a)** Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA multiple access techniques based on **seven** parameters of your choice. State one practical example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of intermodulation distortion in an FDMA satellite transponder. Derive the frequency of the most damaging third-order intermodulation product for two carriers at $f_1$ and $f_2$. How is this distortion mitigated in practice? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B.B(a)

**Comparison Table (6 Marks — 1 mark per parameter row):**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain of Separation** | Frequency | Time | Code |
| **Synchronization** | Loose | Strict (frame) | Very strict (chip) |
| **Guard Requirement** | Frequency guard bands | Time guard intervals | Power control |
| **Susceptibility to IM Distortion** | High | Low | Very low |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Moderate | High | Highest under heavy load |
| **Receiver Complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Practical Example (1 mark split)** | 1G AMPS cellular / FM broadcasting | GSM (2G) cellular | IS-95 (3G) / WCDMA / GPS |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Each technique has its own trade-offs; the choice depends on the type of traffic, available bandwidth, and hardware complexity. Modern 4G/5G systems use a hybrid OFDMA/CDMA approach.

---

#### Model Solution to B.B(b)

**Explanation of Intermodulation (2 Marks):**
In an FDMA satellite transponder, multiple carriers share a single High Power Amplifier (HPA). Since the HPA is a non-linear device (especially near saturation), it generates spurious frequency products when multiple carriers pass through it simultaneously. These products corrupt the desired signals and are called intermodulation (IM) products.

**Derivation of the Third-Order IM Product (4 Marks):**

The input to the non-linear HPA is:

$$v_{in}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

The non-linearity is modelled by a power series:

$$v_{out}(t) = a_1 v_{in}(t) + a_2 v_{in}^2(t) + a_3 v_{in}^3(t) + \dots$$

The cubic term is:

$$a_3 v_{in}^3(t) = a_3 \left[ A \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A \cos(2\pi f_2 t) \right]^3$$

Expanding the cube:

$$a_3 v_{in}^3(t) = a_3 \left[ A^3 \cos^3(2\pi f_1 t) + 3 A^3 \cos^2(2\pi f_1 t) \cos(2\pi f_2 t) + 3 A^3 \cos(2\pi f_1 t) \cos^2(2\pi f_2 t) + A^3 \cos^3(2\pi f_2 t) \right]$$

Using the trigonometric identities:

$$\cos^3(x) = \frac{3}{4}\cos(x) + \frac{1}{4}\cos(3x)$$
$$\cos^2(x)\cos(y) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(y) + \frac{1}{4}\cos(2x + y) + \frac{1}{4}\cos(2x - y)$$

Substituting and collecting the third-order terms (i.e., terms involving three carrier frequencies in total), we obtain frequencies of the form:

$$3f_1, \quad 3f_2, \quad 2f_1 + f_2, \quad 2f_1 - f_2, \quad f_1 + 2f_2, \quad f_1 - 2f_2$$

Among these, the **most damaging** product (because it falls *inside* the FDMA band, close to the original carriers) is:

$$\boxed{f_{IM3} = 2f_1 - f_2}$$

**Mitigation in Practice (1 Mark):**
The HPA is operated with an **output back-off (OBO)** of 3 to 6 dB below its saturation point. This keeps the amplifier in a more linear region, reducing IM power, at the cost of reduced DC-to-RF conversion efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Frequently Lose Marks on FDMA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Formula Confusion (–2 Marks):** Mixing up the two channel-counting formulas. **Fix:** Always draw the spectrum layout first and count the guard bands explicitly.
> 2. **Missing Units (–1 Mark):** Writing a numerical answer without units (Hz, kHz, MHz). **Fix:** Always append the unit to every numerical answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the Floor Function (–1 Mark):** Reporting $N = 1199.33$ instead of $N = 1199$. **Fix:** You cannot have a fractional channel; always use $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$.
> 4. **No Block Diagram (–2 to –3 Marks):** A "neat block diagram" question in KTU carries dedicated marks (typically 3 out of 7) for the diagram itself. **Fix:** Always redraw the FDMA block diagram, even if the question does not explicitly ask for it.
> 5. **Confusing OBO and IBO (–1 Mark):** Output Back-Off (OBO) and Input Back-Off (IBO) are *different* quantities; relate them via the amplifier's AM/AM characteristic.
> 6. **Skipping Trigonometric Expansion (–2 Marks):** In the IM derivation, students often jump straight to the answer $2f_1 - f_2$ without showing the cubic expansion. **Fix:** Show every algebraic step in the answer book.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this section the night before the exam.**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **FDMA = Frequency Domain Separation** of multiple users sharing a common medium.
- [x] Each user gets a **unique, static carrier frequency** and a **dedicated frequency sub-band**.
- [x] All users transmit **simultaneously and continuously** (in pure FDMA).
- [x] FDMA is the **oldest MA technique**; used in 1G cellular, FM radio, and analog satellite links.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- [x] $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_T - 2 B_g}{B_c} \right\rfloor$ — number of FDMA channels
- [x] $s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_i t)$ — composite FDMA signal
- [x] $f_{IM3} = 2 f_1 - f_2$ — most damaging third-order intermodulation product
- [x] $B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ — Carson's rule for FM channel bandwidth

### Critical Engineering Trade-offs
- [x] **Advantage:** Simple transceivers, no time synchronization.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Susceptible to intermodulation distortion in the HPA.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Wastes bandwidth in guard bands.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Inflexible — cannot dynamically reallocate capacity.
- [x] **Mitigation:** Operate HPA with back-off (3–6 dB).

### Common KTU Exam Questions
- [x] Derive the FDMA channel-counting formula.
- [x] Draw and explain the FDMA block diagram.
- [x] Derive the third-order intermodulation product.
- [x] Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA on 5–7 parameters.
- [x] Numerically compute the number of voice channels in a transponder.

### Mnemonics
- [x] **"FDMA = Frequency First"** — separation is in frequency, not time or code.
- [x] **"Guard bands are not optional"** — they are the price of finite filter roll-off.
- [x] **"TWTA + multiple carriers = IM disaster"** — always back off the HPA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TDMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TDMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** is a channel access method for shared-medium networks (typically radio communication systems) that allows multiple users to share the same frequency band by allocating **distinct, non-overlapping time slots** to each user within a repeating time frame. Each user transmits and receives information in rapid succession, one after the other, using the **entire allocated bandwidth** during their assigned slot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *TDMA is a multiple access technique in which the available time domain is divided into mutually exclusive time slots, and each user is assigned one or more specific time slots within a TDMA frame. All users operate on the **same carrier frequency**, but at different instants of time.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single one-way road** (the frequency band) that is shared by **ten cars** (the users) who all need to travel from point A to point B. If everyone tried to drive at the same time, there would be a massive traffic jam (collision/interference). 

**TDMA solution:** A traffic controller stands in the middle and gives each car a **specific 1-minute window** during which it has the *exclusive right* to use the entire road. Car 1 uses the road from 0:00–0:01, Car 2 from 0:01–0:02, and so on. After Car 10 finishes, the cycle restarts with Car 1.

- The **road (bandwidth)** is the same.
- The **time (slot)** is what is divided.
- No two cars are ever on the road at the same instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** In TDMA, **time is shared, but bandwidth is not**. Every user gets the full bandwidth, but only for a brief slice of time. This is the *exact opposite* of **FDMA**, where *bandwidth is shared, but time is continuous for each user*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Frame Period ($T_f$):** Typically **4.615 ms** in GSM (the most popular TDMA standard).
- **Number of Slots per Frame:** GSM uses **8 time slots** per carrier.
- **Carrier Bandwidth:** **200 kHz** per RF channel in GSM.
- **Bit Period (GSM):** Approximately **3.69 μs** per bit.
- **Guard Period:** Small time gap between successive bursts to prevent overlap (typically ~30 μs in GSM).

> [!NOTE]
> These GSM values are *frequently asked* in KTU board exams. Memorize them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of TDMA

The TDMA mechanism can be understood through a structured 5-step logical flow:

- **Step 1 — Bandwidth Allocation:** The total available system bandwidth ($B_{total}$) is allocated to a single RF carrier frequency. Unlike FDMA, no frequency subdivision is performed here.

- **Step 2 — Frame Construction:** Time on the channel is divided into recurring **frames** of fixed duration $T_f$. Each frame is the *fundamental period* of the TDMA cycle.

- **Step 3 — Slot Assignment:** Each frame is further subdivided into $N$ non-overlapping **time slots** ($TS_1, TS_2, \dots, TS_N$). User $i$ is uniquely assigned slot $i$ (or a set of slots).

- **Step 4 — Burst Transmission:** During its assigned slot, the user transmits a **burst** of data using the entire system bandwidth. The burst is short, high-speed, and is then followed by a **guard time** to absorb timing inaccuracies.

- **Step 5 — Synchronization & Recovery:** A **preamble** (training sequence) at the start of each burst allows the receiver to perform synchronization, equalization, and channel estimation before actual data decoding.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why frames?** Frames allow periodic, predictable access — essential for real-time voice traffic.
- **Why guard times?** Propagations delays and clock drifts cause bursts to "bleed" into adjacent slots. A small silent interval prevents inter-slot interference.
- **Why a preamble?** Because users transmit *intermittently*, the receiver must re-learn the channel conditions (fading, multipath) on every burst.

### 2.3 TDMA Frame Structure (GSM-style)

A typical TDMA frame consists of:
1. **Data payload** (user information bits).
2. **Training sequence / midamble** (known bits for equalization).
3. **Tail bits & guard period** (prevent overlap).
4. **Control signaling bits** (SACCH, stealing flags, etc.).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Frame Duration** | $T_f$ | seconds (s) | Total time for one complete cycle of $N$ users. |
| 2 | **Number of Slots per Frame** | $N$ | dimensionless | Number of users that can be supported per carrier. |
| 3 | **Slot Duration** | $T_s = \dfrac{T_f}{N}$ | seconds (s) | Time allocated to one user per frame. |
| 4 | **Burst / Symbol Rate** | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | bits/second (bps) | Each user transmits at this elevated rate *during* its slot. |
| 5 | **Effective Data Rate per User** | $R_{user} = \dfrac{R_b \cdot N_{data}}{N}$ | bps | $N_{data}$ = number of info bits per slot. |
| 6 | **Frame Efficiency** | $\eta_f = \dfrac{N \cdot T_{payload}}{T_f}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Excludes guard time. |
| 7 | **Overall TDMA Efficiency** | $\eta_{TDMA} = \dfrac{T_{payload}}{T_s} = 1 - \dfrac{T_g}{T_s}$ | dimensionless | $T_g$ = guard time. |
