Larger $N$ → less capacity → better CCI. - **To avoid ACI in a single cell:** never assign adjacent channels to the same cell; keep at least one channel gap. - **Transmitted power cancels** in the C/I ratio — C/I is a purely geometric quantity, not a power-engineering quantity. - **System capacity** $\propto \sqrt{1/N}$; doubling capacity requires reducing $N$ by a factor of 4. - **Practical MNOs** (Jio, Airtel, Vi) currently use $N = 1$ (full frequency reuse) in LTE/5G with **OFDM + ICIC** (Inter-Cell Interference Coordination) instead of relying on large $N$ — but the underlying C/I theory remains the foundation.

topics.title: Cell Splitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cell Splitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cell Splitting** is a fundamental capacity-enhancement technique in cellular mobile communication systems in which a high-traffic, congested cell is sub-divided into multiple smaller cells (commonly called **microcells**), each served by its own **Base Station (BS)** operating at a *reduced antenna height* and a *reduced transmitted power*. The new smaller cells retain the same frequency-reuse plan as the parent cell, but because the total number of cells covering a given geographical area increases, the **frequency-reuse factor per unit area rises**, and the **system capacity (Erlangs/km²)** is multiplied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Cell Splitting**
> Cell splitting belongs to the broader family of *capacity-expansion* techniques in cellular system design. It is invariably tested as a numerical/analytical question (Part B, 7–14 marks) involving power scaling, co-channel reuse distance, and interference analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Bottleneck

Imagine a single very popular restaurant that seats **100 customers** and is perpetually full. The owner cannot expand the building outward (legal constraints). The only way to serve more customers is to:
1. **Partition the dining hall into 4 smaller halls of 25 seats each**, each with its own kitchen counter and service staff (analogous to a new base station per cell).
2. **Each new hall uses the same menu** (analogous to the same channel set being reused).
3. **The serving radius of each waiter is smaller** (analogous to *reduced transmit power*).

Total seating capacity rises from 100 to **400**, even though the building footprint never changed. Cell splitting does the same to a cellular network: it *densifies* the radio infrastructure inside the same geographical footprint.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Cell Splitting

- **Original Cell Radius** = $R$
- **Splitting (Diameter-Reduction) Factor** = $k$ (commonly $k = 2$)
- **New Cell Radius** = $R_{new} = R / k$
- **Path-Loss Exponent** = $n$ (typically $2$ in free space, $3$–$4$ in urban environments)
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio** = $q = D / R = \sqrt{3N}$
- **Received Power Threshold** = $P_{r,min}$ (the **receiver sensitivity floor**, often around **$-110$ dBm** for cellular handsets)
- **Minimum Carrier-to-Interference Ratio** = $C/I_{min}$ (often **$18$ dB** for analog AMPS, **$7$ dB** for digital GSM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The golden rule of cell splitting: *Received power at the new cell boundary MUST equal the received power at the old cell boundary.* Otherwise coverage holes appear at the cell edges, and the system fails. This single condition dictates the transmitter-power scaling law.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Cell Splitting Works

Cell splitting exploits three coupled engineering observations:

1. **Traffic is non-uniform.** Urban cores, stadiums, and highways saturate, while rural cells remain underloaded. Splitting is applied *locally* to hotspots, not globally.
2. **Co-channel interference scales with the reuse distance.** Smaller cells (radius $R/k$) tolerate a proportionally smaller $D$, allowing more cells per cluster to coexist.
3. **The signal-to-interference ratio ($C/I$) is preserved** if $D$ and $R$ are scaled by the same factor, so quality of service is unchanged.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Engineering Procedure

| Step | Action | Underlying Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Identify cells where blocking probability exceeds the design threshold (typically **$2$%** for cellular). | Locates the genuine capacity bottleneck. |
| **2** | Choose a splitting factor $k$ (most commonly $k=2$, halving the radius). | Geometric compatibility with the hexagonal grid. |
| **3** | Determine new BS positions at the centers of the new microcells. | The new topology must still tile the coverage area without gaps. |
| **4** | Scale the transmitted power: $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} / k^{n}$. | Keeps the boundary received power constant. |
| **5** | Reduce the antenna height at the new BSs. | Prevents overshoot beyond the smaller cell boundary. |
| **6** | Re-allocate channels to the new microcells. | Maintains the original $C/I$ budget. |
| **7** | Perform *channel re-homing* and re-define the neighbour lists in the Mobile Switching Center (MSC). | Required to support the now-higher handoff rate. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cell Splitting Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are **algebraic**, not decibel. Convert to dB **only** at the final answer stage. The vertical-bar symbol $\vert$ has been deliberately written with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols | Typical KTU Value |
| :- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | New cell radius after splitting | $R_{new} = R_{old} / k$ | $k$ = split factor | $k = 2$ |
| 2 | New transmitted power (received-power equalisation) | $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} / k^{n}$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent | $n = 4$ (urban) |
| 3 | New co-channel reuse distance | $D_{new} = D_{old} / k$ | — | — |
| 4 | Co-channel reuse ratio (preserved) | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | $N$ = cluster size | $N = 7$ |
| 5 | Carrier-to-Interference ratio (preserved) | $C/I = \dfrac{q^{n}}{i_{0}}$ | $i_{0}$ = first-tier co-channel cells = 6 | $C/I \geq 18$ dB (analog) |
| 6 | Number of cells per cluster | $N = \dfrac{i_{0}^{2/n}}{3}$ | For $n = 4, i_0 = 6$ | $N = 7$ |
| 7 | Capacity-multiplication factor | $C_{new} / C_{old} = k^{2}$ | From area scaling | $4$ when $k=2$ |
| 8 | Handoff rate per call | $\lambda_{HO} \propto \dfrac{v \cdot k}{R}$ | $v$ = mobile velocity | — |
| 9 | Free-space path loss (used in derivations) | $L_{FS} = \left( \dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength | — |
| 10 | General power-law path loss | $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}$ | $K$ = constant | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **4G/5G Heterogeneous Networks (HetNets):** Modern LTE/5G deployments use cell-splitting principles in *small-cell* and *femtocell* layering.
- **Traffic Hot-spot Relief:** Operators routinely split macrocells around airports, malls, and cricket stadiums during events.
- **Indoor Penetration:** The reduced antenna height of a microcell improves in-building coverage by reducing the floor-to-floor path loss.
- **Spectrum Re-farming:** When an operator migrates from 2G/3G to 4G/5G, the legacy cell grid is *re-split* to deliver higher spectral efficiency per MHz.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transmit-Power Scaling Law

**Statement:** *If a cell of radius $R$ is split into $k$ concentric microcells (new radius $R_{new} = R/k$), and the received power at the new cell boundary must equal the received power at the old cell boundary, derive the required scaling of the transmitted power.*

**Step 1 — State the received-power model.** Using the general power-law path loss:

$$P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}$$

where $K$ absorbs antenna gains and wavelength terms, and $n$ is the path-loss exponent.

**Step 2 — Write the equality of received power at the two boundaries.**

$$P_{r,old} = P_{r,new}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the model at each boundary.**

$$P_{t,old} \cdot K \cdot R^{-n} = P_{t,new} \cdot K \cdot \left( \dfrac{R}{k} \right)^{-n}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $K$.**

$$P_{t,old} \cdot R^{-n} = P_{t,new} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R}{k} \right)^{-n}$$

**Step 5 — Invert the right-hand-side power using the identity $(a/b)^{-n} = (b/a)^{n}$.**

$$P_{t,old} \cdot R^{-n} = P_{t,new} \cdot k^{n} \cdot R^{-n}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $R^{-n}$ from both sides.**

$$P_{t,old} = P_{t,new} \cdot k^{n}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the new transmitted power.**

$$\boxed{\,P_{t,new} = \dfrac{P_{t,old}}{k^{n}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick numerical sanity check (KTU favourite):** For $k=2$ and $n=4$, $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old}/16$, i.e. a **12 dB power reduction**. For $k=2$ and $n=2$, $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old}/4$, i.e. a **6 dB power reduction**. These two numbers are the *most common* numerical answers in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Co-Channel Reuse Distance After Splitting

**Statement:** *Show that after cell splitting by factor $k$, the co-channel reuse distance is also reduced by the same factor $k$, thereby preserving the $C/I$ ratio.*

**Step 1 — Recall the co-channel reuse ratio in a hexagonal layout.**

$$q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

**Step 2 — Impose the condition that $q$ must be preserved after splitting.** This is the *interference invariance* principle that guarantees the $C/I$ budget is unaffected by the geometry change.

$$\dfrac{D_{new}}{R_{new}} = \dfrac{D_{old}}{R_{old}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R_{new} = R_{old}/k$.**

$$\dfrac{D_{new}}{R_{old}/k} = \dfrac{D_{old}}{R_{old}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $R_{old}/k$.**

$$D_{new} = \dfrac{D_{old}}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\,D_{new} = \dfrac{D_{old}}{k}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the $C/I$ invariance** with $i_{0}=6$ first-tier co-channel interferers:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{q^{n}}{i_{0}} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{6}$$

Since $q$ is unchanged by construction, $C/I$ is also unchanged. **Quality of service is preserved**, but the system now hosts $k^{2}$ times as many cells per unit area.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity-Multiplication Factor

**Statement:** *Quantify the increase in system capacity (channels per unit area) when a cell of area $A$ is split into $k^{2}$ microcells.*

**Step 1 — Original capacity (channels per unit area).** Each cell of area $A_{cell}$ carries $M$ channels. There are $1/A_{cell}$ cells per unit area.

$$C_{old} = \dfrac{M}{A_{cell}} \quad \text{[channels/km}^{2}\text{]}$$

**Step 2 — New cell area** scales with the square of the radius reduction:

$$A_{cell,new} = \pi \left( \dfrac{R}{k} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{A_{cell,old}}{k^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — New number of cells per unit area** scales inversely:

$$\dfrac{1}{A_{cell,new}} = \dfrac{k^{2}}{A_{cell,old}}$$

**Step 4 — New capacity** (assuming the same $M$ per cell — *in practice* extra capacity also comes from sub-channel borrowing, but the geometric gain alone is):

$$C_{new} = \dfrac{M}{A_{cell,new}} = M \cdot \dfrac{k^{2}}{A_{cell,old}} = k^{2} \cdot C_{old}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{C_{new}}{C_{old}} = k^{2}\,}$$

For $k=2$, capacity is quadrupled purely by geometry — *before* any channel-borrowing or sectorisation gains.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Handoff-Rate Penalty

**Statement:** *Demonstrate that cell splitting increases the handoff rate per call, motivating the use of soft handoff in CDMA and 4G/5G.*

**Step 1 — Crossing-time across a cell of radius $R$ for a mobile moving at velocity $v$:**

$$T_{cell} = \dfrac{2R}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Number of handoffs per call** for a total travel time $T_{call}$:

$$N_{HO} = \dfrac{T_{cell,old}}{T_{call}} = \dfrac{v \cdot T_{call}}{2 R_{old}}$$

**Step 3 — After splitting, $R_{new} = R_{old}/k$:**

$$N_{HO,new} = \dfrac{v \cdot T_{call}}{2 R_{new}} = \dfrac{k \cdot v \cdot T_{call}}{2 R_{old}} = k \cdot N_{HO,old}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{N_{HO,new}}{N_{HO,old}} = k\,}$$

For $k=2$, handoffs *double* per call — a critical burden on MSC signalling. Soft-handoff algorithms in CDMA, and X2/S1 signalling in LTE, exist specifically to handle this penalty.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Effects of Cell Splitting by Factor $k$

| System Parameter | Old Cell | New Cell | Multiplicative Effect | Engineering Implication |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Cell radius | $R$ | $R/k$ | $\div k$ | Smaller coverage area |
| Cell area | $\pi R^{2}$ | $\pi R^{2}/k^{2}$ | $\div k^{2}$ | Denser infrastructure |
| Transmit power (for $n=4$) | $P_t$ | $P_t/k^{4}$ | $\div k^{4}$ | Major power saving |
| Co-channel reuse distance | $D$ | $D/k$ | $\div k$ | Tighter frequency reuse |
| Co-channel reuse ratio $q$ | $D/R$ | $(D/k)/(R/k)=D/R$ | unchanged | $C/I$ preserved |
| Capacity (channels/area) | $C$ | $C \cdot k^{2}$ | $\times k^{2}$ | $4\times$ for $k=2$ |
| Handoffs per call | $N_{HO}$ | $k \cdot N_{HO}$ | $\times k$ | More signalling load |
| Number of BSs per unit area | $1/A$ | $k^{2}/A$ | $\times k^{2}$ | Higher capex |
| Site-acquisition complexity | low | high | — | Real-estate bottleneck |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cell Splitting

The Mermaid diagram below models the decision-flow and engineering sequence a Network Planning Engineer follows when activating a cell-splitting procedure in a live cellular network. It traces inputs (traffic data), decision nodes (congestion threshold), and outputs (new microcell deployment) in a top-down engineering workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Traffic Monitoring] --> A1[Collect Blocking Probability per Cell]
    A1 --> A2{Blocking Probability > 2 percent?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[No Action Required]
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Identify Hotspot Cell Cluster]
    A4 --> A5[Choose Splitting Factor k]
    A5 --> A6[Compute New Radius R_new = R / k]
    A6 --> A7[Compute New Tx Power P_t_new = P_t_old / k^n]
    A7 --> A8[Select Microcell BS Sites]
    A8 --> A9[Scale Down Antenna Height]
    A9 --> A10[Reallocate Frequency Channels]
    A10 --> A11[Update MSC Handoff Tables]
    A11 --> A12[Update Neighbour Lists]
    A12 --> A13[Re-run C / I Verification]
    A13 --> A14{C / I above threshold?}
    A14 -- No --> A15[Adjust Sectorisation or Cluster Size]
    A15 --> A13
    A14 -- Yes --> A16[Split Complete and Online]
    A3 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Logical Flow — Why Co-Channel Reuse Survives a Cell Split

The second diagram isolates the *interference-invariance* argument that is the theoretical backbone of cell splitting. It shows how the *ratio* $D/R$ is preserved, even though both $D$ and $R$ shrink in lock-step.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Old Cell Radius R_old] --> B1[Compute D_old = q times R_old]
    B2[New Cell Radius R_new = R_old over k] --> B3[Compute D_new = q times R_new]
    B1 --> B4[Compute q_old = D_old over R_old]
    B3 --> B5[Compute q_new = D_new over R_new]
    B4 --> B6[Check: q_old equals q_new]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 -- Yes --> B7[C over I is Preserved]
    B6 -- No --> B8[Re-tune Cluster Size N]
    B8 --> B3
```

### 4.3 Capacity-Multiplication Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Original Macrocell Coverage Area A] --> C1[Split into 4 Microcells for k=2]
    C1 --> C2[Each microcell has R_new = R_old over 2]
    C2 --> C3[Each microcell has P_t_new = P_t_old over 16 for n=4]
    C3 --> C4[Capacity multiplied by 4]
    C4 --> C5[Same Footprint, 4x Erlangs per sq km]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams intentionally abstract away the *physical* hexagonal tiling in favour of a *functional* representation, which is the KTU-expected convention for system-engineering flowcharts in the Communication Engineering paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define cell splitting. State two advantages and one disadvantage of the cell-splitting technique in cellular systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cell splitting is the process of subdividing a congested cell in a cellular network into smaller cells (microcells), each with its own base station operating at a reduced antenna height and reduced transmitted power, in order to increase the system capacity (channels per unit area) while preserving the carrier-to-interference ratio.

**Advantages:**

1. **Increased capacity:** The number of cells per unit area scales as $k^{2}$, multiplying the available channels per square kilometre by $k^{2}$ (e.g. $4\times$ for $k=2$).
2. **Improved signal quality at the cell edge:** Lower transmit power combined with a shorter propagation distance raises the received power at the new boundary, improving link budget.

**Disadvantage:**

1. **Higher handoff rate:** Handoffs per call scale as $k$, increasing signalling load on the MSC and the risk of dropped calls unless soft-handoff mechanisms are used.

*(Examiner will award full 3 marks only if all three points — definition, two advantages, one disadvantage — are explicitly written.)*

---

#### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**When a cell of radius $R$ is split into microcells of radius $R/2$, derive the relationship between the new transmitted power and the old transmitted power. Assume a path-loss exponent of $n = 4$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** For received-power equalisation at the two cell boundaries:

$$P_{t,old} \cdot R^{-4} = P_{t,new} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R}{2} \right)^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging:

$$P_{t,new} = \dfrac{P_{t,old}}{2^{4}} = \dfrac{P_{t,old}}{16}$$

**Step 3:** In decibels, this is a **$10 \log_{10}(1/16) \approx -12$ dB** reduction.

**Final Answer:** $\;P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} / 16\;$ (or equivalently a $12$ dB drop).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Cell Splitting**
> Students consistently lose marks on the following pitfalls:
> 1. **Forgetting the path-loss exponent $n$.** The transmit-power scaling is $k^{n}$, not $k^{2}$ unless the problem explicitly states free-space propagation.
> 2. **Confusing $D$ (co-channel reuse distance) with $R$ (cell radius).** Many students wrongly halve only one of them, breaking the $C/I$ invariance.
> 3. **Skipping the dB conversion** when the answer must be expressed in dB/dBm.
> 4. **Omitting the handoff-rate consequence** — a favourite 3-mark follow-up.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** that received power at the new boundary equals that at the old boundary. This is the *hypothesis* the entire derivation rests on.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A cellular system uses a cluster size $N = 7$ with a cell radius $R = 4$ km and transmitted power $P_{t} = 40$ W. The path-loss exponent in the operating environment is $n = 4$. The operator wishes to **double the system capacity** by splitting the cells. Calculate:

1. The new cell radius $R_{new}$.
2. The new transmitted power $P_{t,new}$ (in watts and in dBW).
3. The new co-channel reuse distance $D_{new}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the cell-splitting described in part (a), compute:

1. The percentage *increase* in capacity (channels per km²).
2. The percentage *increase* in handoffs per call for a mobile moving at constant velocity across the coverage area.
3. State *two* practical challenges an operator faces while implementing this split.

**Model Solution for Part A(a):**

**Step 1 — Doubling the capacity** means a multiplication factor of $2$ in capacity, i.e. $k^{2} = 2$, so $k = \sqrt{2}$. (Note: doubling the *number of channels per cell* is a different problem; the question says double the *system capacity*.)

**Step 2 — New cell radius:**

$$R_{new} = \dfrac{R}{k} = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — New transmitted power:**

$$P_{t,new} = \dfrac{P_{t,old}}{k^{n}} = \dfrac{40}{(\sqrt{2})^{4}} = \dfrac{40}{4} = 10 \text{ W}$$

In dBW: $\;10 \log_{10}(10) = 10$ dBW.

**Step 4 — Old co-channel reuse distance:**

$$D_{old} = R_{old} \cdot \sqrt{3N} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{21} \approx 4 \times 4.583 \approx 18.33 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — New co-channel reuse distance (using $D_{new} = D_{old}/k$):**

$$D_{new} = \dfrac{18.33}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 12.96 \text{ km}$$

**Model Solution for Part A(b):**

**Step 1 — Capacity increase:**

$$\dfrac{C_{new}}{C_{old}} = k^{2} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{100% increase}$$

**Step 2 — Handoff-rate increase:**

$$\dfrac{N_{HO,new}}{N_{HO,old}} = k = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

Percentage increase $\approx 41.4\%$.

**Step 3 — Practical challenges (any two):**

1. **Site acquisition** — doubling the number of BS sites in a dense urban area is non-trivial due to real-estate and zoning constraints.
2. **Handoff-signalling storm** — $\sim 41\%$ more handoffs per call overload the MSC.
3. **Antenna-height adjustment** — Lowering antenna height on already-deployed towers may require re-engineering the mechanical tilt and downtilt settings.
4. **Channel re-allocation** — Pre-existing channel assignments must be redistributed, a complex combinatorial problem with $N = 7$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of cell splitting** with a neat sketch. Why is it necessary to reduce the transmitted power of the new base stations? Derive the relationship between the old and the new transmitted power when the cell radius is halved and the path-loss exponent is $n = 3$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cellular network originally operates with cluster size $N = 4$, cell radius $R = 10$ km, and $M = 100$ channels per cell. After splitting each cell into 4 microcells (i.e. $k = 2$), determine:

1. The new cluster size if the $C/I$ budget must remain unchanged and the path-loss exponent is $n = 4$.
2. The total number of channels available to the system in a $100$ km $\times 100$ km service area, *before* and *after* splitting.
3. Comment on the trade-off between capacity gain and infrastructure cost.

**Model Solution for Part B(a):**

**Concept:** Cell splitting subdivides a high-traffic cell into smaller microcells, each with its own BS. The original transmission power would over-shoot the smaller cell boundary, causing *co-channel interference* with neighbouring microcells that reuse the same frequency. Hence, the transmit power is reduced in proportion to the radius reduction raised to the path-loss exponent $n$.

**Derivation (with $R_{new} = R/2$ and $n=3$):**

$$P_{t,old} \cdot R^{-3} = P_{t,new} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R}{2} \right)^{-3}$$

$$P_{t,new} = \dfrac{P_{t,old}}{2^{3}} = \dfrac{P_{t,old}}{8}$$

In dB: a **$10 \log_{10}(1/8) \approx -9$ dB** reduction.

**Model Solution for Part B(b):**

**Step 1 — The cluster size $N$ is determined by the $C/I$ requirement.** Since $C/I = q^{n}/6$ and $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$, the cluster size is *uniquely* determined by $n$ alone, *not* by the cell radius. Hence the new $N$ for $n = 4$ is still $N = 7$ (using $N = i_{0}^{2/n}/3 = 6^{2/4}/3 \approx 7$).

**Step 2 — Total channels in $100 \times 100 = 10{,}000$ km² area:**

*Before splitting:*

- Cell area: $A = 2.5981 \cdot R^{2} = 2.5981 \times 100 = 259.81$ km²
- Number of cells: $10{,}000 / 259.81 \approx 38.5 \Rightarrow 38$ cells
- Channels per cluster: $M = 100$
- Total system channels: $38 \times 100 = 3800$

*After splitting:* The cell area becomes $A_{new} = A/4 = 64.95$ km², so the number of cells quadruples to $\approx 154$ cells. Each cell still carries $M = 100$ channels (same cluster), so total system channels become $154 \times 100 = 15{,}400$.

**Step 3 — Capacity gain:** $15{,}400 / 3{,}800 \approx 4.05$, confirming the expected $k^{2} = 4$ factor.

**Step 4 — Trade-off:** The four-fold capacity gain comes at the cost of quadrupling the number of base stations, antenna installations, backhaul links, and site-acquisition efforts. Operators must therefore split only where traffic demand justifies the capex.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (must be word-perfect):** *Cell splitting is the subdivision of a congested cell into smaller cells (microcells), each with its own base station and reduced transmit power and antenna height, to increase system capacity.*
- **Master formula (always quoted first):** $\;P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} / k^{n}\;$
- **For $k=2, n=4$:** $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old}/16$ ($-12$ dB). This is the **most-tested numerical**.
- **For $k=2, n=2$:** $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old}/4$ ($-6$ dB).
- **Co-channel reuse distance also scales:** $D_{new} = D_{old}/k$, preserving $C/I$.
- **Capacity gain:** $k^{2}$ (area argument). For $k=2$, capacity is **quadrupled**.
- **Handoff penalty:** Handoffs per call scale as $k$ — *not* as $k^{2}$.
- **Cluster size $N$ is governed by $n$ alone**, *not* by $R$. The $N$ does not change after splitting.
- **Practical challenges:** Site acquisition, antenna-height adjustment, channel re-allocation, MSC signalling.
- **Receiver sensitivity floor:** New power levels must still clear the receiver's $P_{r,min}$ (typically $-110$ dBm).
- **Receiver threshold $C/I$:** Must remain $\geq 18$ dB (analog) or $\geq 7$ dB (digital) after the split.
- **Draw the diagram first** in the answer booklet — examiners reward visual clarity, especially for the cell-splitting sketch.
- **Always state the received-power-equalisation assumption** before deriving the power formula — marks are reserved for it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-mile revision:** Memorise the four boxed results — (i) $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old}/k^{n}$, (ii) $D_{new} = D_{old}/k$, (iii) $C_{new}/C_{old} = k^{2}$, and (iv) $N_{HO,new}/N_{HO,old} = k$. They are the *core deliverables* in any Part B question on cell splitting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sectoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sectoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sectoring** is a spatial-domain capacity-enhancement technique employed in **cellular mobile communication systems** in which a single hexagonal cell is sub-divided into multiple angular wedge-shaped regions called **sectors**, each illuminated by a **directional (sectoral) antenna** with a defined horizontal beam-width (typically **120°** or **60°**), thereby replacing a single centrally-located **omnidirectional (360°) base-station antenna**.

By deliberately restricting the radiation pattern of the base-station antenna to a narrow angular sector, the number of **co-channel interfering base stations** that fall within the main beam of the desired signal is drastically reduced from **6 (omnidirectional case)** to **2 (for 120° sectoring)** or **1 (for 60° sectoring)**. This directly improves the **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (S/I)** and enables a tighter **frequency-reuse distance**, multiplying the **system capacity** by the sectoring factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Cellular Concepts):** Sectoring is grouped under *capacity-enhancement techniques* alongside **Cell Splitting**, **Cell Sectoring**, and **Repeaters / Microcells**. It is a high-weightage topic and is almost always coupled with a numerical on **co-channel interference reduction** or **capacity improvement factor**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lighthouse on a Roundabout"

Imagine a busy **roundabout (traffic circle)** with six feeder roads entering it from six directions. If you place a **single loudspeaker at the centre** and broadcast an announcement in **all 360°**, the message reaches every road, but it is **interfered with** by announcements coming from the five *other* roundabouts' speakers — total chaos, very low **S/I**.

Now, replace that single loudspeaker with **three directional horns** — one pointing towards roads 1 & 2, the second towards roads 3 & 4, and the third towards roads 5 & 6. Each horn's beam is only **120° wide**. Now, only **one of the two interfering roundabouts** (the one whose horn is pointing towards you) can interfere with your message. The other five are now **spatially filtered out**.

> This is exactly what **3-sector (120°) sectoring** achieves in a cellular layout. The hexagonal cell is the roundabout, and the directional horns are the **sectoral antennas** at the **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Industry / 3GPP Defaults)

| Metric | Standard Value (3-sector) | Standard Value (6-sector) |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Beamwidth | **120°** | **60°** |
| Sectors per Cell | **3** | **6** |
| Front-to-Back Ratio | > **20 dB** | > **30 dB** |
| Gain over Omni | ~ **5–6 dBi** | ~ **8–9 dBi** |
| Co-channel interferers (first tier) | **2** | **1** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / KPI:** The theoretical maximum **capacity-improvement factor** of a perfectly-sectored system equals the **number of sectors (N)**. In practice, the gain is **2.5× for 3-sector** and **4.5× for 6-sector** due to antenna side-lobe leakage, soft-handoff overhead, and trunking inefficiency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Sectoring is Needed — The Underlying Problem

In a standard **omni-directional hexagonal cellular layout** with a cluster size of **N = 7**, the **first tier of co-channel cells** contains exactly **6 interfering base stations**. The **downlink Signal-to-Interference Ratio** in a fully-loaded system is:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{omni}} = \frac{1}{6 \cdot (q^{-4})}
$$

where **q = D / R** is the **co-channel reuse ratio**. For a typical *N = 7* layout, *q = 4.583*, giving *S/I ≈ 18.7* (≈ **12.7 dB**), which is just above the **18 dB** minimum threshold for **4-QAM voice-grade FM** in legacy AMPS.

**The problem:** To push capacity further, we want to *reduce the cluster size N*, but this lowers *q*, which lowers *S/I* below the threshold. **Sectoring re-uses spatial directionality to recover the lost S/I** without increasing N.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Replace** the central omni-directional antenna at the BTS with **N directional antennas** (N = 3 or 6).
2. Each directional antenna has a horizontal **beamwidth = 360° / N** and a high **front-to-back ratio (FTBR)**.
3. Because the antenna pattern is now restricted, of the 6 co-channel cells in the first tier, **only those whose main beam points towards the desired sector** can produce co-channel interference.
4. For a **regular hexagonal layout with 120° sectoring**, the number of first-tier interferers drops from **6 → 2**.
5. For **60° sectoring**, the number drops to **1**.
6. The new **S/I** improves by a factor equal to the ratio of original to new interferers, *i.e.,* **6/2 = 3** for 3-sectoring, and **6/1 = 6** for 6-sectoring.
7. This S/I gain can be traded for a **smaller cluster size (lower N)**, yielding a **capacity gain** of the same factor.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorise every entry below.** The marks allotted (3–7 per question) in KTU ESE always test one of these.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Co-channel reuse ratio | $q = \dfrac{D}{R}$ | Normalised distance between co-channel cells |
| 2 | Co-channel cells in first tier (omni) | $i_0 = 6$ | Always 6 in standard hex layout |
| 3 | Interferers in N-sectoring | $i_N = 2$ (3-sector) or $i_N = 1$ (6-sector) | Geometric reduction |
| 4 | Capacity improvement factor (ideal) | $C_{\text{imp}} = \dfrac{i_0}{i_N} = \dfrac{6}{i_N}$ | Theoretical upper bound |
| 5 | S/I (omnidirectional) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_o = \dfrac{1}{i_0 \cdot q^{-4}}$ | Power-law path-loss model (n=4) |
| 6 | S/I (N-sectoring) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_N = \dfrac{1}{i_N \cdot q^{-4}}$ | Reduced denominator |
| 7 | S/I improvement (dB) | $\Delta_{S/I} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i_0}{i_N}\right)$ | Direct gain in dB |
| 8 | New cluster size possible | $N' \approx \dfrac{N}{C_{\text{imp}}}$ | Capacity scaling |
| 9 | New capacity per cluster | $C' = C \cdot C_{\text{imp}}$ | Channels per cell |
| 10 | Handoff rate (sectored) | $H_s = \dfrac{H_o}{N}$ (approx.) | Increases with N due to more crossing of sector boundaries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sectoring is the *de-facto* standard in every modern **2G/3G/4G/5G macro-cell deployment**. The three "panels" you see on a rooftop cell tower are precisely the **three 120° sector antennas** (often called *alpha*, *beta*, *gamma* sectors). It is also the foundational reason that LTE uses **3-sector hexagonal reuse patterns** with N = 1 (frequency-reuse-1) but aggressive **interference coordination (ICIC)**.

## 2.4 Limitations & Trade-offs (Frequently asked in 2-mark questions)

- **Increased handoff frequency** — Mobile users crossing sector boundaries trigger more handoffs (within the *same* cell, but a different sector).
- **Antenna hardware complexity** — Multiple antennas, feed cables, and tower-top amplifiers (TMA) required.
- **Trunking inefficiency** — Each sector is now a *smaller trunk group*, so the **Grade of Service (GoS)** degrades for the same offered traffic.
- **Sector-to-sector overlap** — A real antenna cannot have an infinitely sharp 120° cut-off; **soft-handoff overlap** of a few degrees is essential.
- **Tilting / pattern distortion** — Mechanical or electrical down-tilting affects sector coverage symmetry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacity Improvement Factor of 3-Sectoring

**Given:** A standard hexagonal cellular system with omni-directional base-station antennas, cluster size $N = 7$, and propagation path-loss exponent $n = 4$. After replacing the omni-antenna with three **120° sectoral antennas**, derive the new capacity and the S/I improvement in dB.

**Step 1 — Write the generic S/I expression under an $n=4$ path-loss model.**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right) = \frac{S}{\sum_{k=1}^{i} I_k} = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=1}^{i} \left(\dfrac{D_k}{R}\right)^{-4}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the first-tier co-channel distance vector.**

For *N = 7*, the six first-tier co-channel cells are located at:

$$
D_k = \left( q R \right) \cdot \big[\cos\theta_k,\ \sin\theta_k\big], \quad \theta_k \in \{0°, 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°\}
$$

Because all six are at the *same* radial distance $D = qR$ from the desired cell centre:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_o = \frac{1}{6 \cdot (qR / R)^{-4}} = \frac{q^4}{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the omnidirectional threshold for AMPS (4-QAM FM).**

For *N = 7* → $q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.5826$:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_o = \frac{(4.5826)^4}{6} = \frac{441}{6} \approx 73.5 \;\;\;( \approx 18.66\ \text{dB} )
$$

This *just exceeds* the **18 dB** minimum. ✓

**Step 4 — Switch to 120° sectoring.**

Of the six co-channel cells, only the **two** whose centre lies *inside* the 120° main beam of the serving sector are now interferers (the remaining four lie in the antenna's back-lobe region with FTBR $\ge 20$ dB and are ignored in the first-order analysis).

$$
i_N = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{3\text{-sec}} = \frac{q^4}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5 \quad(\approx 23.43\ \text{dB})
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the S/I improvement in dB.**

$$
\Delta_{S/I} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{220.5}{73.5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the ideal capacity improvement.**

$$
C_{\text{imp}} = \frac{(S/I)_{3\text{-sec}}}{(S/I)_o} = \frac{6}{2} = 3
$$

So the channel set assigned to a 7-cell cluster can now be redistributed such that effectively **only $N' = 7/3 \approx 2.33$ cells** are required per cluster. Since N must be an integer, the new viable design uses $N' = 3$, giving:

$$
\text{Channels per cell} = \frac{\text{Total channels}}{N'} = 3 \cdot \left(\text{Channels per cell at }N=7\right)
$$

**Conclusion:** 3-sectoring triples the system capacity, or equivalently, allows a $3\times$ increase in user density for the same GoS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Co-Channel Reuse Ratio with 60° (6-Sector) Antennas

**Given:** Repeat the analysis for **6-sectoring (60° beamwidth)**. Find the new S/I and the minimum cluster size that can be sustained while meeting a **15 dB** S/I threshold.

**Step 1 — Identify first-tier interferers.**

With a 60° beam, only **one** of the six first-tier co-channel cells lies inside the main beam:

$$
i_N = 1
$$

**Step 2 — S/I in 6-sectoring.**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{6\text{-sec}} = \frac{q^4}{1} = q^4
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 15 dB threshold.**

$$
10 \log_{10}(q^4) \ge 15\ \text{dB} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad q^4 \ge 31.62 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad q \ge (31.62)^{0.25} = 2.372
$$

**Step 4 — Map q to the smallest valid cluster size N.**

Using $q = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$
\sqrt{3N} \ge 2.372 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 3N \ge 5.626 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N \ge 1.875
$$

Since *N* must be an integer from the allowed set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, \ldots\}$, the smallest feasible cluster size is **N = 3**.

**Step 5 — Compute the new channels per cell.**

If the operator has a total of $M$ duplex channels:

$$
C' = \frac{M}{3} \quad (\text{vs } C_o = M/7)
$$

Capacity ratio:

$$
\frac{C'}{C_o} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33\times
$$

Combined with the **6-fold** S/I improvement, the system can actually push to **N = 1 (universal frequency reuse)** with proper **ICIC** in modern 4G/5G — a direct consequence of aggressive sectoring.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-side Comparison

| Parameter | Omni (0°/360°) | 3-Sector (120°) | 6-Sector (60°) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Beamwidth | 360° | 120° | 60° |
| Interferers $i_N$ | 6 | 2 | 1 |
| S/I (linear) at $q=4.58$ | 73.5 | 220.5 | 441 |
| S/I (dB) | 18.66 dB | 23.43 dB | 26.44 dB |
| $\Delta$ S/I over omni | 0 dB | +4.77 dB | +7.78 dB |
| Capacity gain (ideal) | 1× | 3× | 6× |
| Capacity gain (practical) | 1× | ~2.5× | ~4.5× |
| Handoff rate | $H_o$ | $\approx 3 H_o$ | $\approx 6 H_o$ |
| Trunking efficiency | Best | Moderate | Worst |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question says *"compare sectoring and cell splitting"*, always mention that **sectoring requires no change in cell-site location** (uses *existing* towers and *existing* frequency plan), while **cell-splitting requires new towers and frequency re-planning**. This is a guaranteed 2-marker.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Sector BTS

```mermaid
graph TD
    MSC[MSC Core Network] --> BSC[BSC RNC]
    BSC --> S1[Sector Controller Alpha 120 deg]
    BSC --> S2[Sector Controller Beta 120 deg]
    BSC --> S3[Sector Controller Gamma 120 deg]
    S1 --> A1[120 deg Sector Antenna Panel]
    S2 --> A2[120 deg Sector Antenna Panel]
    S3 --> A3[120 deg Sector Antenna Panel]
    A1 --> RF1[Downlink Uplink RF Chain]
    A2 --> RF2[Downlink Uplink RF Chain]
    A3 --> RF3[Downlink Uplink RF Chain]
    RF1 --> MS1[Mobile Users in Alpha Sector]
    RF2 --> MS2[Mobile Users in Beta Sector]
    RF3 --> MS3[Mobile Users in Gamma Sector]
    A1 --- NB1[First Tier Co-channel Cell A Reduced Interferer 2]
    A2 --- NB2[First Tier Co-channel Cell B Reduced Interferer 2]
    A3 --- NB3[First Tier Co-channel Cell C Reduced Interferer 2]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Co-Channel Interference Reduction Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Omni Antenna 360 deg] --> B[6 First Tier Co-channel Interferers]
    B --> C[S/I low 18.7 dB]
    C --> D[Capacity Limited]
    E[3 Sector Antenna 120 deg] --> F[2 First Tier Co-channel Interferers]
    F --> G[S/I improved 23.4 dB]
    G --> H[Capacity x 3]
    I[6 Sector Antenna 60 deg] --> J[1 First Tier Co-channel Interferer]
    J --> K[S/I improved 26.4 dB]
    K --> L[Capacity x 6]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Hexagonal Cell with 3-Sector Layout (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[Cell Centre BTS] --> SecA[Sector A 120 deg North East]
    H --> SecB[Sector B 120 deg South East]
    H --> SecC[Sector C 120 deg West]
    SecA --> UE1[Users Sector A]
    SecB --> UE2[Users Sector B]
    SecC --> UE3[Users Sector C]
    SecA -.Interferes.-> CCI1[2 Co channel Cells in First Tier]
    SecB -.Interferes.-> CCI2[2 Co channel Cells in First Tier]
    SecC -.Interferes.-> CCI3[2 Co channel Cells in First Tier]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The dotted arrows in the third figure visually prove the **2-interferer rule** for 3-sectoring. In a real hex layout, of the six surrounding co-channel cells, only those whose centre lies in the *azimuthal wedge* of a given sector (e.g., ±60° from the antenna boresight) actually contribute to co-channel interference. The other four are shielded by the antenna's **front-to-back ratio (FTBR ≥ 20 dB)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
**Define sectoring. How does it improve the capacity of a cellular system?**

**Model Answer:**

Sectoring is the technique of subdividing a hexagonal cell into multiple angular sectors (typically **3 × 120°** or **6 × 60°**) by replacing the central omnidirectional base-station antenna with **directional sector antennas**.

**Capacity improvement mechanism:**
- The horizontal beamwidth of the antenna is restricted, which reduces the number of first-tier co-channel interferers from **6 to 2** (3-sector) or **6 to 1** (6-sector).
- The improved **S/I ratio** allows a **smaller cluster size N**, which increases the number of channels per cell and hence the **system capacity**.
- The capacity-improvement factor is theoretically equal to the **number of sectors (N)**, i.e., a 3× gain for 3-sectoring and a 6× gain for 6-sectoring.

*(3 marks — Definition 1, Mechanism 1, Capacity factor 1.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three limitations of sectoring in a cellular mobile system.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Increased intra-cell handoffs** — When a mobile crosses a sector boundary, it must be handed over to the neighbouring sector of the *same* cell. The handoff rate grows almost proportionally with the number of sectors, increasing signalling load on the MSC/BSC.
2. **Trunking inefficiency** — Channels are partitioned into smaller trunk groups (one per sector). The **Grade of Service (GoS)** degrades for the same offered Erlang traffic, especially under bursty loads.
3. **Antenna pattern imperfections** — Real antennas have finite front-to-back ratio, side-lobes, and pattern distortion under mechanical down-tilt, allowing more than the theoretical minimum number of interferers to leak through. *(3 marks — 1 per valid limitation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **3-sector (120°) sectoring** and show how it reduces the number of **first-tier co-channel interferers** from 6 to 2. Derive the resulting **S/I improvement in dB** for a system with $N = 7$, $n = 4$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cellular operator is allotted **832 duplex channels** for an AMPS-like system (S/I threshold = **18 dB**, path-loss exponent $n = 4$). Compare the **system capacity** (channels per cell and total supported users at GoS = 2%) for (i) omnidirectional antennas with $N = 7$, and (ii) 3-sectoring with the smallest valid $N$ that meets the S/I threshold. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution A(a):**

**Diagram (Neat):** Draw a hexagon at the centre. Inside, draw three wedge-shaped sectors meeting at the BTS, each spanning 120°, labelled *α, β, γ*. Mark the **six first-tier co-channel cells** with dotted circles. Shade only the **two** co-channel cells that lie within the main beam of sector *α*. This is the visual proof.

**Step 1 — Co-channel cell count:**

In a 3-sector antenna with FTBR ≥ 20 dB, only the co-channel cells whose azimuth lies within ±60° of the boresight contribute as first-tier interferers. Geometrically, this gives:

$$
i_{3\text{-sec}} = 2
$$

**Step 2 — S/I for N = 7 omni case:**

The co-channel reuse ratio is $q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.5826$. With $n = 4$:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_o = \frac{q^4}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5 \;\;(\approx 18.66\ \text{dB})
$$

**Step 3 — S/I for 3-sectoring:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{3\text{-sec}} = \frac{q^4}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5 \;\;(\approx 23.43\ \text{dB})
$$

**Step 4 — Improvement in dB:**

$$
\Delta_{S/I} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{220.5}{73.5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77\ \text{dB}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for derivation steps 1-2, 2 for step 3, 1 for dB calculation.)*

---

### **Model Solution A(b):**

**Step 1 — Capacity in the omni N = 7 case:**

$$
C_o = \frac{832}{7} \approx 118.86 \;\;\text{channels per cell}
$$

Assuming **2% blocking (GoS = 2%)** and **Erlang-B table** at infinite trunk (or using the standard table value), the **carried traffic per channel ≈ 0.5 Erlang** for very large trunk groups, so the supported traffic per cell is:

$$
A_o \approx 118.86 \times 0.5 \approx 59.4\ \text{Erlangs/cell}
$$

**Step 2 — Smallest valid N for 3-sectoring:**

The S/I must remain $\ge 18$ dB:

$$
10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{q^4}{2}\right) \ge 18 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{q^4}{2} \ge 63.1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad q^4 \ge 126.2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad q \ge 3.348
$$

Then $q = \sqrt{3N} \ge 3.348 \Rightarrow 3N \ge 11.21 \Rightarrow N \ge 3.74$. The smallest valid integer N is **N = 4**.

**Step 3 — Capacity in 3-sector N = 4 case:**

$$
C_{\text{sec}} = \frac{832}{4} = 208\ \text{channels per cell}
$$

Supported traffic per cell (approximate):

$$
A_{\text{sec}} \approx 208 \times 0.5 \approx 104\ \text{Erlangs/cell}
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio and conclusion:**

$$
\frac{C_{\text{sec}}}{C_o} = \frac{208}{118.86} \approx 1.75\times
$$

Because the available channels per cell are *partitioned into 3 sectors* (so each sector has only $208/3 \approx 69$ channels), the per-sector capacity is *less* than the omni case, but the *overall system capacity* (sum across all cells in a large area) is higher by ~75%. Practically, the gain is bounded by trunking efficiency.

*(7 marks: 2 for omni calculation, 3 for S/I inequality to find N = 4, 2 for final ratio.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]* (Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the difference between **omnidirectional**, **3-sector (120°)**, and **6-sector (60°)** antenna configurations in a hexagonal cellular system. Tabulate the S/I ratio for $N = 7$, $n = 4$ in all three cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-sectored cellular system has a **co-channel reuse ratio of 4.6** and a path-loss exponent of 3.5. Compute (i) the **S/I in dB**, (ii) the **minimum cluster size** for an 18 dB threshold, and (iii) the **percentage capacity increase** over a comparable omnidirectional system. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B(a):**

**Conceptual explanation:**

- **Omnidirectional:** A single 360° antenna at the BTS; **6 first-tier interferers**; lowest antenna gain; simplest deployment.
- **3-sector (120°):** Three directional antennas per BTS; **2 first-tier interferers**; ~5 dBi gain; standard for 2G/3G/4G macro.
- **6-sector (60°):** Six directional antennas per BTS; **1 first-tier interferer**; ~8 dBi gain; used for high-capacity urban hotspots.

**S/I Table for $N = 7, n = 4, q = 4.5826$:**

| Configuration | $i_N$ | $S/I$ (linear) | $S/I$ (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Omnidirectional | 6 | 73.5 | 18.66 dB |
| 3-Sector (120°) | 2 | 220.5 | 23.43 dB |
| 6-Sector (60°) | 1 | 441.0 | 26.44 dB |

**Step 1:** For omni, $S/I = q^4 / 6 = 73.5 \to 18.66$ dB.  
**Step 2:** For 3-sector, $S/I = q^4 / 2 = 220.5 \to 23.43$ dB.  
**Step 3:** For 6-sector, $S/I = q^4 / 1 = 441 \to 26.44$ dB.  
**Step 4:** The dB improvement is $+4.77$ dB and $+7.78$ dB over omni for the 3-sector and 6-sector cases respectively.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for table, 1 for each dB improvement line.)*

---

### **Model Solution B(b):**

**Given:** $q = 4.6$, $n = 3.5$, 6-sectoring (so $i_N = 1$), $S/I$ threshold = 18 dB.

**Step (i) — S/I calculation with $n = 3.5$:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right) = \frac{1}{i_N \cdot q^{-n}} = \frac{q^n}{i_N} = \frac{(4.6)^{3.5}}{1}
$$

Computing $(4.6)^{3.5}$:

$$
4.6^2 = 21.16, \quad 4.6^3 = 97.336, \quad 4.6^{0.5} = 2.1448
$$
$$
4.6^{3.5} = 97.336 \times 2.1448 \approx 208.78
$$

In dB:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(208.78) \approx 23.20\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step (ii) — Minimum cluster size for 18 dB threshold:**

$$
q_{\min}^n \ge 10^{1.8} \cdot i_N = 63.1 \times 1 = 63.1
$$
$$
q_{\min} \ge (63.1)^{1/3.5}
$$

Compute $1/3.5 \approx 0.2857$:

$$
q_{\min} = 63.1^{0.2857}
$$

Using logs: $\log_{10}(63.1) = 1.800$, then $0.2857 \times 1.800 = 0.5143$, so $q_{\min} = 10^{0.5143} \approx 3.268$.

Then $N_{\min}$ via $q = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$
3N_{\min} \ge q_{\min}^2 = 10.68 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N_{\min} \ge 3.56
$$

The smallest valid integer N is **N = 4**.

**Step (iii) — Capacity increase over omni:**

For omni, the equivalent computation gives the smallest $N$ for the same 18 dB threshold. Solving $q_{\min}^n \ge 63.1 \times 6 = 378.6$:

$$
q_{\min} = (378.6)^{0.2857}
$$

$\log_{10}(378.6) = 2.578$, then $0.2857 \times 2.578 = 0.7366$, $q_{\min} = 10^{0.7366} \approx 5.453$.

Then $3N_{\min} = 29.73 \Rightarrow N_{\min} = 9.91$, smallest integer **N = 12**.

Capacity per cell in 6-sector: $C_{\text{sec}} = M / 4$.  
Capacity per cell in omni: $C_{\text{omni}} = M / 12$.

Percentage increase:

$$
\%\Delta C = \left(\frac{C_{\text{sec}}}{C_{\text{omni}}} - 1\right) \times 100\% = \left(\frac{12}{4} - 1\right) \times 100\% = 200\%
$$

**The 6-sector system has 3× (i.e., 200% more) capacity per cell than the omni system.**

*(7 marks: 2 for part i, 2 for part ii, 3 for part iii.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Sectoring questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cluster size N to co-channel reuse ratio q first.** Always compute $q = \sqrt{3N}$ *before* applying the S/I formula.
> 2. **Using the wrong path-loss exponent.** For AMPS / 4G macro, the default is **n = 4** (6 dB/octave in linear scale). For 5G mmWave or indoor picocells, **n = 2 to 3** is more realistic. Read the question stem carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting the 10 log₁₀** for dB conversion. Many students write *S/I = 220.5* and stop, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning handoff overhead.** Sectoring without acknowledging the **3× intra-cell handoff rate** is considered an incomplete answer in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Confusing sectoring with cell-splitting.** Sectoring re-uses the *same cell site* with directional antennas; cell-splitting *adds new sites*. Examiners explicitly deduct 2 marks for this mix-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sectoring** = dividing a hexagonal cell into **3 or 6 angular sectors** using **directional (sectoral) antennas** at the BTS.
- **3-sector (120°):** $i_N = 2$; capacity gain ≈ **3× (ideal), 2.5× (practical)**; S/I improves by **+4.77 dB**.
- **6-sector (60°):** $i_N = 1$; capacity gain ≈ **6× (ideal), 4.5× (practical)**; S/I improves by **+7.78 dB**.
- **Front-to-back ratio (FTBR)** of the sector antenna must be **≥ 20 dB** for the 2-interferer model to hold.
- **Co-channel reuse ratio** $q = \sqrt{3N}$; always compute $q$ *first* before applying any S/I formula.
- **Generic S/I formula:** $\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right) = \dfrac{1}{i_N \cdot q^{-n}}$ for path-loss exponent $n$.
- **S/I threshold (legacy AMPS):** **18 dB** for 4-QAM FM voice.
- **Handoff rate** approximately **scales linearly** with the number of sectors — flag this in any 7-mark comparison.
- **Trunking efficiency** drops with more sectors; GoS worsens for the same offered traffic.
- **Sectoring vs Cell Splitting:** Sectoring re-uses *existing* tower & frequencies; cell-splitting requires *new* towers & frequency re-planning.
- **Modern 4G/5G usage:** Every macro cell today is **3-sectored**; combined with **ICIC / CoMP**, it enables **N = 1 (universal) frequency reuse**.
- **Cluster sizes commonly tested in KTU:** $N \in \{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 19\}$ — *always use these, never non-integer values for N.*
- **Memory trick for capacity factor:** *"Number of sectors = the divisor of the original 6 interferers"*, i.e., capacity gain = $6 / i_N$.

> **End of Module 4 – Sectoring Notes. Best of luck for your KTU ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell System Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cell System Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cell System Layout** refers to the systematic geometric arrangement of cells (radio coverage zones) in a cellular mobile network, such that the entire service area is tessellated with minimal overlap while supporting a high user capacity through the principle of **frequency reuse**.

In a cellular system, the coverage region is subdivided into smaller regions called **cells**, each served by a **Base Station (BS)**. The layout defines the shape, size, and spatial arrangement of these cells, the assignment of frequency channels to cells, and the cluster size $N$ that determines how many cells must collectively use the entire allocated spectrum before a frequency can be reused.

> [!NOTE]
> The ideal cell shape for a system layout is a **regular hexagon**, because it approximates a circular radiation footprint with **no overlap and no gaps** (a perfect tessellation), unlike squares (which leave corners with weak coverage) or circles (which leave gaps).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city divided into 7 delivery zones. Each zone has exactly one delivery office (the **Base Station**) that handles all package orders (calls) within its zone. The delivery vans (mobile users) inside a zone are served by their own office. Now, here's the clever part: instead of each office having a unique set of roads, the city planners decided that offices 1, 4, and 7 share the *same* set of roads (same frequencies), as long as they are far enough apart that the delivery vans from one office don't interfere with vans from another office sharing the same road. The pattern of 7 offices is called a **cluster**, and the distance between any two offices using the same "road" must be carefully calculated to prevent **co-channel interference**. The shape of each zone is chosen to be a hexagon (like a honeycomb) because bees have already figured out the most efficient way to pack a region without wasted space!

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Path Loss Exponent ($n$):** Typically **$n = 3$** in cluttered urban environments (between $2$ for free space and $4$ for heavily shadowed areas).
- **Hexagonal Cell Radius ($R$):** The distance from the center of a hexagon to any of its vertices.
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio ($Q$):** The ratio of the distance $D$ between two co-channel cells to the cell radius $R$.
- **S/I Threshold (Minimum):** **18 dB** for narrowband analog cellular systems, and approximately **7–9 dB** for modern digital systems using techniques like coding and equalization.
- **Reuse Cluster Size ($N$):** Determined by $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, with values **1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21...**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hexagons?** In practice, a cell's coverage is determined by the radiation pattern of an omni-directional antenna, which is roughly circular on the ground. Circles cannot tile a plane without gaps. The **regular hexagon** is the largest polygon (with 3, 4, or 6 sides) that can tessellate, and among these, the hexagon most closely approximates a circle, making it the geometric choice for cell layout.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Cell System Layout Design

The design of a cell system layout proceeds in the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Service Area Partitioning:** The total geographical region to be covered is divided into a set of regular hexagonal cells. Each cell has one Base Station at its center for omni-directional antennas.
- **Step 2 — Cluster Formation:** A cluster is a group of $N$ cells that collectively use all the frequency channels allocated to the system. The cluster size $N$ must satisfy:
$$N = i^2 + ij + j^2$$
where $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers (with $i \geq j$), and they represent the **shift parameters** in the hexagonal grid.
- **Step 3 — Frequency Reuse Planning:** Once a cluster of $N$ cells is formed, the same set of frequencies is assigned to the corresponding cells in adjacent clusters. The minimum distance $D$ between two co-channel cells (cells using the same frequency) is:
$$D = R\sqrt{3N}$$
- **Step 4 — Co-channel Interference (CCI) Evaluation:** Since co-channel cells share frequencies, they interfere with each other. The **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (S/I)** is evaluated to ensure quality. For a mobile receiver at the cell boundary, the worst-case S/I is:
$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{i_0} = \frac{Q^n}{i_0}$$
where $i_0$ is the **number of co-channel interferers in the first tier** (typically **6** for omni-directional antennas in a hexagonal layout).
- **Step 5 — Capacity vs. Quality Trade-off:** A smaller $N$ gives higher capacity (more reuse) but increases interference (lower S/I). A larger $N$ improves S/I but reduces capacity. The cluster size $N$ must be chosen such that:
$$\frac{S}{I} \geq \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{minimum}}$$
- **Step 6 — Sectoring and Cell Splitting:** To enhance S/I or expand capacity, the cells are further divided into sectors (typically 3 sectors of 120° each, using directional antennas) and/or split into smaller cells (cell splitting).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Cluster Size | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | Number of cells per cluster; dimensionless |
| Co-channel Reuse Distance | $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ | Distance between co-channel cell centers; km |
| Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Signal-to-Interference Ratio | $\frac{S}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{i_0}$ | Dimensionless (often expressed in dB) |
| S/I in Decibels | $\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{Q^n}{i_0}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| Cell Area (Hexagonal) | $A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}R^2$ | Square kilometers (km²) |
| Total Frequency Channels per Cell | $F_{\text{cell}} = F_{\text{total}}/N$ | Number of channels |
| Minimum $N$ for given S/I | $N \geq \left[i_0 \cdot (S/I)_{\text{min}}\right]^{2/n} / 3$ | Dimensionless |
| Frequency Reuse Distance (alternative form) | $D^2 = 3N R^2$ | Square km |
| Number of co-channel interferers (1st tier, omni) | $i_0 = 6$ | Dimensionless (count) |

> [!NOTE]
> In all equations above, the symbol $\sqrt{}$ denotes square root. Note carefully: $Q$ is **not** the queueing-theory $Q$, it is a **geometric ratio** specific to cellular frequency reuse.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Capacity Planning:** Mobile operators use these formulas to determine how many simultaneous users a given bandwidth and geographic area can support.
- **Interference Management:** Engineers set the cluster size based on S/I requirements to ensure call quality (voice calls, data sessions) is not degraded.
- **Cell Splitting and Sectoring:** As user density increases, the layout is dynamically altered to add capacity (e.g., 4G LTE and 5G NR small cells).
- **Frequency Assignment to Operators:** Regulatory bodies (like TRAI in India, FCC in the US) assign spectrum, and operators must then plan their cell layouts and reuse factors accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Co-channel Reuse Distance $D$

**Goal:** Derive the relationship $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ from hexagonal geometry.

**Step 1 — Hexagonal Geometry Setup**

Consider a hexagonal cell with center $O$ and radius $R$ (center to vertex). The distance from the center of the hexagon to the midpoint of any side (called the **apothem** or **inradius**) is:

$$a = R \cdot \cos(30^\circ) = \frac{R\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Move Along a Row of Cells**

Starting at center $O$ of a cell, move a distance $2R$ along the **row direction** to reach the center of the next cell. This is because the centers of adjacent cells in the same row are separated by a distance $2R$ (since the hexagon has width $2R$ in the horizontal direction).

**Step 3 — Move Along the Column Direction**

The next step moves $j$ cells along an angle of 60° (or 120°) to the previous direction. The unit vector in the column direction has a component of $\sqrt{3}R$ per cell perpendicular to the row direction.

The full vector from the center of one cell to a co-channel cell is:

$$\vec{D} = i \cdot (2R, 0) + j \cdot (\sqrt{3}R, \sqrt{3}R \cdot \tan(60^\circ - 90^\circ))$$

In magnitude squared form (this is the well-known result for hexagonal lattices):

$$D^2 = (2R \cdot i + j \cdot R\sqrt{3})^2 + (j \cdot 3R)^2 / 3$$

After algebraic simplification (using the symmetry of the hexagonal lattice):

$$D^2 = 3R^2(i^2 + ij + j^2) = 3R^2 \cdot N$$

Taking the square root:

$$D = R\sqrt{3N}$$

This is the **co-channel reuse distance**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Signal-to-Interference Ratio (S/I)

**Goal:** Derive the S/I expression for the worst-case mobile station located at the cell boundary.

**Step 1 — Path Loss Model**

The received signal power at a mobile station at distance $d$ from a base station is modeled as:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}$$

where $P_t$ is the transmitted power, $K$ is a constant depending on antenna gains and frequency, and $n$ is the path loss exponent (typically 3 to 4).

**Step 2 — Signal Power at Cell Boundary**

The mobile station is at the cell boundary, at distance $R$ from its serving base station. So:

$$S = P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Interfering Base Station Distance**

The nearest co-channel base stations are at distance $D$ from the mobile. In the worst case (mobile at the cell boundary, lined up with the interfering base station), the distance to the interferer is approximately $D - R \approx D$ (since $D \gg R$). The interference power from one such co-channel interferer is:

$$I_{\text{single}} = P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

**Step 4 — Sum of Interferences**

There are $i_0$ co-channel interferers in the first tier (typically $i_0 = 6$ for omni-directional antennas). All are at approximately distance $D$ from the mobile:

$$I = i_0 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

**Step 5 — Form the Ratio**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_t K R^{-n}}{i_0 \cdot P_t K D^{-n}} = \frac{R^{-n}}{i_0 \cdot D^{-n}}$$

Simplifying by bringing the negative exponents up:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{i_0} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^n = \frac{1}{i_0} \cdot Q^n$$

where $Q = D/R$ is the **co-channel reuse ratio**.

Substituting $Q = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{i_0}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Minimum Cluster Size $N$

**Goal:** Find the smallest $N$ that satisfies the minimum S/I requirement.

**Step 1 — Start from S/I Inequality**

The system must satisfy:

$$\frac{S}{I} \geq \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the S/I Formula**

$$\frac{Q^n}{i_0} \geq \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min}$$

$$Q^n \geq i_0 \cdot \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min}$$

$$Q \geq \left[i_0 \cdot \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min}\right]^{1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Relate $Q$ to $N$**

Since $Q = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$\sqrt{3N} \geq \left[i_0 \cdot \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min}\right]^{1/n}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$3N \geq \left[i_0 \cdot \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min}\right]^{2/n}$$

$$\boxed{N \geq \frac{\left[i_0 \cdot (S/I)_{\min}\right]^{2/n}}{3}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A cellular system has $n = 3$, $i_0 = 6$ (omni-directional antennas), and the minimum acceptable S/I is **15 dB**. Find the minimum cluster size $N$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert S/I to linear scale**

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min} = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} = 31.623$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the formula**

$$N \geq \frac{\left[6 \times 31.623\right]^{2/3}}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the numerator**

$$6 \times 31.623 = 189.74$$

$$(189.74)^{2/3} = ?$$

First, $(189.74)^{1/3} \approx 5.741$ (since $5.741^3 \approx 189.2$).

Then $(189.74)^{2/3} = \left[(189.74)^{1/3}\right]^2 = (5.741)^2 \approx 32.96$.

**Step 4 — Divide by 3**

$$N \geq \frac{32.96}{3} \approx 10.99$$

**Step 5 — Round up to the nearest valid $N$**

The next valid value of $N$ (from $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$) is **$N = 12$** (using $i = 2, j = 2$: $4 + 4 + 4 = 12$).

$$\boxed{N_{\min} = 12}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cluster Geometry and Frequency Reuse

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Total Service Area] --> B[Divide into Hexagonal Cells]
    B --> C[Form Clusters of N Cells]
    C --> D{Choose i, j from i²+ij+j²}
    D --> E[Cluster N=3 with i=1 j=1]
    D --> F[Cluster N=4 with i=2 j=0]
    D --> G[Cluster N=7 with i=2 j=1]
    D --> H[Cluster N=12 with i=2 j=2]
    E --> I[Assign Frequency Set 1]
    F --> J[Assign Frequency Set 2]
    G --> K[Assign Frequency Set 3]
    H --> L[Assign Frequency Set 4]
    I --> M[Reuse in Adjacent Clusters]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Evaluate Co-channel Interference S/I]
    N --> O{S/I Satisfies Minimum}
    O --> P[Acceptable Layout]
    O --> Q[Increase N or Apply Sectoring]
    Q --> R[Cell Splitting for Capacity]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cell System Layout with Shift Parameters

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Cell at Origin 0,0] --> B[Shift by i in row direction]
    A --> C[Shift by j in column direction]
    B --> D[Co-channel Cell A at 2iR, 0]
    C --> E[Co-channel Cell B at jRsqrt3, 3jR]
    D --> F[Co-channel Distance D = Rsqrt3N]
    E --> F
    F --> G[S/I = Qn / i0]
    G --> H[Quality Check]
    H --> I{Acceptable}
    I --> J[Network Deployment]
    I --> K[Adjust N or Use Sectoring]
```

## 4.3 Hexagonal Cluster Layout Description (KTU Diagram Reference)

Since Mermaid cannot render hexagonal tessellations natively, the structure can be visualized as follows (refer to this when drawing on your exam):

```
       ___       ___       ___
      /   \     /   \     /   \
     /  F  \___/  A  \___/  C  \
     \     /   \     /   \     /
      \___/  B  \___/  D  \___/
      /   \     /   \     /   \
     /  E  \___/  ★  \___/  A  \
     \     /   \     /   \     /
      \___/  C  \___/  B  \___/
      /   \     /   \     /   \
     /  D  \___/  F  \___/  E  \
     \     /   \     /   \     /
      \___/     \___/     \___/
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The center cell is the reference cell (★). Cells labeled with the same letter (e.g., all "A" cells) are co-channel cells, separated by the co-channel reuse distance $D$. In this example, the cluster size is **$N = 7$** (cells A through G), and the pattern is generated using shift parameters $i = 2$, $j = 1$ (since $2^2 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1^2 = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$).

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sectoring for Improved S/I

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Original Omni Cell] --> B[3-Sector Configuration 120 degrees]
    A --> C[6-Sector Configuration 60 degrees]
    B --> D[Number of Interferers reduces from 6 to 2]
    C --> E[Number of Interferers reduces from 6 to 1]
    D --> F[S/I improves by factor of 3]
    E --> G[S/I improves by factor of 6]
    F --> H[Smaller N allowed, Higher Capacity]
    G --> H
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define a **cell** in a cellular system. Why are hexagonal cells preferred over circular or square cells for system layout?

**Model Answer:**

A **cell** is the basic geographical unit of a cellular mobile network, defined as the area served by a single Base Station (BS). It represents the radio coverage zone within which mobile users communicate with the BS.

**Why hexagons are preferred:**
- The radio coverage of a base station is roughly **circular** in shape.
- **Circles** cannot tile a plane without leaving gaps (wasted coverage).
- **Squares** can tile, but they leave corners of poor coverage and have more overlap.
- **Regular hexagons** tile a plane perfectly (tessellate) with no gaps and no overlaps, and they most closely approximate the circular radiation pattern of a base station antenna.
- Hence, the hexagonal cell is the **idealized model** used for system layout.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q:** What is the **co-channel reuse ratio $Q$**? State its relationship with the cluster size $N$.

**Model Answer:**

The **co-channel reuse ratio $Q$** is defined as the ratio of the distance $D$ between the centers of two co-channel cells (cells using the same set of frequencies) to the cell radius $R$:

$$Q = \frac{D}{R}$$

It is related to the cluster size $N$ by:

$$Q = \sqrt{3N}$$

A higher $Q$ means co-channel cells are farther apart (less interference but less capacity), while a lower $Q$ means more capacity but more interference.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for defining $Q$, 1 mark for the relationship, 1 mark for the significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **frequency reuse concept** in cellular systems. Derive the expression for the **co-channel interference ratio (S/I)** for a hexagonal cell system with omni-directional antennas. Given that $n = 3$ and the minimum acceptable S/I is **18 dB**, find the minimum cluster size $N$ and the co-channel reuse distance $D$ (in terms of $R$).

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Frequency Reuse Concept and S/I Derivation (7 Marks)

**Frequency Reuse Concept:**

Frequency reuse is the principle of using the same set of frequency channels in non-adjacent cells of a cellular system, separated by a minimum distance $D$, to maximize the system capacity without increasing the total allocated bandwidth.

**Block Layout (Refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**

In a cluster of $N$ cells, each cell is assigned a unique set of frequency channels. The same set is reused in another cluster located at distance $D$. The cells using the same frequencies are called **co-channel cells**.

**Derivation of S/I:**

**Step 1:** The received signal power at a mobile station at distance $R$ from its base station is:

$$S = P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** The interference power from one co-channel base station at distance $D$ is:

$$I_{\text{single}} = P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

**Step 3:** With $i_0 = 6$ co-channel interferers in the first tier (omni-directional case), the total interference is:

$$I = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

**Step 4:** The S/I ratio is:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^n = \frac{Q^n}{6}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $n = 3$, $\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min} = 18$ dB, $i_0 = 6$.

**Step 1 — Convert 18 dB to linear scale:**

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min} = 10^{18/10} = 10^{1.8} = 63.096$$

**Step 2 — Use the cluster size formula:**

$$N \geq \frac{\left[6 \times 63.096\right]^{2/3}}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$6 \times 63.096 = 378.57$$

$$(378.57)^{2/3}$$

First, $(378.57)^{1/3} \approx 7.241$ (since $7.241^3 \approx 379.5$).

Then $(378.57)^{2/3} = (7.241)^2 \approx 52.43$.

**Step 4 — Divide by 3:**

$$N \geq \frac{52.43}{3} \approx 17.48$$

**Step 5 — Round up to the nearest valid $N$:**

The next valid $N$ from the formula $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ is **$N = 19$** (using $i = 3$, $j = 2$: $9 + 6 + 4 = 19$).

$$\boxed{N_{\min} = 19}$$

**Step 6 — Co-channel reuse distance:**

$$D = R\sqrt{3N} = R\sqrt{3 \times 19} = R\sqrt{57} \approx 7.55\,R$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q:** Explain the **cell splitting** and **sectoring** techniques used in cellular system layout. How do they affect the **system capacity** and **co-channel interference**?

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Cell Splitting (7 Marks)

**Definition:**

**Cell splitting** is the process of subdividing a congested cell into smaller cells (called **microcells**), each with its own base station and a corresponding reduction in antenna height and transmitter power.

**Procedure:**

- **Step 1:** The congested cell of radius $R$ is split into smaller cells of radius $R/2$ (typically).
- **Step 2:** Each smaller cell has its own base station, and the transmitter power is reduced by approximately **12 dB** (a factor of 1/4 in radius gives 1/16 in area, requiring ~12 dB power reduction to maintain the same S/I).
- **Step 3:** The new cell radius is:
$$R_{\text{new}} = \frac{R_{\text{old}}}{2}$$
- **Step 4:** The number of channels per cell may be increased, and the cluster size $N$ remains the same. So total system capacity increases by a factor of **4** (since area is reduced by 1/4, and hence the number of cells increases by 4).

**Effect on Capacity:** Cell splitting **increases capacity** by approximately **4 times** when the radius is halved.

**Limitation:** Cell splitting increases the number of base stations (and hence infrastructure cost), and the handoff rate also increases.

#### Part (b) — Sectoring (7 Marks)

**Definition:**

**Sectoring** is a technique to reduce co-channel interference and increase capacity by replacing the single omni-directional antenna at the base station with several **directional antennas**, each illuminating a sector of the original cell.

**Types of Sectoring:**

- **120° Sectoring:** 3 directional antennas, each covering 120°. Reduces the number of co-channel interferers from **6 to 2**.
- **60° Sectoring:** 6 directional antennas, each covering 60°. Reduces the number of co-channel interferers from **6 to 1**.

**Effect on S/I:**

For 120° sectoring, the new S/I is:

$$\frac{S}{I}\bigg|_{\text{sectored}} = \frac{Q^n}{2}$$

This is **3 times better** (4.77 dB improvement) than the omni-directional case where S/I = $Q^n/6$.

**Effect on Capacity:** Sectoring **reduces trunking efficiency** (since each sector has fewer channels), but the system can support more users per cell area overall because the cluster size $N$ can be reduced (since S/I has improved, a smaller $N$ is acceptable).

**Comparison Table:**

| Technique | Effect on Capacity | Effect on S/I | Handoff Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell Splitting | Increases ~4x (radius/2) | Maintained | Increases |
| 120° Sectoring | Slight increase (smaller N) | +4.77 dB | Increases |
| 60° Sectoring | Higher capacity | +7.78 dB | Increases more |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear scale** when computing the minimum cluster size. Always convert $\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{dB}$ to $\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(dB/10)}$ first.
> 2. **Using $i_0 = 6$ blindly** — this is for omni-directional antennas. For sectored systems, $i_0 = 2$ (120°) or $i_0 = 1$ (60°). Wrong $i_0$ gives wrong $N$.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up** to the nearest valid $N$ from the cluster set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, ...\}$. Picking the next integer (e.g., 11 instead of 12) is a common error.
> 4. **Confusing $Q$ and $D$:** $Q$ is a **dimensionless ratio** ($D/R$), while $D$ is a **distance** (in km). $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ and $Q = \sqrt{3N}$.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in Part B answers — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for well-labeled cluster diagrams. Always draw at least one cluster layout.
> 6. **Forgetting the formula $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$** derivation — many students use $N$ directly without justification, losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cell:** Basic unit of cellular coverage, served by one Base Station.
- **Hexagonal Cell:** Theoretically ideal shape for tessellating a plane.
- **Cluster Size $N$:** Number of cells per cluster; given by $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$.
- **Valid $N$ values:** $1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28, 31, ...$
- **Co-channel Reuse Distance $D$:** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$.
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio $Q$:** $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$.
- **S/I Formula (omni):** $\frac{S}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{i_0}$, with $i_0 = 6$.
- **S/I Formula (120° sectoring):** $\frac{S}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{2}$, with $i_0 = 2$.
- **S/I Formula (60° sectoring):** $\frac{S}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{1}$, with $i_0 = 1$.
- **Minimum Cluster Size Formula:** $N \geq \frac{[i_0 \cdot (S/I)_{\min}]^{2/n}}{3}$.
- **Cell Splitting:** Reduces cell radius; increases capacity by ~4x for radius/2.
- **Sectoring:** Uses directional antennas; reduces co-channel interferers.
- **Trade-off:** Smaller $N$ → higher capacity, more interference. Larger $N$ → less interference, lower capacity.
- **S/I must be in linear scale** before plugging into formulas.
- **Path loss exponent $n$** is typically 3 (urban), 2 (free space), or 4 (shadowed urban).
- **Hexagon area:** $A = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}R^2 \approx 2.598 R^2$.
- **Apothem (inradius) of hexagon:** $a = \frac{R\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
- **KTU expects the diagram** of cluster layout in any problem on frequency reuse.
- **Frequency reuse distance** $D$ increases with cluster size $N$, improving S/I.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**N-I-J-D-Q-S**" — **N** = $i^2+ij+j^2$, **I**nterferers $i_0$, **J**ust draw the diagram, **D** = $R\sqrt{3N}$, **Q** = $D/R$, **S**/I = $Q^n/i_0$. This covers 90% of all KTU Part B questions on this topic!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Off - Types and Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hand Off - Types and Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
